       SUM(usage_quantity) AS net_usage_quantity
FROM system.billing.usage
WHERE usage_date >= dateadd(day, -:period_days, current_date())
  AND usage_date < current_date()
  AND usage_unit = 'DBU'
  AND (usage_metadata.cluster_id IS NOT NULL
       OR usage_metadata.warehouse_id IS NOT NULL
       OR usage_metadata.instance_pool_id IS NOT NULL)
GROUP BY usage_date, cloud, workspace_id, billing_origin_product,
         usage_metadata.cluster_id, usage_metadata.warehouse_id, usage_metadata.instance_pool_id,
         product_features.is_serverless
Why it matters

This is the query that turns a vague "you have 149 right-sizing candidates" into "cluster X burned N DBUs a day for two weeks" — it pins DBU spend to the exact compute resource that generated it, so you can dollarize an expensive under-used cluster, spot the zero-event idle warehouses that still cost money, and track warehouse churn and instance-pool reserve. Getting the attribution right protects real money: every mis-attributed or dropped DBU is spend that lands on the wrong team's chargeback or hides waste. Because compute names do not live in billing, this rollup is the keyed foundation you resolve against config tables downstream to name-and-shame the worst offenders.

What it does
  • Totals your net DBUs and pins them to the individual compute resource that spent them — each cluster_id, warehouse_id, or instance_pool_id.
  • Slices every total by day, by workspace, and by product line (JOBS / SQL / DLT / …), and flags whether the compute was serverless or classic.
  • Keeps only DBU lines that carry a compute identifier; charges with no resource id (account-level lines and id-less serverless usage) fall outside this per-resource cut.
  • Returns numeric ids only — no cluster or warehouse names — so the output is meant to be name-resolved against the compute config tables afterward.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
usage_dateThe calendar day the consumption was metered. One row per resource per day; the window is the trailing :period_days days and excludes today.
cloudThe cloud provider (AWS / AZURE / GCP) the usage was billed on.
workspace_idNumeric id of the workspace the resource ran in. Resolve to dev/uat/prod via cost_workspace_names. Not name-attributable here.
billing_origin_productThe product line that generated the usage (JOBS, SQL, DLT, MODEL_SERVING, …). No published closed enum; a blank value means unattributed, not zero.
cluster_idThe classic (all-purpose or jobs) cluster that spent the DBUs, or NULL if this row is a warehouse or pool. Join to classic_clusters_config_current downstream for a name.
warehouse_idThe SQL warehouse that spent the DBUs, or NULL otherwise. Join to sql_warehouse_config_current downstream for a name. Populated for serverless SQL warehouses too.
instance_pool_idThe instance pool the usage drew from, or NULL otherwise. Identifies pool reserve/idle backing cost.
is_serverlessWhether this resource's usage was serverless (true) or classic (false); NULL where the distinction does not apply. Read from product_features.is_serverless.
net_usage_quantityThe net DBUs consumed — SUM(usage_quantity) across all record_types, so corrections (RETRACTION / RESTATEMENT) already net out. This is DBUs, NOT dollars; multiply by a per-DBU rate to dollarize.
Sample output
usage_datecloudworkspace_idbilling_origin_productwarehouse_idis_serverlessnet_usage_quantity
2026-07-04AWS1234567890123456SQLabc123warehouse01true512.7
2026-07-04AWS1234567890123456JOBSNULLfalse1843.2
2026-07-03AZURE9876543210987654SQLdef456warehouse02true0.0
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.billing.usageThe sole source. Provides the billed-consumption fact rows; the query filters to DBU usage that carries a specific compute id, reads the compute identifiers from the usage_metadata struct (cluster_id / warehouse_id / instance_pool_id), the serverless flag from product_features, and nets usage_quantity across all record_types.
Caveats & gotchas
  • DBUs, not dollars: net_usage_quantity is DBU consumption; multiply by a list/negotiated rate to get money.
  • Net across record_types: the SUM already folds in RETRACTION/RESTATEMENT corrections — never filter to ORIGINAL and never re-net downstream.
  • Resource ids are sparse by design: cluster_id, warehouse_id, and instance_pool_id populate only for the compute type that generated the row; the other two are NULL. This cut keeps only rows with at least one populated.
  • Only rows with a compute id survive: the WHERE clause drops lines where all three ids are NULL — that is account-level charges and any id-less serverless usage. Serverless SQL warehouses DO carry a warehouse_id, so they ARE kept and flagged is_serverless = true; 'serverless' is not wholesale excluded. Use the totals query for the full picture.
  • No names in billing: output is numeric ids only. Resolve cluster_id and warehouse_id against the compute config tables (classic_clusters_config_current / sql_warehouse_config_current), and workspace_id via cost_workspace_names — downstream, not by joining names in here.
  • A zero net_usage_quantity is a real signal (e.g. an idle warehouse), not an error; an empty result means no matching compute usage, never $0.
  • Availability: requires Unity Catalog with system.billing enabled and SELECT granted; otherwise the source can raise TABLE_OR_VIEW_NOT_FOUND (degrade to 'not assessed', not zero).
  • Date window: trailing :period_days days, excludes today; treat the most recent day as provisional due to billing populate lag (ingestion_date, not usage_date, is the freshness signal).
→
What you do next
Rank resources by net_usage_quantity over the window, resolve the top cluster_ids and warehouse_ids to names via the config tables, dollarize with a per-DBU rate, and target the expensive under-used clusters for right-sizing and the zero-DBU warehouses for auto-stop or deletion.
← PreviousWhere the Money Goes by Productcost_by_billing_origin_product All Cost & Billing ↑ Next →Ad-hoc notebook DBU costcost_by_notebook
Overview / Cost & Billing / cost_by_notebook
09
Cost & Billing cost_by_notebook

Ad-hoc notebook DBU cost

Net DBU consumption per notebook-attached interactive/all-purpose notebook, sliced by day, cloud, workspace, product line, and serverless-vs-classic.

system.billing.usage
queries/cost/cost_by_notebook.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:cost_by_notebook */
SELECT usage_date, cloud, workspace_id, billing_origin_product,
       usage_metadata.notebook_id     AS notebook_id,
       product_features.is_serverless AS is_serverless,
       SUM(usage_quantity) AS net_usage_quantity
FROM system.billing.usage
WHERE usage_date >= dateadd(day, -:period_days, current_date())
  AND usage_date < current_date()
  AND usage_unit = 'DBU'
  AND usage_metadata.notebook_id IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY usage_date, cloud, workspace_id, billing_origin_product,
         usage_metadata.notebook_id, product_features.is_serverless
Why it matters

Interactive notebook work is human ad-hoc compute — usually a small slice of the bill (the workload split expects roughly 3% ad-hoc), but a single runaway notebook left attached to an oversized all-purpose cluster can quietly burn DBUs for days. This query puts a dollar-able number on that ad-hoc tier and surfaces the one or two notebooks that dominate it, per workspace. It confirms whether ad-hoc really is negligible or has crept up, and gives you the specific notebook_id to chase down.

What it does
  • Totals net DBUs consumed by each notebook that ran on notebook-attached interactive/all-purpose compute.
  • Slices that consumption by day, cloud, workspace_id, and product line so you see where and when ad-hoc work happens.
  • Splits each notebook's usage into serverless vs classic via is_serverless.
  • Only covers notebook-attributable usage — jobs, serverless-editor, and SQL rows (which carry no notebook_id) are excluded entirely, so this is the ad-hoc tier in isolation.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
usage_dateThe calendar day (DATE) the consumption was metered; the trailing window excludes today, and the most recent day is provisional due to billing lag.
cloudThe cloud provider (AWS/AZURE/GCP) the usage was billed on; part of the correct price key if you dollarize later.
workspace_idThe numeric workspace that ran the notebook; resolve to a dev/uat/prod name via system.access.workspaces_latest downstream.
billing_origin_productThe product line that generated the row (typically all-purpose/interactive compute here); blank means unattributed, not zero.
notebook_idThe notebook this DBU consumption is attributed to; there is no notebook NAME in billing, so this id is your only handle — resolve it outside billing.
is_serverlessWhether the notebook ran on serverless (true) or classic (false) compute; drives which rate applies and flags serverless premium.
net_usage_quantityNet DBUs consumed (SUM of usage_quantity across all record_type corrections) for that notebook/day/slice — DBUs, NOT dollars; multiply by a per-DBU rate to get money.
Sample output
usage_datecloudworkspace_idbilling_origin_productnotebook_idis_serverlessnet_usage_quantity
2026-07-03AWS1234567890123456INTERACTIVEa1b2c3d4-5e6f-7a8b-9c0d-1e2f3a4b5c6dfalse48.20
2026-07-03AWS1234567890123456INTERACTIVEf9e8d7c6-b5a4-3210-9f8e-7d6c5b4a3210true6.35
2026-07-04AZURE9876543210987654INTERACTIVEa1b2c3d4-5e6f-7a8b-9c0d-1e2f3a4b5c6dfalse51.90
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.billing.usageThe billed-consumption fact table and sole source for this query; rows are filtered to DBU usage that carries a non-null usage_metadata.notebook_id (notebook-attached interactive/all-purpose compute), then net DBUs are summed across all record_type corrections per notebook per day.
Caveats & gotchas
  • Confidence is needs_confirmation: usage_metadata.notebook_id is documented but populates only for notebook-attached all-purpose/interactive usage — verify it is non-empty in this account before promising a per-notebook view.
  • notebook_id is NULL for jobs, serverless-editor, and SQL usage, so those workloads are absent by design — an empty result means no attributable ad-hoc notebook usage, not zero spend and not an error.
  • No notebook NAME exists in billing; usage_metadata.notebook_path may or may not exist per account. The id must be resolved to a human name elsewhere.
  • net_usage_quantity is DBUs, not dollars — this query does not dollarize; join to a per-DBU price window (keyed on cloud + currency_code + usage_unit + sku_name) to get money, never sum units as dollars.
  • Net figure sums usage_quantity across all record_type values (ORIGINAL/RETRACTION/RESTATEMENT); the query does not filter record_type, so never add a filter to ORIGINAL.
  • Trailing window: usage_date >= current_date - :period_days and < current_date(), so today is excluded and the latest day is provisional (billing populate lag; ingestion_date is the freshness signal).
  • Requires Unity Catalog with system.billing enabled and SELECT granted; ad-hoc notebook cost is expected to be small, so treat this as a confirm-and-flag view — quantify the ~3% ad-hoc tier and catch any single runaway notebook.
→
What you do next
Confirm notebook_id is actually populated, then rank notebooks by net_usage_quantity to verify ad-hoc is the expected small share; chase the top notebook_id (resolved to a name/owner outside billing) if any single notebook is disproportionately large, and check whether it should move off an oversized all-purpose cluster.
← PreviousCost Per Cluster and Warehousecost_by_compute_resource All Cost & Billing ↑ Next →Serving & Vector Spend by Endpointcost_by_serving_endpoint
Overview / Cost & Billing / cost_by_serving_endpoint
10
Cost & Billing cost_by_serving_endpoint

Serving & Vector Spend by Endpoint

A daily breakdown of Model Serving and Vector Search DBU consumption per endpoint, workspace, and usage type, so you can see which AI endpoints drive the bill.

system.billing.usage
queries/cost/cost_by_serving_endpoint.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:cost_by_serving_endpoint */
SELECT usage_date, cloud, workspace_id, billing_origin_product,
       usage_metadata.endpoint_id AS endpoint_id,
       CASE WHEN usage_metadata.endpoint_name IS NULL THEN usage_metadata.endpoint_name
            ELSE concat(substr(usage_metadata.endpoint_name, 1, 2), '****') END AS endpoint_name,
       usage_type,
       SUM(usage_quantity) AS net_usage_quantity
FROM system.billing.usage
WHERE usage_date >= dateadd(day, -:period_days, current_date())
  AND usage_date < current_date()
  AND usage_unit = 'DBU'
  AND billing_origin_product IN ('MODEL_SERVING', 'VECTOR_SEARCH')
GROUP BY usage_date, cloud, workspace_id, billing_origin_product,
         usage_metadata.endpoint_id, usage_metadata.endpoint_name, usage_type
Why it matters

Model Serving and Vector Search together make up the large AI slice of the account bill (roughly two-thirds plus one-seventh in the reference account, ~78%), yet that spend hides behind opaque endpoint ids. This query sizes that spend endpoint-by-endpoint and day-by-day, so the biggest and fastest-growing endpoints surface for right-sizing first. It is a "where the 78% goes" map, not the fix: the actual right-sizing levers (scale-to-zero, provisioned vs used throughput) live only in the serving-endpoints API, not in any system table.

What it does
  • Totals DBU consumption per serving/vector endpoint per day, so each AI endpoint's spend footprint and daily trend are visible.
  • Covers only the MODEL_SERVING and VECTOR_SEARCH product lines, in DBUs (not dollars).
  • Splits each endpoint's usage by usage type (COMPUTE_TIME vs GPU_TIME vs TOKEN) and keeps workspace and cloud separate.
  • Masks the endpoint name to first-2-chars + ****; the raw endpoint id passes through for correlation.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
usage_dateThe calendar day (DATE) the consumption was metered. The window is a trailing `:period_days` days and excludes today, so the most recent day is provisional.
cloudThe cloud provider for the row (AWS / AZURE / GCP). Part of the grain so multi-cloud endpoints stay separated.
workspace_idThe workspace that generated the usage. NULL means account-level / not workspace-attributable. Resolve to dev/uat/prod names via `cost_workspace_names` downstream.
billing_origin_productWhich AI product line the row belongs to — `MODEL_SERVING` or `VECTOR_SEARCH` (the only two in scope).
endpoint_idThe stable serving/vector endpoint identifier (passed through unmasked). Use this to correlate to the serving-endpoints API. May be NULL if the metadata field was not populated on a row.
endpoint_nameThe human endpoint name, masked to its first two characters + `****`. NULL is passed through unchanged (name unavailable), not masked.
usage_typeThe metered dimension for the DBUs — e.g. `COMPUTE_TIME`, `GPU_TIME`, or `TOKEN` — so CPU serving, GPU serving, and token-metered usage are kept apart. Not filtered by the query; these are the values expected for serving/vector rows.
net_usage_quantityNet DBUs consumed for that endpoint/day/usage_type, summed across all record types (ORIGINAL + RETRACTION + RESTATEMENT already net out). This is DBUs, NOT dollars — multiply by a per-DBU rate to dollarize.
Sample output
usage_dateworkspace_idbilling_origin_productendpoint_idendpoint_nameusage_typenet_usage_quantity
2026-07-043419704829384756MODEL_SERVINGep-0a1b2c3d4e5f6789ll****GPU_TIME842.6
2026-07-043419704829384756MODEL_SERVINGep-0a1b2c3d4e5f6789ll****TOKEN15320.0
2026-07-038820193746501122VECTOR_SEARCHep-9f8e7d6c5b4a3210pr****COMPUTE_TIME57.3
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.billing.usageThe sole source. Provides the billed-consumption rows filtered to `billing_origin_product IN ('MODEL_SERVING','VECTOR_SEARCH')` and `usage_unit = 'DBU'`; the query pulls the per-endpoint identity from `usage_metadata.endpoint_id` / `usage_metadata.endpoint_name`, the `usage_type` split (COMPUTE_TIME / GPU_TIME / TOKEN), and SUMs `usage_quantity` (DBUs) across all record types.
Caveats & gotchas
  • `net_usage_quantity` is **DBUs, not dollars** — the query does no price join. Dollarize downstream by joining a per-DBU rate (`list_prices` / `account_prices`); those price casts are unverified, so keep derived dollars labelled list/negotiated-unverified.
  • **Empty result is a real answer, not $0:** it means no serving/vector usage in the window. A missing/Preview or non-permissioned table instead raises `TABLE_OR_VIEW_NOT_FOUND` — both are expected, neither is a fabricated zero.
  • This sizes the **spend**, not the fix — scale-to-zero and provisioned-vs-used throughput config are only in the serving-endpoints API, absent from all system tables.
  • `usage_type` separates `COMPUTE_TIME` / `GPU_TIME` / `TOKEN` — do not sum across them as if interchangeable; they are different metering bases even though all are DBU here.
  • `endpoint_name` is masked to first-2-chars + `****`; `endpoint_id` passes through. NULL name/id means the metadata was not populated on that row, not zero usage.
  • `workspace_id` can be NULL (account-level); names are not in billing — resolve via `cost_workspace_names` downstream, keyed on `workspace_id`.
  • Requires Unity Catalog with `system.billing` enabled and `SELECT` granted. Window is trailing `:period_days` days and **excludes today**; treat the latest day as provisional due to billing populate lag.
  • Nets across all `record_type` values by design — never filter to `ORIGINAL` or you double-count corrections.
→
What you do next
Rank endpoints by `net_usage_quantity` (and by its day-over-day slope) to spot the biggest and fastest-growing serving/vector endpoints, then take the top offenders to the serving-endpoints API to right-size them (enable scale-to-zero, cut provisioned throughput, or retire idle endpoints).
← PreviousAd-hoc notebook DBU costcost_by_notebook All Cost & Billing ↑ Next →Chargeback DBUs by Identitycost_chargeback_by_identity
Overview / Cost & Billing / cost_chargeback_by_identity
11
Cost & Billing cost_chargeback_by_identity

Chargeback DBUs by Identity

Net DBU consumption rolled up per identity (human user vs service principal, with masked run-as / owner / creator) sliced by day, cloud, workspace, and product line.

system.billing.usage
queries/cost/cost_chargeback_by_identity.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:cost_chargeback_by_identity */
SELECT usage_date, cloud, workspace_id, billing_origin_product,
       CASE
         WHEN identity_metadata.run_as IS NULL OR identity_metadata.run_as = '__REDACTED__' THEN 'unknown'
         WHEN identity_metadata.run_as LIKE '%@%' THEN 'user'
         ELSE 'service_principal'
       END                          AS identity_type,
       CASE
         WHEN identity_metadata.run_as IS NULL OR identity_metadata.run_as = '__REDACTED__' THEN identity_metadata.run_as
         WHEN identity_metadata.run_as LIKE '%@%' THEN concat(substr(identity_metadata.run_as, 1, 2), '****@****')
         WHEN identity_metadata.run_as RLIKE '^[0-9a-fA-F]{8}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{12}$' THEN identity_metadata.run_as
         ELSE concat(substr(identity_metadata.run_as, 1, 2), '****')
       END                          AS identity_run_as,
       CASE
         WHEN identity_metadata.owned_by IS NULL OR identity_metadata.owned_by = '__REDACTED__' THEN identity_metadata.owned_by
         WHEN identity_metadata.owned_by LIKE '%@%' THEN concat(substr(identity_metadata.owned_by, 1, 2), '****@****')
         WHEN identity_metadata.owned_by RLIKE '^[0-9a-fA-F]{8}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{12}$' THEN identity_metadata.owned_by
         ELSE concat(substr(identity_metadata.owned_by, 1, 2), '****')
       END                          AS identity_owned_by,
       CASE
         WHEN identity_metadata.created_by IS NULL OR identity_metadata.created_by = '__REDACTED__' THEN identity_metadata.created_by
         WHEN identity_metadata.created_by LIKE '%@%' THEN concat(substr(identity_metadata.created_by, 1, 2), '****@****')
         WHEN identity_metadata.created_by RLIKE '^[0-9a-fA-F]{8}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{12}$' THEN identity_metadata.created_by
         ELSE concat(substr(identity_metadata.created_by, 1, 2), '****')
       END                          AS identity_created_by,
       SUM(usage_quantity) AS net_usage_quantity
FROM system.billing.usage
WHERE usage_date >= dateadd(day, -:period_days, current_date())
  AND usage_date < current_date()
GROUP BY usage_date, cloud, workspace_id, billing_origin_product,
         CASE
           WHEN identity_metadata.run_as IS NULL OR identity_metadata.run_as = '__REDACTED__' THEN 'unknown'
           WHEN identity_metadata.run_as LIKE '%@%' THEN 'user'
           ELSE 'service_principal'
         END,
         identity_metadata.run_as, identity_metadata.owned_by, identity_metadata.created_by
Why it matters

This is the identity-level chargeback cut: it tells you which human user or service principal is actually driving DBU consumption, so spend can be attributed to a person or automated workload even when custom tags are missing. It exposes the service-principal-vs-human split (runaway automation vs interactive human cost) and surfaces untagged-but-attributable spend you can still bill back. Because identity_metadata is sparse and blanked to '__REDACTED__' in FedRamp workspaces, the query deliberately buckets unavailable identities as 'unknown' rather than inventing a principal — telling you honestly how much spend cannot yet be pinned to anyone.

What it does
  • Totals your net DBU consumption per identity, so you can bill it back to a specific human user or service principal.
  • Splits every identity into user vs service_principal vs unknown, cleanly separating human interactive spend from automated / service-account spend.
  • Slices each identity's usage by day, cloud, workspace, and product line for a per-workspace, per-product chargeback view.
  • Carries three masked identity angles — who a workload runs as, who owns it (SQL-warehouse usage only), and who created it — with emails and names redacted but UUIDs and __REDACTED__ passed through.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
usage_dateThe calendar day (DATE) the consumption was metered. The window is a trailing :period_days days and excludes today, so the latest day is provisional.
cloudThe cloud provider for the usage (e.g. AWS / AZURE / GCP). Part of the chargeback grain.
workspace_idNumeric workspace the usage is attributed to. NULL means an account-level SKU that is not workspace-attributable. Resolve to dev/uat/prod names downstream via cost_workspace_names.
billing_origin_productProduct line that generated the usage (JOBS / SQL / DLT / MODEL_SERVING / …). No published closed enum; a blank value is unattributed, not zero.
identity_typeBucket for the run_as principal: 'user' when run_as is an email, 'service_principal' otherwise, and 'unknown' when run_as is NULL or '__REDACTED__' (identity unavailable, not a real principal).
identity_run_asMasked principal the workload executed as — the primary chargeback key. Emails shown as first-2-chars + '****@****', non-UUID names as first-2-chars + '****'; UUIDs and '__REDACTED__'/NULL pass through unchanged.
identity_owned_byMasked owner of the resource (populated for SQL-warehouse usage only; NULL/'__REDACTED__' otherwise). Same masking rules as identity_run_as.
identity_created_byMasked creator of the resource, same masking rules. Sparse and often NULL.
net_usage_quantitySUM(usage_quantity) for this identity/day/cloud/workspace/product, summed across ALL record_types so corrections are already netted out. NOT dollars — multiply by a price rate to dollarize. Caution: usage_unit is NOT in the grouping, so this sums quantity regardless of unit; it is predominantly DBUs but can blend DBU/bytes/hours/tokens where a product line meters more than one unit — split by usage_unit before dollarizing.
Sample output
usage_dateworkspace_idbilling_origin_productidentity_typeidentity_run_asnet_usage_quantity
2026-07-041234567890123456JOBSservice_principal3f8a9c21-1b2c-4d5e-8f90-a1b2c3d4e5f6842.75
2026-07-041234567890123456SQLuserda****@****57.20
2026-07-042233445566778899DLTunknown__REDACTED__410.00
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.billing.usageThe sole source: the account's billed-consumption fact table. The query sums usage_quantity across all record_types (net of corrections) and groups by the identity_metadata struct (run_as / owned_by / created_by) plus day, cloud, workspace, and billing_origin_product. Note usage_unit is NOT in the grouping, so the sum is not unit-scoped.
Caveats & gotchas
  • identity_metadata is sparse and conditional; in FedRamp workspaces it is blanked to the literal '__REDACTED__'. Treat both '__REDACTED__' and NULL as 'identity unavailable' (bucketed 'unknown'), never as a real principal.
  • owned_by populates for SQL-warehouse usage only; created_by is often NULL. Expect many blank owner/creator cells outside SQL.
  • net_usage_quantity is SUM(usage_quantity) with usage_unit NOT in the GROUP BY, so a single output row can blend DBU/bytes/hours/tokens where a product line meters more than one unit. It is DBUs for most rows but is NOT dollars — split by usage_unit and join a price rate before dollarizing; never sum unrelated unit families as one number.
  • Net corrections: usage_quantity is summed across all record_types (ORIGINAL/RETRACTION/RESTATEMENT), so it is already net — never re-net downstream or filter to ORIGINAL.
  • Emails are masked to 'ab****@****' and non-UUID names to 'ab****' in the SQL; UUIDs and '__REDACTED__' pass through. Drop the mask CASE (or run a --no-redact variant) for an internal user leaderboard.
  • Trailing :period_days-day window that excludes today (today's billing is incomplete); the most recent day is provisional due to populate lag.
  • workspace_id is NULL for account-level SKUs; billing has no workspace/user names — resolve workspace_id via cost_workspace_names downstream.
  • Requires Unity Catalog with system.billing enabled and SELECT granted; a feature the account doesn't use yields a valid empty result, which is not $0 and not an error.
→
What you do next
Rank identities by net_usage_quantity to build a chargeback / SP-vs-human leaderboard: bill the top consumers back to their team, chase the service principals driving automated spend, and quantify the 'unknown' bucket as the share of usage you cannot yet attribute — then close that gap with tags or de-redaction. Scope to a single usage_unit (e.g. DBU) before dollarizing.
← PreviousServing & Vector Spend by Endpointcost_by_serving_endpoint All Cost & Billing ↑ Next →The Non-DBU Cloud Spendcost_cloud_infra
Overview / Cost & Billing / cost_cloud_infra
12
Cost & Billing cost_cloud_infra

The Non-DBU Cloud Spend

A daily rollup of the cloud provider's own infrastructure and network/egress charges (real billed dollars, not DBUs) broken out by cloud and currency over the trailing window.

system.billing.cloud_infra_cost
queries/cost/cost_cloud_infra.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:cost_cloud_infra */
SELECT usage_date, cloud, currency_code,
       SUM(cost)  AS net_infra_cost,
       COUNT(*)   AS record_count
FROM system.billing.cloud_infra_cost
WHERE usage_date >= dateadd(day, -:period_days, current_date())
  AND usage_date < current_date()
GROUP BY usage_date, cloud, currency_code
Why it matters

This is the 10-25% of Databricks spend the `usage`/DBU tables never show: the cloud provider's own instance-hour and network/egress bill. Unlike every other query in this domain, `cost` here is a real billed dollar, not a DBU that still needs a price rate applied, so it directly closes the gap between the DBU-based cost model and the actual invoice. Ignoring it means under-reporting true platform cost and mis-sizing egress-heavy or instance-heavy workloads. An empty result is meaningful too: it signals the cloud-provider cost export is not enabled, not that infra cost is zero.

What it does
  • Totals the real billed dollars of non-DBU cloud infrastructure and egress cost per day, so you see spend that the DBU usage tables completely miss.
  • Slices that spend by cloud and by currency_code, keeping each currency on its own line rather than blending them.
  • Reports a per-row record_count alongside each daily total as a coverage/sanity signal for how many infra cost slices rolled up.
  • Covers a trailing :period_days window and deliberately excludes today; an empty result means the cloud-infra export is not enabled, not $0.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
usage_dateThe billing day the infra cost was incurred. The window is the trailing `:period_days` days and excludes today, so the most recent day present may still be filling in.
cloudThe cloud provider the charge came from (e.g. `AWS`). In practice this is almost always AWS, since the source table is largely AWS-only.
currency_codeThe currency of the `net_infra_cost` dollar figure on this row. Rows in different currencies are kept separate and must never be summed together.
net_infra_costThe summed real billed cloud-infrastructure/egress cost for that day/cloud/currency, expressed in the row's currency. This is an actual dollar amount, NOT a DBU quantity.
record_countHow many underlying infra-cost slices rolled into this row — a coverage/sanity signal, not a cost. A count that suddenly drops can indicate export gaps.
Sample output
usage_datecloudcurrency_codenet_infra_costrecord_count
2026-07-04AWSUSD1284.55312
2026-07-03AWSUSD1197.02308
2026-07-04AWSEUR64.1011
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.billing.cloud_infra_costThe sole source. Holds the cloud provider's own infrastructure charge (instance hours, network/egress) that sits outside DBU pricing. The query sums its monetary column (`cost`, a real billed dollar) and counts rows, grouped by the table's own `usage_date` / `cloud` / `currency_code` dimensions. Largely AWS-only and empty on many accounts.
Caveats & gotchas
  • `cost` is a REAL billed dollar in the row's currency (instance hours + network/egress), NOT a DBU — it is the one column in this domain that is already money and needs no price rate applied.
  • Preview and largely AWS-only. Many accounts have this table empty because the cloud-provider cost export / network policy is not enabled — an empty result must render 'not assessed', never $0.
  • Empty != zero != error: a missing/disabled or non-permissioned table can raise TABLE_OR_VIEW_NOT_FOUND; both empty and error degrade honestly to 'not assessed', never a fabricated 0.
  • Column names are unverified on-workspace: the monetary column is assumed to be `cost` (DOUBLE) and the date column `usage_date` per the roadmap. If `cost` is absent the query errors and the source degrades to not_assessed rather than fabricating a 0; if `currency_code` is absent the rollup still binds on `usage_date` + `cloud`.
  • Do NOT LEFT JOIN compute change-history tables (`compute.warehouses` / `clusters`) into this rollup — they fan out rows and double-count SUM(cost). Any warehouse/cluster attribution must first dedupe the dimension to its latest row per id, in a separate step.
  • Never sum `net_infra_cost` across different `currency_code` rows — convert first if a single total is needed.
  • Date window is the trailing `:period_days` days and excludes today (today's billing is incomplete); the most recent day is provisional due to populate lag.
  • This is separate from the DBU `usage` table — add it to DBU-based cost, do not reconcile it against DBUs as if they were the same number.
→
What you do next
If rows are present, add `net_infra_cost` (per currency) on top of your DBU-dollarized spend to get true platform cost, and watch egress-heavy days; if the result is empty, treat cloud-infra as 'not assessed' and enable the cloud-provider cost export / network policy (AWS) so this ~10-25% of spend becomes visible.
← PreviousChargeback DBUs by Identitycost_chargeback_by_identity All Cost & Billing ↑ Next →DBSQL Allocation Gap Checkcost_dbsql_allocation_gap
Overview / Cost & Billing / cost_dbsql_allocation_gap
13
Cost & Billing cost_dbsql_allocation_gap

DBSQL Allocation Gap Check

Two labelled daily DBU rollups for SQL-warehouse (DBSQL) usage in one result set — one from raw billing, one from Databricks' fair-split attribution — so you can difference them to size unattributed / cross-subsidized shared-pool DBUs.

system.billing.usagesystem.billing.attributed_usage
queries/cost/cost_dbsql_allocation_gap.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:cost_dbsql_allocation_gap */
SELECT 'raw' AS source_kind,
       u.usage_date, u.cloud, u.billing_origin_product, u.usage_unit,
       SUM(u.usage_quantity) AS net_usage_quantity
FROM system.billing.usage u
WHERE u.usage_date >= dateadd(day, -:period_days, current_date())
  AND u.usage_date < current_date()
  AND upper(u.usage_unit) = 'DBU'
  AND u.billing_origin_product = 'SQL'   -- DBSQL scope ONLY (attributed_usage doesn't cover jobs/DLT)
GROUP BY u.usage_date, u.cloud, u.billing_origin_product, u.usage_unit
UNION ALL
SELECT 'attributed' AS source_kind,
       a.usage_date, a.cloud, a.billing_origin_product, a.usage_unit,
       SUM(a.usage_quantity) AS net_usage_quantity
FROM system.billing.attributed_usage a
WHERE a.usage_date >= dateadd(day, -:period_days, current_date())
  AND a.usage_date < current_date()
  AND upper(a.usage_unit) = 'DBU'
  AND a.billing_origin_product = 'SQL'   -- same DBSQL scope on both sides
GROUP BY a.usage_date, a.cloud, a.billing_origin_product, a.usage_unit
Why it matters

Shared SQL-warehouse pools quietly cross-subsidize teams: one group's queries run on a pool another cost center pays for, so the raw bill and the fair-split attribution diverge. The difference between the two rollups is the DBSQL DBU volume that raw billing shows but attribution can't tie to a consuming entity — the pool of spend you cannot fairly charge back. Sizing that gap tells you how much of your SQL-warehouse bill is currently unattributable, and whether shared pools are masking who is really driving cost.

What it does
  • Returns two labelled rollups in one result — a raw side straight from the billing fact table and an attributed side from Databricks' fair-split table — both scoped to DBSQL only (billing_origin_product = 'SQL'), so you difference them yourself rather than the query pre-computing a gap.
  • Reports net DBUs per day, with billing corrections already netted out, so each side is a clean daily consumption total you can line up against the other.
  • Keeps the two sources side by side rather than reconciling them internally — the allocation gap is simply the raw total minus the attributed total for the same day and cloud, exposing the DBSQL DBUs billing shows but attribution can't tie to a consumer.
  • Everything is in DBUs, not dollars, and covers a trailing :period_days window ending yesterday.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
source_kindWhich rollup the row belongs to: 'raw' (from system.billing.usage) or 'attributed' (from system.billing.attributed_usage). Difference the two for the same usage_date/cloud to get the allocation gap; never row-join them.
usage_dateThe calendar day of consumption (excludes today, which is still incomplete).
cloudCloud provider for the usage (e.g. AWS/AZURE/GCP). Line raw up against attributed within the same cloud when differencing.
billing_origin_productProduct line — always 'SQL' here, since both sides are scoped to DBSQL. Confirm 'SQL' is the literal your account uses for SQL warehouses (check cost_by_billing_origin_product).
usage_unitThe metering unit — always 'DBU' here (filtered). Confirms the figure is DBUs, not dollars.
net_usage_quantityNet DBUs consumed for that day/cloud, summed across all record_types so RETRACTION/RESTATEMENT corrections are already netted. Compare the 'raw' value against the 'attributed' value to size the gap.
Sample output
source_kindusage_datecloudbilling_origin_productusage_unitnet_usage_quantity
raw2026-07-04AWSSQLDBU1840.5
attributed2026-07-04AWSSQLDBU1612.0
raw2026-07-04AZURESQLDBU730.0
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.billing.usageThe raw billed-consumption side. Supplies net DBUs per day for DBSQL usage (billing_origin_product = 'SQL'), summed across all record_types so corrections net out. Labelled source_kind = 'raw'.
system.billing.attributed_usageDatabricks' fair-split allocation of shared-pool DBUs back to the consuming entity, DBSQL-only. Supplies the attributed net DBUs for the same SQL scope. Labelled source_kind = 'attributed'. Schema assumed to mirror billing.usage and unverified on-workspace.
Caveats & gotchas
  • DBUs, not dollars — net_usage_quantity is DBU volume; multiply by a SQL SKU price rate to get money.
  • attributed_usage coverage is DBSQL (SQL warehouses) ONLY — it does not track jobs/DLT/all-purpose. The gap is meaningful only because BOTH sides are scoped to billing_origin_product = 'SQL'; comparing raw-all vs attributed would report a fake gap for everything attribution simply doesn't track.
  • Do NOT join the two tables row-to-row, and never equate run_as = executed_by — run_as is the workload's principal, the attributed identity is the query author; treating them as the same is the must-fix identity bug. The query emits both rollups via UNION ALL and lets you difference them.
  • attributed_usage schema is assumed to mirror billing.usage (usage_quantity, usage_unit, usage_date, billing_origin_product, cloud) but is NOT verified on-workspace; a missing column errors the query, which should degrade to 'not assessed' rather than fabricate a 0 (confidence: needs_confirmation).
  • Confirm the literal product value for SQL warehouses on this account — 'SQL' is assumed; verify against cost_by_billing_origin_product.
  • Net figures SUM usage_quantity across all record_types (corrections already net) and filter usage_unit = 'DBU' — never re-net downstream or filter to ORIGINAL.
  • Trailing :period_days window, excludes today (incomplete billing); treat the most recent day as provisional due to populate lag.
  • Empty != zero != error: no rows can mean no DBSQL usage (valid empty), while a Preview/disabled/non-permissioned attributed_usage table can raise TABLE_OR_VIEW_NOT_FOUND — neither is $0.
  • Both tables require Unity Catalog with system.billing enabled and SELECT granted.
→
What you do next
Difference the raw and attributed net_usage_quantity for each usage_date/cloud: a persistent positive gap flags DBSQL DBUs that can't be attributed to a consumer — i.e. cross-subsidized shared SQL-warehouse pools. Track the unattributed % over time, and split or tag heavily shared warehouses so their spend becomes chargeable to the teams actually driving it.
← PreviousThe Non-DBU Cloud Spendcost_cloud_infra All Cost & Billing ↑ Next →Default-Storage DSU by API Tiercost_default_storage_dsu
Overview / Cost & Billing / cost_default_storage_dsu
14
Cost & Billing cost_default_storage_dsu

Default-Storage DSU by API Tier

Net default-storage (DSU) usage broken out per day, cloud, SKU, storage API tier (TIER_1 vs TIER_2), and catalog.

system.billing.usage
queries/cost/cost_default_storage_dsu.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:cost_default_storage_dsu */
-- NEEDS CONFIRMATION: billing_origin_product = 'DEFAULT_STORAGE' literal is UNVERIFIED.
-- Using the confirmed signal as the primary filter: WHERE usage_metadata.storage_api_type IS NOT NULL
SELECT usage_date, cloud, sku_name, usage_type, usage_unit,
       usage_metadata.storage_api_type AS storage_api_type,
       usage_metadata.catalog_id       AS catalog_id,
       SUM(usage_quantity) AS net_usage_quantity
FROM system.billing.usage
WHERE usage_date >= dateadd(day, -:period_days, current_date())
  AND usage_date < current_date()
  AND usage_metadata.storage_api_type IS NOT NULL   -- confirmed default-storage signal (safer fallback)
GROUP BY usage_date, cloud, sku_name, usage_type, usage_unit,
         usage_metadata.storage_api_type, usage_metadata.catalog_id
Why it matters

Default (managed) storage and its per-API-operation charges are easy to overlook because they show up as bytes and API operations rather than headline compute DBUs, yet they accrue continuously across every catalog. Splitting the usage by TIER_1 vs TIER_2 API operations shows whether a workload is hammering the more expensive tier, and the per-catalog cut points at which managed catalog is driving the growth. This is the signal an admin uses to catch a runaway ingestion or listing pattern before it compounds into a material line item.

What it does
  • Totals your default-storage (DSU) consumption over the trailing window, keeping only rows that carry a storage-API signal — the confirmed way to isolate managed storage without relying on an unverified product literal.
  • Slices that total by day, cloud, sku_name, usage_type, and usage_unit so each storage flavour (stored bytes vs API operations) stays in its own unit-correct bucket.
  • Breaks the storage activity into TIER_1 vs TIER_2 via storage_api_type, and attributes every slice to the owning catalog_id.
  • Returns a net figure that already folds in billing corrections, so no restatement double-counts the storage total.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
usage_dateThe calendar day the storage usage was metered. The most recent day in the window is provisional due to billing populate lag.
cloudThe cloud provider for the row (AWS/AZURE/GCP). Same catalog on different clouds bills as separate rows.
sku_nameThe billing SKU for this default-storage charge — the pricing basis; dollarize it against a price table downstream, not here.
usage_typeThe kind of metered activity, e.g. STORAGE_SPACE (bytes at rest) or API_OPERATION (per-operation storage calls). Never blend the two.
usage_unitThe unit net_usage_quantity is measured in for this row (e.g. STORAGE_SPACE bytes vs API operation counts). Rows with different units are never summed together.
storage_api_typeThe default-storage API tier — typically TIER_1 vs TIER_2 — indicating the operation class; the presence of this value is what marks the row as default storage.
catalog_idUUID of the Unity Catalog whose managed storage generated the usage; use it to attribute cost to a specific catalog.
net_usage_quantityNet usage in usage_unit (bytes or API-operation count — NOT dollars), summed across all record_type corrections so retractions/restatements already net out.
Sample output
usage_datecloudsku_nameusage_typeusage_unitstorage_api_typecatalog_idnet_usage_quantity
2026-07-04AWSSTANDARD_STORAGEAPI_OPERATIONAPI_OPERATIONTIER_1c1a2b3c4-5d6e-7f80-9012-abcdef012345184320
2026-07-04AWSSTANDARD_STORAGEAPI_OPERATIONAPI_OPERATIONTIER_2c1a2b3c4-5d6e-7f80-9012-abcdef0123455120
2026-07-04AWSSTANDARD_STORAGESTORAGE_SPACESTORAGE_SPACETIER_1d2b3c4d5-6e7f-8091-a234-bcdef01234569663676416
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.billing.usageThe sole source. Provides the billed default-storage rows, filtered to those carrying a usage_metadata.storage_api_type signal (IS NOT NULL), and supplies usage_date, cloud, sku_name, usage_type, usage_unit, the usage_metadata.storage_api_type and usage_metadata.catalog_id sub-fields, and the net-summed usage_quantity.
Caveats & gotchas
  • DBUs/bytes are not dollars: net_usage_quantity is bytes or API-operation counts in usage_unit — multiply by a price rate downstream to get money. This query does no price join.
  • Never sum across usage_unit families (STORAGE_SPACE bytes vs API_OPERATION counts are different units).
  • The primary filter is the confirmed signal usage_metadata.storage_api_type IS NOT NULL, NOT the unverified billing_origin_product = 'DEFAULT_STORAGE' literal (that product enum has no published closed list — do not hardcode it).
  • metastore_id, the other default-storage key, is AWS-only (NULL on Azure/GCP) and is intentionally omitted; add a per-cloud key if you need it.
  • Net across corrections: usage_quantity is summed over all record_type values — do not re-net downstream or filter to ORIGINAL.
  • Date window is a trailing :period_days days ending yesterday (usage_date >= dateadd(day, -:period_days, current_date()) and usage_date < current_date()); today is excluded and the most recent day is provisional (billing populate lag; ingestion_date is the freshness signal).
  • Requires Unity Catalog with system.billing enabled and SELECT granted; an account with no default-storage activity returns a valid empty result — that is 'not assessed', never $0.
  • catalog_id is a raw UUID with no name; resolve the catalog name via a config/UC lookup downstream.
→
What you do next
Rank catalogs by net_usage_quantity within each usage_type, watch the TIER_1 vs TIER_2 API-operation split for a spike, and dollarize the top catalogs/SKUs against the list-price table to decide whether a catalog's ingestion or access pattern needs tuning.
← PreviousDBSQL Allocation Gap Checkcost_dbsql_allocation_gap All Cost & Billing ↑ Next →GenAI Token & GPU Spendcost_genai_token_gpu
Overview / Cost & Billing / cost_genai_token_gpu
15
Cost & Billing cost_genai_token_gpu

GenAI Token & GPU Spend

Net TOKEN / GPU_TIME / ANSWER consumption per day, broken out by SKU, product line, usage type, serving type, and serving endpoint.

system.billing.usage
queries/cost/cost_genai_token_gpu.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:cost_genai_token_gpu */
SELECT usage_date, cloud, sku_name, billing_origin_product, usage_type, usage_unit,
       product_features.serving_type  AS serving_type,
       CASE WHEN usage_metadata.endpoint_name IS NULL THEN usage_metadata.endpoint_name ELSE concat(substr(usage_metadata.endpoint_name, 1, 2), '****') END AS endpoint_name,
       usage_metadata.endpoint_id     AS endpoint_id,
       SUM(usage_quantity) AS net_usage_quantity
FROM system.billing.usage
WHERE usage_date >= dateadd(day, -:period_days, current_date())
  AND usage_date < current_date()
  AND usage_type IN ('TOKEN', 'GPU_TIME', 'ANSWER')
GROUP BY usage_date, cloud, sku_name, billing_origin_product, usage_type, usage_unit,
         product_features.serving_type, usage_metadata.endpoint_name, usage_metadata.endpoint_id
Why it matters

GenAI and model-serving usage is billed in tokens and GPU-time on their own SKUs, not in DBUs, so it is invisible to any dollarization that assumes a per-DBU rate — this query is how an admin sees that spend at all. It isolates which serving endpoints and SKUs are burning tokens or GPU-hours, so a runaway pay-per-token endpoint or an always-on provisioned-throughput deployment shows up as a growing line before it lands as a bill shock. Because it is sliced by day, it doubles as the input to AI-cost anomaly detection. Each row must be priced against its own SKU's list-price row; multiplying these quantities by a DBU rate produces a meaningless number.

What it does
  • Surfaces only GenAI-shaped consumption — the TOKEN, GPU_TIME, and ANSWER usage types — and hides everything DBU-denominated.
  • Reports net consumption (net_usage_quantity) per day for each combination of SKU, product line, usage type, serving type, and serving endpoint, already net of billing corrections.
  • Attributes consumption down to the individual serving endpoint (endpoint_name masked, endpoint_id intact), which populates only for model-serving and vector-search rows.
  • Keeps usage_unit on every row so tokens, GPU-time, and answer counts are never accidentally added together — they are different units on different SKUs.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
usage_dateThe metered day (DATE). The window is the trailing :period_days days and excludes today; treat the most recent day as provisional due to billing populate lag.
cloudThe cloud provider for this usage slice (AWS / AZURE / GCP). Part of the key you would price on.
sku_nameThe specific billing SKU that generated the usage — this is the SKU you must look up in list_prices/account_prices to price the row, never a DBU rate.
billing_origin_productThe product line that emitted the usage (e.g. MODEL_SERVING, VECTOR_SEARCH). No published closed enum; a blank is 'unattributed', not zero.
usage_typeWhich GenAI meter this is: TOKEN, GPU_TIME, or ANSWER. Determines what usage_unit and net_usage_quantity mean.
usage_unitThe unit net_usage_quantity is counted in (tokens, GPU-time hours, answers, etc.). Varies by usage_type — do not sum across different units.
serving_typeThe product_features.serving_type sub-classification of the serving workload; NULL where the choice doesn't apply.
endpoint_nameThe serving/vector-search endpoint name, masked to its first two characters plus '****'. NULL when the row is not endpoint-attributable (populates only for model-serving / vector-search).
endpoint_idThe endpoint's stable id (passed through unmasked) — the reliable join/grouping key when the masked name collides or is NULL.
net_usage_quantitySummed usage_quantity in usage_unit for the group — tokens / GPU-time / answers, NOT dollars and NOT DBUs. Already net across all record_type corrections.
Sample output
usage_datesku_namebilling_origin_productusage_typeusage_unitendpoint_namenet_usage_quantity
2026-07-04MODEL_SERVING_FOUNDATION_MODEL_TOKENSMODEL_SERVINGTOKENTOKENll****4820100
2026-07-04MODEL_SERVING_GPU_INFERENCEMODEL_SERVINGGPU_TIMEGPU_HOURcu****36.5
2026-07-03VECTOR_SEARCH_SERVINGVECTOR_SEARCHANSWERANSWERpr****1240
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.billing.usageThe billed-consumption fact table and the only source here — filtered to the GenAI-relevant usage types (TOKEN, GPU_TIME, ANSWER) and summed to net consumption per day/SKU/endpoint. Endpoint name/id, serving type, product line and usage unit all come from its usage_metadata and product_features structs.
Caveats & gotchas
  • Confidence is **confirmed**, but usage_metadata endpoint fields (endpoint_name/id) populate only for model-serving and vector-search rows — expect NULL endpoints on other GenAI SKUs.
  • TOKEN/GPU_TIME/ANSWER rows are **not DBU-denominated**: net_usage_quantity is tokens / GPU-time / answers. Price each via its own SKU row in list_prices — never the DBU rate.
  • usage_unit varies by usage_type; different units are never summed together. Always read net_usage_quantity together with usage_unit.
  • Net across corrections: net_usage_quantity SUMs usage_quantity over all record_type values (ORIGINAL/RETRACTION/RESTATEMENT) — the query applies no record_type filter. Do not filter to ORIGINAL and do not re-net downstream.
  • Empty is not zero and not an error: an account with no GenAI/serving usage returns no rows (valid empty), while a disabled/unpermissioned system table raises TABLE_OR_VIEW_NOT_FOUND — treat both as 'not assessed', never $0.
  • Date window is the trailing :period_days days and **excludes today**; the most recent day is provisional due to billing populate lag (ingestion_date, not usage_date, is the freshness signal).
  • endpoint_name is masked in-SQL to first-2-chars + '****' (NULL is preserved as NULL); endpoint_id is passed through unmasked. Drop the mask CASE for an internal endpoint leaderboard.
  • Requires Unity Catalog with system.billing enabled and SELECT granted. billing_origin_product has no published closed enum, so product literals are treated as unverified and a blank means 'unattributed'.
→
What you do next
Rank endpoints and SKUs by net_usage_quantity within each usage_unit, price each against its own SKU's list-price row (not the DBU rate), and chase the largest or fastest-growing token/GPU consumers — right-size or throttle a runaway pay-per-token or provisioned endpoint before it becomes a bill shock.
← PreviousDefault-Storage DSU by API Tiercost_default_storage_dsu All Cost & Billing ↑ Next →Billed Data Egress Signalcost_networking_egress
Overview / Cost & Billing / cost_networking_egress
16
Cost & Billing cost_networking_egress

Billed Data Egress Signal

One row per usage-date, cloud, networking SKU, region pair, client, and Delta Sharing recipient, giving the net billed networking volume (bytes or hours) over the trailing window.

system.billing.usage
queries/cost/cost_networking_egress.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:cost_networking_egress */
SELECT usage_date, cloud, sku_name, usage_type, usage_unit,
       usage_metadata.source_region      AS source_region,
       usage_metadata.destination_region AS destination_region,
       usage_metadata.networking_client  AS networking_client,
       usage_metadata.recipient_id       AS recipient_id,
       SUM(usage_quantity) AS net_usage_quantity
FROM system.billing.usage
WHERE usage_date >= dateadd(day, -:period_days, current_date())
  AND usage_date < current_date()
  AND usage_type IN ('NETWORK_BYTE', 'NETWORK_HOUR')
GROUP BY usage_date, cloud, sku_name, usage_type, usage_unit,
         usage_metadata.source_region, usage_metadata.destination_region,
         usage_metadata.networking_client, usage_metadata.recipient_id
Why it matters

Cross-region and cross-cloud data egress is a real, recurring line on the bill that DBU-based cost views miss entirely, and it is the closest in-account signal for what Delta Sharing to external recipients is actually moving. Breaking the networking volume out by region pair, client, and recipient shows an admin which flows and which sharing partners drive egress, so a chatty pipeline or an over-broad share can be caught before it compounds. Because true egress is billed largely on the cloud side, this is a directional signal to reconcile against the cloud cost export, not the final dollar figure.

What it does
  • Isolates only the networking slice of billing (NETWORK_BYTE and NETWORK_HOUR) and reports the net volume with restatements already accounted for, so no manual correction handling is needed.
  • Breaks that volume out by day, cloud, and networking sku_name, and further by source/destination region pair and networking client to show where traffic flows.
  • Surfaces Delta Sharing egress by exposing recipient_id, so volume attributable to a specific external sharing partner stands on its own row.
  • Reports the amount as a raw networking volume in bytes or hours (per usage_unit) — never DBUs or dollars.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
usage_dateThe calendar day the networking consumption was metered. The window excludes today, so treat the most recent day as provisional due to billing populate lag.
cloudThe cloud provider (AWS / AZURE / GCP) the charge was billed on.
sku_nameThe specific networking SKU that generated the row; identifies the kind of egress/networking charge.
usage_typeEither NETWORK_BYTE (volume-based egress) or NETWORK_HOUR (time-based networking), the only two types this query keeps.
usage_unitThe unit net_usage_quantity is expressed in — bytes for NETWORK_BYTE, hours for NETWORK_HOUR. Never DBUs; do not price at a DBU rate.
source_regionOrigin region of the traffic. ALWAYS NULL on GCP — read a GCP null as 'not reported by the cloud', not as 'no egress'.
destination_regionDestination region of the traffic; the pair with source_region reveals cross-region flows. Also ALWAYS NULL on GCP.
networking_clientThe client/component that generated the networking usage; NULL when not attributable to a specific client.
recipient_idThe Delta Sharing recipient the egress is attributable to; populated only for share-driven egress, otherwise NULL.
net_usage_quantityNet metered networking volume for the row (SUM of usage_quantity across all record types), in the units given by usage_unit — bytes or hours, not dollars.
Sample output
usage_datecloudusage_typeusage_unitdestination_regionrecipient_idnet_usage_quantity
2026-07-04AWSNETWORK_BYTEbytesus-west-23f9a2c14-8b6e-4d21-9c07-1a2b3c4d5e6f48213004112
2026-07-04AWSNETWORK_BYTEbyteseu-central-1(null)9120334455
2026-07-04GCPNETWORK_HOURhours(null)(null)168
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.billing.usageThe sole source. Rows are filtered to the two networking usage types (NETWORK_BYTE and NETWORK_HOUR) and netted across all record_type corrections; the region, client, and recipient dimensions are read from the usage_metadata struct.
Caveats & gotchas
  • DBUs-are-not-dollars, and here not even DBUs: usage_unit is bytes/hours. Do NOT price net_usage_quantity at the DBU rate — this table alone has no dollars.
  • source_region and destination_region are ALWAYS NULL on GCP. A GCP null means 'the cloud did not report it', never 'no egress occurred'.
  • This is the closest billed-egress signal, but true egress is billed largely cloud-side; reconcile with the cloud cost export (system.billing.cloud_infra_cost / provider export) rather than treating this as complete.
  • Net always: usage_quantity is summed across all record_type values (ORIGINAL / RETRACTION / RESTATEMENT already net out). Never filter to ORIGINAL.
  • Trailing window of :period_days that EXCLUDES today (usage_date >= current_date - :period_days AND < current_date); today's billing is incomplete and the latest day lags via ingestion_date.
  • Empty is not zero and not error: an account not doing egress or Delta Sharing returns no rows; a disabled/unpermissioned system table raises TABLE_OR_VIEW_NOT_FOUND. Both are 'not assessed', not $0.
  • recipient_id / networking_client / region fields are sparse — populated only for the row that generated them, otherwise NULL; do not read NULL as a zero bucket.
  • Requires Unity Catalog with system.billing enabled and SELECT granted.
→
What you do next
Rank the region pairs, clients, and Delta Sharing recipients by net_usage_quantity to find the top egress drivers, then convert those bytes to dollars by reconciling against the cloud provider's cost export (regions unavailable on GCP) and tighten the chattiest cross-region flow or over-broad share.
← PreviousGenAI Token & GPU Spendcost_genai_token_gpu All Cost & Billing ↑ Next →Can You Trust the Bill?cost_restatement_trust_metric
Overview / Cost & Billing / cost_restatement_trust_metric
17
Cost & Billing cost_restatement_trust_metric

Can You Trust the Bill?

Per cloud, the net billed usage alongside its original, retracted, and restated components plus the latest ingestion date, quantifying how much consumption was later corrected and proving the net figure never double-counts those corrections.

system.billing.usage
queries/cost/cost_restatement_trust_metric.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:cost_restatement_trust_metric */
SELECT cloud,
       SUM(usage_quantity) AS net_usage_quantity,
       SUM(CASE WHEN record_type = 'ORIGINAL'    THEN usage_quantity      ELSE 0 END) AS original_usage_quantity,
       SUM(CASE WHEN record_type = 'RETRACTION'  THEN ABS(usage_quantity) ELSE 0 END) AS retracted_abs_quantity,
       SUM(CASE WHEN record_type = 'RESTATEMENT' THEN usage_quantity      ELSE 0 END) AS restatement_usage_quantity,
       MAX(ingestion_date) AS max_ingestion_date
FROM system.billing.usage
WHERE usage_date >= dateadd(day, -:period_days, current_date())
  AND usage_date < current_date()
GROUP BY cloud
Why it matters

Databricks silently corrects prior billing by appending RETRACTION and RESTATEMENT rows rather than editing history, so any rollup that filters to `ORIGINAL` overstates spend while a naive re-net downstream can understate or double-count it. This query gives an admin a hard, per-cloud trust number: how many DBUs of a period's usage were later restated, and confirmation that summing across all record types nets corrections out exactly once. It also surfaces the most recent `ingestion_date` so the admin knows how fresh the billing data actually is before quoting a cost figure to finance.

What it does
  • Splits your billed usage by cloud and reports the trustworthy net DBU/unit total for the trailing window.
  • Breaks that net into its three moving parts: the original charge, the amount later retracted, and the amount restated — the raw material for a '% of usage later restated' trust metric.
  • Proves the net does not double-count corrections: net equals original minus retracted_abs plus restatement on every row.
  • Reports the latest ingestion date per cloud as a data-freshness/lag check on how current the billing feed is.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
cloudThe cloud provider (e.g. `AWS`, `AZURE`, `GCP`) the usage was billed on; one row per cloud present in the window.
net_usage_quantityThe trustworthy net metered amount (DBUs/bytes/hours/tokens, NOT dollars) summed across ALL record types — corrections already netted out. This is the figure to quote.
original_usage_quantityThe metered amount from ORIGINAL rows only — the pre-correction gross. Do not use this as a net total.
retracted_abs_quantityThe absolute magnitude of usage pulled back by RETRACTION rows (sign-flipped to positive so it reads as 'how much was clawed back').
restatement_usage_quantityThe usage added back by RESTATEMENT rows — the corrected re-charge that replaces a retraction. Restated % of net = restatement / net.
max_ingestion_dateThe most recent load date for this cloud's billing rows — the freshness signal; a stale value here means the period's numbers are still settling.
Sample output
cloudnet_usage_quantityoriginal_usage_quantityretracted_abs_quantityrestatement_usage_quantitymax_ingestion_date
AWS999500.001000000.0012000.0011500.002026-07-05
AZURE500000.00500000.000.000.002026-07-05
GCP199800.00200000.005000.004800.002026-07-04
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.billing.usageThe sole source. Every billed-consumption row is read across all `record_type` values (ORIGINAL, RETRACTION, RESTATEMENT) and rolled up per `cloud`; `usage_quantity` supplies the net and the three component totals, and `ingestion_date` supplies the freshness/lag signal.
Caveats & gotchas
  • `usage_quantity` is DBUs/bytes/hours/tokens, NOT dollars — this is a volume trust metric, not a spend figure; multiply by a price rate elsewhere to get money.
  • Never read `original_usage_quantity` as the total: net must SUM across ALL record types. RETRACTION/RESTATEMENT already net out ORIGINAL — that is exactly what this query proves.
  • Different `usage_unit` families (DBU vs STORAGE_SPACE bytes vs TOKEN vs hours) are blended in these SUMs because there is no unit filter; interpret the number as 'mixed metered units per cloud', not pure DBUs, if the account uses several unit types.
  • Date window is a trailing `:period_days`-day span that EXCLUDES today (today's billing is incomplete); the most recent day remains provisional due to populate lag.
  • `ingestion_date` (≠ `usage_date`) is the freshness signal — a lagging `max_ingestion_date` means the period's figures are still settling.
  • Requires Unity Catalog with `system.billing` enabled and `SELECT` granted; an unpermissioned/disabled table raises TABLE_OR_VIEW_NOT_FOUND — that is 'not assessed', not zero restatement.
  • An empty result means no usage in the window, not $0 or a clean bill; zero retracted/restatement columns mean no corrections occurred, which is the healthy case.
→
What you do next
Compute restated % as `restatement_usage_quantity / net_usage_quantity` per cloud: if it is small and `max_ingestion_date` is current, quote `net_usage_quantity` (dollarized) to finance with confidence; if it is large or ingestion is stale, hold the number as provisional and re-run once the feed catches up.
← PreviousBilled Data Egress Signalcost_networking_egress All Cost & Billing ↑ Next →Model-Serving Cost Mode Per Endpointcost_serving_mode_by_endpoint
Overview / Cost & Billing / cost_serving_mode_by_endpoint
18
Cost & Billing cost_serving_mode_by_endpoint

Model-Serving Cost Mode Per Endpoint

Per-endpoint model-serving consumption over the trailing window, broken out by billed signal (serving type, usage type, and scale-from-zero LAUNCH flag) so each endpoint's cost mode can be inferred, with a bounded, unverified list-dollar figure.

system.billing.usagesystem.billing.list_prices
queries/cost/cost_serving_mode_by_endpoint.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:cost_serving_mode_by_endpoint */
SELECT
  u.usage_metadata.endpoint_id           AS endpoint_id,
  CASE WHEN u.usage_metadata.endpoint_name IS NULL THEN u.usage_metadata.endpoint_name ELSE concat(substr(u.usage_metadata.endpoint_name, 1, 2), '****') END AS endpoint_name,
  u.cloud                                AS cloud,
  u.sku_name                             AS sku_name,
  u.usage_type                           AS usage_type,
  u.usage_unit                           AS usage_unit,
  u.product_features.serving_type        AS serving_type,
  CASE WHEN upper(u.sku_name) LIKE '%SERVERLESS_REAL_TIME_INFERENCE_LAUNCH%'
       THEN TRUE ELSE FALSE END          AS is_launch_sku,
  SUM(u.usage_quantity)                  AS net_usage_quantity,
  SUM(u.usage_quantity * lp.list_rate)   AS net_list_cost
FROM system.billing.usage u
LEFT JOIN (
  SELECT sku_name, cloud, usage_unit, price_start_time, price_end_time,
         CAST(pricing.effective_list.default AS DOUBLE) AS list_rate   -- UNVERIFIED path; see caveats
  FROM system.billing.list_prices
) lp
  ON  u.sku_name = lp.sku_name
  AND u.cloud    = lp.cloud
  AND u.usage_end_time >= lp.price_start_time
  AND (lp.price_end_time IS NULL OR u.usage_end_time < lp.price_end_time)
WHERE u.usage_date >= dateadd(day, -:period_days, current_date())
  AND u.usage_date <  current_date()
  AND u.billing_origin_product = 'MODEL_SERVING'
GROUP BY
  u.usage_metadata.endpoint_id,
  u.usage_metadata.endpoint_name,
  u.cloud,
  u.sku_name,
  u.usage_type,
  u.usage_unit,
  u.product_features.serving_type,
  CASE WHEN upper(u.sku_name) LIKE '%SERVERLESS_REAL_TIME_INFERENCE_LAUNCH%' THEN TRUE ELSE FALSE END
Why it matters

Model serving is one of the fastest-growing and most opaque lines on a Databricks bill, and the single biggest lever - how an endpoint is configured (pay-per-token, provisioned throughput, or scale-from-zero) - is NOT recorded in any system table, only in the serving API. This query recovers that cost mode from the billed signals themselves, so an admin can spot a provisioned GPU endpoint that should be pay-per-token, or an endpoint racking up scale-from-zero cold-start LAUNCH charges. Because the LAUNCH SKU is billed separately, it makes cold-start waste visible per endpoint. The attached dollars are list and unverified, so they size the opportunity but must not be quoted as the actual invoice.

What it does
  • Totals net model-serving consumption (net_usage_quantity) per serving endpoint over the trailing :period_days window, netting out billing corrections.
  • Splits each endpoint by the signals that reveal its cost mode - serving_type (foundation/GPU/model/feature), usage_type (token vs GPU-time vs compute-time), and whether the spend is a scale-from-zero cold-start (is_launch_sku).
  • Attaches a bounded list-dollar figure (net_list_cost) by pricing each usage row at its point-in-time list rate - shown as list/unverified, and left blank where the price path is unavailable rather than guessed.
  • Keeps cloud and sku_name alongside so multi-cloud endpoints and the exact billed SKU stay distinguishable.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
endpoint_idThe serving endpoint's UUID, passed through unchanged - the stable key to correlate with the serving-endpoints API for the true workload config.
endpoint_nameThe endpoint name masked to its first two characters plus '****' (a NULL name stays NULL); a readability hint, not a reliable identifier - use endpoint_id to join.
cloudThe cloud provider (AWS/AZURE/GCP) the usage was billed on; part of the price-join key, so the same endpoint on two clouds appears as separate rows.
sku_nameThe exact billed SKU string; the '%SERVERLESS_REAL_TIME_INFERENCE_LAUNCH%' pattern here is what drives is_launch_sku.
usage_typeThe metered activity - TOKEN (pay-per-token inference), GPU_TIME (GPU throughput), COMPUTE_TIME (CPU serving), ANSWER, etc. - a primary tell of the cost mode.
usage_unitThe unit net_usage_quantity is counted in (DBU, TOKEN, GPU-seconds, ...); rows in different units are NOT comparable or summable.
serving_typeThe serving product feature - FOUNDATION_MODEL, GPU_MODEL, MODEL, FEATURE, or null - distinguishing foundation-model APIs from custom/GPU-provisioned endpoints.
is_launch_skuTRUE when the row is a scale-from-zero cold-start LAUNCH charge; concentrated TRUE spend on an endpoint flags cold-start churn that a warm minimum replica would remove.
net_usage_quantityNet metered consumption (SUM of usage_quantity across all record_types) in usage_unit - DBUs / tokens / GPU-seconds, NOT dollars.
net_list_costnet_usage_quantity priced at the point-in-time LIST rate - a pre-discount, UNVERIFIED dollar estimate; NULL means the price path/join was unavailable (list cost unavailable), never $0.
Sample output
endpoint_idendpoint_namecloudsku_nameusage_typeusage_unitserving_typeis_launch_skunet_usage_quantitynet_list_cost
4a1f9c22-0e7d-4b3a-9c11-2f8e6d0a1b34ll****AWSSTANDARD_FOUNDATION_MODEL_TOKENTOKENTOKENFOUNDATION_MODELfalse4520000316.40
7e3b8d10-5a92-41c6-bf4e-9d0c2a7f1e88gp****AZUREPREMIUM_GPU_MODEL_SERVINGGPU_TIMEDBUGPU_MODELfalse1280.589.64
c9d4e2a1-8b30-4f77-a6e2-1c5b9f0d3e21re****AWSPREMIUM_SERVERLESS_REAL_TIME_INFERENCE_LAUNCHCOMPUTE_TIMEDBUMODELtrue42.0NULL
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.billing.usageThe billed-consumption fact table; filtered to billing_origin_product = 'MODEL_SERVING' over the trailing window and summed to net DBUs/tokens per endpoint, providing serving_type, usage_type, usage_unit, the LAUNCH SKU signal, cloud, and the endpoint id/name.
system.billing.list_pricesPublic list (pre-discount) price history; LEFT-joined point-in-time on sku_name + cloud + usage window to supply the per-unit list_rate that multiplies net usage into net_list_cost (via the UNVERIFIED pricing.effective_list.default cast).
Caveats & gotchas
  • Availability gating: rows appear only if Unity Catalog + system.billing are enabled with SELECT granted; MODEL_SERVING rows exist only if the account actually uses model serving. An empty result means serving is not in use in the window - a real answer, not $0, and not an error.
  • Cost mode is INFERRED, never read from config. Databricks does not expose workload_type / workload_size / scale_to_zero_enabled in system tables (those are serving-endpoints API fields only). Pay-per-token vs provisioned vs scale-from-zero is deduced from serving_type, usage_type and the LAUNCH SKU pattern - treat it as a strong hint, not ground truth.
  • net_usage_quantity is DBUs / tokens / GPU-seconds in usage_unit, NOT dollars. TOKEN rows and DBU rows are different unit families and must never be summed together; because usage_unit is a grouping column, each output row is already single-unit.
  • net_list_cost is LIST (pre-discount) and UNVERIFIED: it relies on the list_prices.pricing.effective_list.default -> DOUBLE cast path that docs type only as 'object'. Same caveat as cost_dollarized_by_sku_day. It is NULL when the price path or point-in-time join is absent (dollarization degrades to 'list cost unavailable') and never invents a rate - do not promote it to a billed headline.
  • Net across corrections: usage_quantity is summed over ALL record_type values (ORIGINAL / RETRACTION / RESTATEMENT already net out). Never filter to ORIGINAL.
  • Price join is point-in-time and keyed on sku_name + cloud + usage window (usage_end_time >= price_start_time AND (price_end_time IS NULL OR usage_end_time < price_end_time)); usage_unit is selected in the price subquery but is NOT part of the ON clause. Dropping the NULL branch would silently zero recent usage.
  • endpoint_name is masked in the SQL to first-2-chars + '****'; a NULL name stays NULL. endpoint_id (a UUID) is passed through unchanged. Drop the mask CASE for an internal, un-redacted view.
  • Date window is a trailing :period_days days that EXCLUDES today (today's billing is incomplete); the most recent day is provisional due to populate lag.
→
What you do next
Rank endpoints by `net_usage_quantity` within each `serving_type`/`usage_type` family, then act on the inferred cost mode: for `is_launch_sku = TRUE` rows with heavy launch DBUs, set a minimum provisioned replica (scale-from-zero cold-starts are burning spend); for `FOUNDATION_MODEL` + `TOKEN` rows, cap or route high-token endpoints; for `GPU_MODEL` provisioned rows, confirm the endpoint is actually driving throughput before renewing the GPU reservation. Because scale-to-zero/workload-size config is NOT in system tables, cross-check the shortlist against the serving-endpoints API before changing anything.
← PreviousCan You Trust the Bill?cost_restatement_trust_metric All Cost & Billing ↑ Next →The Base Billing Rollupcost_totals_by_sku_day
Overview / Cost & Billing / cost_totals_by_sku_day
19
Cost & Billing cost_totals_by_sku_day

The Base Billing Rollup

Net metered consumption (DBUs/bytes/tokens/hours) per day, cloud, workspace, SKU, product line, usage type and serverless flag, with the correction-record breakdown that proves the net figure is trustworthy.

system.billing.usage
queries/cost/cost_totals_by_sku_day.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:cost_totals_by_sku_day */
SELECT usage_date, cloud, workspace_id, sku_name, billing_origin_product, usage_type, usage_unit,
       product_features.is_serverless AS is_serverless,
       SUM(usage_quantity) AS net_usage_quantity,
       SUM(CASE WHEN record_type = 'ORIGINAL'    THEN usage_quantity ELSE 0 END) AS original_usage_quantity,
       SUM(CASE WHEN record_type = 'RETRACTION'  THEN usage_quantity ELSE 0 END) AS retraction_usage_quantity,
       SUM(CASE WHEN record_type = 'RESTATEMENT' THEN usage_quantity ELSE 0 END) AS restatement_usage_quantity,
       COUNT(*) AS record_count
FROM system.billing.usage
WHERE usage_date >= dateadd(day, -:period_days, current_date())
  AND usage_date < current_date()
GROUP BY usage_date, cloud, workspace_id, sku_name, billing_origin_product, usage_type, usage_unit,
         product_features.is_serverless
Why it matters

This is the foundational rollup every other cost view is built on: it is where an admin sees how much metered consumption the account actually incurred and can trust that billing corrections are not double-counted. Getting the net wrong here (for example by filtering to ORIGINAL records and missing later retractions/restatements) silently inflates every downstream dollar figure. The per-workspace and serverless-vs-classic cuts let FinOps allocate spend to dev/uat/prod and spot the serverless mix, while the restatement split surfaces how much of the bill was later revised. Note the amounts are DBUs/units, not dollars — dollarizing happens in a separate priced query.

What it does
  • Totals your metered consumption for each day and breaks it down by cloud, workspace, SKU, product line (billing_origin_product), usage type and unit, and whether the compute was serverless.
  • Reports the trustworthy net amount (net_usage_quantity) alongside the original, retracted, and restated pieces, so you can see how much of a line was later corrected.
  • Keeps amounts in native units (usage_unit — DBUs, storage bytes, tokens, hours), never dollars, and never blends different unit families together.
  • Preserves account-level SKUs (rows where workspace_id is empty) as consumption that is not attributable to a single workspace.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
usage_dateThe calendar day the consumption was metered (the recommended date-partition column). The window is a trailing :period_days days and excludes today, whose billing is still incomplete.
cloudThe cloud provider for the usage (e.g. AWS, AZURE, GCP). Part of the grain because prices and SKUs differ by cloud.
workspace_idThe workspace that generated the usage; empty/NULL for account-level SKUs (some MODEL_SERVING/account services) — keep those rows as 'not workspace-attributable'. Names are not here; resolve id to dev/uat/prod separately.
sku_nameThe billed SKU (the priced product unit). The finest product identifier and the key you would later join to a price table on cloud + currency + unit + sku_name.
billing_origin_productThe product line that originated the charge (JOBS/SQL/DLT/MODEL_SERVING/DEFAULT_STORAGE/…). No published closed enum, so specific literals are unverified; a blank value means 'unattributed', not zero.
usage_typeWhat kind of metering this is (COMPUTE_TIME, STORAGE_SPACE, NETWORK_BYTE, TOKEN, GPU_TIME, …). Determines which unit family the amount belongs to.
usage_unitThe unit of the amounts on this row — DBU, STORAGE_SPACE (bytes), TOKEN, hours, etc. Never sum amounts across different units.
is_serverlessWhether the compute behind the row was serverless (true) or classic (false); NULL where the distinction does not apply. Drives the serverless-vs-classic mix. Sourced from product_features.is_serverless.
net_usage_quantityThe trustworthy figure: total usage_quantity summed across ALL record types, so retractions and restatements already net out originals. This is DBUs/units, not dollars.
original_usage_quantityThe portion from ORIGINAL records only — the first-reported amount before any correction. Do not use this alone as the net.
retraction_usage_quantityThe portion from RETRACTION records (negative corrections that reverse a prior charge). Already included in net_usage_quantity.
restatement_usage_quantityThe portion from RESTATEMENT records (revised re-postings of a charge). Already included in net_usage_quantity; a large share signals heavy billing restatement.
record_countHow many raw usage rows collapsed into this group — a density/volume signal, not a cost.
Sample output
usage_dateworkspace_idsku_nameusage_unitis_serverlessnet_usage_quantityrestatement_usage_quantityrecord_count
2026-07-031234567890123456ENTERPRISE_SQL_COMPUTEDBUtrue412.500.0096
2026-07-039876543210987654ENTERPRISE_JOBS_COMPUTEDBUfalse128.7512.25144
2026-07-02NULLPREMIUM_MODEL_SERVINGDBUtrue57.000.0024
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.billing.usageThe sole source. The account's billed-consumption fact table; each row is a metered slice of usage for an account/workspace, SKU, date and record_type. The query nets consumption across all correction record types and slices it by the billing dimensions.
Caveats & gotchas
  • DBUs are not dollars: net_usage_quantity and every *_usage_quantity is metered usage in usage_unit (DBUs/bytes/tokens/hours). Dollarize with a price table in a separate query.
  • Always net across ALL record_types — never filter to ORIGINAL. RETRACTION/RESTATEMENT already net out originals; net_usage_quantity is the only trustworthy total.
  • Never sum amounts across different usage_unit families (DBU vs STORAGE_SPACE bytes vs TOKEN vs hours) — the grain deliberately keeps them separate.
  • workspace_id is NULL for account-level SKUs (some MODEL_SERVING/account services); keep those rows as 'not workspace-attributable' rather than dropping them.
