change_timeTimestamp of this (latest) configuration revision; how the newest row per pool was chosen.
workspace_idWorkspace the pool belongs to; the scope key when correlating with workspace-scoped cluster or billing rows.
account_idDatabricks account the pool belongs to.
Sample output
instance_pool_idinstance_pool_namenode_typemin_idle_instancesmax_capacityidle_instance_autotermination_minutespreloaded_docker_imagestagsworkspace_id
0713-abc123-pool-91a2et****i3.xlarge52060[]{"team":"etl","cost_center":"data-eng"}1234567890123456
0713-def456-pool-77c4ml****g4dn.xlarge0815[{"url":"reg.internal/ml-base:2.1"}]{}1234567890123456
0620-ghi789-pool-04f8st****Standard_DS3_v221030[]{"env":"staging"}9876543210987654
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.compute.instance_poolsPublic-Preview slowly-changing config history of instance pools (pre-warmed idle VMs shared by clusters); the query reduces it to the latest row per instance_pool_id and keeps only pools not yet deleted, then reads pool sizing, idle-autotermination, preload and cloud-attribute columns.
Caveats & gotchas
  • **Public Preview table.** `system.compute.instance_pools` may be empty or disabled — an empty-but-valid result or `TABLE_OR_VIEW_NOT_FOUND` (schema not enabled) is expected, not a failure. Empty != zero pools != error.
  • **No dollars, no DBUs.** Nothing here is cost. `min_idle_instances` waste becomes money only after joining node cost from `system.billing.usage` / list prices (billing domain) — do NOT dollarize inside this query.
  • **SCD, not current-state.** The table is a change-log; results are the latest `change_time` per `instance_pool_id` with `delete_time IS NULL`. Skipping that reduction returns stale/duplicate/deleted pools.
  • **Config snapshot — no date window.** Latest-row-wins; there is no `:period_days` / `:lookback_days` parameter and no trailing window.
  • **Regional.** Tables are per metastore region — run per region and union if the account spans several.
  • **Masking.** `instance_pool_name` is truncated to 2 chars + `****`; do not treat it as a full name or key.
  • **Structs selected whole.** `aws/azure/gcp_attributes` and `disk_spec` are STRUCTs; only the host cloud's attribute struct is populated, and extracting a named subfield is unverified.
  • **Sizing fields are bigint.** `min_idle_instances` and `max_capacity` are instance counts, not hours or dollars.
  • Requires Unity Catalog, the `compute` schema enabled on the metastore, and `SELECT` on `system.*`.
→
What you do next
Sort by `min_idle_instances` (highest first) to find pools burning always-on idle VMs; for each, cut the idle floor toward zero and/or lower `idle_instance_autotermination_minutes`, right-size `max_capacity` to real demand, review any non-empty `preloaded_docker_images`, and tag untagged pools — then quantify the savings by joining `node_type` to node cost in the billing domain.
← PreviousSpot Mix and Instance Idle Signalinstance_events_idle_active All Compute ↑ Next →Right-Sizing Evidence From Node Telemetrynode_timeline_utilization
Overview / Compute / node_timeline_utilization
07
Compute node_timeline_utilization

Right-Sizing Evidence From Node Telemetry

One row per cluster / node-type / driver-vs-worker role summarizing observed CPU, memory, CPU-wait and network from per-minute classic-compute hardware telemetry over a trailing window (≤90 days).

system.compute.node_timeline
queries/compute/node_timeline_utilization.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:node_timeline_utilization */
SELECT cluster_id, node_type, driver,
       COUNT(*) AS minute_rows,
       MIN(start_time) AS first_minute, MAX(end_time) AS last_minute,
       AVG(cpu_user_percent + cpu_system_percent) AS avg_cpu_pct,
       MAX(cpu_user_percent + cpu_system_percent) AS peak_cpu_pct,
       AVG(mem_used_percent) AS avg_mem_pct, MAX(mem_used_percent) AS peak_mem_pct,
       AVG(cpu_wait_percent) AS avg_cpu_wait_pct,
       SUM(network_sent_bytes)     AS total_network_sent_bytes,
       SUM(network_received_bytes) AS total_network_received_bytes
FROM system.compute.node_timeline
WHERE start_time >= current_timestamp() - INTERVAL :lookback_days DAYS
GROUP BY cluster_id, node_type, driver
Why it matters

This is the hard evidence that turns a right-sizing hunch into a defensible action: a cluster whose CPU and memory sit chronically low across thousands of observed minutes is genuinely oversized and can drop to a smaller node type or a tighter autoscale range. High CPU-wait flags I/O-bound work that a bigger CPU will not fix (so you avoid upsizing waste), and the network totals are a coarse egress proxy worth watching for cross-region transfer cost. None of these numbers are DBUs or dollars, so savings are realized only after you feed the right-sizing decision back into cluster config and re-price against billing.

What it does
  • Summarizes real hardware usage for each classic cluster, broken out by node type and by driver-vs-worker role.
  • Reports average and peak CPU busy percent and memory-used percent so you can separate chronically-idle nodes from genuinely-busy ones.
  • Surfaces CPU-wait percent (an I/O-bound signal) and total bytes sent and received per grouping as a coarse network/egress proxy.
  • Includes a minute-row count and the first/last observed minute so you know how much evidence backs each row and over what span.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
cluster_idThe classic cluster the telemetry belongs to. Not masked — join to the config snapshot (system.compute.clusters) to recover the (masked) cluster name and owner.
node_typeThe instance/node type observed. Join to `system.compute.node_types` for the vCPU/memory denominator behind the utilization percentages.
driverBoolean: true = the driver node's rows, false = worker rows. Driver and workers are summarized separately because their utilization profiles differ.
minute_rowsCount of node-minute slices behind this row — the evidence weight. A raw row is one minute, not one hour; low counts mean a thin sample (and sub-10-minute nodes may not appear at all).
first_minuteEarliest observed minute-slice start (MIN(start_time)) in this grouping — the beginning of the measured span.
last_minuteLatest observed minute-slice end (MAX(end_time)) in this grouping — the end of the measured span. Combined with first_minute it bounds how recent and how long the observation is.
avg_cpu_pctAverage CPU busy percent (user + system) across all minutes. Chronically low (e.g. single digits) is the primary oversized-cluster signal.
peak_cpu_pctMaximum CPU busy percent (user + system) seen in any single minute. A low peak confirms headroom to downsize; a high peak warns that the node genuinely needs the capacity at times.
avg_mem_pctAverage memory-used percent (0-100). Note it includes background processes, so it never reads truly zero even on an idle node.
peak_mem_pctMaximum memory-used percent in any minute. A peak near 100 warns against downsizing to a smaller-memory node even if the average looks low.
avg_cpu_wait_pctAverage percent of time the CPU waited on I/O. High values mean the workload is I/O-bound — a faster/bigger CPU will not help, so treat it separately from CPU-bound sizing.
total_network_sent_bytesSum of bytes sent across all minutes in this grouping — a coarse egress proxy, in bytes (not dollars). Useful for spotting heavy cross-region/data-transfer clusters.
total_network_received_bytesSum of bytes received across all minutes in this grouping, in bytes. Read alongside sent bytes for total network movement.
Sample output
cluster_idnode_typedriverminute_rowsavg_cpu_pctpeak_cpu_pctavg_mem_pcttotal_network_sent_bytes
0714-201530-abc123m5d.2xlargetrue43206.422.141.83221225472
0714-201530-abc123m5d.2xlargefalse518408.974.355.248318382080
0902-118844-xyz789r5.4xlargefalse210071.598.283.610737418240
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.compute.node_timelineSole source. Per-minute hardware-utilization telemetry for classic-compute nodes (driver and every worker). The query aggregates these node-minute rows into per cluster / node-type / role averages, peaks, counts, and network totals.
Caveats & gotchas
  • Confirmed query, single source — but classic compute ONLY: SQL warehouses and serverless have no node-level rows here at all.
  • Retention is 90 days ONLY. `:lookback_days` must be ≤ 90; a longer window (e.g. a 12-month trend) silently truncates — describe results as 'assessed over the last 90 days,' not the requested span.
  • Trailing window: `start_time >= current_timestamp() - INTERVAL :lookback_days DAYS`. It is a rolling window, so results shift each day.
  • Short-job blind spot: nodes that ran under ~10 minutes may never appear. Absence means 'too short to measure,' not 'idle' — do not read a missing cluster as zero usage.
  • DBUs/dollars are absent entirely. These are behavioral/telemetry percentages and byte counts; dollarizing an oversized finding requires a separate join to `system.billing.usage` and list prices in the billing domain.
  • `avg_mem_pct` / `peak_mem_pct` include background processes, so memory never reads truly 0 — do not treat low memory alone as fully idle.
  • Network bytes are a COARSE egress proxy, not a billed transfer figure, and are in bytes.
  • `minute_rows` counts minute-slices, not hours — divide by 60 for node-hours; a low count is a thin, low-confidence sample.
  • Regional table: run per metastore region and union if the account spans regions.
  • `disk_free_bytes_per_mount_point` is a per-mount map and is deliberately not selected — no disk pressure signal here.
  • Empty result is valid (no classic clusters ran in-window / schema not enabled) and is not the same as zero utilization or an error; requires Unity Catalog and SELECT on `system.compute`.
→
What you do next
Rank clusters by low avg_cpu_pct and avg_mem_pct with high minute_rows (well-evidenced), confirm peak_cpu_pct / peak_mem_pct also leave headroom, then downsize the node type or tighten the autoscale range on those clusters; treat high avg_cpu_wait_pct rows as I/O-bound (do not upsize CPU) and route persistently idle clusters to the auto-stop candidate review.
← PreviousIdle Instance-Pool Waste Snapshotinstance_pools_idle_capacity All Compute ↑ Next →Node Type Hardware Spec Catalognode_types_reference
Overview / Compute / node_types_reference
08
Compute node_types_reference

Node Type Hardware Spec Catalog

A static reference list of every node/instance type available in the account with its vCPU, memory, and GPU specs — the hardware denominator for right-sizing math.

system.compute.node_types
queries/compute/node_types_reference.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:node_types_reference */
SELECT node_type, core_count, memory_mb, gpu_count, account_id
FROM system.compute.node_types
Why it matters

Right-sizing and idle findings are meaningless without a capacity baseline: knowing a cluster's CPU sat at 8% only becomes a dollar decision once you know how many vCPUs and how much memory that node type actually provides. This table is that denominator — it lets an admin translate "chronically low utilization" on a specific node type into "downsize from a 96-core box to a 16-core box." It also surfaces which node types carry GPUs (gpu_count), the most expensive capacity to leave idle. It carries no DBUs or dollars itself; it is a lookup that makes the behavioral tables interpretable.

What it does
  • Lists every node/instance type the account's cloud/region offers, one row each — a plain catalog, not a time series and not aggregated.
  • For each type, reports the hardware it delivers: vCPU cores, memory in MB, and GPU count.
  • Provides the capacity denominator you join to clusters (driver/worker node type) and node_timeline (per-minute utilization) to turn raw utilization into right-sizing decisions.
  • Exposes gpu_count so GPU-bearing types can be identified and their idle-GPU spend prioritized.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
node_typeThe cloud instance-type identifier (e.g. an AWS/Azure/GCP SKU name). This is the join key to clusters.driver_node_type / worker_node_type and node_timeline.node_type — match on it to attach specs to actual running compute.
core_countvCPU cores per single node of this type. Stored as a double. Multiply by node/worker count to get a cluster's total cores for right-sizing math.
memory_mbRAM per single node in megabytes (a long, NOT bytes and NOT GB — divide by 1024 for GB). Per-node, so scale by node count for cluster totals.
gpu_countGPUs attached to one node of this type (long). 0 for CPU-only types; >0 flags GPU capacity, the priciest to leave idle.
account_idThe Databricks account UUID the catalog belongs to. Constant within an account; useful only when unioning exports across accounts.
Sample output
node_typecore_countmemory_mbgpu_countaccount_id
m5d.2xlarge8.0327680e2c1a4b8-1f2d-4c3e-9a7b-0d5f6e8c9a1b
Standard_NC24ads_A100_v424.02293761e2c1a4b8-1f2d-4c3e-9a7b-0d5f6e8c9a1b
r5d.24xlarge96.07864320e2c1a4b8-1f2d-4c3e-9a7b-0d5f6e8c9a1b
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.compute.node_typesThe sole source: a static reference dimension holding one row per node/instance type, exposing per-node hardware specs (core_count, memory_mb, gpu_count). Read whole with no filter or aggregation; node_type is the join key back to clusters.driver/worker_node_type and node_timeline.node_type.
Caveats & gotchas
  • Reference dimension only — one row per node type, no history, no aggregation, no time window. It carries no DBUs and no dollars; right-sizing findings become money only after joining to system.billing.usage and list prices in the billing domain.
  • Units matter: core_count is a double (vCPUs), memory_mb is MB (not bytes and not GB — divide by 1024 for GB), gpu_count is an integer count. Don't confuse memory_mb with bytes.
  • Cloud-specific catalog: the listed node types are only those available in the account's cloud/region, so the set differs across clouds/regions; union exports if you operate in several.
  • Join key is node_type — match it to clusters.driver_node_type / worker_node_type and node_timeline.node_type. Do not join on any other column.
  • Availability: GA but requires Unity Catalog, the compute schema enabled on the metastore, and SELECT on system.compute. Indefinite retention (no purge window).
  • An empty result means the compute schema isn't enabled or lacks grants — not that zero node types exist. Empty != zero != error.
→
What you do next
Load this as a lookup table and join it to classic_clusters_config_current (on driver/worker node type) and node_timeline_utilization (on node_type): where a cluster's average CPU and memory usage sits far below the node's core_count / memory_mb over the window, downsize it to a smaller node type; prioritize any type with gpu_count > 0 that shows low utilization, since idle GPUs are the costliest to keep running.
← PreviousRight-Sizing Evidence From Node Telemetrynode_timeline_utilization All Compute ↑ Next →Live SQL Warehouse Config Baselinesql_warehouse_config_current
Overview / Compute / sql_warehouse_config_current
09
Compute sql_warehouse_config_current

Live SQL Warehouse Config Baseline

One row per live (non-deleted) SQL warehouse showing its latest configuration — type, channel, size, autoscale range, idle auto-stop, and chargeback tags.

system.compute.warehouses
queries/compute/sql_warehouse_config_current.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:sql_warehouse_config_current */
SELECT warehouse_id, CASE WHEN warehouse_name IS NULL THEN warehouse_name ELSE concat(substr(warehouse_name, 1, 2), '****') END AS warehouse_name, workspace_id, account_id, warehouse_type, warehouse_channel,
       warehouse_size, min_clusters, max_clusters, auto_stop_minutes, tags, change_time, delete_time
FROM (
  SELECT warehouse_id, warehouse_name, workspace_id, account_id, warehouse_type, warehouse_channel,
         warehouse_size, min_clusters, max_clusters, auto_stop_minutes, tags, change_time, delete_time,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY warehouse_id ORDER BY change_time DESC) AS rn
  FROM system.compute.warehouses
)
WHERE rn = 1 AND delete_time IS NULL
Why it matters

SQL warehouses bill continuously while RUNNING, so an oversized `warehouse_size` or a high `auto_stop_minutes` (or none at all) quietly burns compute during idle windows between queries. This snapshot is the right-sizing and hygiene baseline: it surfaces warehouses that are too big for their workload, warehouses that never auto-suspend, and warehouses with empty `tags` that can't be charged back to a team or cost center. Fixing these is a direct, recurring compute saving — but note this table carries no DBUs or dollars, so the savings must be quantified later against the billing domain.

What it does
  • Returns the current configuration of every live SQL warehouse in the metastore region — one row per warehouse, deleted warehouses excluded.
  • Slices each warehouse by type (CLASSIC/PRO/SERVERLESS), release channel, and size (XS … 4X_LARGE, plus Beta 5X_LARGE) so you can spot oversized or off-standard warehouses.
  • Exposes the autoscaling range (min_clusters/max_clusters) and the idle auto-stop setting so never-suspending or over-provisioned warehouses stand out.
  • Carries the chargeback tags map so untagged warehouses are visible for cost allocation.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
warehouse_idStable unique identifier of the SQL warehouse; use it to join to warehouse_events or billing usage (workspace-scoped).
warehouse_nameDisplay name, masked in-query to the first 2 characters plus `****` (e.g. `An****`); NULL passes through as NULL, so it is a label hint, not the full name.
workspace_idThe workspace that owns the warehouse; the correct grain for scoping and for joining warehouse-level keys.
account_idThe Databricks account the warehouse belongs to.
warehouse_typeCompute tier: `CLASSIC`, `PRO`, or `SERVERLESS` — drives pricing model and which features/sizes apply.
warehouse_channelRelease channel (e.g. current vs preview) the warehouse runs on.
warehouse_sizeT-shirt size from `XS` to `4X_LARGE` (plus Beta `5X_LARGE` on PRO/SERVERLESS); the primary oversizing signal.
min_clustersLower bound of the autoscaling cluster range — the always-on floor of clusters when running.
max_clustersUpper bound of the autoscaling cluster range — the ceiling the warehouse can scale out to under load.
auto_stop_minutesIdle minutes before the warehouse suspends; a high value (or effectively disabled) means a long idle-billing tail.
tagsMap of chargeback/governance tags; an empty map means the warehouse is untagged and cannot be attributed to a team or cost center.
change_timeTimestamp of this (latest) configuration change — how current the snapshot is for this warehouse.
delete_timeDeletion timestamp; always NULL here because the query keeps only live warehouses.
Sample output
warehouse_idwarehouse_namewarehouse_typewarehouse_sizemin_clustersmax_clustersauto_stop_minutes
a1b2c3d4e5f60718An****PROLARGE1410
f9e8d7c6b5a41320Fi****SERVERLESSX_LARGE11120
0011223344556677Le****CLASSICMEDIUM1210
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.compute.warehousesSlowly-changing config history of SQL warehouses (one row per warehouse per config change). The query reduces it to the single latest row per warehouse_id and keeps only warehouses that are not deleted.
Caveats & gotchas
  • SCD snapshot, not a live view: shows the latest row per `warehouse_id` with `delete_time IS NULL` — skipping that reduction returns stale, duplicate, or deleted rows.
  • No DBUs and no dollars in this table — oversized/idle findings become cost only after joining to `system.billing.usage` and list prices (billing domain).
  • `warehouse_name` is masked to 2 chars + `****` (NULL stays NULL); it is not the full name and not suitable as a join key — use `warehouse_id`.
  • `tags` is a map; treat an empty/absent map as 'untagged', not an error.
  • `warehouse_size` includes `5X_LARGE`, which is Beta and only on PRO/SERVERLESS.
  • Availability: GA, but requires Unity Catalog, the `compute` schema enabled on the metastore, and `SELECT` on `system.compute`.
  • Regional — run once per metastore region and union if you have several.
  • Empty result is valid and expected if the workspace runs no SQL warehouses (empty != zero != error).
  • This is a current-state config snapshot with no time window; it says nothing about actual runtime behavior — pair it with `sql_warehouse_events_activity` / `compute_warehouse_idle_gaps` for real idle evidence.
→
What you do next
Sort the output by `warehouse_size` and `auto_stop_minutes` to build a shortlist of oversized or never-suspending warehouses and any with empty `tags`; downsize or lower auto-stop on the worst offenders, add chargeback tags to the untagged ones, then confirm the savings against the billing domain.
← PreviousNode Type Hardware Spec Catalognode_types_reference All Compute ↑ Next →SQL Warehouse Event Activitysql_warehouse_events_activity
Overview / Compute / sql_warehouse_events_activity
10
Compute sql_warehouse_events_activity

SQL Warehouse Event Activity

One row per SQL warehouse and event type over the window, giving the event count, the first and last time that event fired, and the peak and average number of clusters running when it did.

system.compute.warehouse_events
queries/compute/sql_warehouse_events_activity.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:sql_warehouse_events_activity */
SELECT warehouse_id, event_type,
       COUNT(*)            AS event_count,
       MAX(event_time)     AS last_event_time,
       MIN(event_time)     AS first_event_time,
       MAX(cluster_count)  AS max_cluster_count,
       AVG(cluster_count)  AS avg_cluster_count
FROM system.compute.warehouse_events
WHERE event_time >= current_timestamp() - INTERVAL :period_days DAYS
GROUP BY warehouse_id, event_type
Why it matters

A SQL warehouse bills for every second it is RUNNING, whether or not a query is on it, so a warehouse that resumes and suspends dozens of times a day, or scales its cluster count up and back repeatedly, is quietly burning spin-up time and DBUs. This view lets an admin see, per warehouse, how often it started/stopped/scaled and how many clusters it peaked at, so they can flag chronic resume/suspend churn, spot warehouses that never went RUNNING (idle or abandoned), and catch autoscaling that thrashes. It is the behavioral evidence for tuning auto_stop_minutes and max_clusters. Note it carries no DBUs or dollars itself; the churn it exposes must be dollarized against billing.

What it does
  • Breaks each SQL warehouse down by event type (STARTING, RUNNING, STOPPING, STOPPED, SCALED_UP, SCALED_DOWN) and counts how many times each fired in the window.
  • Shows the first and last time each event occurred per warehouse — so a warehouse with no recent RUNNING/STARTING reads as idle or abandoned.
  • Reports the peak and average cluster count seen at those events, exposing how hard the warehouse's autoscaling worked.
  • Slices strictly per warehouse_id and event_type over a trailing window; it does not stitch events into durations or costs.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
warehouse_idThe SQL warehouse the events belong to. Workspace/region-scoped identifier; pass through as-is.
event_typeThe state transition being counted. Trust only the 6 documented values (SCALED_UP, SCALED_DOWN, STOPPING, RUNNING, STARTING, STOPPED); SCALING_UP/SCALING_DOWN are undocumented and not expected here.
event_countHow many times this event fired for this warehouse in the window. High STARTING/STOPPED counts = resume/suspend churn; high SCALED_UP/SCALED_DOWN = autoscale thrash.
last_event_timeMost recent occurrence of this event. If the newest RUNNING/STARTING is stale, the warehouse has likely been idle since.
first_event_timeEarliest occurrence of this event within the window — the start of the observed activity span for that event.
max_cluster_countThe highest number of clusters running at any occurrence of this event — the autoscaling peak. Compare to the warehouse's configured max_clusters.
avg_cluster_countAverage clusters running across occurrences of this event; a value well below max_cluster_count means the peak was rare.
Sample output
warehouse_idevent_typeevent_countlast_event_timefirst_event_timemax_cluster_countavg_cluster_count
a1b2c3d4e5f60718RUNNING1422026-07-05 18:22:412026-06-06 08:14:0311.0
a1b2c3d4e5f60718SCALED_UP572026-07-05 17:40:102026-06-06 09:02:5542.3
9f8e7d6c5b4a3210STARTING3182026-07-05 23:57:122026-06-06 00:11:4711.0
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.compute.warehouse_eventsSole source: the SQL-warehouse state-transition log. Supplies warehouse_id, event_type, event_time, and cluster_count (clusters running at event time), filtered to the trailing :period_days window and summarized per warehouse and event type.
Caveats & gotchas
  • Confirmed query, but the table is regional — run it per metastore region and union if needed.
  • Requires Unity Catalog with the `compute` system schema enabled and SELECT on system.*; otherwise it errors rather than returning empty.
  • Carries NO DBUs or dollars — churn and cluster counts are behavioral signals only; dollarizing requires a join to system.billing.usage (billing domain).
  • Empty != zero != error: a warehouse with no events in the window (or an account running no SQL warehouses) yields no rows, which means 'no transitions observed,' not 'idle' or 'broken.'
  • Window is the trailing :period_days ending now (current_timestamp), not a fixed calendar range; warehouse_events retention is limited, so events may not exist for long windows.
  • cluster_count = clusters running at the event's moment, not a duration or a billed figure.
  • Trust only the 6 documented event_type values; SCALING_UP/SCALING_DOWN are undocumented and should not be relied on.
  • warehouse_id is workspace/region-scoped; do not join it across regions or assume account-global uniqueness.
→
What you do next
Sort by event_count to find warehouses with the most STARTING/STOPPED cycles (resume/suspend churn) and the highest SCALED_UP counts (autoscale thrash); for churners, lower auto_stop_minutes or cap max_clusters, and for warehouses whose newest RUNNING/STARTING last_event_time is stale, investigate decommissioning. Then dollarize the worst offenders against billing.
← PreviousLive SQL Warehouse Config Baselinesql_warehouse_config_current All Compute ↑ Next →Failing Jobs Ranked by Wasted DBUslakeflow_failed_jobs_wasted_dbus
Overview / Jobs & Pipelines / lakeflow_failed_jobs_wasted_dbus
01
Jobs & Pipelines lakeflow_failed_jobs_wasted_dbus ★ first-audit pick

Failing Jobs Ranked by Wasted DBUs

One row per job that had at least one failed run in the trailing window, with its distinct/failed run counts, the latest failure's termination code, total job DBUs, and a wasted-DBU proxy (job DBUs scaled by failed-run share).

system.lakeflow.job_run_timelinesystem.billing.usage
queries/jobs_pipelines/lakeflow_failed_jobs_wasted_dbus.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:lakeflow_failed_jobs_wasted_dbus */
WITH end_rows AS (
  SELECT workspace_id, job_id, run_id, result_state, termination_code, period_start_time
  FROM system.lakeflow.job_run_timeline
  WHERE period_start_time >= dateadd(day, -:period_days, current_date())
    AND period_end_time < date_trunc('DAY', current_timestamp())   -- drop incomplete current day
    AND result_state IS NOT NULL                                   -- end row only
),
job_runs AS (
  SELECT workspace_id, job_id,
         COUNT(DISTINCT run_id) AS distinct_runs,
         COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN result_state IN ('FAILED','ERROR','TIMED_OUT')
                             THEN run_id END) AS failed_runs
  FROM end_rows
  GROUP BY workspace_id, job_id
),
-- ARGMAX, not MAX: the termination_code of the LATEST failed run, per (workspace_id, job_id).
-- MAX(termination_code) would pick the lexicographically-largest code, not the most recent.
last_fail AS (
  SELECT workspace_id, job_id, termination_code AS last_failed_termination_code
  FROM end_rows
  WHERE result_state IN ('FAILED','ERROR','TIMED_OUT')
  QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
    PARTITION BY workspace_id, job_id ORDER BY period_start_time DESC
  ) = 1
),
-- DBUs billed to each job over the window. usage_metadata.job_id is unique only within a
-- workspace, so we attribute on (workspace_id, job_id). Net across ALL record_types; DBU only.
job_dbus AS (
  SELECT workspace_id,
         usage_metadata.job_id AS job_id,
         SUM(usage_quantity)   AS net_usage_quantity
  FROM system.billing.usage
  WHERE usage_date >= dateadd(day, -:period_days, current_date())
    AND usage_date < current_date()
    AND upper(usage_unit) = 'DBU'
    AND usage_metadata.job_id IS NOT NULL
  GROUP BY workspace_id, usage_metadata.job_id
)
SELECT r.workspace_id,
       r.job_id,
       r.distinct_runs,
       r.failed_runs,
       lf.last_failed_termination_code,
       COALESCE(d.net_usage_quantity, 0)                                 AS net_job_dbus,
       -- Wasted-DBU proxy: job DBUs scaled by the share of runs that failed. Honest proxy,
       -- not exact run-level waste (usage_metadata has no run_id) — labelled as such downstream.
       CASE WHEN r.distinct_runs > 0
            THEN COALESCE(d.net_usage_quantity, 0) * (r.failed_runs / r.distinct_runs)
            ELSE 0 END                                                   AS wasted_dbus_proxy
FROM job_runs r
LEFT JOIN last_fail lf
  ON r.workspace_id = lf.workspace_id AND r.job_id = lf.job_id
LEFT JOIN job_dbus d
  ON r.workspace_id = d.workspace_id AND r.job_id = d.job_id
WHERE r.failed_runs > 0
Why it matters

Failing jobs still burn compute — a job that fails after 55 minutes bills the full 55 minutes of DBUs before it errors out. This query surfaces the biggest DBU-burning failing jobs so an admin can prioritize reliability fixes by dollar impact rather than by raw failure count, and pairs each with the latest failure's root-cause code to point at the fix (quota limits, cluster errors, storage access). The wasted_dbus_proxy is an honest prioritization signal, not an exact refundable amount, so it ranks remediation effort where it recovers the most compute spend.

What it does
  • Finds every job that had at least one failed run in the window, with its run counts and the latest failure's cause code.
  • Attaches the DBUs billed to each job and scales them by the failed-run share to estimate the compute burned on failures.
  • Ranks the waste so you fix the biggest DBU-burning failing jobs first — the estimate is a prioritisation signal, not an exact refund.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
workspace_idThe workspace the job belongs to; part of every join key because job_id is unique only within a workspace.
job_idThe Databricks job (workflow) identifier, passed through unmasked; unique only within its workspace.
distinct_runsInteger count of distinct run_ids that reached an end row (result_state populated) in the window — the denominator of the failed share.
failed_runsInteger count of distinct run_ids whose end state was FAILED, ERROR, or TIMED_OUT. Always >= 1 in this result set.
last_failed_termination_codeThe termination_code of the most recent failed run (by period_start_time), e.g. CLUSTER_ERROR or WORKSPACE_RUN_LIMIT_EXCEEDED. NULL means the code was not populated (pre-Aug-2024 or short-history) — read as 'unknown', not 'no cause'.
net_job_dbusTotal DBUs (not dollars) billed to this job over the window, netted across all record_types and filtered to usage_unit='DBU'. COALESCE'd to 0 when no billing row matched.
wasted_dbus_proxyProxy DBUs (not dollars) attributed to failed runs = net_job_dbus * (failed_runs / distinct_runs). A prioritization signal, not exact run-level waste, since billing has no run_id. Rank on this.
Sample output
workspace_idjob_iddistinct_runsfailed_runslast_failed_termination_codenet_job_dbuswasted_dbus_proxy
123456789012345688442210025CLUSTER_ERROR800200
12345678901234567755111010STORAGE_ACCESS_ERROR15001500
9876543210987654660033486WORKSPACE_RUN_LIMIT_EXCEEDED1200150
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.lakeflow.job_run_timelineRun-level execution log. Filtered to end rows (result_state IS NOT NULL) within the trailing :period_days window (excluding today) to count distinct runs and failed runs per (workspace_id, job_id), and to pick the latest failed run's termination_code via QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER.
system.billing.usageMetered-usage fact. Summed to net DBUs per (workspace_id, usage_metadata.job_id), filtered to usage_unit='DBU' and the same date window, to attach billed DBUs to each job. usage_metadata carries job_id but no run_id.
Caveats & gotchas
  • wasted_dbus_proxy is a PROXY, not exact run-level waste: usage_metadata carries job_id but NO run_id, so DBUs attribute to the JOB and are scaled by failed-run share. A mostly-succeeding job over-attributes.
  • Units: net_job_dbus and wasted_dbus_proxy are DBUs, NOT dollars. There is no DBU-to-$ rate applied here; multiply by your negotiated SKU rate separately to estimate cost.
  • usage<->run join is on (workspace_id, job_id), never job_id alone — job_id is unique only within a workspace, so a bare join fans out across a multi-workspace metastore.
  • usage_quantity is SUM'd across all record_types (ORIGINAL/RETRACTION/RESTATEMENT already net) and filtered to usage_unit='DBU' so bytes/hours/tokens never blend in.
  • last_failed_termination_code is the ARGMAX by period_start_time (latest failed run's code), not MAX() which would be lexicographically wrong. It may be NULL: termination_code was not populated before ~Aug-2024 and several run columns not before late-Nov-2025 — historical NULLs are 'unknown', never a finding.
  • Only end rows count: result_state IS NOT NULL isolates the final slice of hourly-sliced long runs; failure set is result_state IN ('FAILED','ERROR','TIMED_OUT') only (SKIPPED/CANCELLED/BLOCKED excluded).
  • Date window is trailing :period_days and excludes today: run timeline uses period_end_time < date_trunc('DAY', current_timestamp()) and billing uses usage_date < current_date() so the partial current day never skews totals.
  • Empty != zero != error: an account that runs no jobs returns an empty-but-valid result. All reads need Unity Catalog and SELECT granted on system.lakeflow and system.billing. confidence: needs_confirmation.
  • net_job_dbus is COALESCE'd to 0 when a failing job has no matching billing row, so wasted_dbus_proxy is 0 there — absence of billed DBUs, not zero waste.
→
What you do next
Rank the returned jobs by wasted_dbus_proxy, open the top few in the Jobs UI, and use last_failed_termination_code to drive the fix (raise concurrency/queue limits for quota codes, fix compute/storage config for CLUSTER_ERROR/STORAGE_ACCESS_ERROR); target the biggest DBU-burning failing jobs first, treating the proxy as a prioritization signal not an exact billed refund.
← PreviousSQL Warehouse Event Activitysql_warehouse_events_activity All Jobs & Pipelines ↑ Next →Job Tasks On All-Purpose Computelakeflow_jobs_on_all_purpose
Overview / Jobs & Pipelines / lakeflow_jobs_on_all_purpose
02
Jobs & Pipelines lakeflow_jobs_on_all_purpose ★ first-audit pick

Job Tasks On All-Purpose Compute

Per-job, per-cluster task-run counts over the trailing 30 days, tagged with the cluster's source so you can spot job tasks running on all-purpose (UI/API) compute instead of cheaper job clusters.

system.lakeflow.job_task_run_timelinesystem.compute.clusters
queries/jobs_pipelines/lakeflow_jobs_on_all_purpose.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:lakeflow_jobs_on_all_purpose */
WITH task_compute AS (
  SELECT workspace_id, job_id, run_id, task_key, EXPLODE(compute_ids) AS compute_id
  FROM system.lakeflow.job_task_run_timeline
  WHERE period_start_time >= date_add(current_date(), -30)
    AND period_end_time < date_trunc('DAY', current_timestamp())
    AND result_state IS NOT NULL
    AND compute_ids IS NOT NULL
)
SELECT tc.workspace_id, tc.job_id, tc.compute_id, c.cluster_source,
       COUNT(DISTINCT tc.run_id) AS task_runs
FROM task_compute tc
LEFT JOIN (
  SELECT workspace_id, cluster_id, cluster_source
  FROM system.compute.clusters
  QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY workspace_id, cluster_id ORDER BY change_time DESC) = 1
) c
  ON tc.workspace_id = c.workspace_id AND tc.compute_id = c.cluster_id
GROUP BY tc.workspace_id, tc.job_id, tc.compute_id, c.cluster_source
Why it matters

All-purpose (interactive) compute is billed at a materially higher DBU SKU than jobs compute for the same work, so any scheduled job task pinned to a UI- or API-created cluster is quietly overpaying every run. Grouping task-run counts by job and cluster surfaces exactly which jobs are the offenders and how often they run there, letting you prioritize migrating high-frequency jobs onto job clusters (or serverless) for a direct, recurring bill reduction. A NULL cluster_source (unmatched compute_id) is not proof of good behavior — it means the cluster could not be classified and needs manual review.

What it does
  • Looks at which clusters each job's tasks actually ran on, and tags every cluster with how it was created.
  • Flags tasks that ran on all-purpose (UI/API) clusters — billed higher than job clusters for the same work — and counts how many runs did so.
  • A cluster it can't classify is left as 'unknown' rather than assumed fine, so you review it instead of trusting it.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
workspace_idThe workspace the job and cluster belong to. All ids below are unique only within this workspace, so always read a row as the (workspace_id, job_id, compute_id) tuple.
job_idThe job (workflow) whose task ran on this cluster. Workspace-scoped; join back to system.lakeflow.jobs on (workspace_id, job_id) to get the job name.
compute_idThe cluster id (one element of the task's compute_ids array) that the task executed on. Matches system.compute.clusters.cluster_id within the workspace.
cluster_sourceHow the cluster was created: 'UI' or 'API' = all-purpose (the higher-cost anti-pattern to migrate); job-created sources = expected. NULL = the compute_id did not match a deduped cluster row (not assessed — investigate, do not treat as compliant).
task_runsCOUNT(DISTINCT run_id): the number of distinct job runs whose tasks used this cluster in the window. A high value on a UI/API cluster is a frequently-repeating overspend, not a one-off.
Sample output
workspace_idjob_idcompute_idcluster_sourcetask_runs
12345678901234568844220715-091234-ab12cd34UI612
12345678901234567755330715-090011-jobclu99JOB1440
98765432109876546600110715-084455-zz99yy88API48
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.lakeflow.job_task_run_timelinePrimary fact: task-level execution log (hourly-sliced). Filtered to completed end rows (result_state IS NOT NULL) in the trailing 30 days, then EXPLODE(compute_ids) fans each task-state row into one row per cluster the task ran on, yielding (workspace_id, job_id, run_id, task_key, compute_id).
system.compute.clustersCross-domain lookup: SCD2 cluster inventory, deduped to the latest row per (workspace_id, cluster_id) via QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER() ORDER BY change_time DESC. Supplies cluster_source (UI/API = all-purpose vs job-created) via a LEFT JOIN on (workspace_id, compute_id = cluster_id).
Caveats & gotchas
  • Anti-pattern filter is downstream: the query returns every (job, cluster) pairing; you must filter cluster_source IN ('UI','API') yourself to isolate all-purpose usage.
  • EXPLODE drops NULL/empty compute_ids arrays — those task runs are absent entirely, not counted as zero. Report unpopulated-compute rows as a separate 'not assessed' bucket (early-Dec-2025 compute_ids population caveat).
  • NULL cluster_source means the compute_id did not match a cluster row (unpopulated/aged-out compute or SCD2 gap) — 'empty != zero != error'; treat as not assessed, not as compliant.
  • Cross-domain join (confidence: needs_confirmation). Confirm on the target workspace that compute_ids is populated on old rows, and that cluster_source's exact name and value enum ('UI'/'API') and clusters' SCD2 behavior hold.
  • task_runs is a run count, not DBUs or dollars. This query carries no cost figures — quantify savings separately from system.billing.usage; DBUs are not dollars.
  • All join and grouping keys are workspace-scoped (workspace_id, cluster_id/compute_id and workspace_id, job_id); never match on the bare id or you fan out across workspaces.
  • Date window is a fixed trailing 30 days (date_add(current_date(), -30)) and excludes today (period_end_time < date_trunc('DAY', ...)); it is not parameterized by :period_days here.
  • SCD2 dedupe on system.compute.clusters is mandatory (latest row per cluster by change_time); the query already applies it via QUALIFY. Note it does NOT additionally filter delete_time IS NULL, so a deleted-then-latest cluster row can still supply cluster_source.
  • Requires Unity Catalog and SELECT granted on both system.lakeflow and system.compute schemas.
→
What you do next
Filter the output to cluster_source IN ('UI','API'), rank the resulting (workspace_id, job_id, compute_id) rows by task_runs, and migrate the highest-frequency offenders off all-purpose clusters onto job clusters or serverless; separately triage NULL cluster_source rows to confirm the compute could not be classified before assuming compliance.
← PreviousFailing Jobs Ranked by Wasted DBUslakeflow_failed_jobs_wasted_dbus All Jobs & Pipelines ↑ Next →Job Failure Breakdown by Root Causelakeflow_failed_runs
Overview / Jobs & Pipelines / lakeflow_failed_runs
03
Jobs & Pipelines lakeflow_failed_runs

Job Failure Breakdown by Root Cause

A 30-day census of completed job runs bucketed by workspace, launch type, trigger, final outcome, and root-cause code, with raw-row, distinct-run, and failed-row counts for each bucket.

system.lakeflow.job_run_timeline
queries/jobs_pipelines/lakeflow_failed_runs.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:lakeflow_failed_runs */
WITH end_rows AS (
  SELECT workspace_id, job_id, run_id, run_type, trigger_type,
         result_state, termination_code, period_start_time, period_end_time
  FROM system.lakeflow.job_run_timeline
  WHERE period_start_time >= date_add(current_date(), -30)
    AND period_end_time < date_trunc('DAY', current_timestamp())   -- drop incomplete current day
    AND result_state IS NOT NULL                                   -- end row only
)
SELECT workspace_id, run_type, trigger_type, result_state, termination_code,
       COUNT(*)              AS run_rows,
       COUNT(DISTINCT run_id) AS distinct_runs,
       SUM(CASE WHEN result_state IN ('FAILED','ERROR','TIMED_OUT') THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS failed_run_rows
FROM end_rows
GROUP BY workspace_id, run_type, trigger_type, result_state, termination_code
Why it matters

This is the baseline reliability posture for the whole job fleet - it tells an admin which trigger types and which error codes actually dominate failures, so remediation effort goes where the failures concentrate rather than where they are merely visible. A spike in `WORKSPACE_RUN_LIMIT_EXCEEDED` or `MAX_JOB_QUEUE_SIZE_EXCEEDED` signals a capacity or concurrency ceiling that is silently killing runs, while `CLUSTER_ERROR` or `STORAGE_ACCESS_ERROR` points at infrastructure or permissions. Heavy `RETRY_ON_FAILURE` volume is the early warning of flapping jobs that burn compute passing on the second or third attempt. Left unread, these failures cost missed SLAs, wasted DBUs on doomed retries, and on-call time chasing symptoms instead of the dominant cause.

What it does
  • Takes every job run that finished in the last 30 days (excluding today) and sorts them into buckets by workspace, how they were launched, what triggered them, their final outcome, and their root-cause code.
  • For each bucket it reports three counts: raw end rows, distinct actual runs (de-duplicating hourly slices and retries), and how many of those rows are failures.
  • Isolates the documented failure set - FAILED, ERROR, TIMED_OUT - while intentionally leaving SKIPPED, CANCELLED, and BLOCKED out of the failed tally.
  • Surfaces which termination_code and trigger_type combinations dominate, so quota/limit hits and retry storms stand out from routine successes.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
workspace_idThe workspace the runs executed in. Ids and run counts are scoped here - compare failures workspace by workspace.
run_typeHow the run was launched: `JOB_RUN` (a scheduled/triggered job), `SUBMIT_RUN` (one-off API submit), or `WORKFLOW_RUN` (a run inside a parent workflow).
trigger_typeWhat triggered this run, including `RETRY_ON_FAILURE` - a high failed count on that trigger means jobs are repeatedly failing and retrying.
result_stateThe final outcome of the run: `SUCCEEDED`, `FAILED`, `ERROR`, `TIMED_OUT`, `SKIPPED`, `CANCELLED`, or `BLOCKED`. The first three are the 'failed' set.
termination_codeThe root-cause code for how the run ended (e.g. `WORKSPACE_RUN_LIMIT_EXCEEDED`, `MAX_JOB_QUEUE_SIZE_EXCEEDED`, `CLUSTER_ERROR`, `STORAGE_ACCESS_ERROR`). NULL here means the code was not recorded, not that there was no cause.
run_rowsRaw number of end rows in this group. Inflated by hourly slicing and retries - use it only relative to `distinct_runs`, not as a run count.
distinct_runsThe number of actual distinct runs in this group. This is the honest 'how many runs' figure.
failed_run_rowsEnd rows in this group that landed in the failed set. Because groups are split by `result_state`, this equals `run_rows` on a failed row and is 0 on a non-failed row - a convenience flag for filtering to failures.
Sample output
workspace_idrun_typetrigger_typeresult_statetermination_coderun_rowsdistinct_runsfailed_run_rows
1234567890123456JOB_RUNRETRY_ON_FAILUREFAILEDCLUSTER_ERROR473947
1234567890123456JOB_RUNPERIODICTIMED_OUTMAX_JOB_QUEUE_SIZE_EXCEEDED121212
9876543210987654SUBMIT_RUNONE_TIMESUCCEEDEDSUCCESS3102980
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.lakeflow.job_run_timelineThe sole source. Provides the run-level end rows (`result_state IS NOT NULL`) filtered to a 30-day window, supplying `workspace_id`, `run_type`, `trigger_type`, `result_state`, `termination_code`, `run_id`, and the `period_start_time`/`period_end_time` used to scope the window and drop the incomplete current day.
Caveats & gotchas
  • 30-day rolling window: `period_start_time >= date_add(current_date(), -30)`; the incomplete current day is dropped via `period_end_time < date_trunc('DAY', current_timestamp())`, so today's runs never appear.
  • Failed = `result_state IN ('FAILED','ERROR','TIMED_OUT')` only. `SKIPPED`, `CANCELLED`, and `BLOCKED` are deliberately NOT counted as failures.
  • This is a census of ALL completed runs, not just failures: successful runs appear too (with `failed_run_rows` = 0). There is no failed-only filter in the query - you filter to failures yourself by reading rows where `result_state` is in the failed set.
  • `run_rows` != `distinct_runs`: long runs are sliced hourly and retries reuse the same `run_id`, so raw row counts overstate the number of actual runs. Trust `distinct_runs` for how many runs there were.
  • Only end rows are counted (`result_state IS NOT NULL`); mid-run slices are excluded by design.
  • `termination_code` did not populate before ~Aug-2024 - on short-history accounts it can be NULL for every row, which is 'not assessed,' not 'no root cause.'
  • An account that runs no jobs returns an empty (but valid) result - empty is not zero and not an error.
  • This is a pure count query: no DBUs and no dollars. It tells you how often things fail, not what the failures cost (see `lakeflow_failed_jobs_wasted_dbus` for that).
  • Ids are workspace-scoped: `run_id` and `job_id` are unique only within a `workspace_id`. All grouping keys on the workspace, so never pool runs across workspaces by id alone.
→
What you do next
Read the rows where `result_state IN ('FAILED','ERROR','TIMED_OUT')`, rank the `termination_code` values by `distinct_runs` to find the dominant root causes, then drill into the workspaces and trigger types (especially `RETRY_ON_FAILURE`) that concentrate the failures — and route each top code to its owner (quota/limit codes to capacity planning, `CLUSTER_ERROR`/`STORAGE_ACCESS_ERROR` to platform).
← PreviousJob Tasks On All-Purpose Computelakeflow_jobs_on_all_purpose All Jobs & Pipelines ↑ Next →Job Health-Rule Coveragelakeflow_health_rule_coverage
Overview / Jobs & Pipelines / lakeflow_health_rule_coverage
04
Jobs & Pipelines lakeflow_health_rule_coverage

Job Health-Rule Coverage

Per-workspace counts of active jobs that have a configured health rule versus none, with a separate bucket for jobs where the health_rules column has not been populated yet.

system.lakeflow.jobs
queries/jobs_pipelines/lakeflow_health_rule_coverage.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:lakeflow_health_rule_coverage */
WITH latest_jobs AS (
  SELECT workspace_id, job_id, name, health_rules, delete_time
  FROM system.lakeflow.jobs
  QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
    PARTITION BY workspace_id, job_id ORDER BY change_time DESC
  ) = 1
)
SELECT workspace_id,
       COUNT(*) AS active_jobs,
       -- NULL = column not yet populated (unknown), reported as a separate not-assessed bucket
       SUM(CASE WHEN health_rules IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS jobs_health_rules_null,
       -- confident "has a health rule": non-null AND a non-empty rule set
       SUM(CASE WHEN health_rules IS NOT NULL AND CARDINALITY(health_rules) > 0
                THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)                            AS jobs_with_health_rule,
       -- confident "no health rule": non-null but empty rule set (populated window, no rule)
       SUM(CASE WHEN health_rules IS NOT NULL AND CARDINALITY(health_rules) = 0
                THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)                            AS jobs_no_health_rule
FROM latest_jobs
WHERE delete_time IS NULL
GROUP BY workspace_id
Why it matters

Health rules are how a job self-reports when a run fails, runs too long, or misses an SLA — without them, a job can silently degrade or hang and no one is paged. This query tells an admin how many active jobs across each workspace have zero automated failure/duration alerting, which is a governance and reliability gap that directly precedes missed data deliveries and runaway compute. Crucially, it separates "confidently no rule" from "column not yet populated," so on short-history accounts you do not chase a false gap. Closing the uncovered count is a concrete step toward provable operational oversight.

What it does
  • Counts, per workspace, how many active (non-deleted) jobs exist right now after collapsing each job's change history to its current version.
  • Splits those jobs into three buckets: has a configured health rule, confidently has no health rule, and not assessed because the health_rules column is empty/unpopulated.
  • Keeps the not-assessed (jobs_health_rules_null) bucket separate on purpose so a fully-blank column on a short-history account reads as "unknown," never as "zero coverage."
  • Produces a coverage scorecard you can read as jobs-with-alerting vs jobs-without, one row per workspace.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
workspace_idThe workspace the counts belong to; all job ids are scoped to it. One row per workspace.
active_jobsTotal active, non-deleted job definitions in the workspace after SCD2 dedupe. This is the denominator; the three buckets below sum to it.
jobs_health_rules_nullJobs whose health_rules column is NULL — treat as "not assessed, column not yet populated," NOT as "no rule configured." If this equals active_jobs, the account is short-history and coverage cannot be judged.
jobs_with_health_ruleConfident coverage: jobs with a non-null, non-empty health-rule set (CARDINALITY > 0). These have automated failure/duration alerting configured.
jobs_no_health_ruleConfident gap: jobs that exist in a populated window but carry an empty rule set (CARDINALITY = 0) — genuinely no health rule. These are the actionable remediation targets.
Sample output
workspace_idactive_jobsjobs_health_rules_nulljobs_with_health_rulejobs_no_health_rule
123456789012345612003882
2345678901234567646400
3456789012345678153111
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.lakeflow.jobsThe SCD2 change-history catalog of job definitions. The query takes the latest row per (workspace_id, job_id) by change_time, drops user-deleted jobs (delete_time IS NULL), and inspects each surviving job's health_rules column to classify coverage.
Caveats & gotchas
  • Confidence: needs_confirmation — this query is not yet workspace-verified.
  • health_rules is late-populated (not before ~late-Nov-2025). A row where jobs_health_rules_null equals active_jobs means the column is not yet populated — read as 'not assessed,' never as 'no coverage.'
  • jobs_with_health_rule uses CARDINALITY(health_rules) > 0, which assumes health_rules is an array/struct. If it is scalar/STRING on your account, the coverage split degrades to the NULL bucket rather than mis-reporting — confirm the column type.
  • Empty result != zero jobs != error: an account with no jobs returns no rows; a workspace missing entirely from output simply ran no active jobs.
  • SCD2 dedupe is mandatory: the query keeps the latest row per (workspace_id, job_id) by change_time, then filters delete_time IS NULL. Counting raw change-history rows would inflate every bucket.
  • job_id is unique only within a workspace — dedupe and grouping key on (workspace_id, job_id), never job_id alone, to avoid cross-workspace fan-out.
  • No date window: this is a current-state snapshot of active job definitions, not a trailing-period aggregation.
  • Requires Unity Catalog and SELECT granted on the system.lakeflow schema.
→
What you do next
For each workspace, close the coverage gap: take the jobs_no_health_rule count and attach health rules (failure/duration alerting) to those jobs. Where jobs_health_rules_null dominates, do not act on it as a gap — wait for the health_rules column to populate before assessing coverage.
← PreviousJob Failure Breakdown by Root Causelakeflow_failed_runs All Jobs & Pipelines ↑ Next →Orphaned And Handed-Off Jobslakeflow_job_ownership_orphans
Overview / Jobs & Pipelines / lakeflow_job_ownership_orphans
05
Jobs & Pipelines lakeflow_job_ownership_orphans

Orphaned And Handed-Off Jobs

Per-workspace counts of active jobs whose creator or run-as identity is missing, whose creator and run-as differ (a handoff or service-principal pattern), or that look orphaned because no known identity runs them.

system.lakeflow.jobs
queries/jobs_pipelines/lakeflow_job_ownership_orphans.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:lakeflow_job_ownership_orphans */
WITH latest_jobs AS (
  SELECT workspace_id, job_id, name, creator_user_name, run_as_user_name, delete_time
  FROM system.lakeflow.jobs
  QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
    PARTITION BY workspace_id, job_id ORDER BY change_time DESC
  ) = 1
),
-- normalise unavailable / redacted identity placeholders to NULL so they are never
-- counted as a real owner or a real mismatch.
norm AS (
  SELECT workspace_id, job_id, name,
         CASE WHEN creator_user_name IS NULL
                OR trim(creator_user_name) = ''
                OR upper(creator_user_name) = '__REDACTED__'
              THEN NULL ELSE creator_user_name END AS creator_user_name,
         CASE WHEN run_as_user_name IS NULL
                OR trim(run_as_user_name) = ''
                OR upper(run_as_user_name) = '__REDACTED__'
              THEN NULL ELSE run_as_user_name END  AS run_as_user_name
  FROM latest_jobs
  WHERE delete_time IS NULL
)
SELECT workspace_id,
       COUNT(*) AS active_jobs,
       SUM(CASE WHEN creator_user_name IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS jobs_creator_null,
       SUM(CASE WHEN run_as_user_name  IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS jobs_runas_null,
       -- confident mismatch: both identities known & different (handoff / SP run-as)
       SUM(CASE WHEN creator_user_name IS NOT NULL
                 AND run_as_user_name IS NOT NULL
                 AND creator_user_name <> run_as_user_name
                THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)                                AS jobs_owner_mismatch,
       -- orphan candidate: run-as identity unknown but creator known (no one clearly runs it)
       SUM(CASE WHEN run_as_user_name IS NULL AND creator_user_name IS NOT NULL
                THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)                                AS jobs_orphan_runas_unknown
FROM norm
GROUP BY workspace_id
Why it matters

Ownership accountability is a governance control: a job whose run-as identity is unknown has no clear responsible party, and a job whose creator and run-as differ is a legitimate but review-worthy handoff or service-principal pattern that can outlive the person who owns it. When an owner leaves or a service principal is decommissioned, these jobs silently keep running (or silently break) with no one accountable. This is a governance posture signal, not a cost figure — it tells you where to tighten ownership before an audit or an offboarding does it for you.

What it does
  • Reports one row per workspace summarizing the ownership health of its active jobs (deleted jobs excluded, latest configuration only).
  • Splits out how many jobs have an unknown creator (jobs_creator_null) or unknown run-as identity (jobs_runas_null) so a workspace whose identity columns simply haven't populated yet degrades to not assessed rather than reading as all-orphaned.
  • Counts confident ownership mismatches — jobs where both identities are known and different — the handoff / service-principal run-as cases worth a look.
  • Counts orphan candidates — jobs with a known creator but no known run-as identity, i.e. no one clearly runs them.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
workspace_idThe workspace the counts below belong to; all job ids and identities are scoped to it, so numbers never blend across workspaces.
active_jobsTotal active (non-deleted, latest-version) jobs in the workspace — the denominator for every other count.
jobs_creator_nullHow many active jobs have no usable creator identity (NULL, blank, or a redacted/__REDACTED__ placeholder). If this equals active_jobs, the creator column is simply not populated on this account — treat as not assessed, not a finding.
jobs_runas_nullHow many active jobs have no usable run-as identity, under the same normalization. Equal to active_jobs means the column is unpopulated (not assessed), not that every job is orphaned.
jobs_owner_mismatchConfident mismatches: jobs where both creator and run-as are known, non-redacted, and different. A legitimate handoff or service-principal run-as pattern that is worth reviewing, not automatically a problem.
jobs_orphan_runas_unknownOrphan candidates: jobs with a known creator but an unknown run-as identity — no one clearly runs them. This is a subset of jobs_runas_null (the portion that also has a known creator).
Sample output
workspace_idactive_jobsjobs_creator_nulljobs_runas_nulljobs_owner_mismatchjobs_orphan_runas_unknown
12345678901234561200080
223344556677889945454500
3344556677889900602563
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.lakeflow.jobsThe SCD2 catalog of job (workflow) definitions and the only table this query reads. It supplies workspace_id, job_id, name, creator_user_name, run_as_user_name, delete_time (to keep only active jobs), and change_time (to pick the latest configuration row per job before counting).
Caveats & gotchas
  • Identity columns are late-populated: creator_user_name and run_as_user_name were not populated before ~late-Nov-2025, so on short-history accounts they are NULL for every job. A fully-NULL column means not assessed, never a finding — that is why jobs_creator_null / jobs_runas_null are exposed.
  • Empty != zero != error: a NULL identity is UNATTRIBUTABLE (unknown owner), not proof of an orphan; a workspace with jobs_creator_null = active_jobs simply hasn't populated the column.
  • FedRAMP / redacted workspaces emit '__REDACTED__' for identities; these are normalized to NULL and treated as unavailable, never counted as a mismatch.
  • A confident owner mismatch requires BOTH identities populated AND non-redacted AND different — it flags handoffs / service-principal run-as, which are legitimate patterns to review, not violations.
  • jobs is an SCD2 change-history table: you must take the latest row per (workspace_id, job_id) by change_time, then keep delete_time IS NULL, or counts inflate.
  • job_id is unique only within a workspace, so all counting is keyed on (workspace_id, job_id) — never job_id alone.
  • This is a governance/ownership posture signal, not a cost or DBU figure. There is no date window — it reflects the current latest state of each active job.
→
What you do next
Review the workspaces with non-zero jobs_orphan_runas_unknown and jobs_owner_mismatch first: reassign or confirm a valid run-as (service principal) for each orphan candidate, and validate that each mismatch is an intended handoff. Where jobs_creator_null / jobs_runas_null equals active_jobs, mark the workspace not assessed until the identity columns populate rather than acting on it.
← PreviousJob Health-Rule Coveragelakeflow_health_rule_coverage All Jobs & Pipelines ↑ Next →Jobs Stuck Waiting On Capacitylakeflow_job_queue_time
Overview / Jobs & Pipelines / lakeflow_job_queue_time
06
Jobs & Pipelines lakeflow_job_queue_time

Jobs Stuck Waiting On Capacity

Per-job queue-wait time (total seconds and approximate p95 per run) over the trailing window, with a separate counter of runs whose queue time is unknown, so admins can find jobs that sit waiting on capacity before they start.

system.lakeflow.job_run_timeline
queries/jobs_pipelines/lakeflow_job_queue_time.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:lakeflow_job_queue_time */
WITH end_rows AS (
  SELECT workspace_id, job_id, run_id, queue_duration_seconds
  FROM system.lakeflow.job_run_timeline
  WHERE period_start_time >= dateadd(day, -:period_days, current_date())
    AND period_end_time < date_trunc('DAY', current_timestamp())   -- drop incomplete current day
    AND result_state IS NOT NULL                                   -- end row only
)
SELECT workspace_id, job_id,
       COUNT(DISTINCT run_id)                                   AS distinct_runs,
       SUM(queue_duration_seconds)                              AS queue_s_total,
       percentile_approx(queue_duration_seconds, 0.95)         AS queue_s_p95,
       SUM(CASE WHEN queue_duration_seconds IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS runs_queue_null
FROM end_rows
GROUP BY workspace_id, job_id
Why it matters

Queue time is jobs sitting idle waiting on capacity or concurrency limits before they even start — latency your users feel and, indirectly, SLAs you miss, with no code bug to blame. Ranking jobs by total and p95 queue wait tells an admin exactly which workflows are starved and are candidates for higher concurrency limits or dedicated job compute. Because a NULL queue value means 'unknown,' not 'no wait,' the separate `runs_queue_null` bucket keeps a short-history account (where the column isn't populated yet) from being misread as a clean bill of health.

What it does
  • Totals how many seconds each job spent queued (waiting on capacity before it could start) over the trailing window, per job.
  • Reports both the cumulative wait (queue_s_total) and a near-worst-case per-run wait (queue_s_p95) so occasional long stalls surface even when the total looks small.
  • Counts each job's distinct completed runs so you can weigh the queue numbers against how often the job actually ran.
  • Keeps a separate tally of runs whose queue time is unknown (runs_queue_null), so a job with a not-yet-populated column reads as 'not assessed' rather than as having zero wait.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
workspace_idThe workspace the job belongs to. Part of the identifying key because `job_id` is only unique within a workspace.
job_idThe job (workflow) identifier, unique only within its `workspace_id`. Together they name one job across all its runs in the window.
distinct_runsCount of distinct completed runs for this job in the window (end rows only). The denominator for judging how representative the queue figures are.
queue_s_totalTotal seconds this job's runs spent queued (waiting on capacity) over the window. NULL/empty means not populated, not zero. Bigger = more cumulative waiting.
queue_s_p95Approximate 95th-percentile per-run queue wait in seconds — the near-worst-case delay a run of this job sees before it starts. Use this to spot jobs with occasional long waits even if the total looks modest.
runs_queue_nullHow many of this job's end rows had a NULL `queue_duration_seconds` (column not yet populated). If this equals `distinct_runs`, treat the job as 'not assessed — column not populated,' not as zero queue time.
Sample output
workspace_idjob_iddistinct_runsqueue_s_totalqueue_s_p95runs_queue_null
123456789012345688442211335512054001800
1234567890123456775533991122486030
987654321098765444556677889930NULLNULL30
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.lakeflow.job_run_timelineSole source. Provides the run-level execution log; the query reads `queue_duration_seconds` from each run's end row (`result_state IS NOT NULL`), keyed by `workspace_id`, `job_id`, `run_id`, and windowed on `period_start_time`/`period_end_time`. This is the only table where `queue_duration_seconds` exists.
Caveats & gotchas
  • Availability/date gating: `queue_duration_seconds` was not populated before ~late-Nov-2025 — on short-history accounts it is NULL for every run, so `queue_s_total`/`queue_s_p95` come back NULL and the whole result is 'not assessed,' not zero queue.
  • Empty != zero != error: an account that runs no jobs returns an empty (but valid) result; a NULL queue value means 'unknown/not yet populated,' never 'no queue time.' Check `runs_queue_null` against `distinct_runs` before trusting a total.
  • No dollars: queue seconds are not a billing unit and Databricks publishes no DBU->$ rate for duration metrics — never convert `queue_s_*` to money.
  • Seconds, not ms: `queue_s_total` and `queue_s_p95` are in seconds; `queue_s_p95` is an approximate (not exact) 95th percentile.
  • Column lives only here: `queue_duration_seconds` exists solely on `job_run_timeline`, not on `job_task_run_timeline` — do not try to source it task-side.
  • End-row only: metrics count just the run's final slice (`result_state IS NOT NULL`) so long (>1h) hourly-sliced runs are not double-counted; note retries reuse the same `run_id`, so `runs_queue_null` (a row count) can exceed `distinct_runs` (a distinct count) when a run retried.
  • Workspace-scoped keys: `job_id` is unique only within a workspace — every total is grouped on (`workspace_id`, `job_id`); never key on `job_id` alone in a multi-workspace metastore.
  • Date window: trailing `:period_days` ending yesterday — the incomplete current day is dropped so partial-day data never skews totals.
  • Confidence is needs_confirmation: confirm `queue_duration_seconds` actually populates on the target workspace before acting on the numbers.
→
What you do next
Sort by `queue_s_p95` (ignoring jobs where `runs_queue_null` equals `distinct_runs` — those are "not assessed"). For the top queue-heavy jobs, raise the workspace/job concurrency limit or move them onto dedicated job compute so they stop waiting on capacity, then re-check next period.
← PreviousOrphaned And Handed-Off Jobslakeflow_job_ownership_orphans All Jobs & Pipelines ↑ Next →Task-Level Timeout Coverage Gapslakeflow_job_tasks_no_timeout
Overview / Jobs & Pipelines / lakeflow_job_tasks_no_timeout
07
Jobs & Pipelines lakeflow_job_tasks_no_timeout

Task-Level Timeout Coverage Gaps

Per-workspace counts of active job tasks and how many of them have no (or zero) task-level timeout configured, with a separate bucket for the "not yet populated" NULL case.

system.lakeflow.job_tasks
queries/jobs_pipelines/lakeflow_job_tasks_no_timeout.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:lakeflow_job_tasks_no_timeout */
WITH latest_tasks AS (
  SELECT workspace_id, job_id, task_key, timeout_seconds, delete_time
  FROM system.lakeflow.job_tasks
  QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY workspace_id, job_id, task_key ORDER BY change_time DESC) = 1
)
SELECT workspace_id,
       COUNT(*) AS active_tasks,
       SUM(CASE WHEN timeout_seconds IS NULL OR timeout_seconds = 0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS tasks_no_timeout,
       SUM(CASE WHEN timeout_seconds IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS tasks_timeout_null
FROM latest_tasks
WHERE delete_time IS NULL
GROUP BY workspace_id
Why it matters

A task with no timeout can hang indefinitely — waiting on a stuck upstream, a wedged connection, or a runaway query — while its cluster keeps billing DBUs the entire time. Job-level timeouts don't always protect you: a single misbehaving task inside an otherwise-bounded job is a real gap, and this view catches it at the finer task grain. Setting sane task timeouts converts silent open-ended spend into a fast, visible failure. The extra NULL counter keeps you honest: on accounts with little history the timeout column simply isn't populated yet, so a high "no timeout" count may mean "not assessed" rather than a genuine governance failure.

What it does
  • Reports one row per workspace summarizing task-level timeout hygiene across all active job tasks.
  • Counts total active tasks (active_tasks) alongside how many lack an effective timeout — either unset or explicitly zero (tasks_no_timeout).
  • Separates out the tasks whose timeout is specifically NULL (tasks_timeout_null) so you can tell a real gap from a column that Databricks hasn't back-filled yet.
  • Looks only at the current definition of each task and excludes deleted tasks, so counts reflect today's live configuration.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
workspace_idThe Databricks workspace these task counts belong to. Task keys are unique only within a job and job/task ids are unique only within a workspace, so every number here is scoped to this one workspace.
active_tasksNumber of live (non-deleted) tasks in this workspace after collapsing each task to its latest configuration version. The denominator for coverage.
tasks_no_timeoutHow many of those active tasks have no effective timeout — either `timeout_seconds` is NULL or it is 0. These are the tasks that can run open-ended. This count includes the NULL ones.
tasks_timeout_nullThe subset of `tasks_no_timeout` where the value is specifically NULL (not a literal 0). Read as the 'not assessed' bucket: on short-history accounts the column is late-populated, so a large NULL count likely means the data isn't there yet, not that thousands of tasks were misconfigured.
Sample output
workspace_idactive_taskstasks_no_timeouttasks_timeout_null
12345678901234561489240
2233445566778899373737
998877665544332221060
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.lakeflow.job_tasksThe only table read. Per-task definitions inside each job (SCD2 change history). The query takes the latest version of every task by (workspace_id, job_id, task_key), keeps the ones still active, and inspects each task's timeout_seconds.
Caveats & gotchas
  • `system.lakeflow.job_tasks` is SCD2 change history — the query keeps only the latest row per (workspace_id, job_id, task_key) by change_time. Without that dedupe, task counts inflate.
  • `task_key` is unique only within a job, and job/workspace ids are workspace-scoped; all grouping keys on workspace_id — never treat a task_key as globally unique.
  • `timeout_seconds` for tasks is late-populated (~early-Dec-2025). On short-history accounts it is NULL for every task, so tasks_no_timeout can look alarmingly high — read tasks_timeout_null first and degrade a fully-NULL column to 'not assessed,' never a finding.
  • Empty != zero != error: an account that defines no job tasks returns no rows. That is a valid empty result, not a failure.
  • Excludes deleted tasks (delete_time IS NULL). A task removed from a job won't appear even if it once lacked a timeout.
  • Distinguish 'no timeout' from 'NULL timeout': tasks_no_timeout counts both NULL and literal 0; a 0 is a deliberate (or accidental) no-limit setting, while NULL may just be unpopulated data.
  • GA table but requires Unity Catalog and SELECT granted on the system.lakeflow schema; without the grant the query errors rather than returning empty.
  • This is a point-in-time snapshot of current task configuration — there is no date window or lookback; it does not reflect historical timeout settings.
→
What you do next
For each workspace, subtract `tasks_timeout_null` from `tasks_no_timeout` to get the tasks that are genuinely set to 0 or otherwise unbounded, then set an explicit task-level `timeout_seconds` on those tasks so a stuck task fails fast instead of billing compute indefinitely. If `tasks_timeout_null` equals `active_tasks`, treat the workspace as 'not assessed — column not yet populated' and re-run once history accrues.
← PreviousJobs Stuck Waiting On Capacitylakeflow_job_queue_time All Jobs & Pipelines ↑ Next →Jobs Missing a Timeoutlakeflow_jobs_no_timeout
Overview / Jobs & Pipelines / lakeflow_jobs_no_timeout
08
Jobs & Pipelines lakeflow_jobs_no_timeout

Jobs Missing a Timeout

Per-workspace counts of active jobs that have no (or zero) job-level timeout configured, alongside a separate "not yet populated" NULL bucket so the finding never over-claims.

system.lakeflow.jobs
queries/jobs_pipelines/lakeflow_jobs_no_timeout.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:lakeflow_jobs_no_timeout */
WITH latest_jobs AS (
  SELECT workspace_id, job_id, name, timeout_seconds, paused, delete_time, create_time
  FROM system.lakeflow.jobs
  QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY workspace_id, job_id ORDER BY change_time DESC) = 1
)
SELECT workspace_id,
       COUNT(*) AS active_jobs,
       SUM(CASE WHEN timeout_seconds IS NULL OR timeout_seconds = 0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS jobs_no_timeout,
       SUM(CASE WHEN timeout_seconds IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS jobs_timeout_null,
       SUM(CASE WHEN create_time     IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS jobs_create_time_null
FROM latest_jobs
WHERE delete_time IS NULL
GROUP BY workspace_id
Why it matters

A job with no timeout can hang indefinitely — a stuck query, a wedged cluster, or an infinite retry keeps consuming compute until someone notices, which can be hours or days of billed DBUs for zero output. Setting a timeout is a cheap, one-time governance control that caps the blast radius of any single runaway run. This query tells an admin, per workspace, how many active jobs lack that guardrail so they can prioritize which workspaces need a timeout policy applied. Because timeout_seconds only began populating in early Dec 2025, the query deliberately isolates the ambiguous NULL cases so you don't mistake "not yet recorded" for "no timeout set."

What it does
  • Reports one row per workspace covering only active jobs (latest version of each job, deleted jobs excluded).
  • Counts how many of those jobs have no timeout or a zero timeout (jobs_no_timeout) — the governance gap worth acting on.
  • Separately counts jobs whose timeout is NULL (jobs_timeout_null), so you can tell a real 'no timeout' finding apart from a column that simply hasn't been populated yet on a short-history account.
  • Adds a data-quality check on how many jobs are missing a create_time, a signal that the metadata itself is incomplete.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
workspace_idThe workspace the counts belong to. Job ids are unique only within a workspace, so every number here is per-workspace, never account-wide.
active_jobsTotal active (latest-version, non-deleted) jobs in the workspace — the denominator for the timeout-coverage picture.
jobs_no_timeoutJobs whose timeout_seconds is NULL OR 0 — i.e. no effective timeout. This is the headline finding, but note it includes the ambiguous NULLs, so read it together with jobs_timeout_null.
jobs_timeout_nullJobs whose timeout_seconds is specifically NULL. If this equals jobs_no_timeout (and roughly equals active_jobs), the column is likely just not-yet-populated on this account — degrade to 'not assessed' rather than reporting a violation. The gap between jobs_no_timeout and this is the count of jobs with an explicit zero.
jobs_create_time_nullJobs missing a create_time value — a metadata-completeness signal; a high count hints the jobs table is only partially populated for this workspace.
Sample output
workspace_idactive_jobsjobs_no_timeoutjobs_timeout_nulljobs_create_time_null
12345678901234561201860
223344556677889964646464
9988776655443322305330
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.lakeflow.jobsThe only table read. Change-history (SCD2) catalog of job definitions; the query takes the latest version per job, keeps active (non-deleted) jobs, and counts on each job's timeout_seconds and create_time (using delete_time to filter out deleted jobs).
Caveats & gotchas
  • timeout_seconds was not populated before ~early Dec 2025, so a NULL is ambiguous — 'no timeout set' vs 'column not yet populated.' Always read jobs_no_timeout next to jobs_timeout_null; when they match and cover nearly all active_jobs, treat it as 'not assessed,' not a finding.
  • jobs_no_timeout counts both NULL and explicit 0. Only the non-NULL portion (jobs_no_timeout minus jobs_timeout_null) is an unambiguous, deliberately-unbounded job.
  • Counts are per workspace only — job_id is unique within a workspace, so figures must not be summed as if account-global identities.
  • SCD2 dedupe is applied (latest version per job by change_time) and deleted jobs are filtered (delete_time IS NULL); without that, counts would inflate. Only active job definitions are included.
  • Empty result != zero != error: an account that runs no jobs returns no rows; this is valid, not a failure.
  • No date window and no dollars here — this is a current-state metadata snapshot of job configuration, not a usage-over-time or cost query. No DBUs or dollars are involved.
  • Requires Unity Catalog and SELECT granted on the system.lakeflow schema; a missing grant fails the read entirely.
→
What you do next
For each workspace where jobs_no_timeout materially exceeds jobs_timeout_null (i.e. real explicit-zero/no-timeout jobs, not just an unpopulated column), apply a default job-level timeout to those jobs so a single hung run can't burn compute indefinitely; where jobs_timeout_null ≈ active_jobs, defer and re-run once the column has populated.
← PreviousTask-Level Timeout Coverage Gapslakeflow_job_tasks_no_timeout All Jobs & Pipelines ↑ Next →Runs Killed Before They Launchedlakeflow_never_started_runs
Overview / Jobs & Pipelines / lakeflow_never_started_runs
09
Jobs & Pipelines lakeflow_never_started_runs

Runs Killed Before They Launched
