A per-job count of Databricks job runs that were terminated before they ever began executing, broken out by the termination reason, over the trailing 30 days.

system.lakeflow.job_run_timeline
queries/jobs_pipelines/lakeflow_never_started_runs.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:lakeflow_never_started_runs */
SELECT workspace_id, job_id, termination_code,
       COUNT(DISTINCT run_id) AS never_started_runs
FROM system.lakeflow.job_run_timeline
WHERE period_start_time >= date_add(current_date(), -30)
  AND period_end_time < date_trunc('DAY', current_timestamp())
  AND result_state IS NOT NULL
  AND period_start_time = period_end_time     -- never began execution
GROUP BY workspace_id, job_id, termination_code
Why it matters

These runs consumed a scheduling slot but produced zero work, so the failure is almost never a code bug — it is a capacity, quota, or configuration problem such as hitting a workspace run limit, exceeding a job-queue size, or a cluster/storage access error at launch. Left unseen, they mean scheduled SLAs are silently missed while the job never even starts, and the fix (raising concurrency limits, resizing queues, correcting config) is different from and cheaper than debugging failing code. Grouping by termination_code tells an admin exactly which lever to pull.

What it does
  • Counts, per job, how many runs were killed before execution began in the last 30 days.
  • Splits each job's count by termination_code so the launch-blocking reason (quota hit, queue overflow, cluster error) is visible.
  • Keys every row to (workspace_id, job_id) so counts stay correct across a multi-workspace account.
  • Counts each run once even if it was retried or spanned multiple hourly slices — these are distinct never-started runs, not double-counted slices.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
workspace_idThe workspace the job belongs to. job_id is only unique within a workspace, so always read it together with this.
job_idThe job (workflow) whose runs never started. Unique only within its workspace.
termination_codeThe root-cause reason the run was terminated before launch (e.g. WORKSPACE_RUN_LIMIT_EXCEEDED, MAX_JOB_QUEUE_SIZE_EXCEEDED, CLUSTER_ERROR, STORAGE_ACCESS_ERROR). This is the lever to act on. May be NULL on rows before ~Aug-2024 when the column began populating.
never_started_runsCount of distinct runs of this job, with this termination code, that ended before executing (period_start_time = period_end_time). An integer run count, not DBUs or dollars.
Sample output
workspace_idjob_idtermination_codenever_started_runs
1234567890123456778899001122WORKSPACE_RUN_LIMIT_EXCEEDED14
1234567890123456445566778899MAX_JOB_QUEUE_SIZE_EXCEEDED6
9876543210987654112233445566CLUSTER_ERROR3
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.lakeflow.job_run_timelineThe run-level execution log. Each row is a run state slice; the query reads the end row (result_state populated) and flags runs where period_start_time equals period_end_time — meaning the run was terminated before it executed — carrying workspace_id, job_id, run_id, and the root-cause termination_code.
Caveats & gotchas
  • A run is flagged as never-started only when period_start_time exactly equals period_end_time; the query reads the end row (result_state IS NOT NULL) to avoid false positives from hourly slicing of long runs.
  • 30-day trailing window (date_add(current_date(), -30)); the incomplete current day is excluded (period_end_time < date_trunc('DAY', current_timestamp())).
  • termination_code did not populate before ~Aug-2024, so on short-history accounts it can be NULL — treat NULL as 'reason not recorded,' not as a distinct failure class.
  • never_started_runs is a count of runs, not DBUs and not dollars — these runs burned no compute; the cost is the missed schedule, not spend.
  • Empty result means no never-started runs (or the account runs no jobs) — that is a valid healthy outcome, not an error.
  • job_id is workspace-scoped; always group and join on (workspace_id, job_id), never job_id alone.
  • Requires Unity Catalog and SELECT granted on the system.lakeflow schema; an ungranted or non-UC account cannot read this table.
→
What you do next
Read the dominant termination_code per job: for WORKSPACE_RUN_LIMIT_EXCEEDED / MAX_JOB_QUEUE_SIZE_EXCEEDED, raise the workspace concurrency or job-queue limits (or stagger schedules); for CLUSTER_ERROR / STORAGE_ACCESS_ERROR, fix the cluster policy, instance availability, or storage credentials so the run can launch.
← PreviousJobs Missing a Timeoutlakeflow_jobs_no_timeout All Jobs & Pipelines ↑ Next →Where Job Time Actually Goeslakeflow_phase_cold_start
Overview / Jobs & Pipelines / lakeflow_phase_cold_start
10
Jobs & Pipelines lakeflow_phase_cold_start

Where Job Time Actually Goes

Per-job phase totals (setup, queue, execution, cleanup, and overall run) plus 95th-percentile setup and queue times over the trailing 30 days, so you can see how much wall-clock time is startup and wait overhead versus real work.

system.lakeflow.job_run_timeline
queries/jobs_pipelines/lakeflow_phase_cold_start.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:lakeflow_phase_cold_start */
SELECT workspace_id, job_id,
       COUNT(DISTINCT run_id)                    AS runs,
       SUM(setup_duration_seconds)               AS setup_s_total,
       SUM(queue_duration_seconds)               AS queue_s_total,
       SUM(execution_duration_seconds)           AS execution_s_total,
       SUM(cleanup_duration_seconds)             AS cleanup_s_total,
       SUM(run_duration_seconds)                 AS run_s_total,
       PERCENTILE(setup_duration_seconds, 0.95)  AS setup_s_p95,
       PERCENTILE(queue_duration_seconds, 0.95)  AS queue_s_p95,
       SUM(CASE WHEN setup_duration_seconds IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS rows_setup_null
FROM system.lakeflow.job_run_timeline
WHERE period_start_time >= date_add(current_date(), -30)
  AND period_end_time < date_trunc('DAY', current_timestamp())
  AND result_state IS NOT NULL     -- end row carries the final durations
GROUP BY workspace_id, job_id
Why it matters

Every second a job spends provisioning compute (setup) or waiting for a slot (queue) is wall-clock latency your pipelines and downstream SLAs pay for, often with little or no useful work happening. When setup and queue time dwarf actual execution time, you are looking at cold-start overhead that warm pools, pre-provisioned or serverless compute, or higher concurrency limits could reclaim. Isolating this per job tells you which workloads are latency-bound by startup rather than by their own logic, so you tune the right thing instead of over-provisioning blindly.

What it does
  • Breaks each job's run time into its phases — setup, queue, execution, cleanup, and overall run — and reports the total seconds spent in each phase across the window.
  • Adds a p95 for the setup and queue phases (only) so you see the tail — worst-case cold start and wait — not just typical behavior.
  • Counts how many runs each job had so totals can be read per-run, and reports one row per job within each workspace.
  • Exposes rows_setup_null, a count of runs missing setup timing, so a job on a short-history account degrades to "not assessed" instead of falsely reading as zero startup overhead.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
workspace_idThe workspace the job belongs to. Part of the key because job_id is unique only within a workspace.
job_idThe job (workflow) definition, scoped to its workspace. One row per job per workspace.
runsDistinct runs of this job counted in the window (end rows only). Use it as the denominator when judging whether a total is large per-run.
setup_s_totalTotal seconds spent in the setup/provisioning phase across all runs. High relative to execution signals cold-start overhead. NULL when phase durations are not yet populated on this account.
queue_s_totalTotal seconds runs spent queued waiting for capacity before starting. This column exists only on job_run_timeline. NULL when not yet populated.
execution_s_totalTotal seconds of actual execution across all runs — the real work. Compare against setup+queue to judge how much time is overhead vs work.
cleanup_s_totalTotal seconds spent in the post-run cleanup/teardown phase across all runs.
run_s_totalTotal end-to-end run seconds across all runs. The umbrella figure the phase totals decompose.
setup_s_p9595th-percentile setup seconds — the tail cold-start a run hits on a bad day, not the average. NULL when setup durations are unpopulated.
queue_s_p9595th-percentile queue seconds — the worst-case wait a run endured. A large gap vs the typical queue time points to intermittent capacity contention.
rows_setup_nullNumber of this job's runs with a NULL setup duration. If it equals runs, phase timing is simply not populated yet (early-Dec-2025 gating) — read as "not assessed," never as zero overhead.
Sample output
workspace_idjob_idrunssetup_s_totalqueue_s_totalexecution_s_totalcleanup_s_totalrun_s_totalsetup_s_p95queue_s_p95rows_setup_null
12345678901234568844221203600540042000120052200451200
12345678901234569900113090001200600060016800380900
987654321098765411223340NULLNULLNULLNULLNULLNULLNULL40
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.lakeflow.job_run_timelineSole source. Provides the run-level execution log; the query reads the per-phase durations (setup, queue, execution, cleanup, run) from the completed end row of each run (result_state IS NOT NULL) and aggregates them per (workspace_id, job_id).
Caveats & gotchas
  • Late-populated columns: all *_duration_seconds and queue_duration_seconds were not populated before early-Dec-2025. On short-history accounts they are NULL for every run, so totals and p95 come back NULL — that is "not assessed," not zero overhead.
  • Empty != zero != error: a job with rows_setup_null equal to runs has no timing data, which is different from a job that genuinely had zero setup time. Check rows_setup_null before concluding anything.
  • Seconds, not dollars: every *_s_total and *_s_p95 is in seconds of wall-clock time. This query carries no DBUs and no dollars — Databricks publishes no DBU-to-$ rate for phase durations.
  • queue_duration_seconds exists only on job_run_timeline, not on job_task_run_timeline — do not expect a task-level equivalent.
  • End-row only: metrics come from the completed row of each run (result_state IS NOT NULL), which carries the final phase durations and excludes in-progress slices.
  • Workspace-scoped keys: grouping is on (workspace_id, job_id) because job_id is unique only within a workspace; never key on job_id alone in a multi-workspace metastore.
  • Date window: fixed trailing 30 days (date_add(current_date(), -30)); the incomplete current day is excluded (period_end_time < start of today), so partial-day data never skews totals. This query is not parameterized by :period_days.
  • p95 scope: p95 is computed for the setup and queue phases only — there is no execution/cleanup/run p95 in the output.
  • Cost note: exact PERCENTILE can be expensive on very large run volumes — consider PERCENTILE_APPROX at scale.
  • Availability: Unity Catalog required, with SELECT granted on the system.lakeflow schema. Empty (but valid) result if the account runs no jobs.
→
What you do next
Rank jobs where setup_s_total plus queue_s_total is a large fraction of run_s_total (or where setup_s_p95 spikes well above the norm), and move those to warm-pool, pre-provisioned, or serverless compute — or raise concurrency limits for the queue-bound ones — to reclaim cold-start latency. If rows_setup_null equals runs, mark the job "not assessed" and revisit once phase timing populates.
← PreviousRuns Killed Before They Launchedlakeflow_never_started_runs All Jobs & Pipelines ↑ Next →Per-Pipeline Cost and Over-Refreshlakeflow_pipeline_cost
Overview / Jobs & Pipelines / lakeflow_pipeline_cost
11
Jobs & Pipelines lakeflow_pipeline_cost

Per-Pipeline Cost and Over-Refresh

One row per Lakeflow (DLT) pipeline over the trailing window, showing its net DBUs billed, its maintenance DBUs broken out separately, a list-price dollar estimate, and how many times it refreshed and for how long.

system.billing.usagesystem.billing.list_pricessystem.lakeflow.pipelinessystem.lakeflow.pipeline_update_timeline
queries/jobs_pipelines/lakeflow_pipeline_cost.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:lakeflow_pipeline_cost */
WITH pipe_dbus AS (
  -- DBUs billed to each pipeline over the window. Net across ALL record_types; DBU only.
  -- Split maintenance DBUs out via dlt_maintenance_id so they are reported separately.
  SELECT workspace_id,
         usage_metadata.dlt_pipeline_id AS pipeline_id,
         SUM(usage_quantity) AS net_pipeline_dbus,
         SUM(CASE WHEN usage_metadata.dlt_maintenance_id IS NOT NULL
                  THEN usage_quantity ELSE 0 END) AS net_maintenance_dbus
  FROM system.billing.usage
  WHERE usage_date >= dateadd(day, -:period_days, current_date())
    AND usage_date < current_date()
    AND upper(usage_unit) = 'DBU'
    AND usage_metadata.dlt_pipeline_id IS NOT NULL
  GROUP BY workspace_id, usage_metadata.dlt_pipeline_id
),
pipe_list AS (
  -- LIST-$ estimate only (pre-discount, CAST-unverified): SUM(usage_quantity * list_rate)
  -- per pipeline. Engine applies the negotiated discount downstream; never a billed dollar.
  SELECT u.workspace_id,
         u.usage_metadata.dlt_pipeline_id AS pipeline_id,
         MAX(lp.currency_code) AS currency_code,
         SUM(u.usage_quantity * lp.list_rate) AS net_list_cost
  FROM system.billing.usage u
  LEFT JOIN (
    SELECT sku_name, cloud, currency_code, usage_unit, price_start_time, price_end_time,
           CAST(pricing.effective_list.default AS DOUBLE) AS list_rate   -- <-- UNVERIFIED path
    FROM system.billing.list_prices
  ) lp
    ON u.sku_name = lp.sku_name
   AND u.cloud    = lp.cloud
   AND u.usage_end_time >= lp.price_start_time
   AND (lp.price_end_time IS NULL OR u.usage_end_time < lp.price_end_time)
  WHERE u.usage_date >= dateadd(day, -:period_days, current_date())
    AND u.usage_date < current_date()
    AND upper(u.usage_unit) = 'DBU'
    AND u.usage_metadata.dlt_pipeline_id IS NOT NULL
  GROUP BY u.workspace_id, u.usage_metadata.dlt_pipeline_id
),
pipe_meta AS (
  -- SCD2 latest row per (workspace_id, pipeline_id): names-not-ids + type.
  SELECT workspace_id, pipeline_id, name AS pipeline_name, pipeline_type
  FROM system.lakeflow.pipelines
  QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
    PARTITION BY workspace_id, pipeline_id ORDER BY change_time DESC
  ) = 1
),
pipe_updates AS (
  -- Over-refresh signal: distinct updates + total active seconds per pipeline.
  SELECT workspace_id, pipeline_id,
         COUNT(DISTINCT update_id) AS updates,
         SUM(unix_timestamp(period_end_time) - unix_timestamp(period_start_time)) AS active_seconds_total
  FROM system.lakeflow.pipeline_update_timeline
  WHERE period_start_time >= dateadd(day, -:period_days, current_date())
    AND period_end_time < date_trunc('DAY', current_timestamp())
    AND result_state IS NOT NULL   -- update end row only
  GROUP BY workspace_id, pipeline_id
)
SELECT d.workspace_id,
       d.pipeline_id,
       CASE WHEN m.pipeline_name IS NULL THEN m.pipeline_name ELSE concat(substr(m.pipeline_name, 1, 2), '****') END AS pipeline_name,
       m.pipeline_type,
       d.net_pipeline_dbus,
       d.net_maintenance_dbus,
       l.currency_code,
       l.net_list_cost,
       COALESCE(up.updates, 0)              AS updates,
       COALESCE(up.active_seconds_total, 0) AS active_seconds_total
FROM pipe_dbus d
LEFT JOIN pipe_list l
  ON d.workspace_id = l.workspace_id AND d.pipeline_id = l.pipeline_id
LEFT JOIN pipe_meta m
  ON d.workspace_id = m.workspace_id AND d.pipeline_id = m.pipeline_id
LEFT JOIN pipe_updates up
  ON d.workspace_id = up.workspace_id AND d.pipeline_id = up.pipeline_id
WHERE d.net_pipeline_dbus > 0
ORDER BY d.net_pipeline_dbus DESC
Why it matters

DLT/Lakeflow pipelines are a common source of silent spend: a pipeline set to refresh too often, or a continuous pipeline left running, can quietly dominate a workspace's bill. This query ranks pipelines by the DBUs they actually consumed and pairs that with a refresh count and active-second proxy, so an admin can see not just which pipelines cost the most but which are earning that cost through over-refreshing. Splitting maintenance DBUs into their own column means routine housekeeping (compaction, vacuum) is never mistaken for pipeline-logic cost, so tuning effort goes to the right place.

What it does
  • Reports one row per pipeline (scoped to its workspace) over the trailing :period_days window, ranked with the most DBU-hungry pipelines first.
  • Splits each pipeline's consumption into net_pipeline_dbus (total DBUs billed) and net_maintenance_dbus (the housekeeping slice), so routine maintenance is never blamed on pipeline logic.
  • Attaches a list-price dollar estimate (net_list_cost) and its currency - a pre-discount rack-rate approximation, not a billed amount.
  • Adds an over-refresh signal per pipeline: how many distinct updates ran and the total active_seconds_total they were executing, so frequent or long-running refreshers stand out.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
workspace_idThe workspace the pipeline belongs to. Pipeline ids are unique only within a workspace, so always read this together with pipeline_id.
pipeline_idThe DLT/Lakeflow pipeline UUID that the billed DBUs attribute to (from usage_metadata.dlt_pipeline_id).
pipeline_nameHuman-readable pipeline name, masked to its first two characters (e.g. 'sa****'). NULL when the pipeline has no matching latest row in the pipelines catalog - treat NULL as 'name not resolved,' not as a missing pipeline. (A missing/disabled Preview catalog errors the whole query rather than returning NULL.)
pipeline_typeThe pipeline type from the pipelines catalog. NULL when the pipeline is not matched in the (present) catalog.
net_pipeline_dbusTotal net DBUs (a compute-unit quantity, NOT dollars) billed to this pipeline over the window, summed across all record types so restatements/retractions already net out. Only pipelines with net_pipeline_dbus > 0 are returned.
net_maintenance_dbusThe subset of net_pipeline_dbus attributable to maintenance/housekeeping (dlt_maintenance_id present). Also DBUs, not dollars. It is included within net_pipeline_dbus, not additional to it.
currency_codeCurrency of the list-price estimate (e.g. USD). NULL when no list price matched the pipeline's SKUs.
net_list_costLIST-price dollar ESTIMATE = sum of usage_quantity x list_rate (pre-discount rack rate). Treat as an estimate to be multiplied by your negotiated discount, NEVER as a billed dollar amount; the price CAST path is unverified.
updatesCount of distinct pipeline updates (refreshes) that reached an end state (any result) in the window. 0 when the pipeline had no matching end rows in the timeline. A high count relative to peers is the over-refresh signal.
active_seconds_totalTotal seconds the pipeline's updates were actively running over the window (a proxy for compute time, in seconds not dollars). 0 when the pipeline had no matching timeline rows.
Sample output
pipeline_idpipeline_namenet_pipeline_dbusnet_maintenance_dbusnet_list_costupdatesactive_seconds_total
a1b2c3d4-1111-4a2b-8c3d-000000000001sa****4820.5310.02892.30144043200
b2c3d4e5-2222-4b3c-9d4e-000000000002cu****1180.095.5708.009628800
c3d4e5f6-3333-4c4d-ae5f-000000000003re****240.750.0144.45305400
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.billing.usageThe metered-usage fact table; supplies the DBUs each pipeline burned via usage_metadata.dlt_pipeline_id, with maintenance DBUs identified by dlt_maintenance_id. Filtered to DBU units only and to rows carrying a pipeline id. This is the authoritative source for the cost totals.
system.billing.list_pricesList (rack-rate) price reference joined half-open on the effective price window (usage_end_time >= price_start_time AND < price_end_time-or-NULL); supplies list_rate (pricing.effective_list.default) and currency to turn DBUs into a pre-discount dollar ESTIMATE. LEFT-joined so missing prices never drop the DBU row.
system.lakeflow.pipelinesSCD2 catalog of pipelines; supplies the human-readable pipeline_name (masked) and pipeline_type via the latest change-history row per pipeline. Public Preview (a missing/disabled table errors the query with TABLE_OR_VIEW_NOT_FOUND); when present, LEFT-joined so a pipeline absent from the catalog leaves name/type NULL without losing cost attribution.
system.lakeflow.pipeline_update_timelineExecution log of pipeline updates (refreshes); supplies the over-refresh signal - distinct update count and total active seconds per pipeline from end rows only (result_state IS NOT NULL). Public Preview (a missing/disabled table errors the query); when present, LEFT-joined so pipelines with no timeline rows degrade to zeros.
Caveats & gotchas
  • DBUs are NOT dollars: net_pipeline_dbus and net_maintenance_dbus are compute-unit quantities. Only net_list_cost is in dollars, and it is a pre-discount LIST estimate (x your negotiated discount), never a billed amount; the price CAST path (pricing.effective_list.default) is unverified.
  • net_maintenance_dbus is a subset of net_pipeline_dbus (housekeeping), not an extra charge on top of it.
  • Empty != zero != error: an account with no pipelines returns an empty-but-valid result. system.lakeflow.pipelines and pipeline_update_timeline are Public Preview - if a table is disabled/missing it raises TABLE_OR_VIEW_NOT_FOUND and the WHOLE query errors (these are hard CTE references, not gracefully skipped). When a table IS present but has no matching row for a pipeline, the LEFT JOINs degrade pipeline_name/pipeline_type to NULL and updates/active_seconds_total to 0, never dropping the DBU attribution.
  • Ids are workspace-scoped: dlt_pipeline_id is unique only within a workspace, so every join and grouping keys on (workspace_id, pipeline_id) - never pipeline_id alone - to avoid multi-workspace fan-out.
  • pipeline_name is masked to its first two characters; a NULL name means 'not resolved' (no catalog match), not a nonexistent pipeline.
  • Date window: trailing :period_days ending yesterday - billing filters usage_date < current_date() and the update timeline excludes the incomplete current day (period_end_time < date_trunc('DAY', current_timestamp())), so today is never counted.
  • usage is filtered to upper(usage_unit)='DBU' so bytes/hours/tokens never blend into the totals; usage_quantity is summed across all record_types (already net).
  • The list-price join is half-open on usage_end_time (price_end_time NULL = currently effective); a SKU with no matching price contributes DBUs but no dollars (currency_code/net_list_cost NULL).
→
What you do next
Sort by net_pipeline_dbus to find the costliest pipelines, then cross-check their updates and active_seconds_total: a high update count relative to data freshness needs signals over-refreshing - dial back the refresh schedule or switch triggered/continuous mode. If net_maintenance_dbus is a large share, investigate housekeeping settings separately from pipeline logic. Multiply net_list_cost by your negotiated discount for a rough dollar figure, and confirm the price path before quoting it.
← PreviousWhere Job Time Actually Goeslakeflow_phase_cold_start All Jobs & Pipelines ↑ Next →Pipeline Active-Time and Idle-Tail Signallakeflow_pipeline_idle_tail_duration
Overview / Jobs & Pipelines / lakeflow_pipeline_idle_tail_duration
12
Jobs & Pipelines lakeflow_pipeline_idle_tail_duration

Pipeline Active-Time and Idle-Tail Signal

Per-Lakeflow-pipeline count of updates and total active run seconds over the last 30 days, tagged with each pipeline's type and its continuous/development settings so you can spot pipelines whose clusters likely linger idle between refreshes.

system.lakeflow.pipeline_update_timelinesystem.lakeflow.pipelines
queries/jobs_pipelines/lakeflow_pipeline_idle_tail_duration.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:lakeflow_pipeline_idle_tail_duration */
-- NEEDS CONFIRMATION: settings.<key> dot-access is UNVERIFIED (struct vs map).
SELECT u.workspace_id, u.pipeline_id, p.pipeline_type, p.setting_continuous, p.setting_development,
       COUNT(DISTINCT u.update_id) AS updates,
       SUM(unix_timestamp(u.period_end_time) - unix_timestamp(u.period_start_time)) AS active_seconds_total
FROM system.lakeflow.pipeline_update_timeline u
LEFT JOIN (
  SELECT workspace_id, pipeline_id, pipeline_type,
         settings.continuous  AS setting_continuous,
         settings.development AS setting_development
  FROM system.lakeflow.pipelines
  QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY workspace_id, pipeline_id ORDER BY change_time DESC) = 1
) p
  ON u.workspace_id = p.workspace_id AND u.pipeline_id = p.pipeline_id
WHERE u.period_start_time >= date_add(current_date(), -30)
  AND u.period_end_time < date_trunc('DAY', current_timestamp())
  AND u.result_state IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY u.workspace_id, u.pipeline_id, p.pipeline_type, p.setting_continuous, p.setting_development
Why it matters

Continuous and development-mode pipelines keep compute warm and can leave clusters lingering idle long after an update finishes, and that idle tail bills as serverless/DLT DBUs with nothing to show for it. This query surfaces the pipelines doing lots of short updates (a continuous or dev pattern) versus few long ones, so you can target the ones most likely to be wasting money on idle compute. It is a screening signal, not a bill: the active-seconds here measure time spent running, and the actual idle tail must be confirmed against billing idle DBUs before you act.

What it does
  • Lists each DLT/Lakeflow pipeline (per workspace) with how many updates it ran and its total active run time over the trailing 30 days.
  • Tags every pipeline with its pipeline_type and whether it is set to continuous and/or development mode — the settings most associated with idle-lingering compute.
  • Lets you contrast pipelines that fire many short updates against those with a few long refreshes, flagging idle-tail suspects.
  • Reports only *active* run seconds — it does not itself measure idle time, so it is a starting signal you corroborate with billing idle DBUs.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
workspace_idThe workspace the pipeline belongs to; pipeline_id is unique only within a workspace, so always read it together with this.
pipeline_idThe Lakeflow/DLT pipeline identifier. Join back to billing on (workspace_id, dlt_pipeline_id) to confirm idle DBUs.
pipeline_typeThe pipeline's declared type from the deduped catalog. NULL if the pipeline had no matching (or deleted) catalog row.
setting_continuousWhether the pipeline is configured to run continuously (settings.continuous). Continuous pipelines are prime idle-tail suspects. UNVERIFIED struct access — may be NULL/error on accounts where settings is a MAP.
setting_developmentWhether the pipeline runs in development mode (settings.development), where clusters are deliberately kept warm between updates. Same unverified-access caveat.
updatesDistinct pipeline updates (refreshes) completed in the window. Many small updates hints at a continuous/frequent-refresh pattern.
active_seconds_totalSum of each update's active window (period_end_time minus period_start_time) in seconds. This is run time, not idle time, and not dollars.
Sample output
workspace_idpipeline_idpipeline_typesetting_continuoussetting_developmentupdatesactive_seconds_total
1234567890123456a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890MANAGEDtruefalse412148320
1234567890123456b2c3d4e5-f6a7-8901-bcde-f12345678901MANAGEDfalsetrue932040
9876543210987654c3d4e5f6-a7b8-9012-cdef-123456789012MANAGEDfalsefalse2861200
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.lakeflow.pipeline_update_timelineExecution log of pipeline updates (refreshes); the driver table. Supplies workspace_id, pipeline_id, update_id, the period_start_time/period_end_time active-window bounds summed into active seconds, and result_state (end-row filter). Hourly-sliced, Public Preview.
system.lakeflow.pipelinesSCD2 catalog of Lakeflow Declarative (DLT) pipelines; deduped to the latest version per pipeline (ROW_NUMBER over change_time DESC) to attach pipeline_type and the settings.continuous / settings.development flags. Public Preview; settings dot-access is unverified (struct vs map).
Caveats & gotchas
  • Public Preview tables: system.lakeflow.pipelines and pipeline_update_timeline may be disabled or empty; a missing table returns TABLE_OR_VIEW_NOT_FOUND — skip the query, do not treat as an error.
  • Empty != zero != error: an account running no pipelines returns a valid empty result; do not read that as a finding.
  • UNVERIFIED struct access: setting_continuous / setting_development assume settings is a STRUCT (dot-access). On accounts where settings is a MAP this needs settings['key'] and may error or come back NULL — confirm per workspace.
  • active_seconds_total is seconds of ACTIVE run time, not idle time and not dollars — it does not itself prove an idle tail.
  • No idle-tail column exists: the actual post-run idle DBU must be corroborated separately in system.billing.usage joined on usage_metadata.dlt_pipeline_id (DBUs are not dollars). The plan's pipelines.clusterShutdown.delay is not in the documented schema and is deliberately not emitted.
  • Workspace-scoped keys: pipeline_id is unique only within a workspace; the catalog join keys on (workspace_id, pipeline_id).
  • SCD2 dedupe: the pipelines catalog is change-history; only the latest row per pipeline (ROW_NUMBER over change_time DESC) is used, then LEFT JOINed so pipeline_type/settings can be NULL if no current row matches.
  • End-row filtering: only rows with result_state IS NOT NULL are summed, to avoid double-counting hourly-sliced updates.
  • Date window: fixed trailing 30 days (date_add(current_date(), -30)); excludes today (period_end_time < date_trunc('DAY', current_timestamp())) so partial-day data never skews totals.
→
What you do next
Rank pipelines with high update counts and continuous or development mode set to true, then confirm the suspected idle tail by joining system.billing.usage on (workspace_id, usage_metadata.dlt_pipeline_id); for confirmed offenders, switch dev pipelines out of development mode, tune cluster auto-shutdown, or move frequent continuous refreshes to triggered updates.
← PreviousPer-Pipeline Cost and Over-Refreshlakeflow_pipeline_cost All Jobs & Pipelines ↑ Next →Pipeline Refresh Failures And Retrieslakeflow_pipeline_update_failures_retries
Overview / Jobs & Pipelines / lakeflow_pipeline_update_failures_retries
13
Jobs & Pipelines lakeflow_pipeline_update_failures_retries

Pipeline Refresh Failures And Retries

A 30-day breakdown of Lakeflow/DLT pipeline update outcomes, counting how many refreshes ran, how many failed, and how many were fired by retry-on-failure, sliced per workspace, pipeline, update type, trigger, and result state.

system.lakeflow.pipeline_update_timeline
queries/jobs_pipelines/lakeflow_pipeline_update_failures_retries.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:lakeflow_pipeline_update_failures_retries */
WITH end_rows AS (
  SELECT workspace_id, pipeline_id, update_id, request_id, update_type,
         trigger_type, result_state, period_start_time, period_end_time
  FROM system.lakeflow.pipeline_update_timeline
  WHERE period_start_time >= date_add(current_date(), -30)
    AND period_end_time < date_trunc('DAY', current_timestamp())
    AND result_state IS NOT NULL          -- end row only for updates >1h
)
SELECT e.workspace_id, e.pipeline_id, e.update_type, e.trigger_type, e.result_state,
       COUNT(DISTINCT e.update_id) AS updates,
       SUM(CASE WHEN e.result_state = 'FAILED'             THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS failed_update_rows,
       SUM(CASE WHEN e.trigger_type = 'RETRY_ON_FAILURE'   THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS retry_triggered_rows
FROM end_rows e
GROUP BY e.workspace_id, e.pipeline_id, e.update_type, e.trigger_type, e.result_state
Why it matters

Pipeline refreshes that fail or only succeed after retries are the silent tax on a data platform: every failed FULL_REFRESH re-processes data and burns pipeline compute, and a high retry-on-failure count signals a flapping pipeline that is unstable even when it eventually turns green. This view tells an admin which pipelines and which update types (full refresh vs incremental refresh vs validate) are eating reliability budget, so remediation is aimed at the worst offenders rather than the whole fleet. Because these DBUs and re-runs are real spend, cutting failure and retry rates translates directly into lower pipeline cost and fewer late data deliveries.

What it does
  • Counts pipeline refresh outcomes over the last 30 complete days, broken out per workspace, pipeline, update type, trigger, and how the refresh ended.
  • Separates each pipeline's activity by update_type (FULL_REFRESH / REFRESH / VALIDATE) and by result (COMPLETED / FAILED / CANCELED).
  • For each slice, reports how many distinct updates ran, how many were failures, and how many were fired by retry-on-failure — the flapping signal.
  • Only finished updates are counted (the end row of each refresh); still-running updates and the partial current day are excluded.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
workspace_idThe workspace the pipeline lives in. Always read together with pipeline_id, since pipeline ids are unique only within a workspace.
pipeline_idThe Lakeflow/DLT pipeline identifier (a UUID). One pipeline can appear on several rows, one per update-type/trigger/result combination.
update_typeThe kind of refresh: FULL_REFRESH (reprocess everything), REFRESH (incremental), or VALIDATE (dry-run/validation). Full refreshes are the most expensive to fail.
trigger_typeWhat launched the update. RETRY_ON_FAILURE means this update was an automatic retry of a prior failed one — the key instability signal.
result_stateHow the update ended: COMPLETED, FAILED, or CANCELED (note the single L). CANCELED is a user/system cancel, not a failure.
updatesNumber of distinct pipeline updates in this bucket (COUNT DISTINCT update_id). This is the headline volume for the combination.
failed_update_rowsCount of end rows in this bucket whose result was FAILED. Because rows are grouped by result_state, it is non-zero only on rows where result_state = FAILED, so it quantifies the failure count for that line.
retry_triggered_rowsCount of end rows in this bucket triggered by RETRY_ON_FAILURE. Because rows are grouped by trigger_type, it is non-zero only on rows whose trigger_type is RETRY_ON_FAILURE; a high value on a pipeline flags repeated retrying.
Sample output
workspace_idpipeline_idupdate_typetrigger_typeresult_stateupdatesfailed_update_rowsretry_triggered_rows
1234567890123456a1b2c3d4-5e6f-7a8b-9c0d-1e2f3a4b5c6dREFRESHSCHEDULEFAILED12120
1234567890123456a1b2c3d4-5e6f-7a8b-9c0d-1e2f3a4b5c6dFULL_REFRESHRETRY_ON_FAILURECOMPLETED505
9876543210987654f0e9d8c7-b6a5-4321-9f8e-7d6c5b4a3210REFRESHMANUALCOMPLETED4800
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.lakeflow.pipeline_update_timelineThe only table the query reads. It is the execution log of Lakeflow/DLT pipeline updates (refreshes), hourly-sliced for long updates. The query keeps only the final end row of each update (where result_state is populated) over the last 30 complete days, then tallies update counts, failures, and retry-triggered updates per pipeline and outcome.
Caveats & gotchas
  • Public Preview source: system.lakeflow.pipeline_update_timeline may be disabled or empty on the metastore, and a missing table returns TABLE_OR_VIEW_NOT_FOUND. Treat absence/empty as 'not assessed', not as zero failures and not as an error.
  • Empty != zero != error: an account that runs no pipelines returns a valid empty result; do not read that as a clean bill of health.
  • End-row-only: only finished updates are counted (result_state IS NOT NULL). Updates still in flight when the query runs are invisible until they complete.
  • result_state values are COMPLETED / FAILED / CANCELED — note CANCELED has a single L, and CANCELED is not a failure.
  • trigger_type = 'RETRY_ON_FAILURE' is the confirmed retry signal; request_id (read by the query but not surfaced in the output) is what groups a retried/restarted update family in the raw table.
  • Ids are workspace-scoped: pipeline_id is unique only within a workspace, so always key on (workspace_id, pipeline_id) — never pipeline_id alone.
  • Date window is a fixed trailing 30 days (period_start_time >= date_add(current_date(), -30)) and the incomplete current day is excluded (period_end_time < date_trunc('DAY', current_timestamp())); it is not parameterized by :period_days.
  • No dollars or DBUs here — this is a count-only reliability view; pipeline spend comes from a separate pipeline cost query.
  • updates is a COUNT(DISTINCT update_id) while failed_update_rows / retry_triggered_rows are end-row sums; since each update normally has a single end row these usually match, but on any multi-end-row update the row sums can slightly exceed the distinct-update count.
→
What you do next
Sort by failed_update_rows and retry_triggered_rows to find the pipelines with the most failing or retry-driven refreshes, then investigate those pipelines' logic and dependencies — prioritizing FULL_REFRESH failures, which reprocess the most data — to cut the wasted re-runs before they show up on the pipeline cost report.
← PreviousPipeline Active-Time and Idle-Tail Signallakeflow_pipeline_idle_tail_duration All Jobs & Pipelines ↑ Next →DLT Pipeline Fleet Inventorylakeflow_pipelines_inventory_tier
Overview / Jobs & Pipelines / lakeflow_pipelines_inventory_tier
14
Jobs & Pipelines lakeflow_pipelines_inventory_tier

DLT Pipeline Fleet Inventory

A per-workspace census of your live Lakeflow Declarative (DLT) pipelines, counting how many run in each combination of pipeline type, serverless, development-mode, continuous, and product edition.

system.lakeflow.pipelines
queries/jobs_pipelines/lakeflow_pipelines_inventory_tier.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:lakeflow_pipelines_inventory_tier */
-- NEEDS CONFIRMATION: settings.<key> dot-access vs settings['<key>'] map-access is UNVERIFIED.
WITH latest_pipelines AS (
  SELECT workspace_id, pipeline_id, pipeline_type, name, created_by, run_as, settings, delete_time,
         settings.serverless  AS setting_serverless,
         settings.development AS setting_development,
         settings.continuous  AS setting_continuous,
         settings.photon      AS setting_photon,
         settings.edition     AS setting_edition,
         settings.channel     AS setting_channel
  FROM system.lakeflow.pipelines
  QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY workspace_id, pipeline_id ORDER BY change_time DESC) = 1
)
SELECT workspace_id, pipeline_type, setting_serverless, setting_development,
       setting_continuous, setting_edition,
       COUNT(*) AS pipelines
FROM latest_pipelines
WHERE delete_time IS NULL
GROUP BY workspace_id, pipeline_type, setting_serverless, setting_development, setting_continuous, setting_edition
Why it matters

Configuration drift in a DLT fleet is where quiet money leaks and reliability gaps hide: a pipeline left in development mode won't retry or run on production-grade compute, a continuous pipeline keeps compute always-on around the clock, and paying for the ADVANCED edition on a pipeline that only needs CORE features is pure overspend. This is the one-screen fleet map that tells an admin how many pipelines sit in each posture and in which workspace, so misconfigurations and edition-tier overspend get caught before they compound. It reads current state only, so it is safe to run anytime as a governance baseline.

What it does
  • Takes a current-state census of every live DLT pipeline — latest configuration only, with deleted pipelines removed — so the counts reflect the fleet as it stands today.
  • Buckets pipelines by posture: for each workspace it groups on pipeline type, whether it runs serverless, whether it is in development mode, whether it is continuous (always-on) vs triggered, and its product edition (CORE / PRO / ADVANCED).
  • Returns a count of pipelines in every distinct combination, so you can see at a glance how many pipelines are serverless, continuous, or still in dev mode.
  • Keeps every workspace separate, giving a per-workspace fleet breakdown rather than one blended account total.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
workspace_idThe workspace the pipelines live in. Counts are scoped here because pipeline_id is unique only within a workspace; compare postures across workspaces, never merge them.
pipeline_typeThe kind of Lakeflow/DLT pipeline (e.g. the pipeline product/workload type as recorded in the catalog). One dimension of the posture breakdown.
setting_serverlessWhether the pipeline runs on serverless compute (true) vs classic/customer-managed compute (false). NULL means the setting was not present on the row. Access path unverified — see caveats.
setting_developmentWhether the pipeline is in development mode (true) vs production mode (false). Dev-mode pipelines behave differently on retries and cluster reuse; production pipelines left as true are a common misconfiguration.
setting_continuousWhether the pipeline runs continuously / always-on (true) vs triggered on demand (false). Continuous pipelines hold compute open and are the ones to scrutinize for idle cost.
setting_editionThe pipeline PRODUCT edition — CORE, PRO, or ADVANCED — which gates feature availability. This is NOT the billing DLT tier (that lives in billing). Use it to spot pipelines paying for a higher edition than they use.
pipelinesInteger count of live pipelines matching this exact combination of workspace / type / serverless / development / continuous / edition. Sum across rows within a workspace to get its total live pipeline count.
Sample output
workspace_idpipeline_typesetting_serverlesssetting_developmentsetting_continuoussetting_editionpipelines
1234567890123456WORKSPACEtruefalsefalseADVANCED14
1234567890123456WORKSPACEfalsetruetrueCORE3
9876543210987654WORKSPACEtruefalsetruePRO6
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.lakeflow.pipelinesThe sole source — the SCD2 change-history catalog of Lakeflow Declarative (DLT) pipelines. The query takes the latest version of each pipeline, drops deleted ones, reads the nested settings (serverless / development / continuous / edition), and counts pipelines per configuration. Public Preview: may be empty or disabled.
Caveats & gotchas
  • Public Preview: system.lakeflow.pipelines may be disabled or empty on the metastore — a missing table returns TABLE_OR_VIEW_NOT_FOUND and the query should be skipped, not read as an error. Unity Catalog and SELECT on system.lakeflow are required.
  • Unverified struct access: settings.<key> dot-access assumes settings is a STRUCT. On some accounts settings is a MAP requiring settings['serverless'] etc.; the key names are confirmed but the access path is not — confirm per workspace before trusting output.
  • setting_edition is the pipeline PRODUCT edition (CORE / PRO / ADVANCED), not the billing DLT tier, which surfaces separately in billing (product_features.dlt_tier). Do not conflate them.
  • Inventory only: no DBUs, no dollars, no durations. This counts pipeline definitions and their configuration; it says nothing about what any pipeline costs or how long it runs.
  • SCD2 dedupe is applied and deleted pipelines (delete_time not NULL) are excluded, so counts reflect the current live fleet — not historical versions.
  • Ids are workspace-scoped: pipeline_id is unique only within a workspace, so all grouping keys on (workspace_id, ...). An empty result means no live pipelines — not the same as an error or a disabled preview.
  • Point-in-time snapshot, not a date window: there is no trailing :period_days lookback; it reflects current configuration at query time.
→
What you do next
Use the breakdown to right-size your DLT fleet: confirm each cluster of pipelines matches intended posture — flag any production pipelines still stuck in `setting_development=true`, review `setting_continuous=true` pipelines for always-on cost, and reconcile ADVANCED/PRO editions against what each pipeline actually needs. Before trusting the numbers, run the query once and confirm dot-access on `settings` didn't error; if it did, switch to `settings['<key>']` map syntax.
← PreviousPipeline Refresh Failures And Retrieslakeflow_pipeline_update_failures_retries All Jobs & Pipelines ↑ Next →Flapping Jobs That Retrylakeflow_retries_repairs
Overview / Jobs & Pipelines / lakeflow_retries_repairs
15
Jobs & Pipelines lakeflow_retries_repairs

Flapping Jobs That Retry

Per-job retry tallies over the trailing 30 days — how many runs each job had, how many extra attempts those runs racked up, and how many runs needed at least one retry.

system.lakeflow.job_run_timeline
queries/jobs_pipelines/lakeflow_retries_repairs.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:lakeflow_retries_repairs */
WITH end_rows AS (
  SELECT workspace_id, job_id, run_id
  FROM system.lakeflow.job_run_timeline
  WHERE period_start_time >= date_add(current_date(), -30)
    AND period_end_time < date_trunc('DAY', current_timestamp())
    AND result_state IS NOT NULL          -- one non-NULL result_state row per attempt
),
per_run AS (
  SELECT workspace_id, job_id, run_id, COUNT(*) AS attempt_rows
  FROM end_rows GROUP BY workspace_id, job_id, run_id
)
SELECT workspace_id, job_id,
       COUNT(*)             AS distinct_runs,
       SUM(attempt_rows)    AS total_attempt_rows,
       SUM(attempt_rows - 1) AS total_retries,
       SUM(CASE WHEN attempt_rows > 1 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS runs_with_retry
FROM per_run
GROUP BY workspace_id, job_id
Why it matters

A job that only ever succeeds after two or three automatic retries looks green on the dashboard but is quietly unstable — and every failed attempt still spins up compute and burns DBUs before the next try. This query surfaces the jobs doing the most re-running so you can distinguish genuinely healthy jobs from flappy ones that are masking a real reliability problem and wasting money on repeated startups. Left unaddressed, retry-heavy jobs inflate spend and erode trust in your scheduled workloads.

What it does
  • Lists every job that ran in the last 30 days, one line per (workspace_id, job_id), so retry behavior is attributed to the exact job in the exact workspace.
  • Shows how many distinct runs each job had versus how many total attempts those runs consumed — the gap between the two is retries.
  • Reports total_retries (the extra attempts beyond the first for every run combined) and runs_with_retry (how many individual runs needed at least one retry).
  • Counts a retry as any additional completed attempt reusing the same run_id, so a job that always succeeds first try shows zero across the retry columns.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
workspace_idThe workspace the job belongs to. Always read together with `job_id` — job ids are unique only within a workspace.
job_idThe job (workflow) definition id, scoped to its workspace. This is the grain of one output row.
distinct_runsNumber of separate runs of this job that completed in the window (each `run_id` counted once, regardless of how many retries it contained).
total_attempt_rowsTotal completed attempts across all those runs, including retries. Equals `distinct_runs` plus `total_retries`.
total_retriesExtra attempts beyond the first for every run summed together (`total_attempt_rows` minus `distinct_runs`). Zero means no run of this job ever retried.
runs_with_retryHow many individual runs needed at least one retry. Compare against `distinct_runs`: a high ratio means retrying is the norm, not the exception, for this job.
Sample output
workspace_idjob_iddistinct_runstotal_attempt_rowstotal_retriesruns_with_retry
14709876543210988842130976543215063139
147098765432109877100284519002212012000
20391184726509139055441209833113041118
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.lakeflow.job_run_timelineThe only source. Provides one row per run state-slice; the query keeps just the end rows (`result_state IS NOT NULL`) so each real attempt is counted once, then measures how many attempts share the same `run_id` to infer retries. Keyed on `(workspace_id, job_id, run_id)`.
Caveats & gotchas
  • Retries are inferred, not labeled: total_retries = (end rows per run_id) - 1, per the documented visibility caveat. This assumes each extra end row for a run_id is a genuine retry attempt.
  • End-row filtering is mandatory and already applied: only `result_state IS NOT NULL` rows are counted. Without it, a single long run's hourly slices would be miscounted as retries.
  • Ids are workspace-scoped: every grouping and comparison keys on `(workspace_id, job_id)`, never `job_id` alone, to avoid cross-workspace fan-out.
  • Fixed 30-day window: `period_start_time >= current_date()-30` and the incomplete current day is excluded (`period_end_time < date_trunc('DAY', current_timestamp())`), so partial-day data never skews counts. Not parameterized by `:period_days`.
  • Empty is not zero and not an error: an account that runs no jobs returns an empty (but valid) result set.
  • GA table, but requires Unity Catalog and `SELECT` granted on `system.lakeflow`. Counts here are pure run tallies — no DBUs or dollars are produced by this query.
→
What you do next
Sort by `total_retries` (or the `runs_with_retry` / `distinct_runs` ratio) to find the flappiest jobs, then investigate their termination codes and root causes — fix the underlying instability rather than relying on retry-on-failure to paper over it, and reclaim the compute wasted on repeated attempts.
← PreviousDLT Pipeline Fleet Inventorylakeflow_pipelines_inventory_tier All Jobs & Pipelines ↑ Next →Hunting Stale Zombie Jobslakeflow_stale_zombie_jobs
Overview / Jobs & Pipelines / lakeflow_stale_zombie_jobs
16
Jobs & Pipelines lakeflow_stale_zombie_jobs

Hunting Stale Zombie Jobs

Every active (non-deleted) job definition with the timestamp its last run was last seen running and a flag marking those that haven't run in over 30 days (or ever).

system.lakeflow.jobssystem.lakeflow.job_run_timeline
queries/jobs_pipelines/lakeflow_stale_zombie_jobs.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:lakeflow_stale_zombie_jobs */
WITH latest_jobs AS (
  SELECT workspace_id, job_id, name, run_as, creator_id, delete_time
  FROM system.lakeflow.jobs
  QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY workspace_id, job_id ORDER BY change_time DESC) = 1
),
last_run AS (
  SELECT workspace_id, job_id, MAX(period_start_time) AS last_run_start
  FROM system.lakeflow.job_run_timeline
  GROUP BY workspace_id, job_id
)
SELECT j.workspace_id, j.job_id,
       CASE WHEN j.name IS NULL THEN j.name ELSE concat(substr(j.name, 1, 2), '****') END AS name,
       r.last_run_start,
       CASE WHEN r.last_run_start IS NULL
              OR r.last_run_start < date_add(current_date(), -30)
            THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS is_stale_30d
FROM latest_jobs j
LEFT JOIN last_run r ON j.workspace_id = r.workspace_id AND j.job_id = r.job_id
WHERE j.delete_time IS NULL
Why it matters

Zombie job definitions that haven't executed in a month (or have never run at all) clutter the workspace, inflate job inventories, and are often stale scaffolding, abandoned experiments, or handed-off work no one owns anymore. Each is an audit and cleanup candidate: dead definitions can hide orphaned schedules, obsolete credentials, or forgotten service-principal run-as identities. Pruning them shrinks the governance surface and makes the remaining active fleet easier to reason about.

What it does
  • Lists every active job (deleted jobs are excluded) across all workspaces, one row per job.
  • Attaches each job's most recent run activity timestamp — or leaves it blank if the job has never run.
  • Flags each job as stale when its last run was more than 30 days ago or it never ran, via is_stale_30d.
  • Masks job names to their first two characters so the inventory can be shared without leaking full names.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
workspace_idThe workspace the job lives in. Job ids are unique only within a workspace, so always read this together with `job_id`.
job_idThe job's identifier within its workspace. Pair with `workspace_id` to look the job up in the Jobs UI.
nameThe job name masked to its first two characters plus '****' (e.g. 'ni****'). NULL if the job had no name; not a violation.
last_run_startThe most recent run-slice start timestamp for this job (its last-seen-running signal). NULL means the job has never appeared in the run timeline — it has never run (or ran before the log's history).
is_stale_30d1 = stale: the last run started more than 30 days ago, or the job has never run (NULL last_run_start). 0 = ran within the last 30 days. Treat 1 as a cleanup/audit candidate, not an automatic delete.
Sample output
workspace_idjob_idnamelast_run_startis_stale_30d
1234567890123456884213ni****2026-06-30 02:15:000
1234567890123456771004le****2026-01-11 04:00:001
9876543210987654310592da****NULL (never run)1
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.lakeflow.jobsThe catalog of job (workflow) definitions. This SCD2 change-history table is deduped to the latest version per (workspace_id, job_id) to get its current name and deletion status; only jobs with no delete_time (still active) are kept.
system.lakeflow.job_run_timelineThe run execution log. Supplies the most recent run activity timestamp per job (the 'last seen running' signal, via MAX of the run-slice start time). Jobs absent from this log have never run and surface with a NULL last-run date.
Caveats & gotchas
  • `is_stale_30d = 1` includes both 'ran but not in 30+ days' and 'never ran' (NULL `last_run_start`) — inspect the date before acting; treat as an audit candidate, not an auto-delete.
  • A NULL `last_run_start` means the job is absent from the run timeline; on short-history accounts that can mean 'ran before the log began', not truly never run — empty != zero != error.
  • `last_run_start` is MAX(`period_start_time`) from the run timeline, i.e. the start of the most recent run state-slice — a 'last seen running' proxy, not strictly the run's kickoff time.
  • `jobs` is SCD2 change-history: only the latest version per `(workspace_id, job_id)` (by `change_time`) is used, and user-deleted jobs (non-NULL `delete_time`) are excluded — so this counts active definitions only.
  • Ids are workspace-scoped: `job_id` is unique only within a workspace, so always key on `(workspace_id, job_id)` — never `job_id` alone.
  • Job names are masked to their first two characters (`substr(name,1,2)`); NULL names pass through as NULL and are not findings.
  • The 30-day window is a fixed last-seen lookback (`date_add(current_date(), -30)`), not a usage sum — it deliberately does NOT drop the incomplete current day.
  • Requires Unity Catalog and `SELECT` on the `system` schema; both tables are GA. An account with no job definitions returns a valid but empty result; jobs that exist but never ran still return rows (all flagged stale).
  • No dollars or DBUs here — this is a metadata/last-seen audit, not a cost query.
→
What you do next
Review jobs flagged `is_stale_30d = 1`, confirm each is truly abandoned (check the `last_run_start` date and owner), then pause, archive, or delete the dead definitions to clean up the workspace and close ownership gaps.
← PreviousFlapping Jobs That Retrylakeflow_retries_repairs All Jobs & Pipelines ↑ Next →Green Runs Hiding Failed Taskslakeflow_succeeded_with_failed_tasks
Overview / Jobs & Pipelines / lakeflow_succeeded_with_failed_tasks
17
Jobs & Pipelines lakeflow_succeeded_with_failed_tasks

Green Runs Hiding Failed Tasks

Per job, how many runs reported overall SUCCEEDED yet actually contained at least one task that FAILED, ERROR'd, or TIMED_OUT over the trailing 30 days.

system.lakeflow.job_run_timelinesystem.lakeflow.job_task_run_timeline
queries/jobs_pipelines/lakeflow_succeeded_with_failed_tasks.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:lakeflow_succeeded_with_failed_tasks */
WITH job_end AS (
  SELECT workspace_id, job_id, run_id AS job_run_id, result_state AS job_result_state
  FROM system.lakeflow.job_run_timeline
  WHERE period_start_time >= date_add(current_date(), -30)
    AND period_end_time < date_trunc('DAY', current_timestamp())
    AND result_state IS NOT NULL
),
task_end AS (
  SELECT workspace_id, job_id, job_run_id, task_key, result_state AS task_result_state
  FROM system.lakeflow.job_task_run_timeline
  WHERE period_start_time >= date_add(current_date(), -30)
    AND result_state IS NOT NULL
)
SELECT j.workspace_id, j.job_id,
       COUNT(DISTINCT j.job_run_id) AS succeeded_runs,
       COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN t.task_result_state IN ('FAILED','ERROR','TIMED_OUT')
                           THEN j.job_run_id END) AS succeeded_runs_with_failed_task
FROM job_end j
LEFT JOIN task_end t
  ON j.workspace_id = t.workspace_id AND j.job_id = t.job_id AND j.job_run_id = t.job_run_id
WHERE j.job_result_state = 'SUCCEEDED'
GROUP BY j.workspace_id, j.job_id
Why it matters

A run that reports SUCCEEDED at the job level but contains a task that FAILED, ERROR'd, or TIMED_OUT is a silent partial failure: dashboards look green while data was actually dropped, a load was skipped, or a downstream table went stale. These are the incidents that surface days later as missing rows or bad numbers, because nobody was paged. For an admin, a non-zero `succeeded_runs_with_failed_task` count points directly at jobs whose failure-propagation or task-dependency wiring is misconfigured and needs tightening before the next silent gap ships bad data downstream.

What it does
  • Lists each job (by workspace_id + job_id) alongside a count of its runs that finished with an overall status of SUCCEEDED in the last 30 days.
  • For those same green runs, counts how many actually contained at least one task that FAILED, ERROR'd, or TIMED_OUT — the silent partial failures.
  • Surfaces 'green' jobs that quietly dropped work: a run reported success at the job level while a task inside it failed.
  • Treats SKIPPED, CANCELLED, and BLOCKED tasks as not failures, so only genuinely failed/errored/timed-out tasks inflate the flag.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
workspace_idThe workspace the job belongs to. Job ids repeat across workspaces, so always read it together with `job_id`.
job_idThe job (workflow) identifier, unique only within its `workspace_id`. Look this up in `system.lakeflow.jobs` to get the human name and owner.
succeeded_runsHow many distinct runs of this job finished with an overall result of SUCCEEDED in the trailing 30 days. Your baseline of 'green' runs.
succeeded_runs_with_failed_taskThe subset of those green runs that nonetheless contained at least one task reporting FAILED, ERROR, or TIMED_OUT. Any value above 0 is a silent partial failure to investigate; if it equals `succeeded_runs`, every green run was hiding a failed task.
Sample output
workspace_idjob_idsucceeded_runssucceeded_runs_with_failed_task
12345678901234568844221133552120
12345678901234567755339911228814
98765432109876546010203040503030
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.lakeflow.job_run_timelineProvides the run-level outcome: the end row of each run (where `result_state IS NOT NULL`) is filtered to `result_state = 'SUCCEEDED'` to establish the set of runs that reported overall success, keyed by `(workspace_id, job_id, run_id)`.
system.lakeflow.job_task_run_timelineProvides the per-task outcomes within each run; its end rows (`result_state IS NOT NULL`) are matched back to the succeeded runs to detect any task that reported FAILED, ERROR, or TIMED_OUT. Joined via `job_run_id = job_run_timeline.run_id` plus `workspace_id` and `job_id`.
Caveats & gotchas
  • 30-day window: counts only runs whose `period_start_time` is within the last 30 days and whose `period_end_time` is before today (`date_trunc('DAY', current_timestamp())`), so the incomplete current day is excluded.
  • End-row only: both timelines slice long runs into hourly rows and only the final row carries `result_state`; the query filters `result_state IS NOT NULL` on both sides. Do not remove that filter or long runs double-count.
  • Workspace-scoped ids: `job_id` and `job_run_id` are unique only within a workspace; the join keys on `(workspace_id, job_id, job_run_id)` and never on the id alone, to avoid cross-workspace fan-out.
  • The mandatory documented join key is `job_task_run_timeline.job_run_id = job_run_timeline.run_id` (plus `workspace_id`); joining on any other key mismatches tasks to runs.
  • Failure set is fixed: a task counts as failed only for `result_state IN ('FAILED','ERROR','TIMED_OUT')`. `SKIPPED`, `CANCELLED`, and `BLOCKED` are deliberately not counted.
  • Empty is not zero and not an error: an account that runs no jobs returns an empty (but valid) result; both tables are GA but require Unity Catalog and `SELECT` on the `system.lakeflow` schema.
  • No dollars or DBUs here: this is a pure reliability count. It carries no cost columns and says nothing about compute spent on the dropped work.
→
What you do next
For each job where `succeeded_runs_with_failed_task` is non-zero, open its recent SUCCEEDED runs, identify which task keys reported FAILED/ERROR/TIMED_OUT, and fix the job's failure-propagation settings (e.g. mark the task's downstream dependency as required / disable overly permissive "continue on failure") so a dropped task no longer lets the whole run report green.
← PreviousHunting Stale Zombie Jobslakeflow_stale_zombie_jobs All Jobs & Pipelines ↑ Next →Tasks Running Near Timeoutlakeflow_tasks_near_timeout
Overview / Jobs & Pipelines / lakeflow_tasks_near_timeout
18
Jobs & Pipelines lakeflow_tasks_near_timeout

Tasks Running Near Timeout

Per-task run statistics over the lookback window flagging task runs that reached at least 80% of (or exceeded) their configured timeout, so you can tune the bound or fix the slowdown before it trips.

system.lakeflow.job_task_run_timelinesystem.lakeflow.job_tasks
queries/jobs_pipelines/lakeflow_tasks_near_timeout.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:lakeflow_tasks_near_timeout */
WITH latest_tasks AS (
  SELECT workspace_id, job_id, task_key, timeout_seconds, delete_time
  FROM system.lakeflow.job_tasks
  QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
    PARTITION BY workspace_id, job_id, task_key ORDER BY change_time DESC
  ) = 1
),
task_end AS (
  SELECT workspace_id, job_id, task_key, run_id, execution_duration_seconds
  FROM system.lakeflow.job_task_run_timeline
  WHERE period_start_time >= dateadd(day, -:period_days, current_date())
    AND period_end_time < date_trunc('DAY', current_timestamp())   -- drop incomplete current day
    AND result_state IS NOT NULL                                   -- task end row only
)
SELECT t.workspace_id, t.job_id, t.task_key,
       lt.timeout_seconds,
       COUNT(DISTINCT t.run_id)                                          AS task_runs,
       MAX(t.execution_duration_seconds)                                 AS max_exec_s,
       percentile_approx(t.execution_duration_seconds, 0.95)            AS exec_s_p95,
       -- runs whose execution reached >= 80% of a CONFIGURED (non-null, >0) timeout
       SUM(CASE WHEN lt.timeout_seconds IS NOT NULL AND lt.timeout_seconds > 0
                 AND t.execution_duration_seconds >= 0.8 * lt.timeout_seconds
                THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)                                       AS runs_near_timeout,
       -- runs that ran AT/PAST the configured timeout (should have been killed)
       SUM(CASE WHEN lt.timeout_seconds IS NOT NULL AND lt.timeout_seconds > 0
                 AND t.execution_duration_seconds >= lt.timeout_seconds
                THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)                                       AS runs_over_timeout,
       -- degradation buckets: a NULL/zero timeout is "no bound configured" (not a finding);
       -- a NULL execution duration is "column not yet populated" (not assessed).
       SUM(CASE WHEN lt.timeout_seconds IS NULL OR lt.timeout_seconds = 0
                THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)                                       AS runs_no_task_timeout,
       SUM(CASE WHEN t.execution_duration_seconds IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS runs_exec_null
FROM task_end t
LEFT JOIN latest_tasks lt
  ON  t.workspace_id = lt.workspace_id
  AND t.job_id       = lt.job_id
  AND t.task_key     = lt.task_key
GROUP BY t.workspace_id, t.job_id, t.task_key, lt.timeout_seconds
Why it matters

A task that runs at 80-100% of its timeout is one slow input or one bad node away from being force-killed, which turns a green pipeline into a failed run and wastes the compute already spent. Catching these early lets you either raise a too-tight bound or fix the underlying slowdown before it causes a production outage and a costly re-run. The `runs_over_timeout` counter is more alarming: it flags tasks that reached or passed their configured limit yet were not killed, which points to a timeout that is not actually being enforced. Because the assessment depends on two late-populated columns, the query also tells you honestly how much of the fleet it could not evaluate, so you never mistake missing data for a clean bill of health.

What it does
  • Reports one row per task (workspace_id, job_id, task_key) with how many runs it had and how long they took — the worst run (max_exec_s) and the p95 (exec_s_p95) in seconds.
  • Counts how many of those runs crept to 80% or more of the task's configured timeout (runs_near_timeout) and how many actually hit or blew past it without being killed (runs_over_timeout).
  • Separates out the runs it could not judge: those on a task with no timeout configured (runs_no_task_timeout) and those where the duration column is not yet populated (runs_exec_null) — so missing data reads as *not assessed*, never as a pass.
  • Durations only — this view carries no dollars or DBUs; a slow task is a reliability signal, not a billing line.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
workspace_idThe workspace the task ran in. Part of the identity — job_id and task_key are unique only within a workspace, so always read the three together.
job_idThe parent job id (workspace-scoped). Identifies which workflow the task belongs to.
task_keyThe task's name/key within the job (unique only within that job).
timeout_secondsThe configured task-level timeout in seconds from the latest task definition. NULL or 0 means no bound is configured — those runs are counted in runs_no_task_timeout, not as violations.
task_runsDistinct task runs (COUNT DISTINCT run_id) observed for this task in the window. The denominator for the near/over counters.
max_exec_sThe single longest observed execution duration (seconds) across all runs of this task — the worst case. NULL when the duration column is unpopulated for every run.
exec_s_p95Approximate 95th-percentile execution duration (seconds). Compare against timeout_seconds to see how much headroom typical-but-slow runs have left. NULL when no run has a populated duration.
runs_near_timeoutCount of runs that reached >=80% of a configured (non-null, >0) timeout. The early-warning bucket — tasks approaching their limit.
runs_over_timeoutCount of runs that ran at or past the configured timeout yet were still recorded — tasks that should have been killed. The most urgent bucket (always a subset of runs_near_timeout).
runs_no_task_timeoutCount of runs on a task whose configured timeout is NULL or 0 — 'no bound configured', a governance gap but not a timeout finding.
runs_exec_nullCount of runs with a NULL execution duration — the column is not yet populated for those rows, so they are 'not assessed', not clean.
Sample output
workspace_idjob_idtask_keytimeout_secondstask_runsmax_exec_sexec_s_p95runs_near_timeoutruns_over_timeoutruns_no_task_timeoutruns_exec_null
1234567890123456894213765512340ingest_bronze360030372034806200
1234567890123456778451236698120silver_merge01441039500140
9876543210987654553120987764310gold_publish180022NULLNULL00022
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.lakeflow.job_task_run_timelineTask-level execution log (hourly-sliced). Filtered to the end row per task run (result_state IS NOT NULL) to read the final execution_duration_seconds; supplies the observed run durations counted and percentiled here, restricted to the trailing :period_days window excluding the incomplete current day.
system.lakeflow.job_tasksSCD2 per-task definition. Deduped to the latest row per (workspace_id, job_id, task_key) by change_time to read the configured task-level timeout_seconds — the bound each observed duration is compared against.
Caveats & gotchas
  • Both assessment inputs are late-populated (not before ~late Nov / early Dec 2025): execution_duration_seconds on job_task_run_timeline and timeout_seconds on job_tasks. On short-history accounts every row lands in runs_exec_null or runs_no_task_timeout — read as 'not yet assessed', never a clean result.
  • When execution_duration_seconds is NULL for every run of a task, max_exec_s and exec_s_p95 both return NULL (MAX and percentile_approx ignore NULLs) — a NULL there means 'not measured', not zero seconds.
  • Empty != zero != error: an account that runs no jobs returns a valid empty result; missing system tables return TABLE_OR_VIEW_NOT_FOUND (skip, don't treat as failure).
  • A NULL or 0 timeout is 'no bound configured' — deliberately NOT counted as a near-timeout or a violation; it surfaces only in runs_no_task_timeout.
  • No dollars: durations are not a billing unit. max_exec_s / exec_s_p95 / timeout_seconds are all seconds, and there is no DBU-to-dollar conversion for time.
  • Ids are workspace-scoped: job_id and task_key are unique only within a workspace+job, so the join and grouping key on (workspace_id, job_id, task_key) — never the id alone — to avoid cross-workspace fan-out.
  • job_tasks is SCD2: only the latest row per (workspace_id, job_id, task_key) by change_time is used. Skipping the dedupe would fan out and double-count runs.
  • Only the task end row carries the final state, so the timeline is filtered to result_state IS NOT NULL; other slices of a long, hourly-sliced task are intentionally excluded. Because retries reuse a run_id and appear as extra end rows, the near/over/bucket counters count end rows and can exceed task_runs (distinct run_ids).
  • Date window is the trailing :period_days (parameter) and excludes today — period_end_time < the start of the current day — so partial-day data never skews the counts.
  • Confidence is needs_confirmation: the near/over-timeout logic and the underlying columns should be confirmed populated on the target account before acting on findings.
  • Requires Unity Catalog and SELECT granted on the system.lakeflow schema.
→
What you do next
Start with rows where `runs_over_timeout` > 0 (a limit that isn't being enforced), then rows where `runs_near_timeout` is a large share of `task_runs` or `exec_s_p95` sits just under `timeout_seconds`: either raise the timeout to a safe bound or investigate the slowdown before it trips. Treat large `runs_exec_null` / `runs_no_task_timeout` counts as 'not assessed' and re-run once the late-populated columns fill in, rather than assuming those tasks are healthy.
← PreviousGreen Runs Hiding Failed Taskslakeflow_succeeded_with_failed_tasks All Jobs & Pipelines ↑ Next →Job Failure Root-Cause Breakdownlakeflow_termination_taxonomy
Overview / Jobs & Pipelines / lakeflow_termination_taxonomy
19
Jobs & Pipelines lakeflow_termination_taxonomy

Job Failure Root-Cause Breakdown

A 30-day tally, per workspace, of how many job runs ended with each termination code — the root-cause taxonomy of why runs stopped.

system.lakeflow.job_run_timeline
queries/jobs_pipelines/lakeflow_termination_taxonomy.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:lakeflow_termination_taxonomy */
SELECT workspace_id, termination_code,
       COUNT(*)              AS run_rows,
       COUNT(DISTINCT run_id) AS distinct_runs
FROM system.lakeflow.job_run_timeline
WHERE period_start_time >= date_add(current_date(), -30)
  AND period_end_time < date_trunc('DAY', current_timestamp())
  AND result_state IS NOT NULL          -- end row only
  AND termination_code IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY workspace_id, termination_code
Why it matters

Failures rarely announce their cause — this turns a wall of failed runs into a ranked list of root causes per workspace, so you fix the dominant one first instead of guessing. It specifically surfaces quota and capacity ceilings (`WORKSPACE_RUN_LIMIT_EXCEEDED`, `MAX_JOB_QUEUE_SIZE_EXCEEDED`, `CLUSTER_ERROR`, `STORAGE_ACCESS_ERROR`) that are platform-side, not code bugs — the kind an admin can actually fix by raising a limit or fixing access, often unblocking many jobs at once. A concentration of one code in one workspace is a signal to act there; the counts tell you how much pain each cause is really causing.

What it does
  • Buckets the last 30 days of job runs by their root-cause termination code, separately for each workspace.
  • Gives you two counts per code: total end-rows and distinct runs, so retries and hourly-sliced long runs don't inflate the picture.
  • Highlights quota and capacity ceilings (run-limit, queue-size, cluster, storage-access) that are fixable platform-side, not application bugs.
  • Covers only runs that actually carried a termination code — it is a why-did-it-stop taxonomy, not a full success-vs-failure scorecard.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
workspace_idThe workspace the runs belong to. Termination codes are counted per workspace because job/run ids are only unique within a workspace; the same code can appear on multiple rows, one per workspace.
termination_codeThe root-cause code Databricks assigned to why the run stopped (e.g. WORKSPACE_RUN_LIMIT_EXCEEDED, MAX_JOB_QUEUE_SIZE_EXCEEDED, CLUSTER_ERROR, STORAGE_ACCESS_ERROR). This is the finer root-cause classification, not the coarser termination_type (which is deliberately excluded here as unverified).
run_rowsCount of end-rows carrying this code. Because retries reuse a run_id and long runs are hourly-sliced, this can exceed the number of actual runs — use it only alongside distinct_runs.
distinct_runsCount of distinct run_id values that ended with this code — the honest 'how many runs' number. This is the figure to rank and act on.
Sample output
workspace_idtermination_coderun_rowsdistinct_runs
1234567890123456WORKSPACE_RUN_LIMIT_EXCEEDED142118
1234567890123456CLUSTER_ERROR3737
9876543210987654MAX_JOB_QUEUE_SIZE_EXCEEDED6451
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.lakeflow.job_run_timelineThe sole source. Provides the run-level execution log; the query reads only the end row of each run (where result_state is populated) and groups the termination_code carried on that row by workspace.
Caveats & gotchas
  • Not dollars and not DBUs — every number here is a run count; there is no cost dimension in this query.
  • termination_code was not populated before ~Aug-2024; on short-history accounts it is NULL for every run and those rows are filtered out, so an empty result means 'no coded terminations in window,' not zero failures or an error.
  • Only end-rows are counted (result_state IS NOT NULL) and only rows with a non-NULL termination_code — runs that stopped without a code, or never wrote a code, don't appear.
  • This is not a success/failure scorecard: a code can accompany non-failure states too; pair with lakeflow_failed_runs for the FAILED/ERROR/TIMED_OUT view.
  • The coarser termination_type column is intentionally excluded — its value enum is unverified (see lakeflow_termination_type_probe before relying on it).
  • Fixed 30-day window: period_start_time >= today-30 and the incomplete current day is dropped (period_end_time < start of today), so totals never include partial-day data.
  • Grouping is per (workspace_id, termination_code); the same code shows once per workspace and must not be summed across workspaces as if run_ids were globally unique.
  • Requires Unity Catalog and SELECT granted on system.lakeflow; the table is empty if the account runs no jobs.
→
What you do next
Rank each workspace's codes by `distinct_runs`, then attack the dominant one at its source: raise the concurrent-run or queue-size limit for `WORKSPACE_RUN_LIMIT_EXCEEDED` / `MAX_JOB_QUEUE_SIZE_EXCEEDED`, investigate cluster config or cloud capacity for `CLUSTER_ERROR`, and fix storage credentials/paths for `STORAGE_ACCESS_ERROR` — one limit change can unblock many jobs at once.
← PreviousTasks Running Near Timeoutlakeflow_tasks_near_timeout All Jobs & Pipelines ↑ Next →Discover Job Termination-Type Valueslakeflow_termination_type_probe
Overview / Jobs & Pipelines / lakeflow_termination_type_probe
20
Jobs & Pipelines lakeflow_termination_type_probe

Discover Job Termination-Type Values

A runtime discovery probe that lists the distinct raw `termination_type` values appearing on finished job runs per workspace over the last 30 days, with a count of how many end-of-run rows carry each value.

system.lakeflow.job_run_timeline
queries/jobs_pipelines/lakeflow_termination_type_probe.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:lakeflow_termination_type_probe */
-- NEEDS CONFIRMATION: termination_type column exists but its value enum is UNVERIFIED.
-- This probe discovers distinct values at runtime; confirm the column populates on the account.
SELECT workspace_id, termination_type, COUNT(*) AS run_rows
FROM system.lakeflow.job_run_timeline
WHERE period_start_time >= date_add(current_date(), -30)
  AND result_state IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY workspace_id, termination_type
Why it matters

The `termination_type` column is confirmed to exist but its set of possible values is UNVERIFIED, and it does not populate before early December 2025 — so any dashboard or alert built on hardcoded termination-type values could silently break or return nothing on your account. Running this probe first tells you whether the column is actually populated and exactly which values your metastore emits, before you invest in taxonomy logic that depends on it. It is a cheap correctness check that prevents shipping reliability reporting built on assumptions the data does not support.

What it does
  • Lists the distinct raw termination_type values your account actually emits on completed job runs, rather than assuming a fixed value list.
  • Counts how many finished-run rows carry each value, split out per workspace.
  • Covers only the trailing 30 days and only end-of-run rows (runs that reached a final result state).
  • Serves as a populated-or-not check: an all-NULL result means the column is not yet populated on the account, not that runs terminated cleanly.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
workspace_idThe workspace the finished runs belong to. Values are workspace-scoped, so the same termination_type may appear once per workspace.
