termination_typeThe raw, undeduced `termination_type` value straight from the run timeline (the coarse termination class). A NULL here means the column is not populated for those rows — treat it as 'not assessed / not yet populated,' never as a clean/successful termination.
run_rowsThe number of end-of-run timeline rows carrying that termination_type in the workspace. This is a row count of finished-run slices, NOT a distinct-run count, so retries and multi-slice runs can inflate it — use it only to gauge whether/how heavily a value appears, not as a precise run tally.
Sample output
workspace_idtermination_typerun_rows
1234567890123456SUCCESS842
1234567890123456CLIENT_ERROR37
9876543210987654NULL (column not yet populated)1290
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.lakeflow.job_run_timelineThe only table read. Supplies the run-level execution log; the probe reads its raw `termination_type` column (coarse termination class) and `result_state` to isolate finished-run rows, grouped by `workspace_id`.
Caveats & gotchas
  • `termination_type` value enum is UNVERIFIED — the shown sample values are illustrative only; the whole point of this probe is to discover the real ones at runtime.
  • Column is late-populated: not populated before ~late-Nov/early-Dec-2025. On short-history accounts it is NULL for every row — that is 'not yet populated,' not a finding, and empty != zero != error.
  • GA table but Unity Catalog is required and an admin must GRANT SELECT on the `system.lakeflow` schema.
  • An account that runs no jobs returns an empty (but valid) result.
  • `run_rows` is a raw `COUNT(*)` of end-row slices, not `COUNT(DISTINCT run_id)`; retries and hourly-sliced long runs can inflate it. It is a probe, not a precise metric.
  • Date window is a rolling 30 days via `period_start_time >= date_add(current_date(), -30)`; unlike most reliability queries here it does NOT drop the incomplete current day.
  • confidence: needs_confirmation — verifier status is `unverifiable`; confirm the column populates on your account before relying on any values it returns.
→
What you do next
Run this first on a workspace with recent (post-early-Dec-2025) job activity. If you get non-NULL `termination_type` values, record the actual distinct list and use it to drive your termination taxonomy; if every row is NULL, the column is not yet populated on your account — do not build alerting on `termination_type` and fall back to `termination_code` instead.
← PreviousJob Failure Root-Cause Breakdownlakeflow_termination_taxonomy All Jobs & Pipelines ↑ Next →Daily Job Run Mix And Hourslakeflow_workload_mix_hours
Overview / Jobs & Pipelines / lakeflow_workload_mix_hours
21
Jobs & Pipelines lakeflow_workload_mix_hours

Daily Job Run Mix And Hours

A daily breakdown, per workspace, of how many distinct job runs were active and how many execution-seconds they consumed, sliced by run type and trigger type over the trailing 30 days.

system.lakeflow.job_run_timeline
queries/jobs_pipelines/lakeflow_workload_mix_hours.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:lakeflow_workload_mix_hours */
SELECT workspace_id, run_type, trigger_type,
       date_trunc('DAY', period_start_time) AS run_day,
       COUNT(DISTINCT run_id) AS distinct_runs,
       SUM(CASE WHEN result_state IS NOT NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS completed_run_rows,
       SUM(execution_duration_seconds) AS execution_s_total
FROM system.lakeflow.job_run_timeline
WHERE period_start_time >= date_add(current_date(), -30)
  AND period_start_time < date_trunc('DAY', current_timestamp())
GROUP BY workspace_id, run_type, trigger_type, date_trunc('DAY', period_start_time)
Why it matters

This is the portfolio baseline for your Jobs estate: it shows an admin where run volume and compute hours actually go, split by whether runs are scheduled jobs, ad-hoc submit runs, or notebook-driven workflow runs. Unexplained spikes in run counts or execution hours are early signals of runaway schedules, retry storms, or a team quietly moving heavy work onto jobs compute — each of which shows up as DBUs on the bill. Because it is source data for the failed-runs and reliability views, getting the mix right frames where to spend the deeper (and costlier) investigation.

What it does
  • Produces a per-day, per-workspace table of your job runs for the trailing 30 days (today excluded), sliced by run_type (scheduled JOB_RUN, ad-hoc SUBMIT_RUN, notebook-driven WORKFLOW_RUN) and by trigger_type.
  • Counts the distinct runs active in each day bucket, netting out the hourly slicing so a multi-hour run is counted once per day rather than once per hour.
  • Reports how many run end-rows completed (carried a final result) and the total execution-seconds consumed in that bucket, so you see both volume and where the hours land.
  • Deliberately keeps WORKFLOW_RUN in the counts for run-mix visibility only — its compute belongs to the parent notebook, so its seconds are not a job cost you should bill against the job.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
workspace_idThe workspace the runs belong to. Ids are workspace-scoped, so always read the mix per workspace, never pooled across the metastore.
run_typeHow the run originated: JOB_RUN (a defined/scheduled job), SUBMIT_RUN (an ad-hoc one-off submit), or WORKFLOW_RUN (a run launched from within a notebook/workflow). WORKFLOW_RUN is here for counting only — do not attribute its DBUs to the job.
trigger_typeWhat kicked off the run (e.g. a schedule, a manual trigger, RETRY_ON_FAILURE). Lets you separate organic runs from retry-driven volume.
run_dayThe calendar day the run slices are bucketed into, from day-truncating period_start_time (the slice's period start). One row per (workspace, run_type, trigger_type, day); a run crossing midnight can appear in two adjacent day buckets.
distinct_runsCount of distinct run_ids active in the bucket — the true run volume for that day, already netted out of hourly slicing. This is your headline 'how many runs' number (all runs present, not only completed ones).
completed_run_rowsCount of end-rows (slices carrying a final result_state) in the bucket. Read as an 'end-row count,' not a run count: retries reuse the same run_id and add extra end rows, so this can exceed distinct_runs.
execution_s_totalTotal execution_duration_seconds (seconds, not dollars) summed across the bucket. NULL/zero on short-history accounts because this column is not populated before early-Dec-2025 — treat empty as 'not yet populated,' not as zero work.
Sample output
workspace_idrun_typetrigger_typerun_daydistinct_runscompleted_run_rowsexecution_s_total
1470123456789012JOB_RUNSCHEDULED2026-06-2814214853400
1470123456789012WORKFLOW_RUNMANUAL2026-06-28993120
8899223344556677SUBMIT_RUNRETRY_ON_FAILURE2026-06-2847860
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.lakeflow.job_run_timelineThe only table read. Provides the run-level execution log — one row per run state slice (long runs are sliced hourly). Supplies workspace_id, run_id, run_type, trigger_type, result_state (non-NULL marks the run's end row), execution_duration_seconds, and period_start_time, which the query buckets into daily run-mix and hours totals.
Caveats & gotchas
  • Single source: reads only system.lakeflow.job_run_timeline (GA, Unity Catalog required, SELECT on the system schema needed). An account that runs no jobs returns an empty but valid result — empty is not zero and not an error.
  • execution_s_total is in SECONDS, not dollars and not DBUs. This query carries no cost figures; for dollars/DBUs go to the billing-backed cost queries.
  • execution_duration_seconds is late-populated (not before ~early-Dec-2025), so on short-history accounts execution_s_total is NULL/0 for every bucket — degrade to 'not assessed,' never read as 'no work done.'
  • completed_run_rows is an END-ROW count, not a run count: retries reuse the same run_id and produce extra end rows, so it can exceed distinct_runs. Use distinct_runs for true volume.
  • distinct_runs counts DISTINCT run_id on purpose, to net out the hourly slicing of runs longer than one hour; a raw row count would over-count long runs. It counts all runs active in the day, not only completed ones.
  • Bucketing keys on each slice's period_start_time, so a run spanning midnight is split across two day buckets (counted in each day it was active); run_day is not the single run-start day for such runs.
  • WORKFLOW_RUN compute is attributed to the parent notebook — it is kept here only for run-mix counting. Do NOT double-count its DBUs against the job in any cost reconciliation.
  • Ids are workspace-scoped: read and compare the mix per workspace_id, never pooled across a multi-workspace metastore.
  • Fixed 30-day window: slices with period_start_time from date_add(current_date(), -30) up to (but excluding) the start of today, so the incomplete current day never skews the daily totals. It is not parameterized by :period_days.
→
What you do next
Use the daily run mix to right-size your job portfolio: see where run volume and execution hours concentrate (scheduled vs submit vs workflow runs), spot day-over-day spikes worth investigating, and identify high-hour trigger types to target for compute or scheduling tuning. Treat it as a portfolio baseline, then drill into specific workspaces with the reliability and cost queries.
← PreviousDiscover Job Termination-Type Valueslakeflow_termination_type_probe All Jobs & Pipelines ↑ Next →AI Gateway Abuse Signalscompute_ai_gateway_usage
Overview / Model Serving & AI / compute_ai_gateway_usage
01
Model Serving & AI compute_ai_gateway_usage

AI Gateway Abuse Signals

A daily per-workspace, per-endpoint, per-requester breakdown of AI Gateway request volume, its success / rate-limited / error split, input/output token throughput, and p50/p95/max latency.

system.ai_gateway.usage
queries/serving_ai/compute_ai_gateway_usage.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:compute_ai_gateway_usage */
SELECT
  CAST(g.request_time AS DATE)         AS usage_date,
  g.workspace_id                       AS workspace_id,
  CASE WHEN g.endpoint_name IS NULL THEN g.endpoint_name ELSE concat(substr(g.endpoint_name, 1, 2), '****') END AS endpoint_name,
  CASE
    WHEN g.requester IS NULL OR g.requester = '__REDACTED__' THEN g.requester
    WHEN g.requester LIKE '%@%' THEN concat(substr(g.requester, 1, 2), '****@****')
    WHEN g.requester RLIKE '^[0-9a-fA-F]{8}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{12}$' THEN g.requester
    ELSE concat(substr(g.requester, 1, 2), '****')
  END                                  AS requester,
  SUM(g.request_count)                                                              AS total_requests,
  -- NEEDS WORKSPACE CONFIRMATION: 2xx == success; 429 == rate-limit/quota; other == error.
  SUM(CASE WHEN g.status_code BETWEEN 200 AND 299 THEN g.request_count ELSE 0 END)  AS success_requests,
  SUM(CASE WHEN g.status_code = 429 THEN g.request_count ELSE 0 END)                AS rate_limited_requests,
  SUM(CASE WHEN g.status_code IS NOT NULL
            AND NOT (g.status_code BETWEEN 200 AND 299)
            AND g.status_code <> 429 THEN g.request_count ELSE 0 END)               AS error_requests,
  -- Token throughput is its own magnitude, never blended with request counts.
  SUM(COALESCE(g.input_tokens, 0))                                                  AS input_tokens,
  SUM(COALESCE(g.output_tokens, 0))                                                 AS output_tokens,
  -- Latency via percentile_approx (fraction form — NOT the PERCENTILE(col, 95) landmine).
  -- NEEDS WORKSPACE CONFIRMATION: request_duration_ms is the assumed latency column.
  percentile_approx(g.request_duration_ms, 0.5)                                     AS p50_latency_ms,
  percentile_approx(g.request_duration_ms, 0.95)                                    AS p95_latency_ms,
  MAX(g.request_duration_ms)                                                        AS max_latency_ms
FROM system.ai_gateway.usage g
WHERE g.request_time >= dateadd(day, -:period_days, current_date())
  AND g.request_time <  current_date()
GROUP BY
  CAST(g.request_time AS DATE),
  g.workspace_id,
  g.endpoint_name,
  g.requester
Why it matters

AI Gateway sits in front of serving endpoints precisely to catch abuse, and this query turns its raw telemetry into the signals an admin acts on: a single requester driving runaway token volume (tokens are billable throughput on pay-per-token endpoints), a spike in 429s meaning your rate limits or quotas are being hit, or latency blowups degrading every consumer. Left unwatched, one misconfigured client or leaked service-principal credential can quietly multiply token spend or exhaust quota for legitimate users. Because it slices by requester, it lets you attribute that pressure to a specific user or service principal before it becomes a bill or an outage.

What it does
  • Breaks AI Gateway traffic down to one row per day x workspace x endpoint x requester, so you can see exactly who called what, when.
  • Splits each row's request volume into success, rate-limited (429), and error buckets — the core abuse/quota signal.
  • Reports input and output token throughput separately from request counts, so you can spot a requester burning tokens even at modest request volume.
  • Summarizes end-to-end latency per slice as p50 / p95 / max milliseconds to surface latency blowups and slow tails.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
usage_dateThe calendar date (from request_time) the requests were served. One row per day within the window; today is excluded.
workspace_idThe workspace the gateway-fronted endpoint lives in. Use as a join key only within its own workspace scope.
endpoint_nameThe serving endpoint name, masked to its first 2 characters + '****'. NULL is passed through untouched (not masked).
requesterThe individual calling principal — a user or a service principal, never a team. Masked by type: emails to 'xx****@****', raw GUID service principals left intact, other principals truncated, and NULL / '__REDACTED__' sentinels passed through. Do not relabel this as a team.
total_requestsTotal requests in the slice (sum of request_count). Integer count, not DBUs or dollars.
success_requestsRequests with a 2xx status code. Assumed success definition (needs workspace confirmation).
rate_limited_requestsRequests with status_code 429 — the assumed rate-limit/quota-hit literal. A spike here is a signal to investigate quotas, not proof of misuse.
error_requestsRequests with a non-null status code that is neither 2xx nor 429 — everything else that failed.
input_tokensTotal input (prompt) tokens across the slice (nulls treated as 0). A throughput magnitude, kept separate from request counts — never blend the two.
output_tokensTotal output (completion) tokens across the slice (nulls treated as 0). Separate magnitude from request counts.
p50_latency_msApproximate median end-to-end latency in milliseconds (percentile_approx, fraction 0.5) over the assumed request_duration_ms column.
p95_latency_msApproximate 95th-percentile latency in milliseconds (percentile_approx, fraction 0.95) — the tail experienced by the slowest 5% of requests.
max_latency_msThe single slowest request's end-to-end latency in the slice, in milliseconds.
Sample output
usage_dateendpoint_namerequestertotal_requestsrate_limited_requestserror_requestsoutput_tokensp95_latency_ms
2026-07-05pr****da****@****48120320041098400001450
2026-07-05ll****4f8a1c2d-1b3e-4a5f-9c7d-2e6f0a1b3c4d120502240500880
2026-07-04pr****sv****960012800310
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.ai_gateway.usageSole source. Request telemetry captured by Mosaic AI Gateway (the governance/proxy layer fronting serving endpoints). Supplies request_time, workspace_id, endpoint_name, requester (calling principal), status_code, request_count, input/output tokens, and the assumed request_duration_ms latency column. Empty unless AI Gateway is explicitly enabled on an endpoint; column names are unverified for this workspace.
Caveats & gotchas
  • Schema UNVERIFIED: system.ai_gateway.usage column names (including the request_duration_ms latency column and the 429 rate-limit literal) are NOT confirmed for this workspace — treat every metric as needing re-validation. An absent column degrades that one metric to 'unknown', it does not error the whole finding.
  • Availability gating: the table is empty unless AI Gateway is explicitly enabled on an endpoint (opt-in per endpoint, not automatic with serving). Preview / evolving schema; requires Unity Catalog and SELECT on system.ai_gateway.*.
  • Empty != zero != error: if AI Gateway is not in use, the collection preflight skip-sentinels the table and the finding degrades to not_assessed — never a fabricated zero.
  • Not a cost source: these are request and token counters, NOT DBUs or dollars. Serving spend lives in system.billing.usage — cross to billing for the money figure.
  • requester is a principal (user or service principal), never a team — do not relabel it. A 429 or error row is a signal to investigate, not proof of misuse.
  • Token magnitudes are separate from request counts and must never be summed together.
  • Latency uses percentile_approx(col, 0.5/0.95) — the correct Databricks fraction signature — deliberately avoiding the PERCENTILE(col, 95) mistake.
  • Masking: endpoint_name is first-2-chars + '****' (NULL passed through); requester is masked by type (emails, GUIDs kept, others truncated, NULL / __REDACTED__ passed through).
  • Date window: parameterized by :period_days over the half-open range request_time >= current_date - :period_days AND request_time < current_date — whole days, excludes today's partial data.
  • workspace_id is a workspace-scoped key — only join it to other tables within the same workspace.
→
What you do next
Sort by rate_limited_requests, error_requests, or output_tokens to find the requester x endpoint slices under abuse or quota pressure; identify the specific user or service principal driving it, then adjust the endpoint's rate limits/quotas or revoke the offending principal, and cross-reference system.billing.usage to quantify the DBU/dollar impact. Before acting on any number, confirm the ai_gateway.usage column names against this workspace.
← PreviousDaily Job Run Mix And Hourslakeflow_workload_mix_hours All Model Serving & AI ↑ Next →Idle Serving Endpoints You Pay Forcompute_serving_dormant_endpoints
Overview / Model Serving & AI / compute_serving_dormant_endpoints
02
Model Serving & AI compute_serving_dormant_endpoints

Idle Serving Endpoints You Pay For

Every served entity in its latest configuration, paired with its request traffic over the trailing window, so entities that received no requests surface as dormant (`total_requests = 0`, `last_request_date` NULL).

system.serving.served_entitiessystem.serving.endpoint_usage
queries/serving_ai/compute_serving_dormant_endpoints.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:compute_serving_dormant_endpoints */
WITH entities AS (
  -- served_entities is change-history; collapse to the latest config row per entity.
  SELECT
    workspace_id,
    endpoint_id,
    endpoint_name,
    served_entity_id,
    served_entity_name,
    entity_type,
    entity_name,
    entity_version,
    latest_change_time
  FROM (
    SELECT
      se.workspace_id,
      se.endpoint_id,
      se.endpoint_name,
      se.served_entity_id,
      se.served_entity_name,
      se.entity_type,
      se.entity_name,
      se.entity_version,
      MAX(se.change_time) OVER (
        PARTITION BY se.workspace_id, se.endpoint_id, se.served_entity_id
      ) AS latest_change_time,
      ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
        PARTITION BY se.workspace_id, se.endpoint_id, se.served_entity_id
        ORDER BY se.change_time DESC
      ) AS _rn
    FROM system.serving.served_entities se
  )
  WHERE _rn = 1
),
usage_rollup AS (
  -- Per-entity traffic inside the window. Entities with no traffic never appear here,
  -- so the LEFT JOIN below leaves their last_request_date NULL (the dormant signal).
  SELECT
    eu.workspace_id,
    eu.endpoint_id,
    eu.served_entity_id,
    SUM(eu.request_count)          AS total_requests,
    MAX(CAST(eu.request_time AS DATE)) AS last_request_date
  FROM system.serving.endpoint_usage eu
  WHERE eu.request_time >= dateadd(day, -:period_days, current_date())
    AND eu.request_time <  current_date()
  GROUP BY eu.workspace_id, eu.endpoint_id, eu.served_entity_id
)
SELECT
  ent.workspace_id                            AS workspace_id,
  ent.endpoint_id                             AS endpoint_id,
  CASE WHEN ent.endpoint_name IS NULL THEN ent.endpoint_name ELSE concat(substr(ent.endpoint_name, 1, 2), '****') END AS endpoint_name,
  ent.served_entity_id                        AS served_entity_id,
  CASE WHEN ent.served_entity_name IS NULL THEN ent.served_entity_name ELSE concat(substr(ent.served_entity_name, 1, 2), '****') END AS served_entity_name,
  ent.entity_type                             AS entity_type,
  ent.entity_name                             AS entity_name,
  ent.latest_change_time                      AS latest_change_time,
  CAST(ent.latest_change_time AS DATE)        AS latest_change_date,
  COALESCE(ur.total_requests, 0)              AS total_requests,
  ur.last_request_date                        AS last_request_date
FROM entities ent
LEFT JOIN usage_rollup ur
  ON  ent.workspace_id     = ur.workspace_id
  AND ent.endpoint_id      = ur.endpoint_id
  AND ent.served_entity_id = ur.served_entity_id
Why it matters

Provisioned-throughput serving endpoints bill for reserved capacity whether or not anyone calls them, so an endpoint sitting idle for weeks is pure waste. This query surfaces exactly those served entities that received zero requests in the window - the prime candidates to scale to zero or delete. Because it preserves every configured entity and marks no-traffic ones with a NULL last-request date, an admin gets a clean idle list rather than having to infer absence from a busy activity report. The recency floor keeps brand-new endpoints off that list, so the cleanup targets are trustworthy.

What it does
  • Lists every served entity in its most recent configuration - one line per (workspace, endpoint, served entity) - so nothing that is provisioned gets dropped.
  • Attaches each entity's request traffic over the trailing :period_days window: a total request count and the date of its last request.
  • Flags the dormant ones - entities with total_requests of 0 and a blank last_request_date are the idle-but-provisioned endpoints worth scaling down or deleting.
  • Carries each entity's latest config-change date so you can tell a genuinely idle endpoint apart from one that was only just created inside the window.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
workspace_idThe workspace the endpoint lives in. Part of the identity key; the same endpoint name in two workspaces is two distinct rows.
endpoint_idStable identifier of the serving endpoint (passed through unmasked) - use this to locate and act on the endpoint in the API/UI.
endpoint_nameHuman-readable endpoint name, masked to its first 2 characters + '****'. Orientation only; act via endpoint_id.
served_entity_idStable identifier of the served entity within the endpoint (an endpoint can host several). Unmasked join/action key.
served_entity_nameName of the served entity, masked to its first 2 characters + '****'.
entity_typeWhat kind of thing is served - e.g. a registered UC model, a foundation-model route, or an external model.
entity_nameThe underlying entity being served (e.g. the registered model or external model name).
latest_change_timeTimestamp of the most recent configuration change to this served entity - also the recency floor: if this is newer than the window start the entity is a fresh deploy, not truly dormant.
latest_change_dateThe date portion of latest_change_time, for quick reading against the window.
total_requestsRequests this entity served in the trailing window (0 when it had no traffic). A count, not DBUs or dollars; treat the exact number as needing schema re-validation.
last_request_dateDate of the entity's most recent request in the window; NULL is the dormant signal - 'no traffic in the window', not 'never used'.
Sample output
endpoint_idendpoint_nameserved_entity_nameentity_typelatest_change_datetotal_requestslast_request_date
b3e8a1c2-4f7d-4a91-9c02-7f1e2d3a4b5cfr****fr****MODEL2026-01-140(null)
a1d0f9e8-7c6b-4d2a-8e11-3b9c0a1d2e3fch****gp****FOUNDATION_MODEL2026-05-021842202026-07-05
c9f2b7a4-1e3d-4c88-b6a0-5d4e6f7a8b90re****re****EXTERNAL_MODEL2026-07-040(null)
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.serving.served_entitiesThe configuration dimension - describes what each endpoint serves (model, foundation-model route, or external model). It is change-history, so it is collapsed to the latest config row per served entity and supplies endpoint/entity identity, names, type, and the latest change time used as a recency floor.
system.serving.endpoint_usageThe request telemetry fact table - windowed per served entity to produce total request volume and the most recent request date. Entities with no traffic in the window simply never appear here, which is what makes them read as dormant.
Caveats & gotchas
  • `system.serving.*` is empty unless Model Serving is enabled and in use on the metastore (needs Unity Catalog + SELECT on system.serving.*). No serving history yields a valid but empty result; collection preflight degrades the finding to not_assessed rather than fabricating an 'all endpoints dormant' zero.
  • Empty != zero != error: a NULL `last_request_date` means 'no traffic observed in the window' - the dormant signal. It does NOT mean 'never used'; the request may simply predate the window or usage retention may be shorter than the config dimension. Report dormant as 'no requests in the last :period_days days', not 'never used'.
  • Schema NOT confirmed: this query assumes an `endpoint_usage.request_count` column and a `request_time` timestamp, and joins `endpoint_usage` on `endpoint_id`. The CONFIRMED schema has NO `request_count` (volume is COUNT(*)) and `endpoint_usage` carries `served_entity_id`, not `endpoint_id`. Treat `total_requests` and the usage join as needing re-validation; only serving_endpoint_traffic_by_endpoint uses the confirmed shape.
  • `served_entities` is change-history (one row per config change), so it is deduped to the latest row per (workspace_id, endpoint_id, served_entity_id) by change_time DESC before the usage join - do not join it raw or a renamed entity double-counts.
  • Usage is attached with a dimension-preserving LEFT JOIN keyed on all three of workspace_id + endpoint_id + served_entity_id (workspace-scoped); an entity with no traffic keeps total_requests = 0 and last_request_date = NULL rather than dropping out.
  • `latest_change_time` is carried as a recency floor but the query does NOT filter on it: a newly-created/reconfigured entity whose only change is newer than the window start still appears with total_requests = 0, so the consumer must exclude it to avoid mislabeling it dormant. entity_creator/creation-flag columns are assumed but unverified.
  • Not a cost source: these are request counts, not DBUs or dollars, and this query joins no price/billing table. Serving spend lives in system.billing.usage - use dormancy to right-size, then cross to billing for the money figure.
  • Date window is the trailing :period_days, half-open (request_time >= current_date - :period_days AND < current_date) - whole days, excludes today's partial data.
  • `endpoint_name` and `served_entity_name` are masked to their first 2 characters + '****' (NULLs pass through as NULL); `entity_name` and the IDs (endpoint_id, served_entity_id) pass through intact.
→
What you do next
Review every served entity where `total_requests = 0` and `last_request_date` is NULL, confirm its `latest_change_date` predates the window (so it is genuinely idle, not brand-new), then scale that endpoint to zero or delete it — and cross-check the freed spend in system.billing.usage.
← PreviousAI Gateway Abuse Signalscompute_ai_gateway_usage All Model Serving & AI ↑ Next →Daily Serving Endpoint Trafficcompute_serving_endpoint_usage
Overview / Model Serving & AI / compute_serving_endpoint_usage
03
Model Serving & AI compute_serving_endpoint_usage

Daily Serving Endpoint Traffic

A daily rollup of Model Serving traffic per endpoint and served entity, showing total, successful and errored requests plus input/output token totals, with endpoint and entity names joined from the serving configuration.

system.serving.endpoint_usagesystem.serving.served_entities
queries/serving_ai/compute_serving_endpoint_usage.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:compute_serving_endpoint_usage */
WITH entities AS (
  -- served_entities is change-history; keep only the latest config row per entity.
  SELECT
    workspace_id,
    endpoint_id,
    endpoint_name,
    served_entity_id,
    served_entity_name,
    entity_type,
    entity_name,
    entity_version
  FROM (
    SELECT
      se.*,
      ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
        PARTITION BY se.workspace_id, se.endpoint_id, se.served_entity_id
        ORDER BY se.change_time DESC
      ) AS _rn
    FROM system.serving.served_entities se
  )
  WHERE _rn = 1
)
SELECT
  CAST(eu.request_time AS DATE)        AS usage_date,
  eu.workspace_id                      AS workspace_id,
  eu.endpoint_id                       AS endpoint_id,
  CASE WHEN ent.endpoint_name IS NULL THEN ent.endpoint_name ELSE concat(substr(ent.endpoint_name, 1, 2), '****') END AS endpoint_name,
  eu.served_entity_id                  AS served_entity_id,
  CASE WHEN ent.served_entity_name IS NULL THEN ent.served_entity_name ELSE concat(substr(ent.served_entity_name, 1, 2), '****') END AS served_entity_name,
  ent.entity_type                      AS entity_type,
  ent.entity_name                      AS entity_name,
  -- NEEDS WORKSPACE CONFIRMATION: status_code is the assumed HTTP-status column on
  -- endpoint_usage; classify 2xx as success, everything else as error. If the real
  -- column differs, every request below collapses into the 'unknown' bucket and the
  -- finding reports success rate as unknown rather than fabricating one.
  SUM(CASE WHEN eu.status_code BETWEEN 200 AND 299 THEN eu.request_count ELSE 0 END) AS success_requests,
  SUM(CASE WHEN eu.status_code IS NOT NULL AND NOT (eu.status_code BETWEEN 200 AND 299)
           THEN eu.request_count ELSE 0 END)                                         AS error_requests,
  SUM(eu.request_count)                                                              AS total_requests,
  -- Token throughput is a separate magnitude from request counts (never summed with them).
  SUM(COALESCE(eu.served_entity_input_tokens, 0))                                    AS input_tokens,
  SUM(COALESCE(eu.served_entity_output_tokens, 0))                                   AS output_tokens
FROM system.serving.endpoint_usage eu
LEFT JOIN entities ent
  ON  eu.workspace_id     = ent.workspace_id
  AND eu.endpoint_id      = ent.endpoint_id
  AND eu.served_entity_id = ent.served_entity_id
WHERE eu.request_time >= dateadd(day, -:period_days, current_date())
  AND eu.request_time <  current_date()
GROUP BY
  CAST(eu.request_time AS DATE),
  eu.workspace_id,
  eu.endpoint_id,
  ent.endpoint_name,
  eu.served_entity_id,
  ent.served_entity_name,
  ent.entity_type,
  ent.entity_name
Why it matters

This is the workhorse baseline of serving activity: it lets an admin spot endpoints with abnormal error rates (a reliability and possibly abuse signal) and surprising token throughput (the real cost driver for pay-per-token serving) before those turn into a bill surprise. Because a single high-traffic or runaway endpoint can dominate serving spend, seeing per-day request and token volume per entity tells you where to focus right-sizing and governance. Note these are request and token counters only — not DBUs or dollars — so they tell you where the money is likely going, not the figure itself.

What it does
  • Rolls up Model Serving traffic to one row per day × endpoint × served entity over the trailing :period_days window (excluding today).
  • Splits each endpoint-day's requests into successful (2xx), errored (non-2xx) and total counts, so you can read an error rate at a glance.
  • Reports input and output token totals separately from request counts, as an independent view of throughput.
  • Attaches the (masked) endpoint and served-entity names, plus entity_type/entity_name, from the latest serving configuration so the traffic is labeled with what it's actually serving.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
usage_dateThe calendar day (from request_time) the traffic was served; one row per day within the trailing window, excluding today.
workspace_idThe workspace the endpoint lives in; part of the identity used to resolve endpoint and entity names.
endpoint_idThe serving endpoint identifier that handled the requests on this day (taken from endpoint_usage; also part of the join key to served_entities).
endpoint_nameHuman-readable endpoint name, MASKED to first 2 chars + '****' (joined from served_entities); NULL if no matching config row.
served_entity_idThe served entity within the endpoint that actually handled the request (an endpoint can host several).
served_entity_nameHuman-readable served-entity name, MASKED to first 2 chars + '****'; NULL if unmatched.
entity_typeWhat is being served (e.g. a registered model, an external model, a foundation-model route), from the latest config row.
entity_nameThe name of the underlying entity being served (e.g. the model name), from the latest config row; NOT masked.
success_requestsCount of requests with a 2xx status_code on this day; a request COUNT, not dollars. Reports as 0/unknown if the assumed status column is absent.
error_requestsCount of requests with a non-2xx (but non-null) status_code; the error signal for this endpoint-day.
total_requestsAll requests served on this day (success + error + any un-classified); the raw traffic volume.
input_tokensTotal input/prompt tokens served on this day (separate magnitude from request counts; a token count, not dollars).
output_tokensTotal output/completion tokens served on this day (separate magnitude; a token count, not dollars).
Sample output
usage_dateendpoint_nameentity_namesuccess_requestserror_requeststotal_requestsoutput_tokens
2026-07-03pr****llama-3-70b-instruct48210915491253820441
2026-07-03in****fraud-scorer120046120100
2026-07-04pr****llama-3-70b-instruct510024488554904110233
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.serving.endpoint_usageFact / request telemetry table for Model Serving. Supplies request_time, workspace_id, served_entity_id, the assumed endpoint_id, the assumed status_code, request_count, and per-request input/output token columns; drives the date window and every count/token sum. (Schema assumed, not confirmed for this query — README notes the confirmed grain is one row per request with no request_count, tokens named input_token_count/output_token_count, and no endpoint_id column.)
system.serving.served_entitiesConfiguration/dimension table describing what each endpoint serves. Change-history (one row per config change), deduped to the latest row per (workspace_id, endpoint_id, served_entity_id) by change_time DESC and left-joined to supply endpoint_name, served_entity_name, entity_type and entity_name.
Caveats & gotchas
  • Availability-gated: system.serving.* is empty unless Model Serving is enabled and in use on the metastore (needs Unity Catalog + SELECT on system.serving.*). An account that never served a model yields a valid EMPTY result.
  • Empty != zero != error: if the table is absent, collection preflight skip-sentinels it and the finding degrades to not_assessed, never a fabricated 'all endpoints dormant / zero traffic'.
  • SCHEMA UNCONFIRMED (confidence: needs_confirmation): this is one of the two older serving queries. It assumes request_count, served_entity_input_tokens/served_entity_output_tokens, a status_code column, and an endpoint_id column joined on (workspace_id, endpoint_id, served_entity_id). The CONFIRMED reality (per the domain README) is one row per request (NO request_count column), token columns input_token_count/output_token_count, and endpoint_usage carrying only served_entity_id (no endpoint_id). Treat all volume/token numbers, and the endpoint_id join, as needing re-validation.
  • If the real HTTP-status column is not named status_code, every request collapses into neither success nor error bucket and success_requests/error_requests report as unknown rather than a fabricated rate.
  • served_entities is CHANGE-HISTORY (one row per config change), deduped to the latest row per (workspace_id, endpoint_id, served_entity_id) by change_time DESC before the join, so a renamed/reconfigured entity is not counted twice.
  • Not a cost source: these are request/token counters, NOT DBUs or dollars. Serving spend lives in system.billing.usage; input_tokens/output_tokens are token counts, not money.
  • Tokens are a separate magnitude from request counts and are never summed together; input_tokens/output_tokens are independent of success/error/total_requests.
  • Masking: endpoint_name and served_entity_name are truncated to their first 2 characters + '****'; NULLs (unmatched join) are passed through as NULL. entity_name and entity_type are NOT masked.
  • Join keys are workspace-scoped: endpoint identity resolves only when (workspace_id, endpoint_id, served_entity_id) matches served_entities; an unmatched request keeps its usage but shows NULL endpoint_name/served_entity_name/entity_type/entity_name.
  • Date window: trailing :period_days days via half-open range request_time >= dateadd(day, -:period_days, current_date()) AND request_time < current_date() — whole days only, EXCLUDES today's partial data.
→
What you do next
Sort the output by error_requests / total_requests to surface high-error-rate endpoints, and by input_tokens + output_tokens to spot surprising token throughput; then confirm the endpoint_usage column names and the endpoint_id join against your workspace and cross to system.billing.usage for the DBU/dollar figure before acting on any endpoint.
← PreviousIdle Serving Endpoints You Pay Forcompute_serving_dormant_endpoints All Model Serving & AI ↑ Next →Serving Endpoint Traffic Rollupserving_endpoint_traffic_by_endpoint
Overview / Model Serving & AI / serving_endpoint_traffic_by_endpoint
04
Model Serving & AI serving_endpoint_traffic_by_endpoint

Serving Endpoint Traffic Rollup

One row per Model Serving endpoint over the trailing window, giving total requests, the success/error split, input and output token totals, and the date each endpoint was last hit.

system.serving.endpoint_usagesystem.serving.served_entities
queries/serving_ai/serving_endpoint_traffic_by_endpoint.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:serving_endpoint_traffic_by_endpoint */
WITH entities AS (
  SELECT workspace_id, endpoint_id, endpoint_name, served_entity_id
  FROM (
    SELECT
      se.workspace_id, se.endpoint_id, se.endpoint_name, se.served_entity_id,
      ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
        PARTITION BY se.workspace_id, se.endpoint_id, se.served_entity_id
        ORDER BY se.change_time DESC
      ) AS _rn
    FROM system.serving.served_entities se
  )
  WHERE _rn = 1
)
SELECT
  ent.endpoint_id                                   AS endpoint_id,
  CASE WHEN ent.endpoint_name IS NULL THEN ent.endpoint_name ELSE concat(substr(ent.endpoint_name, 1, 2), '****') END AS endpoint_name,
  COUNT(*)                                          AS request_count,
  SUM(CASE WHEN eu.status_code BETWEEN 200 AND 299 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS success_requests,
  SUM(CASE WHEN eu.status_code IS NOT NULL AND NOT (eu.status_code BETWEEN 200 AND 299) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS error_requests,
  SUM(COALESCE(eu.input_token_count, 0))            AS input_tokens,
  SUM(COALESCE(eu.output_token_count, 0))           AS output_tokens,
  MAX(CAST(eu.request_time AS DATE))                AS last_request_date
FROM system.serving.endpoint_usage eu
LEFT JOIN entities ent
  ON  eu.workspace_id     = ent.workspace_id
  AND eu.served_entity_id = ent.served_entity_id
WHERE eu.request_time >= dateadd(day, -:period_days, current_date())
  AND eu.request_time <  current_date()
GROUP BY ent.endpoint_id, ent.endpoint_name
Why it matters

Model Serving is billed by mode — provisioned-throughput endpoints cost the same whether they handle a million requests or ten, while pay-per-token endpoints scale with usage. Putting real request volume next to each endpoint lets an admin catch cost-mode mismatches: a high-traffic endpoint stuck on pay-per-token, or a low-traffic one parked on expensive provisioned throughput. The error split and last-request date also flag endpoints that are failing or effectively idle before the bill confirms the waste.

What it does
  • Rolls up every inference request in the trailing window into one line per serving endpoint, so you see each endpoint's total traffic at a glance.
  • Splits that traffic into successful (HTTP 2xx) versus error requests, exposing endpoints that are quietly failing.
  • Totals input_tokens and output_tokens separately per endpoint — the throughput signal that drives pay-per-token spend (kept distinct from request counts, never blended).
  • Reports each endpoint's last request date so you can spot ones that have gone quiet inside the window.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
endpoint_idStable identifier of the serving endpoint, resolved from the served_entities dimension. Use it as the join key back to billing or config; it survives renames. Can be NULL for usage rows that matched no config row (unresolved traffic).
endpoint_nameHuman-readable endpoint name, masked to its first 2 characters + '****' (NULL passes through as NULL). Orienting label only, not for exact matching.
request_countTotal inference requests to this endpoint in the window (COUNT of request rows). This is request volume, not DBUs or dollars.
success_requestsRequests that returned an HTTP 2xx status. success_requests + error_requests can be less than request_count when status_code is NULL.
error_requestsRequests with a non-null, non-2xx status — the failure signal. A high ratio to request_count means the endpoint is erroring.
input_tokensSum of per-request input token counts (nulls treated as 0). Prompt-side throughput; a separate magnitude from request_count, never added to output_tokens.
output_tokensSum of per-request output token counts (nulls treated as 0). Generation-side throughput; the main driver of pay-per-token cost.
last_request_dateCalendar date of the most recent request in the window. A stale date (well before yesterday) flags an endpoint that has gone dormant.
Sample output
endpoint_idendpoint_namerequest_countsuccess_requestserror_requestsinput_tokensoutput_tokenslast_request_date
a1b2c3d4-0000-4a1b-8c2d-111122223333pr****4821047995215682144019308752026-07-05
f9e8d7c6-0000-4f9e-8d7c-444455556666ll****9129001212845004021182026-07-05
0c1d2e3f-0000-40c1-8d2e-777788889999de****373075120089402026-06-14
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.serving.endpoint_usageThe request-level fact table — one row per inference request. Supplies the traffic count (COUNT(*)), status_code for the success/error split, input_token_count/output_token_count for throughput, and request_time for both the date window and last_request_date. Carries served_entity_id (not endpoint_id).
system.serving.served_entitiesThe change-history configuration dimension. Deduplicated to the latest config row per workspace_id + endpoint_id + served_entity_id (change_time DESC), it resolves served_entity_id into endpoint_id and the human-readable endpoint_name (which endpoint_usage does not carry).
Caveats & gotchas
  • Availability gating: system.serving.* is empty unless Model Serving is enabled and in use (needs Unity Catalog + SELECT on system.serving.*). An account that never served a model yields a valid empty result — the finding degrades to not_assessed, never a fabricated 'all endpoints dormant'.
  • Empty != zero != error: no rows means serving isn't in use, not that endpoints got zero traffic. A missing table (TABLE_OR_VIEW_NOT_FOUND) is skip-sentineled at collection, not a real finding.
  • Not a cost source: request_count and *_tokens are counters, not DBUs or dollars. Serving spend lives in system.billing.usage — use these counts to right-size, then cross to billing for the money figure.
  • Tokens vs requests are different magnitudes: input_tokens and output_tokens are never summed together, and never added to request_count.
  • Grain trap: endpoint_usage is ONE ROW PER REQUEST — there is no request_count column in the source; traffic is COUNT(*). served_entities is change-history and is deduped to the latest config row (change_time DESC) before joining, or a reconfigured entity would double-count.
  • Join key: endpoint_usage carries served_entity_id, not endpoint_id — identity and name come from served_entities. The join is on workspace_id + served_entity_id, so it is workspace-scoped.
  • Unmatched-row bucket: the join is a LEFT JOIN from endpoint_usage, so usage rows whose served_entity_id has no matching served_entities config row are NOT dropped — they collapse into a single row with NULL endpoint_id and NULL endpoint_name. Treat a NULL-endpoint row as unresolved traffic, not a real endpoint.
  • Masking: endpoint_name is truncated to first 2 chars + '****' (NULL stays NULL). Do not treat it as an exact identifier — use endpoint_id.
  • Date window: trailing :period_days via the half-open range dateadd(day, -:period_days, current_date()) <= request_time < current_date() — whole days only, excludes today's partial data.
→
What you do next
For each endpoint, compare request_count and the token totals against how it is billed in system.billing.usage: move a high-volume endpoint off pay-per-token onto provisioned throughput, drop a low-traffic one off expensive provisioned throughput, and open a ticket on any endpoint whose error_requests ratio is high or whose last_request_date has gone stale.
← PreviousDaily Serving Endpoint Trafficcompute_serving_endpoint_usage All Model Serving & AI ↑ Next →Detecting UniForm & Managed-Iceberg Tablesiceberg_uniform_metadata
Overview / Storage & Optimization / iceberg_uniform_metadata
01
Storage & Optimization iceberg_uniform_metadata

Detecting UniForm & Managed-Iceberg Tables

For a single target table, returns its full extended metadata rows so the "Delta Uniform Iceberg" section can be read to detect whether the table is UniForm / managed-Iceberg enabled.

queries/storage/iceberg_uniform_metadata.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:iceberg_uniform_metadata */
-- NEEDS CONFIRMATION: the 'Delta Uniform Iceberg' section EXISTS per doc, but the exact
-- output row/key strings to parse are UNVERIFIED. Confirm on a UniForm-enabled table.
DESCRIBE EXTENDED main.sales.orders;
Why it matters

UniForm and managed-Iceberg tables are governed and billed differently from plain Delta, and they never appear as Predictive Optimization-eligible managed tables, so they are a blind spot in the automatic-maintenance cost picture. Knowing which external/non-Delta tables actually expose Iceberg metadata tells an admin where cross-engine (Iceberg-reader) access is live and which tables sit outside PO's OPTIMIZE/VACUUM coverage. It is the detection step that turns a raw "EXTERNAL / non-DELTA" inventory flag into a confirmed UniForm finding.

What it does
  • Dumps the full extended metadata for one specified table (catalog.schema.table), the column list plus the detailed/table-information section.
  • Surfaces the 'Delta Uniform Iceberg' section within that output, the signal that the table is UniForm / managed-Iceberg enabled.
  • Is a detection probe only — it reports metadata, not size, file counts, or cost.
  • Meant to be pointed at tables the inventory step flagged as external / non-Delta, one table per run.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
col_nameThe name of each column in the table, followed in later rows by detailed-metadata labels (e.g. table properties and the 'Delta Uniform Iceberg' section header). Scan this column for the UniForm/Iceberg entry.
data_typeFor real columns, the column's data type; for the extended detail rows, the value paired with the label in col_name (e.g. the Iceberg metadata value).
commentThe column or property comment when one exists, otherwise null; usually blank for the extended metadata rows.
Sample output
col_namedata_typecomment
order_idbigintNULL
# Detailed Table InformationNULLNULL
Delta Uniform Icebergmetadata_location=s3://warehouse/main/sales/orders/metadata/00042.metadata.jsonNULL
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
Caveats & gotchas
  • Not a system table: this is an on-demand DESCRIBE EXTENDED statement run per target table on a SQL warehouse; no history, no trend, nothing is stored in Unity Catalog.
  • Metadata only: returns NO size, byte counts, or file counts and touches no metric table; use storage_breakdown_analyze for physical size.
  • needs_confirmation / unverifiable: the 'Delta Uniform Iceberg' section exists per Databricks docs, but the exact row/key strings to parse are UNVERIFIED (matches the SQL's own header). No specific key is emitted as fact; a run on a real UniForm-enabled table validates them.
  • Targeting: the engine runs this only on tables the inventory query flagged EXTERNAL / non-DELTA. A DELTA-only table (no UniForm) returns the standard describe output with no Iceberg section, which is valid, not an error.
  • Replace the placeholder: main.sales.orders is an example three-part name hardcoded in the SQL; substitute the actual catalog.schema.table before running.
  • Empty section != error: absence of the UniForm/Iceberg section simply means the table is not UniForm/managed-Iceberg enabled.
  • No date window and no masking apply: this reflects the table's current metadata at run time, not a trailing period.
  • Reader needs the appropriate privilege on the target table for DESCRIBE EXTENDED to return its extended metadata.
→
What you do next
Run this only on tables the inventory query flagged as EXTERNAL / non-DELTA, then scan the returned rows for the "Delta Uniform Iceberg" section to confirm which tables are UniForm / managed-Iceberg enabled and record the exact section key strings so the parser can be finalized; treat presence of the section as the signal, not table size.
← PreviousServing Endpoint Traffic Rollupserving_endpoint_traffic_by_endpoint All Storage & Optimization ↑ Next →Liquid Clustering Work and Costpo_clustering_activity
Overview / Storage & Optimization / po_clustering_activity
02
Storage & Optimization po_clustering_activity

Liquid Clustering Work and Cost

Per managed Unity Catalog table, a 30-day summary of how many liquid/automatic CLUSTERING operations ran, the files and bytes they compacted (removed vs. clustered), and the estimated DBUs that clustering consumed.

system.storage.predictive_optimization_operations_history
queries/storage/po_clustering_activity.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:po_clustering_activity */
SELECT catalog_name, schema_name, table_id, table_name, operation_type,
       COUNT(*) AS op_count,
       SUM(CAST(operation_metrics['number_of_removed_files']        AS BIGINT)) AS removed_files,
       SUM(CAST(operation_metrics['number_of_clustered_files']      AS BIGINT)) AS clustered_files,
       SUM(CAST(operation_metrics['amount_of_data_removed_bytes']   AS BIGINT)) AS removed_bytes,
       SUM(CAST(operation_metrics['amount_of_clustered_data_bytes'] AS BIGINT)) AS clustered_bytes,
       SUM(CAST(usage_quantity AS DECIMAL(38,6)))                              AS clustering_estimated_dbu
FROM system.storage.predictive_optimization_operations_history
WHERE operation_type = 'CLUSTERING'
  AND start_time >= current_date() - INTERVAL 30 DAYS AND start_time < current_date()
GROUP BY catalog_name, schema_name, table_id, table_name, operation_type
Why it matters

Automatic liquid clustering silently rewrites your managed tables and bills the DBUs to your account, so this is where you see which tables are absorbing the most maintenance compute and whether that work is productive. A table with a high op_count and large removed_bytes but poorly chosen clustering keys can burn DBUs re-clustering the same data repeatedly - money spent with little layout benefit. By ranking tables on clustering_estimated_dbu and comparing clustered vs. removed volume, an admin can spot runaway maintenance cost and decide whether to pin clustering columns manually or throttle PO. The DBU figure is an estimate, so it flags candidates for cost review rather than serving as the billed number.

What it does
  • Lists every managed table that liquid/automatic clustering touched in the last 30 days, one row per table, keyed by its stable table_id.
  • Reports how often clustering ran (op_count) and how much it churned - files and bytes removed vs. clustered back - so you can see the compaction ratio per table.
  • Totals the estimated DBUs clustering consumed per table, giving a per-table maintenance cost signal.
  • Excludes today and covers a fixed trailing 30-day window; tables PO never clustered simply don't appear.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
catalog_nameUnity Catalog catalog the clustered table lives in.
schema_nameSchema (database) within the catalog.
table_idStable UC table identity (UUID); use this to track a table across renames, not table_name.
table_nameHuman-readable table name at report time; may have changed if the table was renamed.
operation_typeAlways 'CLUSTERING' here - the query is scoped to incremental liquid/automatic clustering compaction.
op_countNumber of CLUSTERING operations run on this table in the 30-day window; a high count means PO is compacting this table frequently.
removed_filesTotal small/fragmented files PO removed across those ops - the input churn being compacted away.
clustered_filesTotal files PO wrote as the newly clustered output; compare with removed_files to see the compaction ratio.
removed_bytesTotal bytes in the removed files (bytes, not GB) - the data volume read and rewritten.
clustered_bytesTotal bytes written as clustered output (bytes); usually close to removed_bytes since clustering rewrites rather than deletes data.
clustering_estimated_dbuSummed ESTIMATED_DBU consumed by these clustering ops - DBUs (an estimate), not dollars and not the billed amount; multiply by your DBU rate for an approximate cost.
Sample output
schema_nametable_nameop_countremoved_filesclustered_filesremoved_bytesclustering_estimated_dbu
salesfct_orders4218400210051200000000086.500000
marketingweb_events932006409400000000012.250000
financegl_journal11203018000000000.400000
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.storage.predictive_optimization_operations_historyThe only source - the account-wide audit log of Predictive Optimization maintenance ops. This query reads its CLUSTERING rows, casts the file/byte counters out of the operation_metrics map, and sums usage_quantity (ESTIMATED_DBU) per table over 30 days.
Caveats & gotchas
  • Public Preview: requires Unity Catalog with Predictive Optimization enabled (Enterprise tier); only MANAGED UC tables are PO-eligible, so external tables never appear.
  • Regional feature with ~180-day source retention; the fixed 30-day window is well within retention but a longer window would silently truncate.
  • clustering_estimated_dbu is DBUs (ESTIMATED_DBU), not dollars and not the billed line - SUM as an estimate and reconcile against system.billing.usage for authoritative spend.
  • Estimated DBU can lag its operation row by up to ~24h, so the most recent included day is provisional.
  • Date window is fixed 30-day trailing: start_time >= current_date() - 30 days AND < current_date(); the guard intentionally drops today but keeps yesterday.
  • Empty result is valid, not an error: if PO is off, nothing clustered in-window, or the region/feature isn't enabled you get zero rows; an unprovisioned schema raises TABLE_OR_VIEW_NOT_FOUND.
  • operation_metrics is map<string,string> - the 4 file/byte counters are CAST to BIGINT before arithmetic while usage_quantity is a separate column CAST to DECIMAL(38,6); CLUSTERING has exactly these 4 metric keys, disjoint from other operation types.
  • table_id is the stable identity - table_name can be renamed or reused, so key any join or trend on table_id.
  • Scoped to operation_type = 'CLUSTERING' only (incremental liquid clustering compaction), distinct from AUTO_CLUSTERING_COLUMN_SELECTION.
→
What you do next
For each table, compare clustered_files/clustered_bytes against removed_files/removed_bytes to judge how much data churn liquid clustering is chewing through, then multiply clustering_estimated_dbu by your maintenance DBU rate to get an approximate maintenance cost; pin clustering keys manually or throttle PO on any table whose DBU is high but whose clustered vs. removed volume looks like unproductive churn, and reconcile the DBU against system.billing.usage before quoting a dollar figure.
← PreviousDetecting UniForm & Managed-Iceberg Tablesiceberg_uniform_metadata All Storage & Optimization ↑ Next →Clustering Key Churn Detectorpo_clustering_column_churn
Overview / Storage & Optimization / po_clustering_column_churn
03
Storage & Optimization po_clustering_column_churn

Clustering Key Churn Detector

Per managed table (and per distinct old-to-new/reason signature), the auto-clustering column-selection decisions over the last 30 days — whether the keys changed, the old to new column sets, the stated reason, when it last happened, and how many times.

system.storage.predictive_optimization_operations_history
queries/storage/po_clustering_column_churn.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:po_clustering_column_churn */
SELECT catalog_name, schema_name, table_id, table_name,
       operation_metrics['has_column_selection_changed'] AS has_column_selection_changed,
       operation_metrics['old_clustering_columns']        AS old_clustering_columns,
       operation_metrics['new_clustering_columns']        AS new_clustering_columns,
       operation_metrics['additional_reason']             AS additional_reason,
       MAX(end_time) AS last_selection_time,
       COUNT(*)      AS selection_event_count
FROM system.storage.predictive_optimization_operations_history
WHERE operation_type = 'AUTO_CLUSTERING_COLUMN_SELECTION'
  AND start_time >= current_date() - INTERVAL 30 DAYS AND start_time < current_date()
GROUP BY catalog_name, schema_name, table_id, table_name,
         operation_metrics['has_column_selection_changed'], operation_metrics['old_clustering_columns'],
         operation_metrics['new_clustering_columns'], operation_metrics['additional_reason']
Why it matters

Liquid/automatic clustering keys that keep changing mean Databricks is repeatedly re-laying-out the same table's data, which burns maintenance DBUs and never lets data-skipping settle, so queries stay slow. A table whose auto-selected columns churn every few days is a strong signal the workload's access pattern doesn't match any single key, or that the auto-selector is chasing noise. Spotting these lets an admin pin the clustering columns manually and stop paying for layout thrash that delivers no lasting pruning benefit.

What it does
  • Lists, per managed table, the automatic clustering-column selections Predictive Optimization made in the last 30 days.
  • Shows whether the keys actually changed (has_column_selection_changed) and the old to new column sets, plus the reason the selector gave.
  • Flags churn: a high selection_event_count on a changed row means the auto-selector keeps swapping keys, i.e. an unstable layout or a poor key choice.
  • Reports only the selection decision, not cost or files moved; old_clustering_columns = 'None' marks tables that were previously unclustered.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
catalog_nameUnity Catalog catalog the table lives in.
schema_nameSchema (database) within the catalog.
table_idStable UC table identity; use this to track a table across renames rather than the name.
table_nameHuman-readable table name at selection time (can be renamed/reused, so treat as a label).
has_column_selection_changedCategorical string 'true'/'false': whether this column-selection event actually swapped the clustering keys. 'true' is the churn signal.
old_clustering_columnsThe clustering columns before this decision; the literal string 'None' means the table was previously unpartitioned/unclustered.
new_clustering_columnsThe clustering columns the auto-selector chose in this event.
additional_reasonCategorical string explaining why the selector acted (e.g. query-pattern shift); context for the change.
last_selection_timeMAX(end_time): most recent end time an event with this exact table + old-to-new/reason/changed combination occurred within the window.
selection_event_countCOUNT(*): count of column-selection events matching this table and this exact old/new/reason/changed combination over the 30 days; a high count on a 'true' row means the layout is thrashing.
Sample output
catalog_nameschema_nametable_namehas_column_selection_changedold_clustering_columnsnew_clustering_columnsselection_event_count
prodsalesorderstrueorder_datecustomer_id7
prodfinanceledger_entriestrueNoneposting_date1
analyticswebpage_eventsfalsesession_idsession_id3
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.storage.predictive_optimization_operations_historySole source. Filtered to operation_type = 'AUTO_CLUSTERING_COLUMN_SELECTION' and the trailing 30-day window; the four clustering-decision subfields are read out of the operation_metrics map and rolled up per table per distinct old/new/reason/changed signature, with a last-seen end_time and event count.
Caveats & gotchas
  • Source is Public Preview: requires Unity Catalog + Predictive Optimization enabled (Enterprise tier), is regional, and has ~180-day retention.
  • Only managed UC tables are PO-eligible; external tables and views never appear here.
  • Empty result is valid, not an error: PO off, feature/region not enabled, or no column-selection ran in-window all yield zero rows; an unprovisioned schema throws TABLE_OR_VIEW_NOT_FOUND.
  • Fixed 30-day trailing window: start_time >= current_date() - INTERVAL 30 DAYS AND < current_date(); today is intentionally excluded, and history beyond the ~180-day retention is unavailable.
  • Grain is per table PER distinct (old_clustering_columns, new_clustering_columns, additional_reason, has_column_selection_changed) combination, so one churning table can show several rows — not one row per table.
  • Operation_status is NOT filtered, so both successful and failed column-selection events are counted in selection_event_count.
  • All `operation_metrics` values are strings (map<string,string>); these four subfields are categorical, not numeric, and are read without any CAST.
  • `old_clustering_columns` = 'None' means the table was previously unpartitioned/unclustered, not missing data.
  • This query reads no DBU/cost: it only reports the selection decision, not what clustering cost.
  • Rows carry `table_id` (stable identity); `table_name` can be renamed or reused, so trust the id.
→
What you do next
Find tables where `has_column_selection_changed` is `true` with high `selection_event_count` (the auto-clusterer keeps re-picking keys). For those, review the workload's join/filter patterns and pin the clustering columns manually with `ALTER TABLE ... CLUSTER BY` so the layout stops thrashing; leave stable tables (change = `false`) alone.
← PreviousLiquid Clustering Work and Costpo_clustering_activity All Storage & Optimization ↑ Next →Data-Skipping Stats Backfill Trackerpo_data_skipping_backfill
Overview / Storage & Optimization / po_data_skipping_backfill
04
Storage & Optimization po_data_skipping_backfill

Data-Skipping Stats Backfill Tracker

One row per managed table and distinct data-skipping column-change set, showing which stat columns Predictive Optimization added, removed, or newly indexed for pruning, plus the bytes and files it scanned to do so, over the trailing 30 days.

system.storage.predictive_optimization_operations_history
queries/storage/po_data_skipping_backfill.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:po_data_skipping_backfill */
SELECT catalog_name, schema_name, table_id, table_name,
       operation_metrics['added_data_skipping_columns']   AS added_data_skipping_columns,
       operation_metrics['removed_data_skipping_columns'] AS removed_data_skipping_columns,
       operation_metrics['new_data_skipping_columns']     AS new_data_skipping_columns,
       SUM(CAST(operation_metrics['amount_of_scanned_bytes'] AS BIGINT)) AS scanned_bytes,
       SUM(CAST(operation_metrics['number_of_scanned_files'] AS BIGINT)) AS scanned_files,
       MAX(end_time) AS last_event_time
FROM system.storage.predictive_optimization_operations_history
WHERE operation_type = 'DATA_SKIPPING_COLUMN_SELECTION'
  AND start_time >= current_date() - INTERVAL 30 DAYS AND start_time < current_date()
GROUP BY catalog_name, schema_name, table_id, table_name,
         operation_metrics['added_data_skipping_columns'], operation_metrics['removed_data_skipping_columns'],
         operation_metrics['new_data_skipping_columns']
Why it matters

Data skipping is what lets Databricks prune files at query time instead of scanning whole tables, so knowing that Predictive Optimization picked the right stat columns is the difference between a full-table scan and a targeted read on your most expensive queries. This view shows which columns PO chose to add or drop, which is the first thing to check when a table's queries suddenly cost more or a dashboard slows down. If the same table keeps having columns removed and re-added, the auto-selection is thrashing and you may be paying repeatedly to rebuild stats that never settle. It does not prove pruning improved -- and because it does not filter operation_status, a listed decision may even have failed -- but it tells you whether the stats selection is being maintained at all.

What it does
  • Lists managed tables where Predictive Optimization made a data-skipping column-selection decision in the last 30 days, one line per distinct set of column changes.
  • For each, shows which stat columns were added, removed, and the resulting new set of data-skipping columns.
  • Totals the bytes and files scanned to compute those stats, so you can see how heavy the work was per table.
  • Stamps each with the most recent event time. Note it reflects the column-selection decision itself (including any that failed), not proof that query pruning improved.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
catalog_nameUnity Catalog catalog the table lives in.
schema_nameSchema (database) within the catalog.
table_idStable UC table identifier -- the reliable key to join or track a table across renames.
table_nameHuman-readable table name; can be renamed or reused over time, so treat table_id as the true identity.
added_data_skipping_columnsColumn(s) newly given data-skipping stats in this selection (string list). Empty/None means nothing was added this round.
removed_data_skipping_columnsColumn(s) whose data-skipping stats were dropped (string list). Recurring removals signal auto-selection churn.
new_data_skipping_columnsThe full resulting set of columns now carrying data-skipping stats after this decision (string list).
scanned_bytesSUM of amount_of_scanned_bytes across the grouped ops, in BYTES (not DBUs, not dollars).
scanned_filesSUM of number_of_scanned_files across the grouped ops -- an integer file count.
last_event_timeMAX(end_time) -- timestamp of the most recent column-selection event in this group; how current the decision is.
Sample output
catalog_nameschema_nametable_idtable_nameadded_data_skipping_columnsremoved_data_skipping_columnsnew_data_skipping_columnsscanned_bytesscanned_fileslast_event_time
prodsalesa1f3c8e0-2b44-4d91-9a77-0e51d2c6b8aaorders[order_date, customer_id][][order_date, customer_id, region]4831838208012402026-07-05 03:12:44
prodtelemetry7c9e10b2-5f88-4a3e-bb21-9d4407e1f2c3events_raw[event_ts][][event_ts]96636764165122026-07-04 22:47:09
analyticsfinance3d2b6f14-8c07-4e59-9f10-6a1b2c7d0e45ledger_txn[][legacy_region][account_id, txn_ts]1073741824642026-07-02 06:05:31
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.storage.predictive_optimization_operations_historyThe sole source: the account-wide audit log of Predictive Optimization maintenance operations. This query keeps only DATA_SKIPPING_COLUMN_SELECTION rows (any operation_status), reads the added/removed/new data-skipping column lists as strings, and casts the scanned-bytes (amount_of_scanned_bytes) and scanned-files (number_of_scanned_files) counters from the operation_metrics map to BIGINT. The 30-day window filters on start_time; last_event_time is MAX(end_time).
Caveats & gotchas
  • Public Preview, and gated: requires Unity Catalog with Predictive Optimization enabled (Enterprise tier); only MANAGED UC tables are PO-eligible, so external tables never appear.
  • Regional feature with ~180-day source retention; a longer look-back would silently truncate, though this query is fixed at 30 days.
  • Empty != zero != error: if PO is off, nothing ran, or no DATA_SKIPPING_COLUMN_SELECTION op occurred in-window, you get a valid zero-row result; an unprovisioned schema throws TABLE_OR_VIEW_NOT_FOUND instead.
  • operation_status is NOT filtered, so a FAILED column-selection decision appears alongside successful ones -- the query shows that a selection was attempted, not that stats definitively landed; confirm status separately before treating a row as a completed backfill.
  • scanned_bytes is bytes and scanned_files is a file count -- neither is DBUs nor dollars; this query does not select usage_quantity or any cost column.
  • This confirms only that a column-selection decision occurred, NOT that pruning improved -- the actual data-skipping / prune ratio lives in system.query.history and is not joinable here.
  • operation_metrics is map<string,string>; the byte/file values (amount_of_scanned_bytes, number_of_scanned_files) are cast to BIGINT while the column-list fields stay strings. Each operation_type populates a disjoint key set, so the query filters to DATA_SKIPPING_COLUMN_SELECTION before reading its keys.
  • Fixed 30-day trailing window filtered on start_time (start_time >= current_date() - 30 days AND < current_date()); today is intentionally excluded. Note the window keys on start_time while last_event_time is MAX(end_time).
  • table_id is the stable identity across rename/reuse; table_name can be reused, so key on table_id. Both table_id and table_name (plus each distinct added/removed/new column set) are grouping keys, so one table can produce multiple rows.
  • A 6th documented subfield, old_data_skipping_columns, exists but is deliberately not selected here -- a manifest-consistency note, not a correctness issue.
→
What you do next
For each table with churning stat columns (a non-empty `removed_data_skipping_columns` or repeated `new_data_skipping_columns`), confirm a data-skipping column-selection decision ran, then pivot to `system.query.history` for the same tables to check whether the added columns actually improved prune ratio -- pin `delta.dataSkippingStatsColumns` manually if the auto-selection is thrashing. Because this query does not filter `operation_status`, cross-check status before assuming a listed decision actually completed.
← PreviousClustering Key Churn Detectorpo_clustering_column_churn All Storage & Optimization ↑ Next →Predictive Optimization Cost & Coveragepo_maintenance_cost_by_table
Overview / Storage & Optimization / po_maintenance_cost_by_table
05
Storage & Optimization po_maintenance_cost_by_table

Predictive Optimization Cost & Coverage

One row per table x maintenance-type x outcome over the last 30 days, giving the operation count, summed estimated maintenance DBUs, and first/last op times.

system.storage.predictive_optimization_operations_history
queries/storage/po_maintenance_cost_by_table.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:po_maintenance_cost_by_table */
SELECT account_id, workspace_id, metastore_name, catalog_name, schema_name, table_id, table_name,
       operation_type, operation_status, usage_unit,
       COUNT(*)                                  AS operation_count,
       SUM(CAST(usage_quantity AS DECIMAL(38,6))) AS estimated_dbu,
       MIN(start_time) AS first_op_time, MAX(end_time) AS last_op_time
FROM system.storage.predictive_optimization_operations_history
WHERE start_time >= current_date() - INTERVAL 30 DAYS
  AND start_time < current_date()
GROUP BY account_id, workspace_id, metastore_name, catalog_name, schema_name, table_id, table_name,
         operation_type, operation_status, usage_unit
Why it matters

This is the master view of what automatic table maintenance is actually costing you: it turns Predictive Optimization's background OPTIMIZE, clustering, and VACUUM work into a summed DBU line per table so you can see where maintenance spend concentrates. It simultaneously exposes reliability, because splitting each table by `operation_status` surfaces tables whose maintenance ops are landing as `FAILED: INTERNAL_ERROR` rather than succeeding. A table stuck in repeated failures is silently accumulating un-optimized files and storage bloat, while a table with runaway DBUs is a cost outlier worth investigating. The DBU figure is an estimate for triage, not the billed charge.

What it does
  • Rolls up Predictive Optimization's automatic maintenance runs into one line per table, per operation type, per outcome across a fixed trailing 30-day window (excluding today).
  • Reports, for each of those slices, how many operations ran and their total estimated DBU consumption.
  • Separates each table by outcome, so SUCCESSFUL vs. FAILED: INTERNAL_ERROR maintenance is visible side by side for a per-table success-rate read.
  • Stamps each slice with its first and last operation times so you can tell active tables from stale ones.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
account_idDatabricks account UUID that owns the maintenance activity.
workspace_idWorkspace the operation was attributed to; join keys elsewhere are workspace-scoped.
metastore_nameUnity Catalog metastore hosting the table.
catalog_nameUC catalog the table lives in.
schema_nameUC schema (database) the table lives in.
table_idStable UC identity of the table; use this over the name, since names can be renamed or reused.
table_nameHuman-readable table name at the time of the op (not guaranteed stable).
operation_typeThe maintenance kind rolled up in this row: e.g. `CLUSTERING`, `VACUUM`, `AUTO_CLUSTERING_COLUMN_SELECTION`, `DATA_SKIPPING_COLUMN_SELECTION` (and OPTIMIZE/compaction).
operation_statusOutcome for this slice; observed values are `SUCCESSFUL` or `FAILED: INTERNAL_ERROR` (note the embedded colon). Compare counts across statuses for the same table to read its success rate.
usage_unitUnit of the summed quantity; here always `ESTIMATED_DBU` — an estimate, not the billed unit.
operation_countNumber of maintenance operations (COUNT(*)) in this table/type/status slice over the window.
estimated_dbuSum of estimated DBUs (not dollars) consumed by those operations; a triage cost line to reconcile against `system.billing.usage`.
first_op_timeEarliest operation start_time in this slice — when maintenance of this kind first ran in-window.
last_op_timeLatest operation end_time in this slice — recency signal; stale tables lag here.
Sample output
catalog_nameschema_nametable_nameoperation_typeoperation_statusoperation_countestimated_dbuusage_unit
prod_salescuratedorders_factCLUSTERINGSUCCESSFUL42318.740000ESTIMATED_DBU
prod_salescuratedorders_factVACUUMSUCCESSFUL654.120000ESTIMATED_DBU
prod_salesrawevents_streamCLUSTERINGFAILED: INTERNAL_ERROR1127.500000ESTIMATED_DBU
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.storage.predictive_optimization_operations_historyThe sole source: the account-wide audit log of every automatic Predictive Optimization / clustering / VACUUM maintenance operation on Unity Catalog managed tables, each stamped with an estimated DBU cost (`usage_quantity` in `ESTIMATED_DBU`), an outcome (`operation_status`), and a start/end window. The query aggregates its per-operation grain up to per-table, per-op-type, per-status.
Caveats & gotchas
  • Availability: Public Preview, requires Unity Catalog with Predictive Optimization enabled (Enterprise-tier); only managed UC tables are PO-eligible, so external tables never appear. Regional feature with ~180-day retention.
  • `estimated_dbu` is DBUs, not dollars, and is `ESTIMATED_DBU` — an estimate to SUM for triage, not the billed charge; reconcile against `system.billing.usage` for authoritative spend.
  • Empty != zero != error: if PO is off, nothing ran in-window, or the region/feature isn't enabled, expect a valid zero-row result; if the schema isn't provisioned you may get `TABLE_OR_VIEW_NOT_FOUND`.
  • Fixed 30-day trailing window: `start_time >= current_date() - INTERVAL 30 DAYS AND start_time < current_date()`. The guard drops today but keeps yesterday — treat the most recent included day as provisional, since `usage_quantity` can lag its operation row by up to ~24h.
  • `operation_status` includes the literal `'FAILED: INTERNAL_ERROR'` with an embedded colon; match it exactly when filtering.
  • The 30-day window sits well inside the ~180-day source retention, so no truncation here; widening it past retention would silently truncate.
  • Results reflect only what the collector principal can read; needs `SELECT` on `system.storage`.
→
What you do next
Sort by `estimated_dbu` to find your costliest maintenance tables, then filter to `operation_status = 'FAILED: INTERNAL_ERROR'` to catch tables whose PO ops are failing — investigate those (they accrue un-optimized files and storage bloat) and reconcile the top DBU consumers against billed spend before acting.
← PreviousData-Skipping Stats Backfill Trackerpo_data_skipping_backfill All Storage & Optimization ↑ Next →VACUUM Storage Reclaimed Per Tablepo_vacuum_reclaimed_bytes
Overview / Storage & Optimization / po_vacuum_reclaimed_bytes
06
Storage & Optimization po_vacuum_reclaimed_bytes

VACUUM Storage Reclaimed Per Table

One row per managed table showing how many successful VACUUM operations ran in the last 30 days and how many files, bytes, and estimated DBUs they reclaimed.

system.storage.predictive_optimization_operations_history
queries/storage/po_vacuum_reclaimed_bytes.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:po_vacuum_reclaimed_bytes */
SELECT catalog_name, schema_name, table_id, table_name,
       COUNT(*) AS vacuum_op_count,
       SUM(CAST(operation_metrics['number_of_deleted_files']      AS BIGINT))  AS total_deleted_files,
