       SUM(CAST(operation_metrics['amount_of_data_deleted_bytes'] AS BIGINT))  AS total_deleted_bytes,
       SUM(CAST(usage_quantity AS DECIMAL(38,6)))                              AS vacuum_estimated_dbu
FROM system.storage.predictive_optimization_operations_history
WHERE operation_type = 'VACUUM' AND operation_status = 'SUCCESSFUL'
  AND start_time >= current_date() - INTERVAL 30 DAYS AND start_time < current_date()
GROUP BY catalog_name, schema_name, table_id, table_name
Why it matters

VACUUM is what actually deletes stale time-travel and tombstoned data files from your Delta tables, and reclaimed bytes translate directly into cloud object-storage dollars you stop paying every month. This view quantifies how much bloat Predictive Optimization's automatic VACUUM genuinely removed per table, and — read alongside a table's remaining `vacuumable_bytes` — exposes tables where reclaimable waste is piling up because VACUUM rarely or never runs. Databricks has no fail-safe, so storage never cleaned here is storage you keep renting indefinitely.

What it does
  • Lists every managed table that had at least one successful VACUUM in the trailing 30 days, identified by its stable table_id alongside its catalog, schema, and name.
  • Reports per table how many VACUUM operations succeeded and the total deleted files and reclaimed bytes those runs freed up.
  • Sums the estimated DBU each table's VACUUM activity consumed, so you can weigh maintenance cost against storage recovered.
  • Covers only PO-managed VACUUM over a fixed 30-day window and excludes today; tables with no successful VACUUM simply do not appear.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
catalog_nameUnity Catalog catalog the VACUUMed table lives in.
schema_nameSchema (database) within that catalog.
table_idStable UC identifier for the table — use this to join to inventory or storage-size probes, not the name.
table_nameTable name at the time of the operation; can be renamed or reused, so treat `table_id` as the true identity.
vacuum_op_countNumber of successful VACUUM operations on this table in the window; zero-count tables are absent entirely, and a low count on a large table hints VACUUM is lagging.
total_deleted_filesTotal data files physically removed by those VACUUM runs — a file-level measure of cleanup volume.
total_deleted_bytesTotal bytes reclaimed (freed from object storage) across all successful VACUUMs — the headline storage-savings figure; bytes, not dollars.
vacuum_estimated_dbuSummed **ESTIMATED_DBU** these VACUUM ops consumed — DBUs (not dollars, not billed spend), an estimate that can lag its operation row by up to ~24h.
Sample output
catalog_nameschema_nametable_idtable_namevacuum_op_counttotal_deleted_filestotal_deleted_bytesvacuum_estimated_dbu
prod_analyticseventsa1b2c3d4-1111-4a2b-9c3d-0e1f2a3b4c5dclickstream_raw124821018976543200006.482100
prod_analyticsfinanceb2c3d4e5-2222-4b3c-8d4e-1f2a3b4c5d6eledger_snapshots3920412873400000.734000
sandboxscratchc3d4e5f6-3333-4c4d-7e5f-2a3b4c5d6e7fmodel_features_v215712048800000.041500
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.storage.predictive_optimization_operations_historyThe sole source: the account-wide audit log of Predictive Optimization maintenance operations. This query keeps only successful `VACUUM` rows in the trailing 30-day window, reads the two documented VACUUM counters out of the `operation_metrics` map (deleted-file count and reclaimed-byte total), and sums the estimated DBU (`usage_quantity`) per table.
Caveats & gotchas
  • Public Preview: requires Unity Catalog with Predictive Optimization enabled (Enterprise tier); regional feature with ~180-day source retention. Reader needs `SELECT` on `system.storage`.
  • Managed UC tables only — external tables are never PO-eligible and never appear, so this cannot cover them.
  • Empty is not broken: if PO is off, nothing VACUUMed in-window, or the account never ran VACUUM, you get a valid zero-row result; if the schema isn't provisioned you may get `TABLE_OR_VIEW_NOT_FOUND`.
  • `vacuum_estimated_dbu` is ESTIMATED_DBU (DBUs, not dollars, not the billed line) — reconcile against `system.billing.usage` for authoritative spend.
  • Fixed 30-day trailing window that intentionally excludes today (whose DBU is still populating); treat the most recent included day as provisional due to up to ~24h billing lag. Windows beyond ~180 days silently truncate.
  • `operation_metrics` is a `map<string,string>` — the two VACUUM keys (`number_of_deleted_files`, `amount_of_data_deleted_bytes`) are cast to BIGINT; these are the only two documented VACUUM subfields.
  • Reclaimed bytes here measure only what VACUUM already removed — to find unreclaimed waste, pair `total_deleted_bytes` against a table's current `vacuumable_bytes` from the on-demand `storage_breakdown_analyze` probe (DBR 18.0+), which is not in any system table.
  • Only `operation_status = 'SUCCESSFUL'` rows count; failed VACUUMs (`'FAILED: INTERNAL_ERROR'`) are excluded, so this is reclaimed storage, not attempted.
→
What you do next
For each table, compare `total_deleted_bytes` (what VACUUM actually reclaimed over 30 days) against the table's current `vacuumable_bytes` from the `storage_breakdown_analyze` probe: a high remaining vacuumable figure with few or zero recent VACUUM ops flags unreclaimed storage bloat where VACUUM is lagging or not running — schedule/verify PO VACUUM or a manual VACUUM there.
← PreviousPredictive Optimization Cost & Coveragepo_maintenance_cost_by_table All Storage & Optimization ↑ Next →Real Table Size Breakdownstorage_breakdown_analyze
Overview / Storage & Optimization / storage_breakdown_analyze
07
Storage & Optimization storage_breakdown_analyze

Real Table Size Breakdown

A single row of one Delta table's true on-disk footprint, split into active data, time-travel history, and reclaimable (vacuumable) bytes and file counts.

queries/storage/storage_breakdown_analyze.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:storage_breakdown_analyze */
-- On-demand, per-table; NOT a system table and NOT persisted to UC. Engine runs this per target
-- table (Deep tier) on a DBR 18.0+ warehouse, then reads the single-row result.
ANALYZE TABLE main.sales.orders COMPUTE STORAGE METRICS;
-- Result columns to capture: total_bytes, num_total_files, active_bytes, num_active_files,
--   vacuumable_bytes, num_vacuumable_files, time_travel_bytes, num_time_travel_files
Why it matters

This is the only way to learn what a Delta table actually costs to store — Unity Catalog system tables carry no physical size, so without it you are guessing. It separates live data you need from time-travel history and dead files still on disk, letting you see how much of your storage bill is bloat rather than useful data. Pair `vacuumable_bytes` with your storage rate to put a dollar figure on unreclaimed waste, and use `time_travel_bytes` to decide whether retention settings are quietly inflating spend.

What it does
  • Returns one row of the physical size of a single Delta table, run on demand — it is not a system-table query and keeps no history.
  • Splits the footprint into active (live data), time-travel (retained old versions), and vacuumable (dead files a VACUUM could delete) bytes, plus a total.
  • Reports a matching file count alongside each byte figure, so you see both size and small-file pressure.
  • Gives you the raw size numbers to quantify time-travel bloat and dollarize reclaimable storage against your cloud storage rate.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
total_bytesTotal on-disk size of the table in bytes, across all versions and files (active + time-travel + vacuumable). This is the number that maps to your storage bill.
num_total_filesTotal count of data files backing the table. Very high counts relative to size signal small-file bloat that OPTIMIZE/compaction would help.
active_bytesBytes of live, current-version data — what a normal read of the table actually needs. Everything above this is history or dead files.
num_active_filesNumber of files holding the active (current) data.
vacuumable_bytesBytes of dead/tombstoned files that a VACUUM could physically delete right now — this is directly reclaimable waste. Multiply by your storage rate to dollarize it.
num_vacuumable_filesCount of files eligible for reclamation by VACUUM.
time_travel_bytesBytes retained purely to support time travel / rollback within the retention window. Shrinks only if you lower deletedFileRetentionDuration/logRetentionDuration.
num_time_travel_filesNumber of files held for time-travel history.
Sample output
total_bytesnum_total_filesactive_bytesnum_active_filesvacuumable_bytesnum_vacuumable_filestime_travel_bytesnum_time_travel_files
120000000001200800000000080010000000001003000000000300
540000000455000000004000400000005
960000000009600200000000001200560000000006400200000000002000
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
Caveats & gotchas
  • Not a system table: this is an on-demand `ANALYZE TABLE ... COMPUTE STORAGE METRICS` probe run per target table on a warehouse; the result is not stored in UC and not returned by `DESCRIBE EXTENDED`.
  • No history or trend: it is computed at run time only. The collection engine must run it per-table and persist the single-row result itself (Deep tier only).
  • Availability gate: GA but requires **DBR 18.0+**. On older runtimes the statement is unavailable.
  • Bytes, not dollars: all `*_bytes` columns are raw storage bytes — multiply by your cloud storage rate to estimate cost; no price is implied by the query.
  • Scope: returns exactly one row for one Delta table (example uses `main.sales.orders`); there is no aggregation across tables.
  • No fail-safe and no clone bytes are represented in these figures.
  • Empty != zero != error: when the metric is not collected, degrade the finding to 'not assessed - storage size not in system tables; requires ANALYZE / DBR 18.0+' rather than reporting zero.
→
What you do next
If `vacuumable_bytes` is large, run VACUUM to reclaim that storage immediately; if `time_travel_bytes` dominates, review and tighten `deletedFileRetentionDuration`/`logRetentionDuration`; if `num_total_files` is high relative to size, schedule OPTIMIZE/compaction.
← PreviousVACUUM Storage Reclaimed Per Tablepo_vacuum_reclaimed_bytes All Storage & Optimization ↑ Next →Table Inventory by Type and Formattable_inventory_type
Overview / Storage & Optimization / table_inventory_type
08
Storage & Optimization table_inventory_type

Table Inventory by Type and Format

A catalog/schema-level census of every table and view the collector can see, counted by managed/external/view type and by data source format.

system.information_schema.tables
queries/storage/table_inventory_type.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:table_inventory_type */
-- NEEDS CONFIRMATION: only table_type is doc-confirmed; table_catalog/table_schema/data_source_format
-- are plausible information_schema names but UNVERIFIED.
SELECT table_catalog, table_schema, table_type, data_source_format,
       COUNT(*) AS table_count
FROM system.information_schema.tables
GROUP BY table_catalog, table_schema, table_type, data_source_format
Why it matters

This is the baseline map of your Unity Catalog estate: it tells you how many tables live where and, critically, how many are **EXTERNAL** rather than **MANAGED**. Only managed tables are Predictive Optimization-eligible, so the EXTERNAL count directly bounds the PO coverage gap — the slice of your data that automatic OPTIMIZE/VACUUM/clustering will never touch and whose maintenance you must plan and pay for yourself. The `data_source_format` split also flags **ICEBERG**/UniForm candidates that need different handling. Because the view is privilege-aware, the counts also reveal how much of the metastore the collector principal can actually see.

What it does
  • Produces an inventory census of Unity Catalog: how many tables and views exist, broken down by catalog and schema.
  • Splits every group by table type so you can read off MANAGED vs EXTERNAL vs VIEW counts — the EXTERNAL rows are your PO coverage gap.
  • Further splits by data source format (e.g. DELTA vs ICEBERG) to surface Iceberg/UniForm candidates.
  • Carries no size or cost data — it is a headcount of objects, not their bytes.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
table_catalogThe Unity Catalog catalog the group of tables lives in. Column name is plausible but UNVERIFIED in this repo's doc audit — a real run confirms it resolves.
table_schemaThe schema (database) within the catalog. Also an UNVERIFIED information_schema column name pending a customer run.
table_typeMANAGED, EXTERNAL, or VIEW (doc-confirmed). EXTERNAL tables are not PO-eligible; VIEW rows are logical objects with no storage of their own.
data_source_formatThe storage format, e.g. DELTA or ICEBERG, used to flag Iceberg/UniForm candidates. Presence and exact name are UNVERIFIED. May be NULL/blank for views or formats that don't report it.
table_countNumber of tables/views in that catalog + schema + type + format group. An integer object count, not bytes and not cost.
Sample output
table_catalogtable_schematable_typedata_source_formattable_count
prod_analyticssalesMANAGEDDELTA142
prod_analyticssalesEXTERNALICEBERG17
prod_analyticsreportingVIEWNULL23
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.information_schema.tablesThe Unity Catalog catalog-of-tables view; one row per table or view the querying principal has privileges on. This query reads its table_catalog, table_schema, table_type, and data_source_format columns and counts rows into per-group inventory totals.
Caveats & gotchas
  • Requires Unity Catalog; information_schema is a standard UC SQL view exposed under system.information_schema for the metastore.
  • Privilege-aware: a principal sees only tables it has privileges on, so counts reflect the collector's grants, not necessarily the whole metastore.
  • Only table_type (MANAGED/EXTERNAL/VIEW) is doc-confirmed; table_catalog, table_schema, and data_source_format are plausible but UNVERIFIED information_schema names — the customer's run validates whether they resolve.
  • Carries NO size columns — table bytes require the on-demand ANALYZE ... COMPUTE STORAGE METRICS probe (storage_breakdown_analyze), not this query.
  • Empty != broken: a partial or empty result means the principal lacks broad grants or the metastore isn't UC, not that tables don't exist; TABLE_OR_VIEW_NOT_FOUND means the view isn't provisioned.
  • This is a full-inventory snapshot at run time — no date window and no DBU/cost involved; table_count is an object count, never dollars.
  • EXTERNAL tables never appear in the PO history table, so use this EXTERNAL count to size the PO coverage gap rather than expecting PO rows for them.
→
What you do next
Read off the EXTERNAL vs MANAGED split to quantify how much of your estate is outside Predictive Optimization's reach, then plan manual maintenance (or migration to managed) for those tables; use the ICEBERG rows to shortlist UniForm/Iceberg candidates for the iceberg_uniform_metadata probe. If key columns come back unresolved on the real run, treat the column names as needing correction before relying on the breakdown.
← PreviousReal Table Size Breakdownstorage_breakdown_analyze All Storage & Optimization ↑ Next →Delta Time-Travel & Optimize Settingstable_props_time_travel_config
Overview / Storage & Optimization / table_props_time_travel_config
09
Storage & Optimization table_props_time_travel_config

Delta Time-Travel & Optimize Settings

For one managed Delta table, the seven Delta properties that govern time-travel retention, auto-optimize, data-skipping indexing, and deletion vectors, each with its effective value or default.

queries/storage/table_props_time_travel_config.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:table_props_time_travel_config */
-- Per-table; Delta properties are NOT in a system table. Engine runs DESCRIBE EXTENDED
-- (or SHOW TBLPROPERTIES) per managed Delta table and parses the property rows.
--   delta.logRetentionDuration            (default 'interval 30 days')
--   delta.deletedFileRetentionDuration    (default 'interval 1 week')
--   delta.dataSkippingNumIndexedCols      (default 32)
--   delta.dataSkippingStatsColumns        (default none)
--   delta.autoOptimize.optimizeWrite
--   delta.autoOptimize.autoCompact
--   delta.enableDeletionVectors
DESCRIBE EXTENDED main.sales.orders;
Why it matters

Over-long `delta.logRetentionDuration` and `delta.deletedFileRetentionDuration` are the most common silent cause of Delta storage bloat: every extra day of retention keeps deleted/superseded files on disk (and out of reach of VACUUM), inflating your storage bill for time-travel you may never use. This probe is the only place to see those retention windows plus whether auto-optimize (write-side compaction), data-skipping indexing, and deletion vectors are actually configured on a table. Reading it tells an admin whether a table is set up to stay compact and prunable, or quietly accumulating cost.

What it does
  • Reports the two time-travel retention windows for one managed Delta table, so you can spot retention set far longer than needed (the driver of storage bloat).
  • Shows the auto-optimize posture (optimizeWrite, autoCompact) — whether Databricks compacts small files on write for this table.
  • Shows data-skipping coverage: how many leading columns are indexed for stats and any explicitly chosen stats columns, which govern query pruning.
  • Reports whether deletion vectors are enabled (soft-deletes instead of file rewrites), whose default is runtime/workspace-dependent rather than fixed.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
delta.logRetentionDurationHow long the Delta transaction-log history is kept, as a CalendarInterval string like `interval 30 days` (default). Parse to days; longer = more time-travel but more log bloat.
delta.deletedFileRetentionDurationHow long deleted/superseded data files are retained before VACUUM may remove them, e.g. `interval 1 week` (default). Long values keep dead files on disk and are the main storage-bloat lever.
delta.dataSkippingNumIndexedColsNumber of leading columns for which min/max stats are collected for data-skipping (default 32). Fewer indexed columns can weaken query pruning.
delta.dataSkippingStatsColumnsExplicit list of columns to collect data-skipping stats on (default none/unset). When set, it overrides the leading-N-columns behavior.
delta.autoOptimize.optimizeWriteWhether write-side optimized writes are on (`true`/`false`), producing fewer, larger files at write time. Unset means inherit runtime default.
delta.autoOptimize.autoCompactWhether automatic post-write compaction of small files is enabled (`true`/`false`). Unset means inherit runtime default.
delta.enableDeletionVectorsWhether deletion vectors (soft-delete markers instead of file rewrites) are enabled. No fixed default — depends on workspace/DBR runtime, so treat blank as runtime-determined, not off.
Sample output
delta.logRetentionDurationdelta.deletedFileRetentionDurationdelta.dataSkippingNumIndexedColsdelta.autoOptimize.optimizeWritedelta.enableDeletionVectors
interval 30 daysinterval 7 days32truetrue
interval 365 daysinterval 90 days32falsefalse
interval 30 daysinterval 7 days8true(unset)
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
Caveats & gotchas
  • NOT a system table: this runs `DESCRIBE EXTENDED <catalog>.<schema>.<table>` (or `SHOW TBLPROPERTIES`) on a SQL warehouse, per target table — there is no history or trend, only the current state at run time.
  • Per-table only: you must point it at one managed Delta table at a time; it does not scan the account. The shipped SQL targets `main.sales.orders` as a placeholder — edit the table name.
  • Retention values are CalendarInterval strings (e.g. `interval 30 days`), not integers — parse them to days before comparing or alerting.
  • Empty != zero != off: a property absent from output means it is unset and inheriting the runtime/workspace default, not that it is disabled — especially `delta.enableDeletionVectors`, which has no fixed default.
  • DBR 18.0+ constraint: on newer runtimes `logRetentionDuration` must be >= `deletedFileRetentionDuration`; flag tables that violate this.
  • Requires the querying principal to have access to the table; only managed Delta tables carry the full property set.
→
What you do next
For any table where `deletedFileRetentionDuration` or `logRetentionDuration` is far longer than your recovery needs, lower it with `ALTER TABLE … SET TBLPROPERTIES` and run VACUUM to reclaim the newly-eligible dead files; while there, confirm auto-optimize and data-skipping are configured to keep the table compact and prunable.
← PreviousTable Inventory by Type and Formattable_inventory_type All Storage & Optimization ↑ Next →Run-As Privilege Escalation Watchaccess_runas_escalation
Overview / Governance, Access & Security / access_runas_escalation
01
Governance, Access & Security access_runas_escalation ★ first-audit pick

Run-As Privilege Escalation Watch

A 30-day rollup of audited actions where the initiating principal (run_by) differs from the identity the action executed as (run_as), aggregated per service, action, and masked identity pair with event counts and first/last timestamps.

system.access.audit
queries/governance_access/access_runas_escalation.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:access_runas_escalation */
SELECT service_name, action_name,
       CASE
         WHEN identity_metadata.run_by IS NULL OR identity_metadata.run_by = '__REDACTED__' THEN identity_metadata.run_by
         WHEN identity_metadata.run_by LIKE '%@%' THEN concat(substr(identity_metadata.run_by, 1, 2), '****@****')
         WHEN identity_metadata.run_by RLIKE '^[0-9a-fA-F]{8}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{12}$' THEN identity_metadata.run_by
         ELSE concat(substr(identity_metadata.run_by, 1, 2), '****')
       END AS run_by,
       CASE
         WHEN identity_metadata.run_as IS NULL OR identity_metadata.run_as = '__REDACTED__' THEN identity_metadata.run_as
         WHEN identity_metadata.run_as LIKE '%@%' THEN concat(substr(identity_metadata.run_as, 1, 2), '****@****')
         WHEN identity_metadata.run_as RLIKE '^[0-9a-fA-F]{8}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{12}$' THEN identity_metadata.run_as
         ELSE concat(substr(identity_metadata.run_as, 1, 2), '****')
       END AS run_as,
       COUNT(*) AS event_count,
       MIN(event_time) AS first_event_time, MAX(event_time) AS last_event_time
FROM system.access.audit
WHERE identity_metadata.run_by IS NOT NULL
  AND identity_metadata.run_as IS NOT NULL
  AND identity_metadata.run_by <> identity_metadata.run_as
  AND event_date >= current_date() - INTERVAL 30 DAYS
  AND event_date < current_date()
GROUP BY 1, 2, identity_metadata.run_by, identity_metadata.run_as
Why it matters

Run-as escalation is the audit fingerprint of one principal executing under another identity's authority — the exact move behind privilege abuse, lateral movement, or a compromised service principal quietly acting as a human owner. Surfacing every run_by <> run_as pair with counts and recency lets an admin separate legitimate job/service-principal execution from unauthorized impersonation before it turns into data exfiltration or unreviewed permission changes. Because the signal is genuinely sparse (identity_metadata is usually NULL for ordinary actions), each row that does appear is worth an explicit look, and an empty result must be verified rather than trusted as clean.

What it does
  • Surfaces every audited action where the identity that triggered it differs from the identity it ran as — the fingerprint of run-as escalation.
  • Rolls each run-by → run-as pair up with how many times it happened and when it was first and last seen.
  • Masks the identities, and — because the signal is naturally rare — treats an empty result as 'check the audit log is on', not 'all clear'.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
service_nameThe audit service that logged the events (e.g. unityCatalog, jobs, accountsAccessControl). Grouping dimension; tells you which surface the run-as activity occurred on.
action_nameThe specific operation performed. Treat as a free-form label grouped, not a filtered enum; the same run_by/run_as pair may span multiple actions.
run_byThe principal that actually initiated the action, masked (email -> first-2-chars + '****@****' e.g. 'da****@****'; other name -> first-2-chars + '****' e.g. 'op****'; UUID / '__REDACTED__' passed through unchanged). This is the 'who triggered it'.
run_asThe identity the action executed AS, masked by the same CASE logic as run_by. run_by <> run_as by construction (WHERE clause), so this row always represents one identity acting as another.
event_countCOUNT(*) of audited events for this (service, action, run_by, run_as) combination in the window. Higher = more repeated escalation activity; a single one-off may be routine, a sustained high count warrants a look.
first_event_timeMIN(event_time) for this pair in the window. Shows when the run-as relationship first appeared.
last_event_timeMAX(event_time) for this pair. Recency signal; a last_event_time near the window end means the pattern is still active.
Sample output
service_nameaction_namerun_byrun_asevent_countfirst_event_timelast_event_time
jobsrunNowda****@****sv****@****422026-06-08 02:15:032026-07-05 02:15:11
unityCataloggetTablead****@****a1b2c3d4-5e6f-7a8b-9c0d-1e2f3a4b5c6d72026-06-19 14:02:442026-07-04 09:31:20
accountsAccessControlupdatePermissionsop****__REDACTED__12026-06-28 11:47:022026-06-28 11:47:02
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.access.auditSole source. The UC/account audit log is read for every event whose identity_metadata.run_by and run_as are both non-NULL and differ; service_name, action_name, event_time, event_date and the identity_metadata struct supply all grouping, masking, filtering, and timestamp fields.
Caveats & gotchas
  • Preview table: system.access.audit requires UC + system-table sharing enabled on the metastore and SELECT on system.access; TABLE_OR_VIEW_NOT_FOUND means not provisioned, not clean.
  • identity_metadata.run_by / run_as are commonly NULL for ordinary single-user actions, so this query is SPARSE by design across many accounts.
  • Empty != no escalation: if zero rows return, degrade to 'not assessed' and confirm the audit table is populated before treating it as an all-clear.
  • Date window is a fixed trailing 30 days via event_date, and event_date < current_date() EXCLUDES today (only complete prior days are counted).
  • Identity masking is applied: emails become first-2-chars + '****@****' (e.g. 'da****@****'), other names become first-2-chars + '****' (e.g. 'op****'), while UUIDs and '__REDACTED__' pass through unchanged; downstream you cannot recover the raw identity from the output alone.
  • ~15-minute ingest lag on audit; recent events may not yet appear (mitigated here since today is excluded).
  • action_name values are representative, not an exhaustive enum, and GROUP BY expands rows per (service, action) pair, so one escalating identity pair can appear on several rows.
  • Widen the 30-day window only as far as your workspace-configured system.access.* retention allows.
→
What you do next
Triage each masked run_by → run_as pair: confirm the run_by principal is authorized to execute as the run_as identity (expected for job/service-principal ownership) and investigate any pair that is not — starting with the highest event_count and most recent last_event_time. Because the signal is sparse, if zero rows return, verify the audit table is actually populated before concluding "no escalation."
← PreviousDelta Time-Travel & Optimize Settingstable_props_time_travel_config All Governance, Access & Security ↑ Next →Who Changed Permissions, From Whereaccess_admin_role_change_events
Overview / Governance, Access & Security / access_admin_role_change_events
02
Governance, Access & Security access_admin_role_change_events

Who Changed Permissions, From Where

A 90-day rollup of account, access-control, and Unity Catalog audit events grouped by service, action, and acting principal, showing how often each fired, from how many distinct source IPs, and the first/last time it was seen.

system.access.audit
queries/governance_access/access_admin_role_change_events.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:access_admin_role_change_events */
SELECT service_name, action_name,
       CASE
         WHEN actor IS NULL OR actor = '__REDACTED__' THEN actor
         WHEN actor LIKE '%@%' THEN concat(substr(actor, 1, 2), '****@****')
         WHEN actor RLIKE '^[0-9a-fA-F]{8}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{12}$' THEN actor
         ELSE concat(substr(actor, 1, 2), '****')
       END AS actor,
       event_count, distinct_source_ips, first_event_time, last_event_time
FROM (
  SELECT service_name, action_name,
         COALESCE(user_identity.email, user_identity.subject_name) AS actor,
         COUNT(*) AS event_count,
         COUNT(DISTINCT source_ip_address) AS distinct_source_ips,
         MIN(event_time) AS first_event_time, MAX(event_time) AS last_event_time
  FROM system.access.audit
  WHERE service_name IN ('accounts','accountsAccessControl','unityCatalog')
    AND event_date >= current_date() - INTERVAL 90 DAYS
    AND event_date < current_date()
  GROUP BY 1, 2, 3
)
Why it matters

Grant and admin-role changes are the highest-blast-radius actions in the account: one `updatePermissions` or admin grant can silently hand a principal access to sensitive data or the ability to alter other people's access. This gives an admin a defensible, time-bounded record of who altered permissions, how often, and from how many distinct network locations, so anomalous bursts or logins from unexpected IPs surface for review. It is the activity-history counterpart to the current-state grant inventory: the inventory tells you what access exists today, this tells you who moved it and when. Because it groups by the raw `action_name`, it captures the full spread of change actions rather than a hardcoded subset that could miss a renamed or newly added operation.

What it does
  • Rolls up the last 90 days of audit events from the account, account access-control, and Unity Catalog services into one row per service + action + acting principal.
  • Counts how many times each combination fired (event_count) and from how many distinct source IPs (distinct_source_ips) — so a normal actor and an actor spraying from many IPs look different.
  • Timestamps each combination's activity window with its first and last occurrence, so you can see whether a change action is a one-off or ongoing.
  • Masks actor emails and non-UUID names so the output is safe to share, while passing UUID principals and redactions through unchanged.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
service_nameThe audit service that emitted the event — one of accounts, accountsAccessControl, or unityCatalog. Tells you which control surface the change touched.
action_nameThe specific operation recorded (e.g. an update-permissions or set-admin action). Treat this as representative, not an exhaustive enum — read whatever values appear; do not assume a fixed list.
actorThe acting principal (email, else subject_name), masked: emails shown as first-two-chars + ****@****, other names as first-two-chars + ****, UUID principals and __REDACTED__ passed through as-is. May be NULL when the audit event carried no identity.
event_countNumber of matching events for this service/action/actor over the window. A count, not a rate — a high value is a burst worth explaining.
distinct_source_ipsHow many distinct source IP addresses this actor used for this action. A jump here (many IPs for one principal) is an anomaly signal.
first_event_timeTimestamp of the earliest event in the window for this row — when this activity started being seen.
last_event_timeTimestamp of the most recent event for this row — how fresh the activity is; near-now with a high count means it is still happening.
Sample output
service_nameaction_nameactorevent_countdistinct_source_ipsfirst_event_timelast_event_time
accountsAccessControlupdatePermissionsda****@****4732026-04-09 08:12:312026-07-05 17:44:02
unityCatalogsetAdmin3f2504e0-4f89-41d3-9a0c-0305e82c3301212026-06-28 11:03:552026-06-30 09:21:10
accountscreateServicePrincipalad****112026-05-22 14:57:082026-05-22 14:57:08
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.access.auditThe Unity Catalog / account audit log; sole source. The query reads control-plane events from the accounts, accountsAccessControl, and unityCatalog services, taking service_name, action_name, the acting principal (user_identity.email falling back to user_identity.subject_name), source_ip_address, and event_time, windowed on the event_date partition column.
Caveats & gotchas
  • Preview table: requires Unity Catalog with system-table sharing enabled on the metastore and SELECT on system.access; a TABLE_OR_VIEW_NOT_FOUND or empty result means 'not provisioned / not enabled', not 'no changes happened'.
  • Fixed 90-day window on the event_date partition; excludes today (event_date < current_date()), so the current day's changes are not yet included.
  • ~15-minute ingest lag on system.access.audit — treat the most recent data as provisional and possibly incomplete.
  • action_name is representative-not-complete: group by it and read every value; never hardcode or filter to a fixed list like setAdmin/updatePermissions, or you will miss renamed/new actions.
  • Scope is exactly three services (accounts, accountsAccessControl, unityCatalog). Account-level events are global (workspace_id=0); workspace events are regional — this rollup does not separate them.
  • The principal field is user_identity.subject_name (verified live 2026-05-30), NOT subjectName, and is frequently NULL; actor falls back to subject_name only when email is absent (COALESCE), and can still be NULL.
  • actor is masked (email -> da****@****, other names -> xx****) with UUID principals and __REDACTED__ passed through unchanged; do not treat the masked string as the real identity when correlating.
  • This is change-event history, not current grant state — pair it with the grant inventory queries; it does not tell you what access exists now, only who moved it.
→
What you do next
Sort by event_count and distinct_source_ips to triage: for any principal with an unexpected burst of permission/admin actions or activity from an unusual number of source IPs, pull the matching events in system.access.audit and confirm the change was authorized; then reconcile the resulting access against the current-state grant inventory to ensure nothing was left over-privileged.
← PreviousRun-As Privilege Escalation Watchaccess_runas_escalation All Governance, Access & Security ↑ Next →Sensitive Columns Missing Their Maskaccess_classified_unmasked
Overview / Governance, Access & Security / access_classified_unmasked
03
Governance, Access & Security access_classified_unmasked

Sensitive Columns Missing Their Mask

A current-state list of HIGH-confidence auto-classified (PII/sensitive) columns, each showing whether a column mask is applied and flagging the ones that are left unmasked.

system.data_classification.resultssystem.information_schema.column_masks
queries/governance_access/access_classified_unmasked.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:access_classified_unmasked */
SELECT dc.catalog_name, dc.schema_name, dc.table_name, dc.column_name, dc.class_tag, dc.confidence,
       cm.MASK_NAME,
       CASE WHEN cm.MASK_NAME IS NULL THEN true ELSE false END AS is_unmasked
FROM system.data_classification.results dc
LEFT JOIN system.information_schema.column_masks cm
  ON  cm.CATALOG_NAME = dc.catalog_name
  AND cm.SCHEMA_NAME  = dc.schema_name
  AND cm.TABLE_NAME   = dc.table_name
  AND cm.COLUMN_NAME  = dc.column_name
WHERE dc.confidence = 'HIGH'
  AND dc.class_tag IS NOT NULL
Why it matters

This is the headline data-governance gap: columns the classifier is highly confident hold PII or sensitive data, yet no column mask is protecting them, so that data is being returned in the clear to anyone with `SELECT`. Each unmasked row is a candidate exposure that can drive a privacy incident, a compliance finding (GDPR/CCPA/HIPAA), or an audit deficiency. Closing these gaps is cheap relative to the cost of a breach or regulatory penalty — but only if you first confirm the miss is a real gap and not a visibility blind spot of the collecting principal.

What it does
  • Lists every column the scanner flagged as sensitive with HIGH confidence, identified down to catalog_name / schema_name / table_name / column_name and its detected class_tag.
  • For each such column, shows whether a column mask is applied by surfacing the mask's name (or blank when none was found).
  • Flags the exposure with is_unmasked — true when no mask is present, giving you the classified-but-unprotected shortlist.
  • Deliberately narrows to high-confidence, tagged detections so the list stays actionable rather than noisy.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
catalog_nameUnity Catalog catalog containing the classified column.
schema_nameSchema (database) within that catalog.
table_nameTable or view holding the column.
column_nameThe specific column the classifier flagged as sensitive.
class_tagThe detected sensitivity class (e.g. the kind of PII) the scanner assigned to this column.
confidenceScanner confidence for the detection — always `HIGH` here, since lower-confidence rows are filtered out.
MASK_NAMEName of the column mask protecting this column, or NULL/blank if no mask row was visible to the collector.
is_unmaskedtrue = classified sensitive but no mask found (the exposure to review); false = a mask is applied. Note a 'true' can also mean the mask table wasn't visible to the collecting principal, so treat it as a candidate, not a verdict.
Sample output
catalog_nameschema_nametable_namecolumn_nameclass_tagMASK_NAMEis_unmasked
prodcustomersaccountsemailemail_addressNULLtrue
prodcustomersaccountsssnus_ssnmask_ssn_defaultfalse
analyticshremployeeshome_phonephone_numberNULLtrue
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.data_classification.resultsSource of truth for sensitive columns — supplies the catalog/schema/table/column identifiers plus the detected sensitivity `class_tag` and `confidence`; filtered to HIGH-confidence, tagged detections only.
system.information_schema.column_masksCurrent-state mask inventory — matched by catalog/schema/table/column to see whether each classified column has a `MASK_NAME`; a non-match means no mask row was visible, driving the `is_unmasked` flag.
Caveats & gotchas
  • Current-state snapshot, not a time window — no date filter, no `:period_days`; reflects the metastore as of run time.
  • `column_masks` is privilege-aware and object-visibility-scoped: it excludes tables the collector can only BROWSE, so a missing mask row (hence `is_unmasked = true`) may mean 'not visible to the collecting SP,' which OVERSTATES the unmasked count — treat output as a labeled candidate list, not a proven clean/unmasked count.
  • Run collection under a high-privilege audit service principal; even then results are partial and privilege-scoped.
  • `system.data_classification.results` and `system.information_schema.column_masks` are separate feature-gated Public Previews (COLUMN_MASKS needs DBR 12.2 LTS+); data classification also requires its own `system.data_classification` schema enabled distinct from `system.access`.
  • Empty or `TABLE_OR_VIEW_NOT_FOUND` means the feature/schema isn't enabled or no HIGH-confidence classifications exist — that is 'not assessed,' NOT a clean all-clear.
  • Confirm whether `system.information_schema` covers the whole metastore or only the system catalog; a per-catalog UNION may be needed for full coverage.
  • Data classification covers enabled catalogs only and is regional with 13-month retention; unclassified columns are simply absent.
  • Join is on the four discrete identifier columns (catalog/schema/table/column) with `=`; do not substitute LIKE/CONCAT since `_` is a LIKE wildcard.
  • Only HIGH-confidence, class-tagged columns are shown; LOW-confidence detections are intentionally excluded, so the true sensitive surface may be larger.
→
What you do next
Work the `is_unmasked = true` rows: for each HIGH-confidence classified column with no mask, decide whether to apply a column mask (or confirm the collector SP simply couldn't see an existing mask), then re-run to confirm the gap closes.
← PreviousWho Changed Permissions, From Whereaccess_admin_role_change_events All Governance, Access & Security ↑ Next →Column-Level Lineage Blast Radiusaccess_column_lineage_sensitive_reach
Overview / Governance, Access & Security / access_column_lineage_sensitive_reach
04
Governance, Access & Security access_column_lineage_sensitive_reach

Column-Level Lineage Blast Radius

A 90-day rollup of observed column-to-column data flows, one row per source-column to target-column edge (also split by entity_type and direct_access), showing how many times each edge fired, how many distinct principals drove it, and when it last happened.

system.access.column_lineage
queries/governance_access/access_column_lineage_sensitive_reach.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:access_column_lineage_sensitive_reach */
SELECT source_table_full_name, source_column_name, target_table_full_name, target_column_name,
       entity_type, direct_access,
       COUNT(*) AS event_count,
       COUNT(DISTINCT created_by) AS distinct_principals,
       MAX(event_time) AS last_event_time
FROM system.access.column_lineage
WHERE source_table_full_name IS NOT NULL
  AND event_date >= current_date() - INTERVAL 90 DAYS
  AND event_date < current_date()
GROUP BY 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6
Why it matters

When a column is classified PII or is otherwise sensitive, the real exposure is not just the column itself but every downstream column it flows into — each copy is a place the guardrail (mask, tag, row filter) may not have followed. This query is the reach map that turns "this column is sensitive" into "here is every target column it propagated to, how heavily, and by how many people," so you can size the blast radius before a schema change or a breach review and prioritize which unmasked copies to remediate first. Because it is the reach side of the classified-but-unmasked analysis, an underestimate here quietly understates real sensitive-data sprawl.

What it does
  • Maps every observed column-to-column data flow over the trailing 90 days as one row per source-column to target-column pair, so you can trace exactly how far a sensitive column spreads.
  • For each flow, tells you how busy it is (event_count), how many distinct people drove it (distinct_principals), and when it last fired (last_event_time).
  • Slices each flow by what produced it (entity_type) and by whether the access was direct vs. indirect/view-expansion (direct_access).
  • Keeps only flows that have a known source table and deliberately drops sourceless events (e.g. literal inserts), so it is a coverage-bounded reach map, not a complete census.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
source_table_full_nameFully-qualified catalog.schema.table of the upstream table the data flowed from. External/path references may appear as a cloud path string rather than a table name.
source_column_nameThe upstream column that is the origin of this flow — the one whose sensitivity you are tracing.
target_table_full_nameFully-qualified catalog.schema.table of the downstream table the data landed in.
target_column_nameThe downstream column that received data from the source column — a candidate spot where the source's mask/tag may not have carried over.
entity_typeThe kind of entity that produced the lineage edge (e.g. the type of workload/statement UC observed).
direct_accesstrue = a direct read/write between the columns; false = an indirect flow such as view expansion. Use to separate first-hop copies from derived/indirect propagation.
event_countCOUNT(*) of observed events for this exact edge in the window — a rough intensity/frequency signal, not a row or byte count.
distinct_principalsCOUNT(DISTINCT created_by) — the number of distinct identities that drove this edge; how broadly the flow is used, and thus how many people to potentially follow up with.
last_event_timeMAX(event_time) — the most recent timestamp this edge was observed; how fresh the propagation is (stale edges may be legacy flows).
Sample output
source_table_full_namesource_column_nametarget_table_full_nametarget_column_namedirect_accessevent_countdistinct_principalslast_event_time
prod.crm.customersemailprod.analytics.marketing_leadsemailtrue48262026-07-05 22:14:03
prod.crm.customersssnprod.sandbox.dev_copyssn_rawtrue5732026-07-04 09:41:27
prod.crm.customersemailprod.reporting.exec_dashboard_vwcontact_emailfalse1203112026-07-05 06:02:55
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.access.column_lineageSole source. Provides one row per observed column-level lineage edge (source column → target column) per event, including the source/target table full names and column names, the entity_type that produced the flow, the direct_access flag (true = direct read/write, false = indirect/view-expansion), the acting created_by principal, and event_time/event_date. The query filters to rows with a non-null source_table_full_name within the trailing 90-day event_date window and aggregates these edges.
Caveats & gotchas
  • Subset by design: column_lineage only captures operations UC observes with a source. The query further keeps only rows where source_table_full_name IS NOT NULL, dropping sourceless events (e.g. INSERT ... VALUES literals, and MERGE/JDBC/path/temp-view gaps), so this UNDERCOUNTS reach — treat as coverage-bounded, never proof of absence.
  • GA and regional; still requires UC + system-table sharing enabled and SELECT on system.access. An empty or TABLE_OR_VIEW_NOT_FOUND result means 'not captured / not provisioned,' not 'no propagation.'
  • Fixed trailing 90-day window on event_date (>= current_date() - 90 days AND < current_date()) that excludes today; widen only as far as your workspace-configured system.access.* retention allows.
  • event_count is an observed-event tally (COUNT(*)), not a row, byte, or dollar figure — it is only a relative intensity signal.
  • External/path references appear as cloud path strings, not table names; statement_id is SQL-warehouse-only and finer entity_metadata subfields (job_id, notebook_id, sql_query_id, dlt/genie/alert ids) are intentionally excluded from this rollup.
  • The rollup exposes only distinct_principals (COUNT DISTINCT created_by), not created_by itself, so no raw identity is emitted here — but created_by is a UC identity; mask any identity before sharing if you drill down to it.
→
What you do next
Pick your HIGH-confidence classified / sensitive source columns, look up their edges here to enumerate every target column they reach, then confirm each target carries the same mask/tag/row-filter — remediate the unmasked copies (highest event_count x distinct_principals first) and use the map as the impact list before altering or dropping the source table.
← PreviousSensitive Columns Missing Their Maskaccess_classified_unmasked All Governance, Access & Security ↑ Next →Which Columns Are Actually Maskedaccess_column_masks_inventory
Overview / Governance, Access & Security / access_column_masks_inventory
05
Governance, Access & Security access_column_masks_inventory

Which Columns Are Actually Masked

A current-state inventory listing every column in the metastore that has a column mask applied, naming the mask function and the columns it reads.

system.information_schema.column_masks
queries/governance_access/access_column_masks_inventory.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:access_column_masks_inventory */
SELECT table_catalog, table_schema, table_name, column_name,
       mask_name, using_columns
FROM system.information_schema.column_masks
Why it matters

Column masks are the enforcement layer that keeps SSNs, emails, and card numbers from rendering in the clear to unprivileged readers, so knowing exactly which columns carry a mask is the difference between a provable control and an assumption. This inventory is the ground truth you diff against your data-classification results to find sensitive columns that were never masked, or against change history to catch masks that were dropped in a refactor. Because the view is privilege-scoped, a short list can mean either genuinely thin coverage or simply that the collector could not see certain tables, and mistaking the latter for a clean bill of health is how leaks slip through. Treat this as one half of a masking-coverage audit, not a standalone all-clear.

What it does
  • Lists every masked column in the metastore, one row per column, fully qualified by catalog, schema, and table.
  • Names the mask function bound to each column so you can confirm the right masking logic is applied.
  • Shows the extra input columns each mask reads via using_columns (e.g. a group-membership column the mask branches on), or blank when the mask reads only the masked column itself.
  • Returns raw current state with no aggregation or filtering, so the row count is your masked-column population as visible to the collecting principal.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
table_catalogCatalog of the table holding the masked column; part of the three-level name that uniquely identifies the object.
table_schemaSchema (database) of the table holding the masked column.
table_nameTable (or view) whose column is masked.
column_nameThe specific column that has a mask applied — the sensitive field being protected.
mask_nameName of the UDF/mask function bound to this column; identifies the masking logic in effect. Verify it is the intended function, not a stale or no-op mask.
using_columnsAdditional columns the mask function reads as inputs (for example a role or region column it branches on). Empty/NULL means the mask reads only the masked column itself.
Sample output
table_catalogtable_schematable_namecolumn_namemask_nameusing_columns
prodhremployeesssnmain.security.mask_ssnNULL
prodfinancepaymentscard_numbermain.security.mask_panregion
analyticscrmcustomersemailmain.security.mask_email_by_groupis_pii_admin
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.information_schema.column_masksThe sole source: a current-state, one-row-per-masked-column catalog of every column mask applied across the metastore, giving the fully-qualified column, the mask function name, and the input columns the mask reads. Privilege-aware / object-visibility-scoped.
Caveats & gotchas
  • Public Preview and requires DBR 12.2 LTS+ — on older runtimes or if unsupported the query degrades (may return TABLE_OR_VIEW_NOT_FOUND); that is 'not assessed', not 'no masks'.
  • Privilege-aware / object-visibility-scoped: tables the collector can only BROWSE are excluded, so a missing mask row can mean 'not visible to the collector' and would OVERSTATE unmasked coverage — label the count 'partial', never 'clean'.
  • Empty != zero != error: no rows can mean masking is genuinely unused OR the running principal simply cannot see the masked tables. Run under a high-privilege audit principal and still treat results as partial.
  • This is already a compact current-state inventory (one row per masked column) — not pre-aggregated and with no date window; it reflects the metastore as of query time, not a historical trend.
  • system.information_schema is metastore-wide, so results span all catalogs the principal can see in the current metastore/region — not workspace-scoped.
  • using_columns lists mask input columns only; a blank value does not mean the mask is broken — many masks read only the column they protect.
  • Pair with access_data_classification_inventory (or access_classified_unmasked) to find sensitive columns that appear classified but do NOT appear here — the actual coverage gap.
→
What you do next
Diff this masked-column list against your HIGH-confidence classified columns (via access_classified_unmasked) to surface sensitive fields with no mask, and spot-check that each mask_name is the intended function rather than a stale or no-op one — then remediate the gaps by applying or fixing masks. Because the view is privilege-scoped, confirm the collector could actually see the tables in question before declaring any table 'unmasked'.
← PreviousColumn-Level Lineage Blast Radiusaccess_column_lineage_sensitive_reach All Governance, Access & Security ↑ Next →Where Sensitive Data Actually Livesaccess_data_classification_inventory
Overview / Governance, Access & Security / access_data_classification_inventory
06
Governance, Access & Security access_data_classification_inventory

Where Sensitive Data Actually Lives

A deduplicated inventory of every column the auto-classifier flagged as sensitive, one row per catalog/schema/table/column/class-tag/confidence/data-type, carrying the peak match frequency and first/last detection times.

system.data_classification.results
queries/governance_access/access_data_classification_inventory.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:access_data_classification_inventory */
SELECT catalog_name, schema_name, table_name, column_name, class_tag, confidence, data_type,
       MAX(frequency)            AS max_frequency,
       MAX(latest_detected_time) AS latest_detected_time,
       MIN(first_detected_time)  AS first_detected_time
FROM system.data_classification.results
WHERE class_tag IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7
Why it matters

This is your ground-truth map of where the scanner believes PII and other sensitive data physically sits, which is the starting point for every masking, row-filter, and least-privilege decision you make. Without it you are guessing which columns need protection, and an unprotected email/SSN/card column is both a breach-exposure and a compliance-fine liability. Because it covers enabled catalogs only and omits unclassified columns, treat it as a floor on your sensitive-data surface, never a complete census. It pairs directly with the mask inventory to surface the headline gap: classified-but-unmasked columns.

What it does
  • Inventories every column the classifier tagged as sensitive across enabled catalogs, giving you one clean row per column + detected class (e.g. EMAIL, US_SSN).
  • Reports the scanner's confidence (HIGH vs LOW) and the strongest match rate seen for that column, so you can triage firm hits from weak ones.
  • Timestamps each finding with when it was first and most recently detected, useful for spotting newly-appeared sensitive data.
  • Excludes rows with no class tag and deliberately never surfaces the raw sampled values, so the output is safe to share.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
catalog_nameUnity Catalog catalog containing the classified column (enabled catalogs only).
schema_nameSchema within that catalog.
table_nameTable or view holding the column.
column_nameThe specific column the scanner classified.
class_tagThe detected sensitivity class, e.g. EMAIL, US_SSN, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER. Rows with a NULL tag are excluded.
confidenceScanner confidence for this class on this column: HIGH or LOW (part of the row grain). Prioritise HIGH for remediation.
data_typeDeclared column data type (e.g. STRING, INT) at detection time; also part of the row grain.
max_frequencyHighest observed frequency (float 0–1) = the largest share of sampled values that matched this class. Closer to 1 means the whole column looks like this class.
latest_detected_timeMost recent time this class was detected on the column — how fresh the finding is.
first_detected_timeEarliest time this class was detected — roughly when the sensitive data first appeared/was first seen by the scanner.
Sample output
catalog_nameschema_nametable_namecolumn_nameclass_tagconfidencemax_frequency
prodcrmcustomersemail_addressEMAILHIGH0.98
prodcrmcustomersssnUS_SSNHIGH0.91
analyticsstagingraw_eventsnote_textEMAILLOW0.07
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.data_classification.resultsSole source. Holds auto-detected classification (PII/sensitivity) scan hits per column for enabled catalogs. The query reads catalog_name, schema_name, table_name, column_name, class_tag, confidence, data_type, frequency, first_detected_time, latest_detected_time and rolls them up; it deliberately never touches the raw samples array.
Caveats & gotchas
  • Preview feature. Requires BOTH the data-classification feature AND the separate system.data_classification schema enabled — enabling system.access does not enable this. If disabled, degrade to 'schema/feature not enabled', which is NOT the same as an empty (clean) result.
  • Covers ENABLED CATALOGS ONLY; unclassified columns are simply absent. Absence of a row is not proof a column is clean — it is a floor, not a census.
  • confidence is only HIGH or LOW (not a numeric score); max_frequency is a float 0–1 share of matched samples, not a percentage or a count.
  • The raw samples array<string> (up to 5 live sensitive values) is deliberately dropped and must never be collected.
  • Row grain includes confidence and data_type (GROUP BY 1–7), so one column+class can yield more than one row if it was detected at different confidence levels or under different declared data types.
  • 13-month retention; regional (per-region results). Empty != zero != error — an unprovisioned schema yields TABLE_OR_VIEW_NOT_FOUND.
  • No date-window filter and no masking applied here (only class-tag presence is required); it is current-state classification, not a time-boxed audit.
→
What you do next
Sort by confidence=HIGH and high max_frequency, then cross-check each column against the column-mask and row-filter inventories (or the access_classified_unmasked query) and apply a mask/filter to any HIGH-confidence sensitive column that has none.
← PreviousWhich Columns Are Actually Maskedaccess_column_masks_inventory All Governance, Access & Security ↑ Next →Dead-Table Cleanup Candidatesaccess_dead_table_candidates
Overview / Governance, Access & Security / access_dead_table_candidates
07
Governance, Access & Security access_dead_table_candidates

Dead-Table Cleanup Candidates

Every MANAGED or EXTERNAL table in your account that never showed up as a read (lineage source) during the look-back window, listed with its owner and staleness age as cleanup candidates to investigate.

system.access.table_lineagesystem.information_schema.tables
queries/governance_access/access_dead_table_candidates.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:access_dead_table_candidates */
WITH lineage_window AS (
  SELECT *
  FROM system.access.table_lineage
  WHERE event_date >= dateadd(day, -:period_days, current_date())
    AND event_date < current_date()
),
-- Every table that appeared as a lineage SOURCE in the window (any direct_access
-- value — an indirect/view-mediated read of a base table still proves it was read,
-- so we must NOT exclude direct_access=false here or we over-flag base tables).
-- Prefer the discrete *_catalog/_schema/_name columns over CONCAT vs _full_name,
-- because _full_name can be backtick-quoted for reserved-word identifiers.
source_tables AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT
         source_table_catalog AS catalog,
         source_table_schema  AS schema,
         source_table_name    AS name
  FROM lineage_window
  WHERE source_table_name IS NOT NULL
),
-- The managed/external tables this account holds (privilege-scoped inventory).
inventory AS (
  SELECT table_catalog,
         table_schema,
         table_name,
         table_type,
         table_owner,
         created,
         last_altered
  FROM system.information_schema.tables
  WHERE table_catalog <> 'system'
    AND table_schema <> 'information_schema'
    AND table_type IN ('MANAGED', 'EXTERNAL')
)
SELECT inv.table_catalog,
       inv.table_schema,
       inv.table_name,
       inv.table_type,
       CASE
         WHEN inv.table_owner IS NULL OR inv.table_owner = '__REDACTED__' THEN inv.table_owner
         WHEN inv.table_owner LIKE '%@%' THEN concat(substr(inv.table_owner, 1, 2), '****@****')
         WHEN inv.table_owner RLIKE '^[0-9a-fA-F]{8}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{12}$' THEN inv.table_owner
         ELSE concat(substr(inv.table_owner, 1, 2), '****')
       END AS table_owner,
       inv.created,
       inv.last_altered,
       -- Age in whole days since the object was last altered (NULL -> age unknown).
       datediff(current_date(), DATE(inv.last_altered)) AS days_since_altered
FROM inventory inv
LEFT JOIN source_tables src
  ON  inv.table_catalog = src.catalog
  AND inv.table_schema  = src.schema
  AND inv.table_name    = src.name
WHERE src.name IS NULL          -- never appeared as a lineage source in the window
Why it matters

Stale MANAGED and EXTERNAL tables quietly accrue storage cost and expand the governance and breach surface long after anyone stops reading them, yet no one wants to be the person who drops a table that turns out to feed a quarterly report. This query narrows the whole account down to a focused shortlist of tables with no observed reads, each paired with an owner to ask and an age to prioritize. Critically, absence from lineage is a candidate signal, not proof of disuse, so it prevents accidental drops by forcing a cross-check rather than handing you a delete list. The payoff is targeted storage reclamation and a smaller attack surface, with the owner already identified for follow-up.

What it does
  • Lists your MANAGED and EXTERNAL tables that were never observed being read during the trailing :period_days window (excluding today).
  • Excludes the system catalog and information_schema, so only your own governed data objects appear.
  • Attaches each candidate's masked owner, its created / last_altered timestamps, and a days_since_altered staleness age.
  • Produces a cross-check candidate list, not a proven-dead list: view-mediated (indirect) reads still keep a base table off it.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
table_catalogCatalog of the candidate table (system catalog excluded by design).
table_schemaSchema of the candidate table (information_schema excluded).
table_nameName of the MANAGED/EXTERNAL table that never appeared as a lineage source in the window.
table_typeEither MANAGED or EXTERNAL; the only two object types considered here (views/other types are not candidates).
table_ownerMasked owning principal to follow up with; emails show first two chars then '****@****', other names first two chars then '****', UUIDs and '__REDACTED__' shown as-is, NULL if unknown.
createdWhen the object was created (may be NULL/late-populated on some object types).
last_alteredWhen the object was last altered per the catalog (NULL means age unknown, not new).
days_since_alteredWhole days from last_altered to today, i.e. how stale the object looks; NULL when last_altered is NULL (age unknown). Higher = staler, but not proof of disuse.
Sample output
table_catalogtable_schematable_nametable_typetable_ownerdays_since_altered
prod_analyticsmarketingcampaign_snapshot_2023MANAGEDda****@****612
prod_rawingestlegacy_clickstream_extEXTERNALa1b2c3d4-1234-5678-9abc-def012345678418
sandboxscratchtmp_join_backupMANAGEDsv****NULL
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.access.table_lineageSupplies the set of tables that DID appear as a read (lineage source) in the trailing :period_days window; any direct_access value counts, so view-mediated reads still protect a base table. Tables absent from this set become candidates.
system.information_schema.tablesThe privilege-scoped object universe (MANAGED/EXTERNAL tables with owner, created, last_altered), excluding the system catalog and information_schema; every table here that has no matching lineage-source read is emitted.
Caveats & gotchas
  • A candidate is NOT proven unused: a SELECT with no downstream write, and any write-only target, emit no lineage source row at all. This is a cross-check list, never a DROP recommendation.
  • Lineage is captured only when Unity Catalog observes the operation; MERGE / JDBC / path / temp-view reads are gaps that can falsely flag a live table.
  • Indirect / view-mediated reads (direct_access=false) are deliberately counted as 'appeared as a source' so a view-mediated read does not falsely mark its base table dead.
  • Retention on system.access.* is workspace-configurable: a short :period_days over-flags quarterly / long-tail tables. Widen the window only as far as retention allows.
  • table_lineage is GA with rolling 365-day retention (but still workspace-configurable); requires UC + SELECT on system.access.
  • information_schema.tables is privilege-aware: tables the collector service principal cannot see are simply absent, so a missing table is not 'dead'. Label results partial.
  • Only MANAGED and EXTERNAL tables are considered; the system catalog and information_schema are excluded by design.
  • created / last_altered can be NULL or late-populated on some object types; treated as 'age unknown', never as a finding.
  • Window is the trailing :period_days parameter and excludes today (event_date < current_date()); it applies to the lineage read-evidence only, while the inventory (information_schema.tables) is current-state with no date filter.
  • table_owner is masked (email -> 'da****@****', other names -> first two chars + '****'); UUIDs and '__REDACTED__' pass through unchanged. Values are DBU-free identifiers, no cost involved.
→
What you do next
Treat the list as cleanup candidates only: for each table, cross-check whether it was actually read via `system.access.audit`, `query.history`, and `system.storage.table_metrics_history` (and widen `:period_days` toward your workspace's `system.access.*` retention) before ever proposing a DROP or archive to the owner shown.
← PreviousWhere Sensitive Data Actually Livesaccess_data_classification_inventory All Governance, Access & Security ↑ Next →Who Holds Which Grantsaccess_grants_inventory
Overview / Governance, Access & Security / access_grants_inventory
08
Governance, Access & Security access_grants_inventory

Who Holds Which Grants

A current-state, privilege-scoped inventory of table- and catalog-level grants, rolled up to one row per object scope, privilege type, and grantee with the number of grants and distinct objects each covers.

system.information_schema.table_privilegessystem.information_schema.catalog_privileges
queries/governance_access/access_grants_inventory.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:access_grants_inventory */
SELECT object_scope, PRIVILEGE_TYPE,
       CASE
         WHEN GRANTEE IS NULL OR GRANTEE = '__REDACTED__' THEN GRANTEE
         WHEN GRANTEE LIKE '%@%' THEN concat(substr(GRANTEE, 1, 2), '****@****')
         WHEN GRANTEE RLIKE '^[0-9a-fA-F]{8}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{12}$' THEN GRANTEE
         ELSE concat(substr(GRANTEE, 1, 2), '****')
       END AS GRANTEE,
       grant_count, distinct_objects
FROM (
  SELECT 'TABLE' AS object_scope, PRIVILEGE_TYPE, GRANTEE,
         COUNT(*) AS grant_count,
         COUNT(DISTINCT TABLE_CATALOG || '.' || TABLE_SCHEMA || '.' || TABLE_NAME) AS distinct_objects
  FROM system.information_schema.table_privileges
  GROUP BY 1, 2, 3
  UNION ALL
  SELECT 'CATALOG' AS object_scope, PRIVILEGE_TYPE, GRANTEE,
         COUNT(*) AS grant_count,
         COUNT(DISTINCT CATALOG_NAME) AS distinct_objects
  FROM system.information_schema.catalog_privileges
  GROUP BY 1, 2, 3
)
Why it matters

This is the foundational least-privilege review artifact: it shows, per principal, how many objects each privilege actually reaches, so you can spot over-broad access — a single grantee holding `SELECT` on hundreds of tables, or `ALL PRIVILEGES` at catalog scope — before it becomes an exfiltration or blast-radius risk. Concentrated or wildcard grants are exactly the audit findings that fail SOC 2 / access-recertification and widen the surface for a compromised credential. Because `information_schema` is privilege-aware, treat this as a floor on exposure, not a complete grant graph — anything it surfaces is real, but it can miss grants the collector cannot see.

What it does
  • Builds a baseline privilege map of who holds what across two scopes — TABLE and CATALOG grants — combined into one list.
  • For each grantee and privilege, reports both how many grants they hold (grant_count) and how many distinct objects that spans (distinct_objects), so you see reach at a glance.
  • Masks every grantee identity (emails, names) while passing service-principal UUIDs through, so the output is safe to share.
  • Returns a privilege-scoped current-state snapshot — it reflects only what the collecting principal can see, and is deliberately incomplete versus a full SHOW GRANTS.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
object_scopeThe level the grant applies at — either `TABLE` (table-level grants) or `CATALOG` (catalog-level grants). Tells you the breadth of the object the privilege is attached to.
PRIVILEGE_TYPEThe privilege granted, e.g. `SELECT`, `MODIFY`, `USE CATALOG`, `ALL PRIVILEGES`. Watch for broad or wildcard privileges held widely.
GRANTEEThe principal (user, group, or service principal) holding the grant, masked for safe sharing. Emails show as `da****@****`, other names truncated to `xx****`, UUID service principals and `__REDACTED__` (and NULL) left intact.
grant_countHow many individual grant rows this grantee holds for this privilege at this scope — a raw COUNT(*). A large number signals broad reach for one principal. Normally equals `distinct_objects` since the source grain is one row per object.
distinct_objectsHow many distinct objects (fully-qualified tables, or catalogs) that privilege spans for this grantee — the blast radius. Compare against `grant_count` to gauge concentration.
Sample output
object_scopePRIVILEGE_TYPEGRANTEEgrant_countdistinct_objects
TABLESELECTda****@****397397
CATALOGUSE CATALOGda****66
TABLEALL PRIVILEGESa1b2c3d4-5e6f-7a8b-9c0d-1e2f3a4b5c6d5858
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.information_schema.table_privilegesTable-level grant state — one row per grant (grantee × privilege × table). Supplies the `TABLE` scope rows; grants are counted and distinct fully-qualified tables (`TABLE_CATALOG`.`TABLE_SCHEMA`.`TABLE_NAME`) are tallied per grantee × privilege.
system.information_schema.catalog_privilegesCatalog-level grant state — one row per grant (grantee × privilege × catalog). Supplies the `CATALOG` scope rows; grants are counted and distinct `CATALOG_NAME` values tallied per grantee × privilege.
Caveats & gotchas
  • Privilege-aware = always partial: `information_schema` shows only grants the RUNNING principal can see, on objects it can see. Even a high-privilege audit SP CANNOT reproduce a full SHOW GRANTS graph.
  • Label output 'partial — privilege-aware; incomplete vs SHOW GRANTS'. Never assert this is a complete grant graph.
  • Empty or `TABLE_OR_VIEW_NOT_FOUND` means UC/system-table sharing isn't provisioned or nothing is visible to the collector — NOT 'no grants exist' and NOT a clean all-clear.
  • Requires Unity Catalog and `SELECT` on `system.information_schema.*` for the collecting principal.
  • `IS_GRANTABLE` is always 'NO' (reserved) so it is deliberately not collected — you cannot tell WITH GRANT OPTION grants apart here.
  • Current-state snapshot only — no time window, no history of who granted it or when (pair with `access_admin_role_change_events` for the change log).
  • `GRANTEE` is masked for safe sharing: emails → `da****@****`, other principal names → first two chars + `****`, UUID service principals, `__REDACTED__`, and NULL pass through intact.
  • Covers only TABLE and CATALOG scope; schema, connection, credential and external-location grants are in the sibling `access_grants_inventory_extended`.
  • `distinct_objects` counts distinct fully-qualified objects visible to the collector — under-counts if some objects are hidden from the running principal.
  • Because the source grain is one row per grantee × privilege × object, `grant_count` and `distinct_objects` are normally identical; a gap between them only appears if the source view emits duplicate grant rows.
→
What you do next
Use this as your least-privilege baseline: scan for grantees or privilege types with a high grant_count or broad distinct_objects (especially wildcard privileges like ALL PRIVILEGES or MODIFY held broadly), flag over-broad access for review, and cross-check against the full SHOW GRANTS output since this inventory is privilege-scoped and incomplete.
← PreviousDead-Table Cleanup Candidatesaccess_dead_table_candidates All Governance, Access & Security ↑ Next →Grants on Schemas and Integrationsaccess_grants_inventory_extended
Overview / Governance, Access & Security / access_grants_inventory_extended
09
Governance, Access & Security access_grants_inventory_extended

Grants on Schemas and Integrations

A privilege map counting who holds which grants on schemas, UC connections, credentials, and external locations, one row per object scope, privilege, and grantee.

system.information_schema.schema_privilegessystem.information_schema.connection_privilegessystem.information_schema.credential_privilegessystem.information_schema.external_location_privileges
queries/governance_access/access_grants_inventory_extended.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:access_grants_inventory_extended */
-- SAFE as written (uses only GRANTEE/PRIVILEGE_TYPE, shared across all privilege views).
-- NEEDS CONFIRMATION before reading any per-view object-name column: DESCRIBE each sibling view.
SELECT 'SCHEMA' AS object_scope, PRIVILEGE_TYPE,
       CASE
         WHEN GRANTEE IS NULL OR GRANTEE = '__REDACTED__' THEN GRANTEE
         WHEN GRANTEE LIKE '%@%' THEN concat(substr(GRANTEE, 1, 2), '****@****')
         WHEN GRANTEE RLIKE '^[0-9a-fA-F]{8}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{12}$' THEN GRANTEE
         ELSE concat(substr(GRANTEE, 1, 2), '****')
       END AS GRANTEE,
       COUNT(*) AS grant_count
FROM system.information_schema.schema_privileges GROUP BY 1,2,GRANTEE
UNION ALL
SELECT 'CONNECTION', PRIVILEGE_TYPE,
       CASE
         WHEN GRANTEE IS NULL OR GRANTEE = '__REDACTED__' THEN GRANTEE
         WHEN GRANTEE LIKE '%@%' THEN concat(substr(GRANTEE, 1, 2), '****@****')
         WHEN GRANTEE RLIKE '^[0-9a-fA-F]{8}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{12}$' THEN GRANTEE
         ELSE concat(substr(GRANTEE, 1, 2), '****')
       END,
       COUNT(*)
FROM system.information_schema.connection_privileges GROUP BY 1,2,GRANTEE
UNION ALL
SELECT 'CREDENTIAL', PRIVILEGE_TYPE,
       CASE
         WHEN GRANTEE IS NULL OR GRANTEE = '__REDACTED__' THEN GRANTEE
         WHEN GRANTEE LIKE '%@%' THEN concat(substr(GRANTEE, 1, 2), '****@****')
         WHEN GRANTEE RLIKE '^[0-9a-fA-F]{8}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{12}$' THEN GRANTEE
         ELSE concat(substr(GRANTEE, 1, 2), '****')
       END,
       COUNT(*)
FROM system.information_schema.credential_privileges GROUP BY 1,2,GRANTEE
UNION ALL
SELECT 'EXTERNAL_LOCATION', PRIVILEGE_TYPE,
       CASE
         WHEN GRANTEE IS NULL OR GRANTEE = '__REDACTED__' THEN GRANTEE
         WHEN GRANTEE LIKE '%@%' THEN concat(substr(GRANTEE, 1, 2), '****@****')
         WHEN GRANTEE RLIKE '^[0-9a-fA-F]{8}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{12}$' THEN GRANTEE
         ELSE concat(substr(GRANTEE, 1, 2), '****')
       END,
       COUNT(*)
FROM system.information_schema.external_location_privileges GROUP BY 1,2,GRANTEE
Why it matters

Schemas and integration objects (connections, credentials, external locations) are the common blind spots in a least-privilege review — a broad grant on a credential or external location can hand a principal reach into external systems or raw storage that a table-level audit never sees. This query extends the baseline grant map beyond tables and catalogs so you can spot over-broad or stale grants on exactly the objects that bridge Unity Catalog to the outside world. Because these views are privilege-aware, the result is always a partial floor of the real grant graph, never a complete `SHOW GRANTS`.

What it does
  • Inventories the current grant state across four object scopes — schemas, connections, credentials, and external locations — that sit outside the table/catalog baseline.
  • Rolls the grants up to a count for each combination of object scope, privilege type, and grantee, so you see how many objects each principal holds a given privilege on.
  • Masks every grantee identity (emails and generic names truncated; UUIDs and __REDACTED__ passed through) so the output is safe to share.
  • Returns a partial, privilege-scoped view — it reflects only the grants the running principal can see, so counts are a floor, not a full audit.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
object_scopeThe object family the grant sits on: one of SCHEMA, CONNECTION, CREDENTIAL, or EXTERNAL_LOCATION. This is a literal label, not a per-object name.
PRIVILEGE_TYPEThe privilege granted (e.g. USE_SCHEMA, USE_CONNECTION, CREATE_EXTERNAL_TABLE, READ_FILES). Group by it; do not assume a fixed enum.
GRANTEEThe principal holding the grant, identity-masked: emails become 'da****@****', other names become 'da****', and UUIDs / '__REDACTED__' are left intact. NULL passes through as NULL.
grant_countHow many grant rows (grantee × privilege × object within this scope) matched — effectively the number of objects in that scope on which this grantee holds this privilege. Integer count, and a floor because the view is privilege-aware.
Sample output
object_scopePRIVILEGE_TYPEGRANTEEgrant_count
SCHEMAUSE_SCHEMAda****@****14
CREDENTIALACCESSb3f1c2a9-4d5e-4f6a-8b7c-9d0e1f2a3b4c2
EXTERNAL_LOCATIONREAD_FILESda****1
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.information_schema.schema_privilegesSchema-level grant state; supplies the SCHEMA-scope rows. Only GRANTEE and PRIVILEGE_TYPE are read.
system.information_schema.connection_privilegesGrant state on UC connections (e.g. Lakehouse Federation / external system connections); supplies the CONNECTION-scope rows. Only GRANTEE and PRIVILEGE_TYPE are read.
system.information_schema.credential_privilegesGrant state on UC credentials; supplies the CREDENTIAL-scope rows. Only GRANTEE and PRIVILEGE_TYPE are read.
system.information_schema.external_location_privilegesGrant state on UC external locations; supplies the EXTERNAL_LOCATION-scope rows. Only GRANTEE and PRIVILEGE_TYPE are read.
Caveats & gotchas
  • Privilege-aware = partial, always: each view shows only grants the running principal can see on objects it can see; even a high-privilege audit SP cannot reproduce a full SHOW GRANTS, so treat grant_count as a floor.
