  • Requires Unity Catalog + system-table sharing enabled on the metastore and SELECT on system.information_schema; otherwise you get empty or TABLE_OR_VIEW_NOT_FOUND.
  • Empty != clean != error: CONNECTION and EXTERNAL_LOCATION scopes are empty when no connections / external locations are defined — that is 'none configured', not 'no risk'.
  • Current-state snapshot only — no date window, no history; pair with access_admin_role_change_events for how grants got there.
  • Confidence is needs_confirmation: only GRANTEE and PRIVILEGE_TYPE (shared across all privilege views) are used, so the query is safe as written, but the per-view object-name columns are inferred and unverified — DESCRIBE each sibling view before reading any object-name column.
  • STORAGE_CREDENTIAL_PRIVILEGES is deprecated and intentionally excluded; routine / volume / metastore privilege views exist but are not queried here.
  • Identities are masked for safe sharing; you cannot resolve the real principal from the output alone.
  • object_scope is a fixed literal per UNION branch (SCHEMA / CONNECTION / CREDENTIAL / EXTERNAL_LOCATION), not the name of the specific schema or location the grant is on.
→
What you do next
Scan the high-count and high-privilege rows on CREDENTIAL and EXTERNAL_LOCATION scopes first — these bridge UC to external storage and systems — and open a least-privilege review for any grantee holding broad or unexpected grants, then confirm against a live SHOW GRANTS since these counts are a privilege-scoped floor.
← PreviousWho Holds Which Grantsaccess_grants_inventory All Governance, Access & Security ↑ Next →Login Concentration & Anomaly Rollupaccess_login_concentration
Overview / Governance, Access & Security / access_login_concentration
10
Governance, Access & Security access_login_concentration

Login Concentration & Anomaly Rollup

A 30-day rollup of account authentication activity, one row per masked principal x source IP x service x action, with success and non-success event counts and a first/last-seen window.

system.access.audit
queries/governance_access/access_login_concentration.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:access_login_concentration */
SELECT CASE
         WHEN COALESCE(user_identity.email, user_identity.subject_name) IS NULL OR COALESCE(user_identity.email, user_identity.subject_name) = '__REDACTED__' THEN COALESCE(user_identity.email, user_identity.subject_name)
         WHEN COALESCE(user_identity.email, user_identity.subject_name) LIKE '%@%' THEN concat(substr(COALESCE(user_identity.email, user_identity.subject_name), 1, 2), '****@****')
         WHEN COALESCE(user_identity.email, user_identity.subject_name) RLIKE '^[0-9a-fA-F]{8}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{12}$' THEN COALESCE(user_identity.email, user_identity.subject_name)
         ELSE concat(substr(COALESCE(user_identity.email, user_identity.subject_name), 1, 2), '****')
       END AS principal,
       source_ip_address, service_name, action_name,
       COUNT(*) AS event_count,
       SUM(CASE WHEN response.status_code = 200 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS success_count,
       SUM(CASE WHEN response.status_code <> 200 OR response.status_code IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS non_success_count,
       COUNT(DISTINCT source_ip_address) AS distinct_source_ips,
       MIN(event_time) AS first_event_time, MAX(event_time) AS last_event_time
FROM system.access.audit
WHERE service_name = 'accounts'
  AND event_date >= current_date() - INTERVAL 30 DAYS
  AND event_date < current_date()
GROUP BY COALESCE(user_identity.email, user_identity.subject_name), 2, 3, 4
Why it matters

Account logins are the front door to your Databricks estate, and a compromised credential is the cheapest way for an attacker to get in. This rollup lets you spot the tells of credential stuffing or a stolen token: a burst of non-success events against a single account, one principal showing up across an unusual number of source IPs (visible as many rows for the same masked principal), or activity that doesn't match a human's normal pattern. Because it breaks activity out by action_name, it shows the mix of MFA-based, token-based, and other login paths so you can confirm strong-auth is actually being used. Catching this early is the difference between a blocked login attempt and a full account takeover.

What it does
  • Rolls up the last 30 days of account authentication events into one line per masked principal, source IP, service, and action.
  • For each line, totals how many events occurred and splits them into successful (status_code = 200) versus non-success or unknown-status attempts.
  • Because each line is already scoped to a single source IP, an identity fanning out across many addresses shows up as many separate rows for the same masked principal — not as a large number in any single column.
  • Stamps each line with the first and last event time in the window, giving you the activity span at a glance.
  • Masks every identity (emails to da**@**, other names truncated, UUIDs and __REDACTED__ left intact) so the output is safe to share.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
principalThe acting identity, masked for safe sharing: emails become `da****@****`, other names are truncated to `xx****`, and UUID-form or `__REDACTED__` values pass through unchanged. Sourced from user_identity.email, falling back to subject_name; can be NULL (this field is frequently unpopulated).
source_ip_addressThe IP the events in this row came from. Each row is one IP, so an identity appearing across many rows (many IPs) — or from an unfamiliar geography — is the anomaly signal.
service_nameAlways `accounts` here (the query filters to account authentication), confirming these are login/credential events rather than other control-plane actions.
action_nameThe specific authentication operation (e.g. mfaLogin, tokenLogin, and others). These values are representative, not an exhaustive list — read whatever appears; do not assume a fixed set.
event_countTotal audited events for this principal x IP x service x action combination in the window. High values on a single account warrant a look.
success_countHow many of those events returned `status_code = 200` (succeeded).
non_success_countHow many events had a status other than 200 or a NULL status. A high non_success_count with few successes looks like failed/blocked attempts (possible credential stuffing); the NULL-inclusive form is defensive because the status_code type is unverified. success_count + non_success_count always equals event_count.
distinct_source_ipsCOUNT(DISTINCT source_ip_address) — but the query already GROUPs BY source_ip_address, so each row covers exactly one IP and this value is effectively always 1. It is NOT a per-principal fan-out count. To measure an identity spreading across many IPs, count how many rows share the same masked principal instead.
first_event_timeEarliest event timestamp in the 30-day window for this row — the start of the observed activity span.
last_event_timeLatest event timestamp in the window for this row — how recently the activity occurred.
Sample output
principalsource_ip_addressaction_nameevent_countsuccess_countnon_success_countdistinct_source_ips
da****@****203.0.113.24mfaLogin484711
da****@****198.51.100.7tokenLogin12041211921
bo****@****192.0.2.55mfaLogin9901
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.access.auditThe only table read. Filtered to account authentication events (service_name = 'accounts'), it supplies the acting principal (user_identity.email or user_identity.subject_name), source_ip_address, service_name, action_name, response.status_code (200 = success), and event_time/event_date used for windowing.
Caveats & gotchas
  • distinct_source_ips is degenerate: the query GROUPs BY source_ip_address, so every row covers a single IP and this column is effectively always 1. It cannot be used to rank principals by IP fan-out or sorted on directly — aggregate rows per masked principal (COUNT of distinct source_ip_address across rows) for that.
  • Preview table: requires Unity Catalog with system-table sharing enabled and SELECT on system.access. TABLE_OR_VIEW_NOT_FOUND or an empty result means 'not provisioned / no matching events,' not 'no risky logins.'
  • Counts are event counts, not dollars — nothing here is spend.
  • service_name = 'accounts' is confirmed, but the specific action_name values (mfaLogin, tokenLogin, ...) are representative, not a complete enum. The query groups by action_name rather than hardcoding a filter list; do the same when interpreting.
  • Identity comes from user_identity.subject_name (verified live 2026-05-30) — NOT subjectName — and is frequently NULL, so some rows have a NULL principal.
  • response.status_code int-vs-long type is unverified; the '<>200 OR NULL' form is deliberately defensive, which is why non_success_count also absorbs NULL-status events. success_count and non_success_count are complementary and always sum to event_count.
  • Account-level events are global (workspace_id=0); workspace events are regional. Scope accordingly.
  • ~15-minute ingest lag — treat the most recent hour as provisional and potentially incomplete.
  • Fixed 30-day window: event_date from current_date() - 30 days up to but excluding today (today's partial day is not included).
  • All identities are masked in the output; do not expect raw emails or usernames.
→
What you do next
Because distinct_source_ips is always 1 at this grain, do not sort on it. Instead sort rows by non_success_count to surface accounts racking up failed/blocked attempts, and separately aggregate rows per masked principal to count how many distinct source IPs each identity spans. Pivot on the masked principal: confirm with the account owner, force a credential/token rotation and MFA re-enrollment if unexplained, and block anomalous source IPs. Also scan the action_name mix to confirm MFA-based login is actually in use rather than weaker paths.
← PreviousGrants on Schemas and Integrationsaccess_grants_inventory_extended All Governance, Access & Security ↑ Next →Blocked Inbound Network Attemptsaccess_network_inbound_denials
Overview / Governance, Access & Security / access_network_inbound_denials
11
Governance, Access & Security access_network_inbound_denials

Blocked Inbound Network Attempts

A 30-day rollup of inbound (ingress) network-policy enforcement events, grouped by policy outcome, rule, request path, principal, and source IP, with a count and first/last timestamps per group — the query groups by outcome rather than filtering it, so it surfaces whatever outcomes the table records (DENY / DENY_DRY_RUN denials in practice).

system.access.inbound_network
queries/governance_access/access_network_inbound_denials.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:access_network_inbound_denials */
-- NEEDS CONFIRMATION: source.ip nested subfield name is UNVERIFIED. A wrong name errors the
-- whole statement — make it defensive (drop or replace with the verified subfield).
SELECT policy_outcome, rule_label, request_path,
       CASE
         WHEN authenticated_as IS NULL OR authenticated_as = '__REDACTED__' THEN authenticated_as
         WHEN authenticated_as LIKE '%@%' THEN concat(substr(authenticated_as, 1, 2), '****@****')
         WHEN authenticated_as RLIKE '^[0-9a-fA-F]{8}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{12}$' THEN authenticated_as
         ELSE concat(substr(authenticated_as, 1, 2), '****')
       END AS authenticated_as,
       source.ip AS source_ip,
       COUNT(*) AS denial_count,
       MIN(event_time) AS first_event_time, MAX(event_time) AS last_event_time
FROM system.access.inbound_network
WHERE event_time >= current_timestamp() - INTERVAL 30 DAYS
GROUP BY 1, 2, 3, authenticated_as, 5
Why it matters

These rows are the audit trail of inbound connections your ingress network policy evaluated and (for DENY) blocked — the difference between "our IP allowlist is working" and "we never had one configured." A cluster of denials on a sensitive `request_path` from an unexpected `source_ip` is an early signal of a misconfigured client, a stale allowlist entry locking out legitimate users, or a probe against the account. Because the query does not filter the outcome, always confirm the `policy_outcome` column before treating a row as blocked — and note `DENY_DRY_RUN` rows were only simulated, not actually stopped. Equally important, an empty result is not an all-clear: it usually means no ingress policy is configured at all, so nothing is being enforced.

What it does
  • Rolls up inbound network-policy enforcement events from the last 30 days, one line per distinct outcome + rule + request path + principal + source IP — in practice these are denials, since that is what the source table records.
  • Collapses repeat activity so a source IP or principal that keeps getting blocked by the same rule on the same path shows up as a single line, not thousands.
  • Gives each group a denial_count plus the first_event_time and last_event_time, so you can tell a one-off from a sustained pattern.
  • Masks the principal identity (emails and plain names truncated, UUIDs and __REDACTED__ left as-is, NULL kept as NULL) so the output is safe to share.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
policy_outcomeThe enforcement result for the group — `DENY` (blocked for real) or `DENY_DRY_RUN` (would have been blocked, but the policy is in audit/simulation mode and traffic was let through). The query does not filter this column, so read it to know whether a row was actually blocked or only simulated.
rule_labelThe label of the ingress-policy rule that matched. Identifies which rule in your network policy fired.
request_pathThe inbound request path that was evaluated/denied — the endpoint/resource the connection was trying to reach.
authenticated_asThe principal behind the request, masked for sharing (`da****@****` for emails, `da****` for other names, UUIDs and `__REDACTED__` passed through, NULL left as NULL). Tells you who was blocked.
source_ipThe originating IP of the request (from the unverified `source.ip` subfield, aliased `source_ip`). Use it to distinguish a known-bad or unexpected network from a legitimate client caught by a stale allowlist.
denial_count`COUNT(*)` of events in this outcome/rule/path/principal/IP group over the window. High counts mark sustained blocking; low counts a one-off.
first_event_time`MIN(event_time)` — the earliest event in the group, i.e. when this pattern started.
last_event_time`MAX(event_time)` — the most recent event in the group, i.e. whether it is still happening.
Sample output
policy_outcomerule_labelrequest_pathauthenticated_assource_ipdenial_countfirst_event_timelast_event_time
DENYcorp-ip-allowlist/api/2.0/clusters/listda****@****203.0.113.471282026-06-09 08:14:022026-07-06 22:51:37
DENYcorp-ip-allowlist/api/2.0/sql/statements5f3c9a12-4b7e-4c1a-9d2f-8e6b1a0c7d34198.51.100.932026-06-28 03:02:102026-06-28 03:07:44
DENY_DRY_RUNtighten-egress-partners/api/2.1/jobs/runs/submit__REDACTED__192.0.2.201412026-06-15 11:20:552026-07-05 17:09:12
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.access.inbound_networkThe only source. Inbound (ingress) network-policy enforcement events, one row per event. NOTE: this query applies NO filter on policy_outcome — it selects and GROUPs BY policy_outcome and returns every outcome in the table; the table is documented to record DENY / DENY_DRY_RUN enforcement events, which is why the result reads as denials. Reads policy_outcome, rule_label, request_path, authenticated_as (principal, then masked), the source.ip nested subfield (unverified name), and event_time — this table has no event_date column.
Caveats & gotchas
  • NO OUTCOME FILTER IN THE QUERY: the SQL applies no WHERE clause on policy_outcome — it GROUPs BY it and returns every outcome present in the table. 'Denials only' holds only because the inbound_network table is documented to record DENY / DENY_DRY_RUN enforcement events; the query does not enforce it, so if the table ever contains other outcomes they would appear. Read the policy_outcome column rather than assuming all rows are denials.
  • No allowed-traffic baseline: because network enforcement tables record denials, there is effectively no allow row to compare against, so you cannot compute an allow/deny ratio from this result.
  • Empty != clean: an empty result usually means no ingress network policy is configured (nothing is being enforced), not that nothing was blocked. Treat as 'not assessed,' never all-clear.
  • Preview feature, regional (workspace-scoped) table. `TABLE_OR_VIEW_NOT_FOUND` means the schema isn't provisioned — a valid outcome, not zero.
  • 30-day retention, hard-capped: the window is fixed at 30 days (`event_time >= current_timestamp() - INTERVAL 30 DAYS`) regardless of any `:period_days` parameter — you cannot look back further even if you ask.
  • No `event_date` column: filtering is on `event_time` against `current_timestamp()`, so the window is a rolling 30 days by timestamp (not a partition-date window).
  • `source_ip` is UNVERIFIED: the `source.ip` nested subfield name is not confirmed. A wrong identifier errors the entire statement — confirm with `DESCRIBE` and drop/replace the subfield if it fails.
  • Requires UC + system-table sharing and `SELECT` on `system.access`; `DENY_DRY_RUN` rows were NOT actually blocked (policy in simulation mode), so they do not represent traffic that was stopped.
  • Principal is masked (emails/names truncated to first 2 chars, UUIDs and `__REDACTED__` preserved, NULL kept as NULL) — you cannot recover the full identity from this output alone.
→
What you do next
Triage the highest `denial_count` groups first: confirm each blocked `source_ip` / `authenticated_as` is genuinely unwanted (probe or misconfig) versus a legitimate client caught by a stale allowlist, then update the ingress rule accordingly. Because the query does not filter `policy_outcome`, scan the outcome column for any non-DENY values before assuming everything shown was blocked. If the result is empty, verify an ingress network policy is actually configured — the guardrail may not exist. Promote any `DENY_DRY_RUN` rules to real `DENY` once the simulated denials look correct.
← PreviousLogin Concentration & Anomaly Rollupaccess_login_concentration All Governance, Access & Security ↑ Next →Blocked Egress Exfiltration Attemptsaccess_network_outbound_denials
Overview / Governance, Access & Security / access_network_outbound_denials
12
Governance, Access & Security access_network_outbound_denials

Blocked Egress Exfiltration Attempts

A 30-day rollup of outbound (egress) network-policy denials, grouped by source and destination type, access type, and destination, with DNS/storage rejection detail and denial counts.

system.access.outbound_network
queries/governance_access/access_network_outbound_denials.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:access_network_outbound_denials */
SELECT network_source_type, destination_type, access_type, destination,
       dns_event.rcode                AS dns_rcode,
       storage_event.rejection_reason AS storage_rejection_reason,
       COUNT(*) AS denial_count,
       MIN(event_time) AS first_event_time, MAX(event_time) AS last_event_time
FROM system.access.outbound_network
WHERE event_time >= current_timestamp() - INTERVAL 30 DAYS
GROUP BY 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6
Why it matters

This is your exfiltration alarm: it shows what outbound traffic your egress policy actually blocked, broken down by where it was headed and why it was rejected. A spike in denials to an unfamiliar storage bucket or DNS name can be an early signal of data leaving the guardrails — a leaked credential, a misconfigured job, or an insider moving data out. Because the surface only records denials with no allowed-traffic baseline, you cannot compute an allow/deny ratio here, and an empty result means "no egress policy configured / preview not populated," not "zero exfiltration" — so absence of rows is never proof you are safe.

What it does
  • Rolls up egress policy denials over the trailing 30 days into one line per unique combination of source type, destination type, access type, and destination.
  • Counts how many times each blocked egress pattern occurred (denial_count) and stamps when it was first and last seen.
  • Surfaces the reason for the block where available — a DNS response code for DNS destinations, a storage rejection reason for storage destinations (each is empty for the other destination types).
  • Shows denials only — there is no allowed-traffic baseline, so it cannot express an allow-vs-deny ratio.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
network_source_typeThe kind of workload/source the blocked egress originated from (e.g. serverless compute type). Use it to attribute denials to a source class.
destination_typeThe category of the blocked destination — notably `DNS` or `STORAGE`, which govern whether the DNS or storage detail columns are populated.
access_typeThe nature of the attempted access for this denial pattern.
destinationWhere the traffic was trying to go (host, storage endpoint, or resolved name). Your primary triage field — unfamiliar destinations warrant investigation.
dns_rcodeThe DNS response code for the denial; populated only when `destination_type = DNS`, otherwise `NULL`.
storage_rejection_reasonThe reason the storage egress was rejected; populated only when `destination_type = STORAGE`, otherwise `NULL`.
denial_countHow many denial events matched this exact combination in the window. Integer count of blocked attempts — higher means a more persistent or busier blocked pattern.
first_event_timeEarliest denial timestamp for this pattern in the window — when this blocked behavior first appeared.
last_event_timeMost recent denial timestamp — whether this pattern is still active or has stopped.
Sample output
network_source_typedestination_typedestinationdns_rcodestorage_rejection_reasondenial_countlast_event_time
SERVERLESSDNSexfil-c2.example.netREFUSEDNULL2142026-07-06 22:14:03
SERVERLESSSTORAGEs3://unknown-external-bucketNULLNOT_IN_ALLOWLIST372026-07-05 09:41:52
SERVERLESSEXTERNAL198.51.100.24:443NULLNULL52026-07-02 17:08:11
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.access.outbound_networkSole source. Egress network-policy enforcement events — the exfiltration-control audit surface. This query keeps denials only and aggregates them; `dns_event.rcode` and `storage_event.rejection_reason` are read as nested detail, and `event_time` (this table has no `event_date` column) drives the 30-day window.
Caveats & gotchas
  • Preview and feature-gated: requires a configured egress network policy. Empty table means "not assessed — no egress policy / preview not populated," NOT zero exfiltration.
  • Denials only — there is no allowed-traffic baseline, so no allow/deny ratio can be computed.
  • `dns_rcode` is NULL unless `destination_type = DNS`; `storage_rejection_reason` is NULL unless `destination_type = STORAGE`. Empty is expected, not an error.
  • This table has no `event_date` column — windowing is on `event_time` against `current_timestamp()`. Outbound retention is 365 days, but this query is fixed to the trailing 30 days.
  • Regional table — results reflect the region you query, not the whole account.
  • `TABLE_OR_VIEW_NOT_FOUND` (schema not provisioned) and an empty result are both valid "not assessed" outcomes, never a false all-clear.
→
What you do next
Triage the highest `denial_count` rows and any unfamiliar `destination`: confirm whether each blocked egress is expected (a legitimate integration the policy is over-blocking) or hostile (potential exfiltration). For hostile patterns, trace the `network_source_type` back to the originating workload/principal and rotate credentials or lock down the job; for legitimate ones, adjust the egress allowlist. If the result is empty, verify an egress network policy is actually configured before concluding there is no exfiltration risk.
← PreviousBlocked Inbound Network Attemptsaccess_network_inbound_denials All Governance, Access & Security ↑ Next →PII Leaking Into Untagged Columnsaccess_pii_propagation_untagged
Overview / Governance, Access & Security / access_pii_propagation_untagged
13
Governance, Access & Security access_pii_propagation_untagged

PII Leaking Into Untagged Columns

One row per direct column-lineage flow where a sensitivity-tagged source column fed an untagged target column, naming the responsible principal and how many times it happened.

system.access.column_lineagesystem.information_schema.column_tags
queries/governance_access/access_pii_propagation_untagged.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:access_pii_propagation_untagged */
WITH lineage_window AS (
  SELECT source_table_catalog, source_table_schema, source_table_name, source_column_name,
         target_table_catalog, target_table_schema, target_table_name, target_column_name,
         created_by
  FROM system.access.column_lineage
  WHERE direct_access = true                       -- direct flows only; excludes view expansion
    AND source_column_name IS NOT NULL
    AND target_column_name IS NOT NULL
    AND source_table_catalog <> 'system'
    AND event_date >= dateadd(day, -:period_days, current_date())
    AND event_date < current_date()
),
-- Sensitivity-tagged columns (manual governance tags). We compare on the tag_name
-- against common PII/sensitivity conventions, case-insensitively. -- NEEDS WORKSPACE
-- CONFIRMATION: the real tag_name(s) used here; if the convention differs this set
-- under-detects, which is the safe direction (we never invent a tag match).
sensitive_tags AS (
  SELECT catalog_name, schema_name, table_name, column_name,
         tag_name, tag_value
  FROM system.information_schema.column_tags
  WHERE lower(tag_name) IN (
          'pii', 'sensitivity', 'sensitive', 'data_classification', 'classification',
          'confidentiality', 'phi', 'pci'
        )
     OR lower(tag_value) IN (
          'pii', 'sensitive', 'confidential', 'restricted', 'phi', 'pci'
        )
),
-- Every column that carries ANY governance tag (used to decide if a TARGET is tagged
-- at all). Join with '=' on the four discrete identifier columns (must-fix: never LIKE).
any_tagged_column AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT catalog_name, schema_name, table_name, column_name
  FROM system.information_schema.column_tags
)
SELECT lw.source_table_catalog,
       lw.source_table_schema,
       lw.source_table_name,
       lw.source_column_name,
       st.tag_name  AS source_tag_name,
       st.tag_value AS source_tag_value,
       lw.target_table_catalog,
       lw.target_table_schema,
       lw.target_table_name,
       lw.target_column_name,
       CASE
         WHEN lw.created_by IS NULL OR lw.created_by = '__REDACTED__' THEN lw.created_by
         WHEN lw.created_by LIKE '%@%' THEN concat(substr(lw.created_by, 1, 2), '****@****')
         WHEN lw.created_by RLIKE '^[0-9a-fA-F]{8}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{4}-[0-9a-fA-F]{12}$' THEN lw.created_by
         ELSE concat(substr(lw.created_by, 1, 2), '****')
       END AS created_by,
       COUNT(*) AS event_count
FROM lineage_window lw
-- SOURCE column must be sensitivity-tagged (= join on discrete columns).
JOIN sensitive_tags st
  ON  lw.source_table_catalog = st.catalog_name
  AND lw.source_table_schema  = st.schema_name
  AND lw.source_table_name    = st.table_name
  AND lw.source_column_name   = st.column_name
-- TARGET column must be UNTAGGED: anti-join against any tag (= join, must-fix).
LEFT JOIN any_tagged_column tt
  ON  lw.target_table_catalog = tt.catalog_name
  AND lw.target_table_schema  = tt.schema_name
  AND lw.target_table_name    = tt.table_name
  AND lw.target_column_name   = tt.column_name
WHERE tt.column_name IS NULL          -- target carries NO governance tag -> propagation gap
GROUP BY lw.source_table_catalog, lw.source_table_schema, lw.source_table_name,
         lw.source_column_name, st.tag_name, st.tag_value,
         lw.target_table_catalog, lw.target_table_schema, lw.target_table_name,
         lw.target_column_name, lw.created_by
Why it matters

Masking, row filters, and access policies are usually keyed off governance tags, so a sensitive value that flows into a new column which lost its tag silently escapes every one of those controls — the data is still PII, but nothing downstream treats it as such. This query pinpoints exactly those propagation gaps and hands you the principal (`created_by`) who created each flow, so you can re-tag the target and follow up on the pipeline that dropped the tag. Left unaddressed, these are the columns most likely to surface unmasked in a breach or an audit finding. Because tag coverage depends entirely on your own tagging discipline, this is a gap-finder, not a clean bill of health.

What it does
  • Finds sensitive-to-untagged leaks: each row is a sensitivity-tagged source column whose data flowed directly into a target column that carries no governance tag at all.
  • Shows the full source and target identity (catalog, schema, table, column) plus the source's tag name and value, so you can see what classification was lost in transit.
  • Names the responsible principal in created_by (email/name masked, service-principal UUIDs passed through) and counts how many lineage events (event_count) drove each source-to-target gap.
  • Covers only direct flows (view-mediated propagation is intentionally excluded) over a trailing window of :period_days, ending yesterday.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
source_table_catalogCatalog of the tagged source column that the sensitive data originated from.
source_table_schemaSchema of the tagged source column.
source_table_nameTable of the tagged source column.
source_column_nameThe sensitivity-tagged source column — the thing that should keep propagating its classification.
source_tag_nameThe governance tag key on the source (e.g. `pii`, `data_classification`) that marks it sensitive; free-text and case-sensitive in your metastore.
source_tag_valueThe tag value on the source (e.g. `restricted`, `phi`); may be blank if only the tag_name conveyed sensitivity.
target_table_catalogCatalog of the untagged target column the data flowed into.
target_table_schemaSchema of the untagged target column.
target_table_nameTable of the untagged target column.
target_column_nameThe target column that received sensitive data but carries NO governance tag — the propagation gap to fix.
created_byMasked identity of the principal responsible for the flow (email → `da****@****`, other names → first two chars + `****`, service-principal UUIDs and `__REDACTED__` passed through, NULL if unknown) — who to follow up with.
event_countNumber of lineage events observed for this exact source→target→principal gap in the window; higher means a recurring/automated pipeline, not a one-off.
Sample output
source_table_schemasource_column_namesource_tag_nametarget_table_nametarget_column_namecreated_byevent_count
customersemailpiidaily_exportemail_addrda****@****14
hrssndata_classificationcomp_flatssn_raw2f3c9a10-4b7e-4c2a-9f11-8de0a1b2c3d43
billingcard_panpcifraud_featurespan_copyjo****27
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.access.column_lineageSupplies the column-to-column data-flow edges over the look-back window; filtered to direct_access=true so only physical read/write flows (not view expansion) count, and carries created_by, the principal responsible for the flow.
system.information_schema.column_tagsUsed twice: once to identify which SOURCE columns carry a PII/sensitivity tag (matched case-insensitively on common tag_name/tag_value conventions), and once as the full set of tagged columns to prove a TARGET column carries NO governance tag at all.
Caveats & gotchas
  • Requires UC + system-table sharing and `SELECT` on `system.access`; column_lineage is GA but a subset by design (no source row for `INSERT ... VALUES`, MERGE/JDBC/path/temp-view gaps) — this is a coverage-bounded candidate list, never proof of absence.
  • Empty result is ambiguous: it can mean 'no PII is tagged anywhere' rather than 'no propagation gaps.' Coverage depends entirely on your account's tagging discipline.
  • `information_schema.column_tags` is privilege-aware, so the tag inventory (both the 'is source sensitive' and 'is target tagged' checks) only reflects what the collecting principal can see.
  • Sensitivity is inferred heuristically from common tag_name/tag_value names (pii, phi, pci, classification, etc.), matched case-insensitively — your real tag convention is UNVERIFIED; if it differs the query under-detects (the safe direction), never invents a match.
  • Direct flows only (`direct_access=true`): view-expansion / indirect edges are intentionally excluded, so PII propagating through views is invisible here by design.
  • Trailing window of `:period_days` ending yesterday (excludes today); widen `:period_days` only as far as your workspace-configured `system.access.*` retention allows.
  • `created_by` is masked for safe sharing; do not treat masked strings as unique identities.
  • A target is treated as 'untagged' only when it has NO row in column_tags at all (any tag, not just a sensitivity tag, keeps it out of the result); the anti-join uses `=` on the four discrete identifier columns — `LIKE CONCAT(...)` would let `_` act as a wildcard and falsely mark targets as tagged, hiding real gaps.
→
What you do next
Re-apply the correct governance tag to each `target_column_name` (and add a mask/row filter if your controls key off tags), then follow up with the masked `created_by` principal — prioritizing high `event_count` rows — to fix the pipeline that is dropping the classification.
← PreviousBlocked Egress Exfiltration Attemptsaccess_network_outbound_denials All Governance, Access & Security ↑ Next →Row Filter Coverage Inventoryaccess_row_filters_inventory
Overview / Governance, Access & Security / access_row_filters_inventory
14
Governance, Access & Security access_row_filters_inventory

Row Filter Coverage Inventory

A current-state list of every table in the metastore that has a row-level access filter attached, naming the filter and the columns it evaluates.

system.information_schema.row_filters
queries/governance_access/access_row_filters_inventory.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:access_row_filters_inventory */
SELECT table_catalog, table_schema, table_name,
       filter_name, target_columns
FROM system.information_schema.row_filters
Why it matters

Row filters are the control that enforces row-level access — e.g. a rep only seeing their own region's rows — so this inventory is how you confirm those guardrails actually exist on the tables you think are protected. A sensitive table that is missing from this list has no row filter, meaning every reader who can query it sees all rows. Because the view is privilege-aware, a short list can equally mean "few filters" or "the collector can't see the rest," so treat it as a coverage floor, not a clean bill of health, when auditing least-privilege exposure.

What it does
  • Lists one row per table that has a row filter applied, so the row count is your inventory of row-level-protected tables across the metastore.
  • Names the attached filter (filter_name) and the columns it evaluates (target_columns) so you can see which policy governs each table and what it keys on.
  • Identifies each table by its full three-part path (table_catalog, table_schema, table_name) so findings map directly to objects you can inspect or grant on.
  • Shows only what the running principal is allowed to see, so absence of a table is not proof it is unfiltered.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
table_catalogCatalog of the filtered table — the first part of its three-level name.
table_schemaSchema (database) containing the filtered table.
table_nameName of the table that has a row filter attached; each row here is a table confirmed to have row-level filtering.
filter_nameThe name of the row-filter function bound to the table — the policy actually enforcing the row-level restriction.
target_columnsThe column(s) the filter function reads to decide which rows a caller may see (e.g. a region or tenant key); tells you what the filter keys on.
Sample output
table_catalogtable_schematable_namefilter_nametarget_columns
prod_salescrmopportunitiesmain.security.region_filter["region"]
prod_hrpeoplecompensationmain.security.dept_row_filter["department_id"]
prod_financeledgertransactionsmain.security.tenant_scope["tenant_id","business_unit"]
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.information_schema.row_filtersThe sole source: a current-state inventory of row filters applied across the metastore, one row per filtered table, supplying the table identity (table_catalog/schema/name), the attached filter's name, and the columns the filter reads.
Caveats & gotchas
  • Public Preview and requires DBR 12.2 LTS+ — on older runtimes or where the feature isn't provisioned you may get nothing or TABLE_OR_VIEW_NOT_FOUND.
  • Privilege-aware / object-visibility-scoped: only tables the running principal can see are returned. A missing table means 'not filtered OR not visible to the collector' — this OVERSTATES how many tables are unprotected. Always label results 'partial'.
  • Empty != zero != error: no rows can mean no row filters exist, or the feature/schema isn't enabled, or nothing is visible — never read it as a confirmed 'all tables filtered' or 'none need filters'.
  • system.information_schema is metastore-wide (not workspace-scoped), so filter identity here is not tied to any single workspace.
  • This is a pure current-state inventory — no date window, no history, no usage. It cannot tell you when a filter was added, whether it fires correctly, or whether it is the right policy.
  • To measure unfiltered exposure, compare this list against your table inventory rather than assuming absence equals safety.
→
What you do next
Cross-reference this list against your inventory of sensitive tables (from classification and tag inventories): any sensitive table NOT appearing here has no row filter and should be reviewed for a row-level policy — but first confirm the collector's visibility so you don't chase false gaps.
← PreviousPII Leaking Into Untagged Columnsaccess_pii_propagation_untagged All Governance, Access & Security ↑ Next →Table Lineage Blast Radiusaccess_table_lineage_blast_radius
Overview / Governance, Access & Security / access_table_lineage_blast_radius
15
Governance, Access & Security access_table_lineage_blast_radius

Table Lineage Blast Radius

A 90-day rollup of table-level data-flow edges, each classified as READ, WRITE, READ_WRITE, or UNKNOWN, with how many times it occurred, how many distinct principals drove it, and when it last happened.

system.access.table_lineage
queries/governance_access/access_table_lineage_blast_radius.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:access_table_lineage_blast_radius */
SELECT source_table_full_name, target_table_full_name, source_type, target_type,
       entity_type, direct_access,
       CASE WHEN source_type IS NOT NULL AND target_type IS NULL THEN 'READ'
            WHEN target_type IS NOT NULL AND source_type IS NULL THEN 'WRITE'
            WHEN source_type IS NOT NULL AND target_type IS NOT NULL THEN 'READ_WRITE'
            ELSE 'UNKNOWN' END AS access_class,
       COUNT(*) AS event_count,
       COUNT(DISTINCT created_by) AS distinct_principals,
       MAX(event_time) AS last_event_time
FROM system.access.table_lineage
WHERE event_date >= current_date() - INTERVAL 90 DAYS
  AND event_date < current_date()
GROUP BY 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7
Why it matters

Before you alter or drop a table, you need to know what reads from it and who depends on it — dropping a table that silently feeds a dozen downstream tables or reports is a self-inflicted outage. This query turns raw lineage into a per-edge blast-radius map so an admin can see the downstream reach and the number of distinct principals touching each relationship. The critical risk is misreading it: because MERGE, JDBC, path-based, and temp-view operations are never captured, an empty result is a coverage gap, not proof the table is unused — treating it as 'safe to drop' is how you break a pipeline nobody knew existed.

What it does
  • Rolls up 90 days of table-level lineage into one row per distinct data-flow edge (a source table, a target table, and their entity/access attributes).
  • Labels each edge READ, WRITE, READ_WRITE, or UNKNOWN based on which side of the flow is present — so you can tell consumers of a table from producers of it.
  • Quantifies each edge with an event count, a distinct-principals count, and the most-recent event time, so hot, widely-used relationships stand out from one-off or stale ones.
  • Separates direct reads/writes from indirect view-expansion access, keeping first-hand usage distinct from inherited access.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
source_table_full_nameFully-qualified name of the upstream table being read from; NULL on write-only events (nothing was read).
target_table_full_nameFully-qualified name of the downstream table being written to; NULL on read-only events (nothing was written).
source_typeEntity type of the source side (e.g. TABLE, PATH); NULL when there is no source, which the CASE reads as a WRITE.
target_typeEntity type of the target side; NULL when there is no target, which the CASE reads as a READ.
entity_typeThe kind of operation/entity that produced the lineage edge (the observed job/query/entity category).
direct_accesstrue = a direct read/write of the table; false = indirect access via view expansion — do not count indirect edges as first-hand usage.
access_classDerived label from the source_type/target_type NULL pattern: READ (source only), WRITE (target only), READ_WRITE (both present), or UNKNOWN (neither) — the plain-English direction of the flow.
event_countNumber of lineage events (COUNT(*)) collapsed into this edge over the 90-day window — a usage-frequency proxy, not a cost or row count.
distinct_principalsCount of distinct `created_by` identities that drove this edge — how many different people/service principals depend on the relationship.
last_event_timeMost recent event_time (MAX) this edge was observed — use it to tell live relationships from stale ones.
Sample output
source_table_full_nametarget_table_full_nameaccess_classdirect_accessevent_countdistinct_principalslast_event_time
prod.sales.ordersNULLREADtrue4210172026-07-05 23:41:02
NULLprod.finance.daily_revenueWRITEtrue9622026-07-05 05:12:44
prod.sales.ordersprod.finance.daily_revenueREAD_WRITEfalse3112026-07-04 05:12:40
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.access.table_lineageSole source: table-level data-flow lineage, one row per observed source→target edge per event. Supplies both table names, `source_type`/`target_type` (whose NULL pattern derives the READ/WRITE/READ_WRITE class), `entity_type`, `direct_access`, `created_by` (counted as distinct principals), and `event_time`/`event_date` (used for the 90-day window).
Caveats & gotchas
  • Window is a fixed trailing 90 days on `event_date` and excludes today (`event_date >= current_date() - INTERVAL 90 DAYS AND event_date < current_date()`) — there is no `:period_days` parameter here, and today's partial-day events are not included.
  • Retention: table_lineage is GA with rolling 365-day retention, but actual retention on `system.access.*` is workspace-configurable, so a 90-day window may still under-cover if retention was shortened.
  • Empty lineage = NOT CAPTURED, never 'unused' — MERGE, JDBC, path-based, and temp-view operations are not observed. This is a coverage gap; never read absence as 'safe to drop.'
  • The `access_class` CASE keys on the parallel `source_type` / `target_type` NULLs (not the `*_full_name` NULLs): source present + target NULL = READ, target present + source NULL = WRITE, both present = READ_WRITE, neither = UNKNOWN — reproducing the documented read/write rule.
  • `direct_access = false` means indirect / view-expansion access, not a direct read or write — do not treat indirect edges as first-hand usage.
  • Requires UC + system-table sharing and `SELECT` on `system.access`; `TABLE_OR_VIEW_NOT_FOUND` or an empty result is a valid 'not provisioned / not captured' outcome, not a clean bill.
  • Regional table — results reflect only the region/workspace scope where the lineage was recorded.
  • `distinct_principals` counts distinct `created_by` values per edge; the identities themselves are not emitted as a column here.
→
What you do next
Pick the table you plan to change or drop, filter to rows where it appears as `source_table_full_name` (access_class READ / READ_WRITE), and read off `distinct_principals` and the downstream `target_table_full_name` values to size the blast radius before you touch it — then cross-check against `system.access.audit` and query history, because empty lineage means "not captured," not "safe to drop."
← PreviousRow Filter Coverage Inventoryaccess_row_filters_inventory All Governance, Access & Security ↑ Next →Manual Governance Tag Inventoryaccess_tags_inventory
Overview / Governance, Access & Security / access_tags_inventory
16
Governance, Access & Security access_tags_inventory

Manual Governance Tag Inventory

A rollup of every manual governance tag applied at column and table scope, showing each tag name/value pair and how many objects carry it.

system.information_schema.column_tagssystem.information_schema.table_tags
queries/governance_access/access_tags_inventory.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:access_tags_inventory */
SELECT 'COLUMN' AS object_scope, TAG_NAME, TAG_VALUE,
       COUNT(*) AS tagged_object_count
FROM system.information_schema.column_tags
GROUP BY 1, 2, 3
UNION ALL
SELECT 'TABLE' AS object_scope, TAG_NAME, TAG_VALUE,
       COUNT(*) AS tagged_object_count
FROM system.information_schema.table_tags
GROUP BY 1, 2, 3
Why it matters

Manual tags are how you assert governance intent — marking columns and tables as PII, confidential, cost-center-owned, or retention-bound — and they drive tag-based access policies and downstream lineage checks. This query tells you whether that tagging discipline actually exists or is aspirational: sparse or inconsistent tag values mean policies keyed on tags silently protect nothing. It is the manual-tag counterpart to auto-detected classification, so gaps here are places where humans, not the scanner, were supposed to label sensitivity and did not.

What it does
  • Inventories every manual governance tag in the metastore, split into two scopes: column-level and table-level.
  • For each distinct tag name and value, tells you how many objects carry it — so you see which tags are used heavily versus barely at all.
  • Gives a discipline snapshot you can compare against auto-classification: which sensitivity/ownership labels humans actually applied, and where coverage is thin.
  • Deliberately covers only COLUMN and TABLE scope — catalog, schema, and volume tags are excluded as unverified.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
object_scopeWhich object level the tag sits on: the literal `'COLUMN'` (from column_tags) or `'TABLE'` (from table_tags). Use it to separate fine-grained column labels from coarser table labels.
TAG_NAMEThe free-text, case-sensitive tag key (e.g. `pii`, `data_sensitivity`, `cost_center`). Treat two names differing only in case as distinct.
TAG_VALUEThe free-text, case-sensitive value assigned for that tag name (e.g. `high`, `confidential`). May be blank if tags are applied as keys without values.
tagged_object_countNumber of objects (columns or tables, per object_scope) carrying that exact tag name/value pair. An integer count of objects, privilege-scoped to what the running principal can see — a low number can mean thin tagging or limited visibility.
Sample output
object_scopeTAG_NAMETAG_VALUEtagged_object_count
COLUMNpiiemail42
COLUMNdata_sensitivityhigh118
TABLEcost_centerfinance7
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.information_schema.column_tagsSource of manually-applied column-level governance tags; contributes the COLUMN-scope rows, counting objects per tag name/value pair.
system.information_schema.table_tagsSource of manually-applied table-level governance tags; contributes the TABLE-scope rows, counting objects per tag name/value pair.
Caveats & gotchas
  • Privilege-aware / privilege-scoped: the inventory reflects only tags on objects the running principal can see — label results "partial," never a complete metastore-wide census. A missing tag can mean "not visible," not "not tagged."
  • Empty is not the same as clean: no rows can mean nothing is tagged OR nothing is visible. UC must be present with system-table sharing; treat absence as "not assessed."
  • Scope-limited by design: only COLUMN_TAGS and TABLE_TAGS are read (verbatim-confirmed). CATALOG_TAGS / SCHEMA_TAGS / VOLUME_TAGS are inferred-only and intentionally excluded — do not assume the answer covers those scopes.
  • Manual tags here are distinct from auto-detected classification in system.data_classification.results — this measures human-applied labels, not scanner findings; do not conflate the two.
  • Tag names and values are free-text and case-sensitive, so near-duplicate spellings/casings appear as separate rows and can fragment counts.
  • No date window and no cost/DBU figures — this is a current-state metadata inventory; tagged_object_count is a count of objects, not spend or usage.
→
What you do next
Rank tag name/value pairs by tagged_object_count to find sparsely-used or fragmented (case/spelling-inconsistent) tags, then reconcile column-scope sensitivity tags against auto-classification results to find sensitive columns humans failed to label — and confirm any tag-based access policies are keyed to values that objects actually carry.
← PreviousTable Lineage Blast Radiusaccess_table_lineage_blast_radius All Governance, Access & Security ↑ Next →Vector Search Query Traffic by Endpointaccess_vector_search_traffic
Overview / Governance, Access & Security / access_vector_search_traffic
17
Governance, Access & Security access_vector_search_traffic

Vector Search Query Traffic by Endpoint

A daily count of Vector Search query and scan events per endpoint over the trailing window, used to tell which endpoints are actually being used versus provisioned-but-idle.

system.access.audit
queries/governance_access/access_vector_search_traffic.sql View source ↗ 
/* databricks_audit:access_vector_search_traffic */
SELECT event_date,
       action_name,
       CASE WHEN request_params['endpoint_name'] IS NULL THEN request_params['endpoint_name'] ELSE concat(substr(request_params['endpoint_name'], 1, 2), '****') END AS endpoint_name,
       COUNT(*) AS event_count
FROM system.access.audit
WHERE service_name = 'vectorSearch'
  AND action_name IN (
        'queryVectorIndex', 'queryVectorIndexNextPage',
        'queryVectorIndexRouteOptimized', 'scanVectorIndex',
        'scanVectorIndexRouteOptimized')
  AND event_date >= dateadd(day, -:period_days, current_date())
  AND event_date < current_date()
GROUP BY event_date, action_name, request_params['endpoint_name']
Why it matters

Vector Search endpoints bill for provisioned compute whether or not anything queries them, so an endpoint that costs money every day but serves zero query/scan traffic is pure waste. This query is the traffic half of idle-endpoint detection: cross-referenced with Vector Search spend, an endpoint that shows spend but has NO row here is a provisioned-but-unqueried retire candidate. Retiring dead endpoints directly removes recurring compute charges, while confirming live traffic protects you from cutting one that is quietly in use.

What it does
  • Lists every Vector Search query and scan action recorded in the audit log over the trailing :period_days window (ending yesterday), counted per endpoint per day.
  • Breaks the traffic down by action so you can see whether an endpoint is serving live queries, paginated reads, or index scans.
  • Produces an event count per day so activity trends (and silent gaps) are visible day by day.
  • Masks each endpoint name to its first two characters — this is a usage signal, not a full inventory. An endpoint that bills spend but never appears here is the idle/retire candidate; absence of this data means idle simply cannot be assessed.
How to read the output
ColumnHow to read it
event_dateThe calendar day (partition column) on which the Vector Search events occurred; the window is the trailing :period_days days and excludes today.
action_nameThe specific Vector Search operation — one of queryVectorIndex, queryVectorIndexNextPage, queryVectorIndexRouteOptimized, scanVectorIndex, or scanVectorIndexRouteOptimized. Query = live retrieval, scan = index scan.
endpoint_nameThe Vector Search endpoint that received the traffic, truncated to its first two characters plus '****' for safe sharing; NULL passes through if the audit event carried no endpoint_name. Match back to a full name by prefix when correlating with spend.
event_countNumber of audited events for that day/action/endpoint combination. A positive count is proof of use; no row at all for an endpoint means no query/scan traffic in the window.
Sample output
event_dateaction_nameendpoint_nameevent_count
2026-07-02queryVectorIndexpr****1842
2026-07-02queryVectorIndexNextPagepr****310
2026-07-03scanVectorIndexst****4
Illustrative synthetic rows — not real account data
System tables it reads
TableRole in this query
system.access.auditThe Unity Catalog audit log; the query reads only Vector Search events (service_name='vectorSearch') for the confirmed query/scan action set, pulls the endpoint from request_params['endpoint_name'], and counts events per day per action per endpoint.
Caveats & gotchas
  • Availability: system.access.audit is Preview and requires UC + system-table sharing enabled on the metastore plus SELECT on system.access; a TABLE_OR_VIEW_NOT_FOUND or empty result is not proof of 'no traffic.'
  • Empty != zero != error: an endpoint missing from the output means no query/scan events were captured, which is the whole point (idle candidate) — but only when paired with spend. If this source is unavailable, idle cannot be assessed and the finding stays visibility-only; never assume idle from absence alone.
  • event_count is a count of audit events, NOT dollars and NOT DBUs — it says nothing about cost by itself; retire decisions require joining to Vector Search spend.
  • The idle/retire conclusion comes from combining this with cost_vector_search_spend in-engine (an endpoint that bills but has no row here). Do not read this query on its own as a retire recommendation.
  • service_name='vectorSearch' and the five query/scan action names are confirmed in Databricks docs; the endpoint is read from request_params['endpoint_name'], which can be NULL and then passes through as NULL (unmasked).
  • endpoint_name is truncated to its first two characters — prefix collisions are possible, so correlate to spend by prefix carefully rather than assuming a unique match.
  • ~15-minute audit ingest lag; the window excludes today (event_date < current_date) so the most recent partial day is not counted.
  • Window is the trailing :period_days ending yesterday; widen it only as far as your workspace-configured system.access retention allows — a short window can make a genuinely-used-but-infrequent endpoint look idle.
→
What you do next
Join this endpoint-traffic list against your Vector Search spend: any endpoint that incurs spend but has zero rows here over a representative window is a retire candidate — confirm ownership, then decommission or downsize it to stop the recurring compute charge. Where traffic exists, keep the endpoint and note its usage pattern.
← PreviousManual Governance Tag Inventoryaccess_tags_inventory All Governance, Access & Security ↑
